title: Reactive and Decoupled Control of Mobile Manipulators via Spatially Modulated Velocity Fields
authors: Tadej Petrič, Leon Žlajpah
abstract: Mobile manipulators enable robotic systems to operate flexibly in extended, dynamic, and unstructured environments. A key challenge in such systems is achieving coordinated and adaptive behavior between the mobile base and the manipulator without relying on global localization or predefined plans. This paper presents a reactive and fully local control strategy for mobile base based on spatially modulated velocity fields defined in the mobile base’s frame. The method enables real-time end-effector tracking across the full workspace by exploiting only the internal kinematics of the arm. Using distance- and angle-based activation thresholds, the controller generates smooth linear and angular velocity commands for mobile base that support context-aware transitions between stationary, extension, and retreat modes. Importantly, the control framework is decoupled from the manipulator, allowing compatibility with autonomous planning, teleoperation, or physical guidance. The proposed architecture is particularly well suited for dynamic and collaborative manufacturing scenarios, where mobile robots must operate safely, robustly, and without dependence on global pose estimates. Experiments with a real mobile manipulator demonstrate stable and precise behavior across diverse trajectories, confirming the suitability of the method for adaptive, robust, and autonomous robotic systems.

title: VAPE-Net: An Extrinsic-Pose-Agnostic Viewpoint-Aware Progressive Enhancement Framework for Pure-Visual Vehicle-Infrastructure Cooperative Perception
authors: Jiankun Peng, Luwei Wang, Di Wu, Shen Li, Shuangzhi Yu, Ziyu Guo, Xiaoshuang Che, Chunye Ma
abstract: Camera-based Vehicle-Infrastructure Cooperative 3D object detection (VIC3D) faces challenges including cross-view feature misalignment caused by viewpoint discrepancies, as well as semantic degradation and redundancy introduced by direct feature concatenation. To alleviate these issues, a pure-visual 3D object detection framework, VAPE-Net, is proposed for VIC3D. The framework comprises two key modules: (1) A Viewpoint-Aware Enhancement (VAE) module is introduced on both the vehicle-side and infrastructure-side branches. Instead of requiring precise extrinsic matrices for rigid cross-view geometric alignment, it captures semantic and geometric consistency by modeling feature correlations across adjacent and cross-view frames while only conditioning on static camera metadata, thereby improving robustness under moderate calibration perturbations. (2) A Stage-wise Cascaded Feature Fusion (SCFF) module is used to fuse voxel features in multiple stages, with a balancing mechanism to reduce information loss and suppress noise during fusion. Experiments on the real-world DAIR-V2X dataset demonstrate that VAPE-Net has a significant advantage over common fusion methods, achieving 16.05% AP3D and 22.03% APBEV. This research expands the model paradigm for cooperative perception dominated by vision, providing new insights and support for the theoretical framework of VIC3D.

title: A Time-Space Fusion Early Diagnostic Framework for PEM Fuel Cell Faults with Deep Learning
authors: Shengxiang Fu, Dongeon Kim, He Qi, Namwook Kim, Chunhua Zheng
abstract: An accurate fault diagnostic method of proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells can guide adjustments for control strategies and enable the system to operate under optimal conditions. However, the performance of existing PEM fuel cell fault diagnostic methods remains subject to uncertainty and limited verifiability due to the lack of real fault data, and the consideration of non-uniformity inside the stack is insufficient under faulty conditions. To address this issue, a time-space fusion early diagnostic framework for PEM fuel cell faults is proposed in this study, which is designed to provide early warnings at the onset of electrochemical mechanism deviation rather than the manifestation of performance degradation. Polarization tests are conducted to extract the related abnormalities data, and three temporal latent indicators are constructed for developing a deep learning (DL)-based method to predict the internal dynamic state of the stack. Besides, two spatial indicators are defined to characterize cell-level voltage non-uniformity as complementary information for early fault detection. By integrating the states of predicted temporal latent indicators and spatial indicators, a set of early diagnostic rules for membrane dry-out, flooding, and oxygen starvation is ultimately formulated. The results show that the proposed framework has higher credibility and mechanism consistency.

title: Gait adaptation to asymmetric foot-ground compliance applied by robotic footwear
authors: Mark Price, Wouter Hoogkamer, Meghan E. Huber
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Perturbing foot-ground interaction dynamics has shown promise for eliciting adaptations to inter-limb weight-bearing symmetry, a critical target for rehabilitation of asymmetrical neuromotor deficits affecting gait. To date, this perturbation paradigm has been delivered via adjustable stiffness treadmills, in which the treadmill deck displaces under the walker’s foot during the stance phase of gait. Recently, we developed robotic footwear capable of delivering asymmetrical ground stiffness perturbations during walking, offering a portable experimental platform for asymmetrical ground stiffness perturbations, allowing them to be executed overground and on conventional treadmills. In this study, we quantified kinetic and spatio-temporal gait adaptation to a foot-ground compliance perturbation delivered by the novel robotic footwear. We found that participants changed their vertical ground reaction force impulse, vertical push-off peak, and peak braking force to favor the unperturbed-side limb after the perturbation was removed, indicating a shift in neuromotor control elicited by the perturbation. We conclude that the robotic footwear can elicit weight bearing adaptations similar to adjustable stiffness treadmills, and that the dissipative mechanical properties of the shoes likely play a key role in the direction of the adaptation.

title: Momentum Gradient Descent with Kinematic Prediction-based Initialization for High-Accuracy Star Sensor Attitude Estimation
authors: Huijuan Zhang, Yizhuo Zhu, Changlin Wang, Yuanjin Yu
abstract: The traditional gradient descent method for attitude estimation is prone to local optimum, and the real-time performance is poor in dynamic environments. To address these issues, this paper proposes an improved attitude estimation algorithm based on star sensor, which integrates momentum gradient descent (MGD) with kinematic prediction-based initialization (KPI). Firstly, the momentum term is introduced to accumulate the historical gradient information, so that the iterative direction could be optimized and the iterative oscillations could be effectively suppressed. Furthermore, the initial value prediction mechanism is designed based on the continuity of spacecraft dynamics. The attitude quaternion of the previous moment is fused with the angular velocity measured by the gyroscope, and the recursive estimation of the initial value is dynamically achieved through the attitude kinematic equation. Key MGD parameters are determined via dedicated sensitivity analysis to balance convergence speed and accuracy. During the attitude maneuver mode, MGD reduces the MAE by 73.28% and the RMSE by 72.48% compared with GD. For attitude stability control mode, MGD decreases the MAE by 72.20% and the RMSE by 72.09% relative to GD. Furthermore, in contrast to MGD with temporal recursive initialization, the proposed MGD-KPI further reduces the MAE by 79.86% and the RMSE by 79.76%, while achieving the shortest execution time. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed MGD with KPI algorithm achieves significant improvements in accuracy and computational efficiency for attitude estimation.

title: Field-agnostic Beam Focusing and Tracking for ISAC
authors: Hyunwoo Park, Donggeon Kim, Andrea Conti, Sunwoo Kim, Moe Z. Win
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Fast beam beam focusing and tracking (BFT) serves as a key enabler for delivering precise location awareness and high communication capacity in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) for next-generation wireless networks. However, major challenges arise from user mobility and the inherent coexistence of near field and far-field regions in extremely large-scale antenna arrays, leading to critical field-model mismatches and prohibitively high computational complexity, which demand efficient and adaptive tracking solutions. This paper proposes a method that addresses the coverage discontinuity between the near-field and far-field, as well as the computational difficulties associated with BFT. To achieve computationally efficient field-agnostic BFT, which implies being agnostic to near/far-field coverage discontinuities, two strategies are proposed. First, an optimization problem for radar-like echo signal processing of integrated sensing and communication is formulated to enable field-agnostic BFT across fields while reducing beam training overhead. Next, a deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) framework is used to provide a computationally efficient solution for real-time execution. Results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves robust and precise tracking across all fields with low complexity, outperforming conventional tracking methods. Further more, the proposed method is more computationally efficient than existing methods, thus enabling real-time BFT for next-generation wireless networks.

title: Curvilinear Particle-in-Cell Simulations of Plasma Sheath Effects on Impedance of VLF Antenna in the Magnetosphere
authors: Kevin A. Shipman, Patrick L. Colestock, Gian Luca Delzanno, Mark A. Gilmore, Quinn R. Marksteiner, Daniil Svyatsky, Nikolai Yampolsky
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: In this paper we investigate how a magnetospheric plasma sheath effects the impedance of a spaceborne very low frequency (VLF) dipole. It has been suggested that a VLF antenna can be used to generate whistler waves to remediate magnetically trapped high-energy electrons from Earth’s radiation belts. The magnetosphere is composed of a low-density plasma that forms a nonlinear plasma sheath around the antenna, changing the impedance of the antenna and therefore changing how the effective antenna is able to couple radiation to the background environment. Therefore it is important to characterize the plasma sheath behavior in terms of impedance and sheath structure. We use a three-dimensional electrostatic curvilinear particle-in-cell (CPIC) code to simulate a electrically short dipole antenna operating in the Earth’s inner magnetosphere. The impedance of the sheath is approximated and is compared to existing radiation impedance models and the plasma sheath structure and time-behavior is characterized.

title: Participatory Design Back to its Political Roots : Insights from the Full Papers
authors: Daniela Selloni
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: UAV Swarm-Based Dynamic Differential GNSS with Collaborative Multipath Mitigation: Low-Altitude Augmentation System for Emergency Positioning
authors: Yanyan Zhao, Lilin Yan, Yibo Wu, Lang Mi, Hongwei Zhao
abstract: GNSS differential augmentation assumes precisely known reference coordinates and controllable multipath. When emergency scenarios force reference stations onto UAV swarms, this premise collapses: multipath errors exhibit non-Gaussian heavy tails (kurtosis 5–15), biasing least-squares corrections; UAV coordinates carry 0.3–0.5 m uncertainty propagating to all users; and independent estimation in sparse formations amplifies uncertainty. The core finding is that correction-side robustification benefits all users through the “one-to-many” architecture, and probabilistic distribution fusion guarantees optimality under worst-case perturbations. A three-layer cascaded framework is constructed: the maximum correntropy criterion (MCC) replaces least-squares for clock bias estimation, with the analytical optimal kernel bandwidth derived under a Gaussian mixture multipath model and a MAD adaptive strategy designed; a precision-weighted multi-source correction distribution fusion framework is established, proved distributionally robust optimal via Wasserstein uncertainty sets; James-Stein shrinkage injects formation geometry constraints, with an analytical upper bound derived linking shrinkage gain to the geometry matrix condition number. Three-scenario vehicle experiments yield a horizontal RMSE of 0.45 m in open areas, consistent with the theoretical lower bound, an average 51.0% improvement over the best baseline (53.3% in urban canyons), and initialization under 5 minutes. Ablation experiments confirm MCC contributes 36.7% error reduction, and the cascaded gain significantly exceeds the best pairwise combination.

title: Enhancing Cell-Free SWIPT IoT Networks By Active RIS
authors: Zaixin Lu, Yao Zhang, Hao Fang, Peipei Chen, Shaowei Jiang, Jianrong Bao, Longxiang Yang
abstract: In the context of Internet of Things (IoT) networks, this work investigates an active reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-aided cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) system and establishes an integrated analytical frame-work that rigorously characterizes both the inherent double-fading effect of RIS-assisted propagation and the power-gain coupling introduced by the active RIS. A weighted sum-rate (WSR) maximization problem is then formulated under the constraints of access point (AP) power budgets, the RIS power consumption limitations, and the receiver energy harvesting requirements. To manage the resulting highly non-convex structure, the original problem is equivalently reformulated as a weighted minimum mean squared error (WMMSE) minimization problem, which enables the development of an efficient alternating optimization algorithm. At each iteration, the MMSE decoding vectors are firstly updated in closed form, while the energy signal covariance matrices, the AP information beamformers, and the active RIS reflection coefficients are subsequently optimized via semi-definite programming, successive convex approximation, and augmented Lagrangian method, respectively. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the adoption of an active RIS effectively mitigates the double-fading effect and achieves performance comparable to that of passive RIS with significantly fewer reflecting elements, while the proposed joint optimization strategy yields substantial WSR gains and further enlarges the performance gap between the optimized active and passive RIS schemes.

title: Tactile-Sensor-Embedded Treadmill for Deep Learning-Based Step-Length Measurement
authors: Sejun Park, Jaehyeon Baik, Yunho Choi, Kyung-Joong Kim, Hosu Lee
abstract: Step-length is a widely used metric for assessing health and disease status. However, existing step-length measurement systems are often expensive or require body-attached sensors, while vision-based alternatives struggle with reliable foot tracking. To address these limitations, we propose a deep-learning-based approach for step-length estimation using a tactile-sensor-embedded treadmill. Baseline step-length was extracted from pressure data using noise filtering and a stacking algorithm. Then, stepwise features were used to refine the estimates through regression modeling, and multiple models were compared using leave-one-out cross-validation. The deep neural network model achieved the best performance, exhibiting a percentage error lower than those of previously reported approaches. These findings suggest that the proposed method offers a cost-effective, nonwearable alternative for accessible quantitative gait analysis.

title: DB-CoGNN: A Dual-Branch Synergistic Contrastive Graph Neural Network for Robust Small-Signal Stability Assessment
authors: Hailiang Li, Zhaoming Song, Shiyuan Zhang, Jinghan He
abstract: Conventional small-signal stability assessment methods based on graph neural network face limitations in capturing the cross-scale dynamic features of power systems. Message-passing models easily suffer from over-smoothing when stacked deeply to capture long-range dependencies, while global self-attention models, despite directly modeling long-range relationships, tend to lose local details and incur high computational costs. Addressing these issues, this paper proposes a Dual-Branch Synergistic Contrastive Graph Neural Network (DB-CoGNN). The architecture designs a parallel dual-branch structure: a local encoder preserves crucial topological inductive bias to precisely capture neighborhood dynamics, while a global encoder employs a sparse multi-hop mechanism to effectively capture long-range dependencies while reducing computational complexity. These two branches dynamically synergize through a gated cross-attention mechanism, facilitating interaction between local and global information. Furthermore, an innovative multi-level contrastive learning framework acts as a self-supervised regularizer, enforcing physical consistency between the dual representations and significantly enhancing the model’s generalization ability to unseen operating conditions and topologies. Validations on the Texas 2000-bus system demonstrate that DB-CoGNN exhibits significant performance and robustness advantages over classical GNN models.

title: LGIEA: A Local-Global Information Guided Evolutionary Algorithm for Influence Maximization
authors: Haipeng Yang, Chuang Liu, Xinxiang Xu, Yuanyuan Ge, Yao Lu, Lei Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: The influence maximization (IM) problem has attracted the attention of many researchers. However, most of the existing algorithms do not fully use the comprehensive information (local and global information) of the network, which causes the performance of these algorithms to decrease on some networks. To this end, this paper proposes a local-global information guided evolutionary algorithm (LGIEA) for IM problem in social networks. In LGIEA, the local-global information of the network is fully utilized to find the nodes with high potential influence during the evolutionary optimization. Specifically, local information pertains to the neighborhood surrounding the node, whereas global information is associated with the shell value of the node. By leveraging the aforementioned local-global information, we propose an evolutionary algorithm that constructs a novel local-global metric and employs a search space reduction strategy to guide population evolution. Additionally, we design strategies for initialization, crossover and mutation to improve the evolutionary process. Experimental results on 12 real-world networks and four simulated networks verify the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed LGIEA in comparison with nine representative baseline algorithms and four variants of LGIEA.

title: Compact High-Linearity and Wide-Range Permittivity Measurement Phase-Variation Sensor Based on Electronically Tunable Structure
authors: Lili Qu, Jiawei Wang, Shuie Shi, Mengyao Li, Yumiao Wang
abstract: This article presents a compact phase- variation sensor that achieves high linearity and a large phase variation at a single frequency for wide-range permittivity measurements. The sensing principle operates by translating variations in the dielectric constant of the material under test (MUT) into a measurable phase shift of the reflection coefficient at the operating frequency, which serves as the output variable. To prioritize high linearity over extreme sensitivity, the sensing part utilizes a two- cascaded different‑impedance coupled-line (TCDICL) structure, where a controllable phase slope is created by adjusting the width of the second line. The measurement range is further extended by using two varactors placed at the TCDICL terminals, allowing the sensor to cover four distinct dielectric constant ranges: 1–5, 5–10, 10–17, and 17–23 with good impedance matching and a large phase variation. The proposed design was validated using a fabricated prototype sensor, which demonstrated a maximum linearity of 0.97 and a phase variation range of 637° across the dielectric constant range of 1 to 23. Due to its exceptionally compact overall and sensing area dimensions, the proposed sensor achieves a very high figure of merit (FoM = 13255°/λ²). This FoM is defined as the ratio of maximum sensitivity to the sensing area size, expressed in terms of the squared guided wavelength λ². Experiments reveal that, compared with other highly sensitive phase-variation sensors, the proposed device is more suitable for wide-range measurement of low dielectric constant materials.

title: On the role of stubbornness in Nash equilibrium problems
authors: Barbara Franci, Filippo Fabiani, Lorenzo Zino
abstract: We consider Nash equilibrium (NE) seeking in partial-decision information regimes where the data circulating across the agents’ communication network are affected by the agents’ stubborn behavior. Unlike traditional linear averaging approaches, the communication protocol is here based on a nonlinear, state-dependent dynamics that captures the agents’ biases, which hinders the direct application of standard techniques. Building on the explicit characterization of the convergence rate of the opinion dynamics resulting from the proposed scheme, and leveraging operator theory, we embed the communication pattern into two novel NE seeking algorithms for which we derive rigorous convergence guarantees.

title: Feature Map Compression for Split Learning in Mobile Wireless Environment: Theory and Practice
authors: Wenxuan Zhou, Zhihao Qu, Baoliu Ye, Shen-Huan Lyu, Miao Cai, Mingtao Ji, Song Guo
abstract: Split Learning (SL) enables collaborative model training by distributing computation between resource constrained mobile edge devices and a central server. In this distributed framework, mobile edge devices compute the early layers of the model and transmit feature maps to the server. However, due to practical mobile wireless bandwidth limitations, these feature maps should be compressed before transmission. In this paper, we propose a lightweight Mask-Encoded Spar sification (MS) compressor that enhances Top-k sparsification with a narrow-bit mask, significantly reducing compression error while improving computational and communication efficiency. Our theoretical analyses reveal that feature map compression generally induces estimator bias in the gradients. Furthermore, we establish the relationships between compression error and convergence, as well as between the choice of cutlayer and output error. The first relationship demonstrates that reducing compression error facilitates convergence, confirming the advantage of MS, whereas the second provides theoretical guidance for determining the cutlayer, indicating that earlier layers are more sensitive to compression errors. Extensive experiments on vision and language models demonstrate that our method outperforms existing compression techniques in terms of compression error and model performance, achieving up to a 21.38× speedup in communication efficiency under wireless environments.

title: Horizontal Transfer Strategy Based Collaborative Optimization for Power Grid Emergency Resource Scheduling
authors: Jiahua Liu, Huizhong Shi, Nan Ye, Chengyue Yang, Mingyu Ma, Chuanmin Mi, Ningna Liao
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In power system emergency management, timely resource scheduling is critical for rapid recovery and loss reduction. However, traditional vertical allocation has high crossregional barriers, poor collaboration, inaccurate supply-demand matching, and delayed response, restricting post-disaster recovery efficiency. To solve these problems, this paper proposes a collaborative scheduling optimization method with horizontal allocation for grid emergency resources. It realizes accurate demand matching via cross-regional resource sharing, and optimizes the scheme using an incentive adaptive genetic algorithm (IAGA) to minimize cost and maximize efficiency. Experiments show that compared with the traditional vertical model, the proposed method reduces total emergency scheduling cost by 16.86%, improves resource utilization by 20%, and shortens response time by 18%. It significantly enhances allocation accuracy and efficiency, verifies feasibility and effectiveness in power system emergency management, and provides an innovative strategy and theoretical support for the timeliness and economy of grid emergency responses, suitable for post-disaster management of power systems and smart grids.

title: Novel Structure and Control Strategy for Electrolysis Hydrogen Powered by Offshore Wind Power
authors: Xiaoyan Zhang, Yiping Yang, Xurui Huang, Yujing Zhang, Ying Yang, Dingrong Ou, Ziyu Yu, Yingyuan Zhu, Bosen Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The utilization of offshore wind power is confronted with a series of problems such as expensive cables. Using offshore wind power to produce hydrogen is a solution to overcome these barriers. Furthermore, the produced green hydrogen is also an important factor in carbon neutrality goals. Without cables to be connected to the power grid, the biggest problem is the power balance and how to achieve maximum power point tracking of the wind turbine. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel system structure and establishes its model. Furthermore, a power closed-loop control strategy is proposed, which is developed based on the natural output characteristics and the rotor speed of the wind turbine. Therefore, it substantially reduces engineering complexity, which is of paramount importance in the offshore scenario. MATLAB/Simulink simulations validate the stability of the system and the effectiveness of the strategy.

title: Improved YOLOv8-Based Small Target Defect Identification in Substations
authors: Hengzhe Shi, Zhengnan Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the challenges in equipment detection within substation environments-namely complex operational conditions and scarce defect samples-this study proposes an improved model, YOLOv8-BFT. Without altering the core structure of the YOLOv8 network, the model integrates a class-frequency-based long-tail resampling strategy at the image level, combined with a high-resolution fine-tuning mechanism to enhance detection performance for low-frequency classes and small targets. Experimental results on a real-world substation dataset comprising 17 equipment categories, with variations in lighting, weather, and viewing angles, demonstrate that YOLOv8-BFT achieves precision, recall, mAP@0.5, and mAP@0.5:0.95 scores of 0.6911, 0.5016, 0.5285, and 0.3621, respectively. These metrics outperform baseline models YOLOv5 and YOLOv8, verifying its robustness in complex operational conditions and offering a feasible solution for practical engineering applications.

title: PQV Modeling and Voltage Regulation Strategy for Distributed Photovoltaic Systems Under Reverse Power Flow Conditions
authors: Zhenhuan Xu, Liang Cao, Hongwei Zhang, Bing Fan
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the extensive integration of distributed photovoltaic (PV) systems into low-voltage distribution networks, reverse power flow is likely to occur when the PV output exceeds the local load demand during the daytime, leading to voltage rise at the end nodes of the lines. To address the voltage limit violation risk under reverse power flow conditions in 380 V low-voltage distribution networks, this paper starts from the active powerreactive power-voltage (PQV) coupling relationship and establishes a PQV mathematical model under reverse power flow conditions. It analyzes the mechanism of how active and reactive power affect the voltage variation at nodes. Based on this, considering the regulation characteristics of energy storage batteries, supercapacitors, and controllable reactive power compensation devices, a PQV-based active-reactive power coordinated voltage regulation strategy is proposed. This strategy takes voltage limit violation as the trigger condition, prioritizing active power absorption to suppress the voltage rise caused by reverse power flow, and complementing it with reactive power regulation for precise voltage control. The proposed model and regulation strategy are verified through MATLAB simulation. The results show that, without reducing the PV output, this method can effectively control the node voltage within the allowable range under reverse power flow conditions during the daytime, demonstrating good engineering application prospects.

title: Digital Twin-Empowered Vehicle–Road Cooperation: A Perception and Virtualization Paradigm for Connected and Automated Vehicles
authors: Wenwei Yue, Zifan Sha, Di Zhou, Peibo Duan, Fa Zhu, Moustafa Youssef
abstract: As the crown jewel of intelligent transportation, connected and automated vehicles (CAVs) have sparked a phenomenal burst of interest to ultimately achieve the accident-free transportation system with high control capacity. However, current CAVs rely mainly on vehicle onboard equipment and edge devices for sensing and communication, and their accuracy and reliability are severely restricted by complex road environments and arbitrary human behavior. Additionally, the testing costs for intelligent vehicle–road collaboration systems and automated vehicles are exorbitant, coupled with extremely high deployment and operational complexities. Moreover, improper decision making can easily precipitate traffic accidents, thereby reducing the safety and efficiency of transportation systems. In lieu of this, we propose a digital twin (DT)-enabled vehicle–road cooperation system (VRCS) with the large-scale deployment of roadside Internet of Things (IoT) devices, which enables the ubiquitous perception and virtualization of CAVs. It also provides real-time digital mapping and intelligent control for the coordination between CAVs and smart road infrastructure. Specifically, we first propose an integrated “device-edge-DT” vehicle–road cooperation architecture to achieve the optimum utilization and digitalization of both vehicle onboard and roadside resources. Furthermore, a DT-driven sensor scheduling framework is employed to satisfy the information requirements for the VRCS. Finally, simulations show that the proposed DT-enabled vehicle–road cooperation architecture outperforms the baseline with more than 15.6% greater robustness, 53.6% higher traffic efficiency, and 8.3% better real-time responsiveness, advancing the vision of safe, efficient, and intelligent transportation.

title: Pathwise guessing in categorical time series with unbounded alphabets
authors: Jean-René Chazottes, Sandro Gallo, Daniel Y. Takahashi
abstract: The following learning problem arises naturally in various applications: Given a finite sample from a categorical or count time series, can we learn a function of the sample that (nearly) maximizes the probability of correctly guessing the values of a given portion of the data using the values from the remaining parts? Unlike classical approaches in statistical inference, our approach avoids explicitly estimating the conditional probabilities. We propose a non-parametric guessing function with a learning rate independent of the alphabet size. Our analysis focuses on a broad class of time series models that encompasses finite-order Markov chains, some hidden Markov chains, Poisson regression for count processes, and one-dimensional Gibbs measures. We provide a margin condition that controls the rate of convergence for the risk. Additionally, we establish a minimax lower bound for the convergence rate of the risk associated with our guessing problem. This lower bound matches the upper bound achieved by our estimator up to a logarithmic factor, demonstrating its near-optimality.

title: Broadband and Wide-Angle Conformal Active Metasurface for Continuous and Dynamic RCS Modulation
authors: Haiqing Chen, Tonghao Liu, Linying Wang, Gu Liu, Weihan Li, Chaoqun Ge, Dongshu Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: Metasurfaces have attracted significant research attention due to their exceptional capabilities in manipulating electromagnetic (EM) waves, which have achieved a wide range of groundbreaking applications. Active metasurfaces represent an emerging class of reconfigurable platforms that can dynamically tailor functionalities in real-time, especially in radar cross section (RCS) modulation. Nevertheless, conventional designs for metasurfaces are not only difficult to conform to curved surface structures, but also suffer from narrow bandwidth and strong angular dependence, which tremendously limit their practical application. In this article, we propose a conformal active metasurface (CAM) that enables continuous and dynamic RCS modulation on curved targets. By strategically adjusting the bias voltages of regionally distributed p-i-n diodes to achieve adaptive impedance matching, the CAM can efficiently absorb EM waves across a broad frequency spectrum and a wide range of incident angles. Through theoretical analysis, optimal design, and experimental validation, we demonstrate that the proposed CAM achieves an average continuous dynamic RCS reduction of more than 10 dB, with a maximum reduction of 27 dB in simulation and 26 dB in measurement within 8–15 GHz, which is well maintained across an incident angle range of 0°–50°. This facilitates continuous and dynamic modulation of the target’s RCS in adaptation to variations in the surrounding EM environment, allowing the target to merge seamlessly with conventional platforms in the reflection state, preventing the detection of anomalous scattering signatures, while enabling active stealth functionality in the absorption state. This work provides a feasible strategy for designing highly adaptable, broadband, and wide-angle CAMs, with strong potential for applications in radar stealth, wireless communications, and integrated sensing systems.

title: Scanpath Prediction in Panoramic Videos Via Expected Code Length Minimization
authors: Mu Li, Kanglong Fan, Kede Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Scanpath prediction in panoramic videos is a challenging task due to the spherical geometry and multimodality of the input, and the inherent uncertainty and diversity of the output. To give a complete treatment of these characteristics, we first present a simple criterion for scanpath prediction based on principles from lossy data compression. This criterion suggests minimizing the expected code length of quantized scanpaths, corresponding to fitting a discrete conditional probability model via maximum likelihood. We condition the probability model on two modalities: a viewport sequence as the deformation-reduced visual input and a set of relative past scanpaths projected onto respective viewports as the aligned path input. Furthermore, we parameterize it by a product of discretized Gaussian mixture models to capture the uncertainty and diversity of scanpaths from different humans. In doing so, the training of the probability model does not rely on the specification of “ground-truth” scanpaths for imitation learning. We also introduce a proportional–integral–derivative (PID) controller-based sampler to generate realistic human-like scanpaths from the learned probability model. Experimental results demonstrate that our method consistently produces better quantitative scanpath results in terms of prediction accuracy (by comparing to the assumed “ground-truths”) and perceptual realism (through machine discrimination) over a wide range of prediction horizons. We additionally verify the perceptual realism improvement via a formal psychophysical experiment and the generalization improvement on several unseen panoramic video datasets.

title: Stability and Performance Enhancement of IoT-Enabled Dual-Axis Solar Tracker Using Red Colobuses Monkey Optimization Algorithm
authors: Pasala Gopi, S. Venkateswarlu, Meisam Mahdavi
publish: IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering
abstract: This study focuses on the design, development, and performance evaluation of the dual-axis solar tracker (DaST) system to maximize solar energy harvesting. The proposed system rotates solar panels along both horizontal and vertical axes, dynamically adjusting their orientation to follow the sun’s trajectory using a NodeMCU ESP8266 microcontroller. A novel Internet of Things (IoT) framework enables real-time system monitoring and performance optimization. A fully functional prototype of the IoT-enabled solar tracking system (STS) has been developed and rigorously tested to validate its performance and effectiveness in real-world applications. This research develops a transfer function model of the DaST to analyze the system dynamics under different control schemes. The stability and robustness of the DaST system with uncertainty, including the proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controller, are evaluated through performance degradation curves. The coefficients of the PID controller are optimized using the red colobuses monkey optimization (RCMO) algorithm. Finally, a comparative analysis was conducted to assess the performance of the DaST system with the RCMO Algorithm (RCMOA)-based PID controller against the firefly algorithm (FA), cuckoo search algorithm (CSA), and conventional PID controllers. The results indicate that the RCMO algorithm exhibits better stability and performance across all uncertainty levels compared to the other methods.

title: Adaptive Feature Enhancement for SAR-based Ship Detection
authors: Weiyang Chen, Mao Ye
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) enables reliable maritime surveillance, yet ship detection remains challenging due to limited training data and complex ocean clutter. To address these issues, we propose a concise yet effective two-fold enhancement framework for robust SAR ship detection. First, a target-driven data enhancement strategy is introduced by extracting ship targets and synthesizing them with pure ocean backgrounds to reduce the impact of ship wakes and complex land backgrounds on ship data generation. Second, an adaptive feature enhancement mechanism based on a Mixture of Experts (MoE) is designed, where a gating network dynamically selects specialized expert sub-networks to refine multi-scale feature representations. This adaptive routing enables spatially aware feature enhancement and improves discriminability under complex maritime conditions. Extensive experiments on benchmark SAR datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving superior detection accuracy and robustness. These results validate the effectiveness of combining data-level enhancement with expert-driven feature refinement for SAR ship detection.

title: IEEE CS Career Center
authors: N/A

title: Mapping AI Literacy Expectations in Technical and Professional Communication: Evidence From 137 AI-Referenced Job Postings
authors: Shijie Liu, Jianfen Chen
abstract: Background: This exploratory study examines the emerging requirements of artificial-intelligence (AI) literacies reflected in technical and professional communication (TPC) job postings and provides empirical insights into how employers articulate these literacies in hiring practices. Literature review: We situate this study within the existing scholarship regarding job postings analysis in TPC, AI in TPC, and AI literacy. Research questions: How do employers reference AI literacy in TPC job postings? How are AI literacy dimensions distributed across the TPC job description domains? Methodology: We collected job postings published between March 1 and June 10, 2025, across ten major online recruitment platforms, focusing on US-based positions that fall into six TPC job categories that explicitly referenced AI. This process yielded 137 eligible postings for analysis. Employing a multistage and mixed-method design for data analysis, we coded AI literacies using the AI literacy framework and coded the job descriptions using the four TPC job description domains. Results: Our quantitative findings show growing integration of AI literacy into TPC roles, with uneven emphasis on specific AI literacy dimensions across TPC positions. Employers prioritize practical applications of AI tools, while ethical considerations receive little attention. Our topical modeling analysis also indicates that AI literacy is an integrative skill and functions as an embedded capability in TPC jobs. Conclusion: This study has significant implications for TPC educators. AI literacy should be taught as an integrative skill that augments human expertise. TPC programs should embed AI instruction across the curriculum while continuing to emphasize the core competencies essential to professional communication.

title: TwinRL-Onco: A World Model-Empowered Digital Twin Framework with Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Venetoclax Resistance Trajectory Prediction and Adaptive Therapy Optimization in Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia
authors: Zi Wang, Chenghao Ge, Yufan Hu, Destin Bai, Wei Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL) presents considerable therapeutic obstacles due to the development of treatment-resistant disease, especially with BCL-2 inhibitors like venetoclax, and is among the most challenging haematological malignancies to treat. A critical unmet computational need is the ability to predict patient-specific treatment resistance trajectories and to develop optimal adaptive therapy schedules based on a patient's history and experience with treatments. To address these issues, we have developed TwinRL-Onco, a novel computational framework that fuses digital twin technology, driven by world model-based simulations, with hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL), to predict in silico treatment response and optimize the adaptive therapy process. TwinRL-Onco consists of three synergistic components: (1) a Variational Recurrent State-Space Model (VRSSM); (2) a hierarchical policy structure with two levels of control; and (3) a resistance trajectory predictor that uses Monte Carlo rollouts generated from the world model to simulate treatment response. TwinRL-Onco achieved an AUROC of 0.917 for 12-month simulated prediction of progression, an improvement of 4.6 percentage points over the DreamerV3 algorithm and 7.0 percentage points over the LSTM-Traj algorithm. TwinRL-Onco is an emphasis on an artificial intelligence-based online environment for hypothesis generation; all on-site metrics indicate inventory-only consistency and need actual clinical validation before any conclusions regarding clinical prediction.

title: Harmonic-excited Closed-slot Brushless Wound Rotor Synchronous Machine with Reduced Torque Ripple
authors: Zuhair Abbas, Ghulam Jawad Sirewal, Jin Hur
abstract: This paper presents a brushless wound rotor synchronous machine (WRSM) employing a self-excitation method based on harmonic field interaction and a closed-slot stator design for torque ripple reduction. In the proposed configuration, a harmonic magnetic field is established and regulated in the air gap through voltage induction in a rotor-mounted harmonic winding, providing self-excitation without brushes and slip rings. To further enhance electromagnetic performance, a third-harmonic component is injected into the armature current to introduce a controlled third-harmonic magnetomotive force (MMF) component for brushless excitation and achieve a higher air-gap flux density. The closed-slot stator topology contributes additional field smoothing by minimizing permeance variation along the air gap. Analytical modeling and two-dimensional finite element analysis (FEA) demonstrate that the proposed closed-slot B-WRSM achieves approximately 49% reduction in torque ripple compared with a semi-closed slot configuration, while maintaining comparable average torque and efficiency. Analytical modeling shows that the reduction in slot-induced permeance harmonics directly suppresses torque ripple components associated with both fundamental and harmonic MMFs. The combined approach of closed-slot geometry and third-harmonic current injection provides an effective means to improve torque smoothness and excitation stability, making the design suitable for high-performance and precision drive applications.

title: Efficient Partially-Connected Hybrid Beamforming for Communication Capacity Maximization in FD-DFRC Systems
authors: Mingjie Chen, Yanzhao Hou, Siyuan Li, Songning Gao, Gaoze Mu, Yongan Zheng, Zhiqing Wei, Qimei Cui, Xiaofeng Tao
abstract: Dual-Function Radar and Communication (DFRC) system with full-duplex (FD) sensing and half-duplex (HD) communication is regarded as a promising way to improve safety and efficiency in vehicular networks, which have been widely used in vehicular networks. To achieve higher communication capacity, this paper extends the beamforming design of full-duplex sensing and communication DFRC systems to general scenarios within vehicular networks. Compared to the fully-connected hybrid beamforming architecture, the partially-connected hybrid beamforming architecture holds great potential of reducing hardware costs, thereby facilitating practical implementation in real-world wireless systems. However, the unique block-diagonal structure of its analog beamforming matrix introduces additional design challenges. This paper investigates an efficient partially-connected hybrid beamforming algorithm for FD-DFRC multi-input multi-output (MIMO) systems. Specifically, we formulate a multi-objective optimization problem to jointly optimize the dual-functional transmit beamformers for information receiving vehicles (IRVs) and the base station (BS), while also optimizing the received beamformers for information transmission vehicles (ITVs) at the full-duplex vehicle terminal (FD-VT). In particular, we decompose the original problem of maximizing the sum rate into several subproblems. We design an efficient algorithm based on lower bound maximization, Riemannian manifold optimization methods, and the alternating direction multiplier method (ADMM) algorithm. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed scheme effectively optimizes the sum rate while maintaining radar performance thresholds, requiring fewer radio frequency chains, and adhering to energy offloading constraints.

title: Integrating Network and Attack Graphs for Service-Centric Impact Analysis
authors: Joni Herttuainen, Vesa Kuikka, Kimmo K. Kaski
abstract: Cyberattacks on enterprise networks exploit complex dependencies among infrastructure, services, and applications, which challenge traditional analysis methods that focus on attack paths or network topology in isolation. In this study, we introduce a novel probabilistic multilayer modelling framework, based on influence propagation in networks, that integrates attack graphs with the communication network topology, enabling a service-centric impact analysis of cyberattacks. Our method captures both the vulnerability exploitability and network connectivity, allowing us to assess the likelihood of attack propagation and cumulative impacts across interconnected services. By integrating standard vulnerability metrics (such as CVSS) with the network-level connectivity probabilities, the framework provides a cohesive view of the dynamics of cyberattacks. We validate this approach using a realistic case study of an enterprise network, demonstrating its ability to determine critical nodes, vulnerabilities, and service dependencies that significantly influence attack outcomes. Our findings show that integrating network and attack graph perspectives offers more actionable insights into risk assessment and mitigation planning, advancing the analysis of cyberattacks in complex networked environments.

title: ICAD: Rethinking the Role of Inference and Cues for the Anomaly Detection of Time Series in IIoT
authors: Zhichao Wu, Li Zhu, Zitao Yin, Shunqi Zhang, Xirong Xu, Xiaopeng Wei, Xin Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Time series anomaly detection in real-world Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) systems is pivotal for identifying unsafe conditions and implementing timely preventive measures. While diffusion models are popular for capturing complex patterns, they often struggle to balance diversity and fidelity across scenarios due to limited exploration of context-window logical inference relationships and trend-pattern cues. To address these challenges, we propose ICAD, a novel method that rethinks the role of inference and cues in IIoT time series anomaly detection. ICAD defines trend patterns and uncertainties in textual form and utilizes a fine-tuned large language model to encode these descriptions as conditions for the diffusion model, thereby enhancing its generalization across diverse data distributions. Additionally, a “reasoning network for contextual window” mechanism is designed to capture temporal dependencies between adjacent windows, complemented by multi-scale and spatial feature adaptive fusion modules to further enhance the predictive performance. Empirical evaluations across four benchmark datasets and a large-scale ethylene oxide production process demonstrate that ICAD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, confirming its effectiveness and practicality in overcoming current anomaly detection model limitations.

title: EEG-Based Depression Detection with Temporal-Spectral and Graph Cross-Attention in a Sensor Signal-Processing Architecture
authors: Yongxin Wang, Yuan Sui, Haimei Yu, Zhihui Li, Haijun Lin
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) provides a noninvasive basis for depression detection. However, building robust models remains challenging due to the nonstationary temporal-spectral dynamics, subject-dependent inter-channel relationships, and weakly constrained fusion across heterogeneous EEG representations. This paper proposes a Temporal-Spectral and Graph Cross-Attention Network (TS-GCAN) for EEG-based depression detection within a sensor signal processing framework, and develops a structurally constrained collaborative analysis paradigm. The proposed framework integrates multi-scale temporal-spectral encoding, prior-constrained adaptive graph learning, structure-aware cross-domain attention fusion, and prototype-based aggregation into a unified pipeline, thereby enhancing coordinated modeling across complementary representations. Experimental results on two public resting-state EEG datasets (PRED+CT and HUSM) show that TS-GCAN achieves 94.52% accuracy (Acc), 93.73% specificity (Spe), 94.56% F1-score (F1), and 0.948 AUC on PRED+CT, and 91.89% Acc, 91.54% Spe, 91.62% F1, and 0.970 AUC on HUSM. Paired statistical hypothesis testing across folds further confirms that the proposed method achieves significant performance improvements over competing baselines (p < 0.05). In addition, TS-GCAN maintains a moderate model size and computational cost while achieving millisecond-level inference latency. It should be noted that the results on PRED+CT are obtained from a subject-level balanced subset, with labels defined by BDI questionnaire thresholds. In contrast, HUSM provides complementary evidence under clinically grounded DSM-IV diagnostic labels. Therefore, the reported findings should be interpreted in the context of the current subject-level evaluation protocol and differing label definitions.

title: Decomposition and Successive Decomposition Methods and Algorithms for Nonconvex Optimization
authors: Yiqing Zhai, Ying Cui, Danny H. K. Tsang
abstract: Existing results on decomposition methods and algorithms for nonconvex problems are minimal. Parallel decomposition algorithms do not exist for nonconvex problems with coupling nonlinear equality constraints. Besides, decomposition structures (i.e., coupling variables and constraints) are not fully exploited in designing decomposition methods and algorithms. In this paper, we consider nonconvex problems with decomposition structures that are more general than those handled in the existing literature. We propose primal and dual decomposition and successive primal and successive dual decomposition methods and algorithms for these nonconvex problems, which exploit decomposition structures, allow for parallel and distributed implementations, produce the original nonconvex problems’ stationary points, and offer good opportunities to achieve superior tradeoff between convergence performance and computation time. Finally, we compare the proposed methods and algorithms and provide examples and numerical results to demonstrate their respective values. Notably, the proposed decomposition and successive decomposition methods and algorithms generalize the basic ones for convex problems, and the proposed successive decomposition methods and algorithms extend the existing ones for nonconvex problems, together enriching the decomposition theory.

title: Site-Specific Channel Modeling and Optimization of RIS-Assisted Multiuser MISO Systems
authors: Ziqi Liu, Wei Yu, Sean Victor Hum
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper presents a physics-based channel modeling and optimization framework for reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted downlink multi-user multiple-input single-output (MU-MISO) communication systems in site-specific environments. A hybrid ray-tracing (RT) and full-wave electromagnetic analysis approach is developed to construct a deterministic channel model that explicitly captures multipath propagation, RIS scattering behavior, and mutual coupling effects through a non-diagonal load impedance representation. Based on this model, an alternating optimization scheme jointly updates the base-station (BS) beamformer and RIS load impedances to maximize the minimum achievable rate under a total transmit power constraint and practical capacitance limits. The objective of the proposed framework is to provide a reliable initial assessment of the system-level impact of RIS deployment in realistic propagation scenarios. To evaluate this capability, the RIS is operated in a column-paired 1-bit control mode that enables exhaustive evaluation of all realizable configurations in both simulation and measurement. Performance is compared at the distribution level through achievable-rate histograms across all configurations and further examined under small user-location variations. The observed agreement between simulation and measurement demonstrates that the proposed framework reliably captures practical performance trends and provides useful guidance for the design and deployment of RIS-assisted MU-MISO systems in site-specific environments.

title: Reconfigurable Magnetic Field Shaping via Current-Controlled Relay Coils for Wirelessly Powering Capsule Endoscopy
authors: Chen Gao, Shiyang Luo, Haotian Shi, Hao Zhang, Yongxin Guo
abstract: Wireless power transfer (WPT) for wireless capsule endoscopy (WCE) extends the operation time by replacing the capacity-limited on-board batteries with a receiver for a continuous power supply. However, the magnetic field pattern is usually fixed once the transmitting (Tx) coil is determined. Due to the uneven dwelling time of the capsule along the gastrointestinal tract, the desired charging region may vary in different practical scenarios. Under existing Tx configurations, such variation is mainly accommodated by adjusting the overall field intensity rather than the spatial field distribution, which may cause unnecessary magnetic exposure outside the target region. To address this limitation, a relay-based reconfigurable magnetic-field-shaping method is proposed to enable switching between the axial-dominant mode and the radial-dominant mode without rewinding the coil. Specifically, an analytical relay-based current-control method is formulated by parametrizing the Tx coil array, and the desired field pattern is realized through joint optimization of the array variables. In addition, field uniformity and electromagnetic exposure are compared with representative existing designs under unified conditions. Experimental results show that the proposed design achieves measured current ratios of 0.705 and 0.509 for the axial-dominant and radial-dominant modes, corresponding to measured field uniformities of 88% and 75.3%, respectively.

title: FlashSAM: Lightweight Vision Model for Multi-UAV Token Communication in Low-Altitude Wireless Networks
authors: Feibo Jiang, Siwei Tu, Li Dong, Kezhi Wang, Kun Yang, Ruiqi Liu, Cunhua Pan, Jiangzhou Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing
abstract: Token Communication (TokenCom) is a promising paradigm for low-altitude wireless networks, as it focuses on transmitting task-relevant core information, particularly in environments with uncertainty, noise, and stringent bandwidth constraints. However, existing TokenCom systems still face several challenges, including inefficient knowledge base construction, ineffective token encoding, and limited support for multi-user token sharing. To address these issues, we propose a Lightweight Vision Model-based Multi-Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) To ken Communication (LVM-MTC) system. First, we develop a lightweight Segment Anything Model (SAM), termed FlashSAM, which incorporates a set of lightweight convolutional modules to significantly reduce the number of model parameters. Building on FlashSAM, we construct a Lightweight Knowledge Base (LKB) to enable efficient object-level perception. Next, we design an Efficient Token Codec (ETC) based on the Masked Autoencoder (MAE) architecture. ETC improves compression efficiency at both the pixel and token levels, and provides lightweight token decoding tailored for resource-constrained UAVs. Furthermore, we propose a Multi-UAV Token Sharing (MTS) scheme for multi UAV TokenCom. By measuring token similarity across UAVs, MTS consolidates similar tokens and transmits them through broadcast transmission, thereby further improving transmission efficiency. Finally, simulation results validate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed LVM-MTC system.

title: BAPA-Net: Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection in Remote Sensing via Background-Aligned Prototype Aggregation
authors: Chao Li, Qi Zhang, Xiao Liang, Yuhang Xiao, Shenzhi Li, Peng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: While large-scale supervised learning has catalyzed remarkable strides in remote sensing object detection, its real-world efficacy is frequently stifled by the formidable costs of expert annotation and the inherent scarcity of high-quality samples. Cross-domain few-shot object detection (CD-FSOD) has emerged as a strategic paradigm to alleviate this dependency by facilitating knowledge transfer from label-rich natural domains to data-starved remote sensing environments. However, the pronounced geospatial domain shifts and intricate background interference often lead to suboptimal generalization in existing frameworks. To address these impediments, we introduce the Background-Aligned Prototype Aggregation Network (BAPA-Net), a consolidated framework meticulously crafted to enhance the invariance of cross-domain representations. Specifically, BAPA-Net integrates three pivotal mechanisms: (1) Cross-Domain Background Fusion Alignment (CBFA), which decouples and synchronizes source-target background distributions through an innovative sampling-clustering- aggregation pipeline, effectively mitigating low-level feature interference; (2) Soft Mask-guided Prototype Augmentation (SMPA), which employs dynamic probabilistic Gaussian masks to construct a continuous spatial attention mechanism, thereby filtering peripheral noise and refining prototype representativeness; and (3) Prototype Retrieval and Knowledge Aggregation (PRKA), which leverages a cross-domain Cache bank and attention-weighted retrieval to aggregate multi-source information adaptively, ensuring persistent exploitation of limited labeled samples. Extensive evaluations across diverse benchmarks, including high-altitude, underwater, and deep-space scenarios, demonstrate that BAPA-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methodologies. Specifically, our framework achieves substantial performance gains, improving the mAP by 5.0% to 10.8% relative to the baseline across 1, 5, and 10-shot protocols. Our results validate the framework’s robustness in bridging the semantic gap between natural and remote sensing domains. The code is open source: https://github.com/JackLeeAI/BAPA-Net.
