title: YOLOv8-EfficientViT for Robust Personnel Detection in Substation Operation Scenes
authors: Ming Xue
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Accurate and real-time detection of on-site personnel is essential for safety supervision in intelligent substations. However, personnel detection in substation environments remains challenging due to complex structural backgrounds and frequent partial occlusion caused by transformers, steel frameworks, and other equipment. Conventional convolution-based detectors mainly rely on local receptive fields and may struggle to capture long-range contextual information under such conditions. To address these issues, this paper proposes a YOLOv8-EfficientViT model for substation personnel detection. The conventional convolutional backbone of YOLOv8 is replaced with an EfficientViT-based feature extractor to enhance global feature modeling while maintaining computational efficiency. The proposed framework preserves the lightweight one-stage detection architecture and supports real-time deployment on edge devices. Experiments conducted on a visible-light substation personnel dataset containing 6004 images demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior detection accuracy compared with the baseline YOLOv8 model, particularly under partial occlusion scenarios. Meanwhile, the model maintains competitive inference speed and reduced computational complexity. The results indicate that integrating an efficient transformer-based backbone is an effective and practical solution for robust personnel detection in complex industrial environments.

title: Analysis of China's Dual Carbon Control Progress and Its Impacts on the Power System
authors: Yuan Chen, Qing Yang, Jinyu Wen
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Achieving carbon peaking and carbon neutrality is a broad and profound transformation across economic and social systems. The Chinese government has officially initiated a dual-control system for carbon emission. However, as the key component of low-carbon energy transition, China's power system has not yet taken a systematic analysis of the impacts of the dual-control mechanism, as well as its corresponding impact pathways and mechanism. To address this issue, the paper first reviews the policy background and motivations for China's transition from the previous dual-control system based on energy consumption to the current dual-control system focused on carbon emissions. It then conducts a comparative analysis of the key features of both approaches and summarizes prominent domestic and international research hotspots related to this topic. On this basis, considering China's own overall institutional framework for carbon emission dual-control, it explores and analyzes the implications of policy implementation for various components of the power system—including power generation, transmission, and demand loads. Finally, the paper concludes by identifying the challenges faced by the power system in terms of planning, dispatch and market, and proposes corresponding technical support requirements and recommendations.

title: Research on Synergistic Optimization of Source-Load-Storage Based on Integrated Energy
authors: Jinghong Zhou, Zhewei Cao, Jiangkai Jia, Xin Zhang, Yifan Zhao, Bin Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To resolve renewable integration challenges, we propose an integrated energy system deeply coupling multienergy storage and flexible loads. MATLAB simulations confirm that compared to the unoptimized baseline (Scenario 4) with a cost of 8666.63 yuan and 757.33 kg emissions, Scenario 1 reduces operating costs by 2.1% to 8486.54 yuan, cuts carbon emissions by 152.97kg (20.2%), and boosts new energy utilization by 5.13%

title: Early Anomaly Detection of Wind Turbine Main Bearings Based on Multi-Head Self-Attention Network
authors: Junshuai Yan, Can Wang, Peng Hu, Yan Du
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: An early anomaly detection method for main bearings of wind turbine based on a Multi-Head Self-Attention neural network is proposed in this paper. Firstly, the Quartile Algorithm and Grey Relational Analysis are used to perform data cleaning and feature selection on the data. Secondly, based on the MHSA network, a normal behavior model for the main bearing temperature of wind turbine is established to deeply mine the spatio-temporal features contained in massive high-dimensional SCADA time-series data. Finally, anomaly indicators based on the Kernel Density Estimation and sliding window algorithm are constructed to realize the automatic identification of early anomalies in main bearings. Validation and analysis are conducted using actual operating data from wind farms. The results show that the proposed method has excellent detection performance and can detect early anomalies in main bearings an average of 291.3 hours in advance.

title: Reliable Multi-Modal Object Re-Identification via Modality-Aware Graph Reasoning
authors: Xixi Wan, Aihua Zheng, Zi Wang, Bo Jiang, Jin Tang, Jixin Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Multi-modal data provides abundant and diverse object information, crucial for effective modal interactions in Re-Identification (ReID) task. However, existing approaches often overlook the quality variations in local features and fail to fully leverage the complementary information across modalities, particularly in cases where features are of low quality. In this paper, we propose to address this issue by leveraging a novel graph reasoning model, termed the Modality-aware Graph Reasoning Network (MGRNet). Specifically, we first construct modality-aware graphs to enhance the extraction of fine-grained local details by effectively capturing and modeling the relationships between patches. Subsequently, the selective graph nodes swap operation is employed to alleviate the adverse effects of low-quality local features by considering both local and global information, enhancing the representation of discriminative information. Finally, the swapped modality-aware graphs are fed into the local-aware graph reasoning module, which propagates multi-modal information to yield a reliable feature representation. Another advantage of the proposed graph reasoning approach is its ability to reconstruct missing modal information by exploiting inherent structural relationships, thereby minimizing disparities between different modalities. Experimental results on four benchmarks (RGBNT201, Market1501-MM, RGBNT100, MSVR310) indicate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance in multi-modal object ReID. The code for our method will be available upon acceptance.

title: Autonomous Causal Discovery: Evaluating LLMs' Priors and Constraint Strategies for Reliability_supp1-3689960.pdf
authors: Xiangyu Wang
abstract: Expert-guided Causal Structure Learning (CSL) incorporates prior knowledge to improve the accuracy of causal discovery, yet the acquisition of such knowledge is often restricted by the availability of human experts. While Large Language Models (LLMs) provide an alternative source of causal priors, LLM-derived knowledge can be inconsistent with the true causal structure due to hallucinations or contextual misinterpretations. This paper introduces a structural constraint measurement framework, which defines constraint strength and constraint quality to describe reliability and effectiveness, enabling a systematic evaluation of LLM-derived constraints. Using this framework, we evaluate five categories of structural constraints: Edge Existence (EEC), Edge Forbidden (EFC), Path Existence (PEC), Path Forbidden (PFC), and Order Constraints (OC). Our theoretical and empirical analyses demonstrate that while EEC offers high constraint strength, it exhibits low quality when derived from LLMs; conversely, PFC and OC provide a balanced trade-off between search-space pruning and reliability. Building on these insights, we propose a two-level CSL optimization framework that partitions the search space by node order and refines the structure using global path constraints. The results show that this framework provides an effective way to incorporate noisy LLM-derived priors into CSL, particularly in settings where expert knowledge is limited.

title: Mitigate Catastrophic Remembering via Continual Self-Paced Dual-Knowledge Purification for Noisy Lifelong Person Re-Identification_supp1-3693671.pdf
authors: Jiahuan Zhou
abstract: Current Lifelong Person Re-Identification (LReID) methods focus on tackling the data streams with accurate labels. When the data streams with noisy labels are given, their performance is severely degraded since the model continually remembers erroneous knowledge induced by the label noise. Moreover, the well-known issue of catastrophic forgetting in LReID is exacerbated by noisy labels, which disrupt the retention of correct knowledge from previous models. Such a practical Noisy LReID task is important but challenging, and few works have attempted to address it. In this paper, we pioneer the investigation of Noisy LReID and propose a Continual Self-Paced Dual-Knowledge Purification (CSDP) method to address the catastrophic remembering of erroneous knowledge, catastrophic forgetting of correct knowledge, and limited acquisition capacity, simultaneously. Specifically, both local distribution information and global classification results are jointly leveraged to enhance the recognition and correction of noisy labels. In addition, a self-paced learning paradigm is introduced to facilitate the progressive accumulation of reliable knowledge. Finally, a Noisy LReID benchmark is constructed to support research on this task. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed CSDP method, under comparable computational overhead, outperforms state-of-the-art methods by a large margin. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/zhoujiahuan1991/TPAMI-CSDP</uri>

title: ‘It's not only my Issue, but also a Community Issue’: an Atlas of Design Actions for Commoning Flood Adaptation
authors: Francesca Vanelli, Paul Minifie, Giulia Galluccio, Monica Lavagna
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: Mediating Digital Security: Participatory Learning with Older Adults in Smartphone Café Sessions
authors: Parvin Ghadamighalandari, Roodabeh Seif, Margarita Grinko, Konstantin Aal, Mahya Mirzaeekharghani, Bilal Mohamad, Claudia Mueller, Volker Wulf
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: Integrating Robust CRT with Signal Unwrapping for Modulo Sampling
authors: Chutong Shen, Wenyi Yan, Yimin D. Zhang, Lu Gan
abstract: Two-channel modulo sampling based on the robust Chinese remainder theorem (RCRT) enables closed-form, point wise reconstruction, yet its recovery remains fundamentally limited by the effective RCRT threshold. This letter addresses this limitation through a hybrid architecture that integrates RCRT based recovery with difference-based unwrapping. The RCRT stage is reinterpreted as a coarse modulo operator with an expanded threshold, while the difference stage resolves the residual ambiguity by exploiting temporal structure. The proposed scheme extends recovery beyond the intrinsic RCRT range and simultaneously relaxes the oversampling requirement for a fixed difference order. Theoretical analysis establishes the resulting sampling condition, and the effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through both simulations and hardware experiments.

title: Estimating the Temperature Distribution of Pouch-Type Li-Ion Batteries Based on a Distributed-Parameter Electrothermal Coupling Model
authors: Jiale Xie, Mingyu Song, Zongyang Song, Zhongbao Wei, Zhekang Dong
abstract: Pouch-type lithium-ion batteries (PLiBs) often suffer from nonuniform temperature distributions, which accelerate degradation and increase thermal safety risks. To address this, a 2-D electrothermal coupled model is developed by discretizing the cell into grid elements, each represented by a second-order equivalent circuit. Electrothermal interactions are captured through electrical and thermal resistances. Model parameters are identified using a hybrid method that combines parameter mapping with particle swarm optimization, and are validated under dynamic stress test and multirate discharge conditions. Experimental results demonstrate high predictive accuracy, with mean voltage errors below 7.5 mV and temperature deviations within 1 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^\boldsymbol{\circ}$</tex-math></inline-formula>C. The model also successfully reproduces localized hot spots near the current tabs during high-rate discharge. Analysis reveals two coupled causes of thermal nonuniformity: uneven current distribution due to ohmic drops in the current collectors, and heterogeneous heat dissipation across the cell surface. The superposition of these effects exacerbates temperature nonuniformity. This modeling framework offers a robust tool for the design and optimization of battery thermal management systems, providing practical insights into improving safety and extending the service life of large-format PLiBs.

title: Research on Optimization Method of Medium Voltage Target Network Based on Reliability and Economy
authors: Yuze Yang, Jian Guan, Jiepeng Huang, Luying Cai, Hong Hu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the continuous development of the economy and the iteration of science and technology, users' demand for electricity is no longer limited to meeting daily needs but has higher requirements for power supply reliability. However, higher reliability also implies higher construction costs. How to balance the relationship between the two and realize the reasonable optimization of the medium voltage target network has become a core issue in current distribution network planning. To address this problem, this paper conducts research on the optimization method of the medium voltage target network based on reliability and economy. It quantifies power supply reliability into an economic indicator through power outage losses. Under certain boundary conditions, the construction cost and this economic indicator are integrated as the objective for optimizing the medium voltage target network, and an optimization model for the medium voltage target network is built combined with the minimum spanning tree algorithm. Case simulations verify that the network optimized by this method has better comprehensive economic benefits and can effectively improve the optimization efficiency of the medium voltage target network.

title: Short-Term Load Forecasting Based on Graph Convolutional and Kolmogorov-Arnold Network
authors: Zeming Li, Peiying Li
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Short-term electric load is influenced by factors such as meteorological conditions and consumption behavior, exhibiting nonlinearity and multi-scale temporal dependencies. We propose GCKAN, a short-term load forecasting model integrating a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN), a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN). First, historical load segments are encoded as causally constrained, multi-granularity temporal graphs, and the GCN aggregates temporal-neighborhood features while enhancing local trends. Next, with state-decay and harmonic time modulation, the GRU captures long-term and periodic dynamics. Finally, the KAN head maps high-dimensional features to symbolically expressible loadresponse functions, improving interpretability without sacrificing accuracy. Experiments on a public hourly load dataset from Panama and a private single-node dataset from Guizhou show that GCKAN achieves the best performance: on the Panama dataset, MAE and RMSE drop by 6.11% and 12.05%; on the Guizhou dataset, by 7.90% and 14.51%. The model maintains high predictive accuracy and adapts to abrupt load changes and peakvalley fluctuations. Ablation studies confirm that the proposed multi-granularity temporal graph encoding, the GRU with statedecay and temporal-modulation mechanisms, and the KAN are key contributors to the gains.

title: A Concise Review of Resilience Assessment for Power Systems Against Typhoons
authors: Wenzhe Zheng, Zhenguo Wang, Wenqi Fan, Fei Yi, Fu Gao, Hui Hou
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The increasing frequency and intensity of typhoon disasters pose significant threats to the security and reliability of modern power systems. This paper provides a comprehensive review of the resilience assessment framework by synthesizing current methodologies in component vulnerability modeling, multi-dimensional resilience performance metrics, and uncertainty handling mechanisms. The review systematically examines the integration of typhoon wind field simulations, fragility curve development, and fault correlation analysis to quantify system degradation under extreme wind loads. Furthermore, it categorizes multi-dimensional metrics, specifically the Failure Loss, Extent, and Persistence indicator system and comprehensive resilience indices, which evaluate the evolution of risk throughout the disaster lifecycle. By characterizing the transition from short-term structural damage prediction to mid-to-long-term risk evolution, this review provides a theoretical foundation for identifying critical grid vulnerabilities and enhancing resilience in typhoon-prone regions.

title: Hierarchical Long-Term Operation Optimization of Flexible Resources Considering Multi-Scale Temporal Characteristics
authors: Yonggang Jiao, Yangyang Qi, Xuan Wen
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The efficient long-term operation of flexible resources in renewable-rich power systems necessitates coordinated decision-making across multiple time scales. This paper proposes a tri-layer hierarchical optimization framework to address the coupling challenges between long-term investment and short-term dispatch. The model integrates annual capacity planning, quarterly/monthly balancing, and weekly/intraday realtime dispatch into a cohesive system. A bidirectional coordination mechanism is designed to ensure consistency between layers, effectively incorporating the time-varying operational characteristics of diverse resources. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed model significantly outperforms single-scale approaches: it increases annual revenue by 12.3%, reduces carbon emissions by 19.0%, and drastically curtails renewable energy waste while enhancing system stability. The proposed framework offers a practical and scalable solution for optimizing flexible resource operations in high-renewable penetration scenarios

title: High-Sensitivity MEMS Optical Switch with Wide Spectral Response
authors: Chenyang Liu, Lei Zhang, Yuehao Zhang, Liming Zheng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the endurance bottleneck caused by standby power consumption in embodied intelligent agents, this paper proposes a near-zero-power wake-up scheme based on a broadband infrared absorber and a MEMS optical switch. Systematical modeling was conducted using FDTD and Finite Element Methods. Results show that the designed absorber achieves an average absorptivity over 80% in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8-14 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> band (peaking at 98% at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8.8 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex>) with a fabrication-friendly linewidth <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(>1 \mu ~\mathrm{m})$</tex>. In the mechanical domain, the switch utilizes a bimetallic beam mechanism to achieve a high sensitivity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.284 \mu ~\mathrm{m} / \mu \mathrm{W}$</tex> and a low trigger threshold of 216 nW. Combined with a symmetric temperature compensation design, this device enables high-sensitivity, interference-resistant physical wake-up, supporting the long-term autonomous operation of embodied agents.

title: Research on Data Integrity Verification and Tracing in the Power Internet of Things
authors: Shanyi Xie, Kai Zhang, Siyao Xu, Yuze Chen, Ying Li, Zhiwei Xu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Aiming at the security threats of eavesdropping and tampering that may arise during terminal data collection in the Power Internet of Things (PIoT), this paper proposes a data integrity verification and tracing scheme suitable for data collection networks by combining asymmetric encryption and digital signature technologies. Considering the tree topology of data collection networks, the scheme integrates network topology with hash calculation and adopts a multi-level hash approach, enabling the data server to verify the integrity of all terminal data under a gateway in a single operation. Furthermore, if the integrity verification fails, the abnormal nodes in the data transmission process can be accurately located through a step-by-step hash comparison method, thus achieving effective anomaly tracing of data.

title: Research and Application of Artificial Intelligence in Pattern Recognition of Power Grid Connection
authors: Chen Ye, Shangxing Ye, Hang Zheng, Bingwu Lou, Gui Cheng, Qian Wu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The rationality of distribution network wiring is crucial to the safety and economy of the power grid. This paper investigates the application of artificial intelligence (AI) to distribution network wiring pattern recognition, and proposes a comprehensive recognition method based on the combination of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL). This method constructs a distribution network topology diagram, extracts node and edge features, and combines graph neural networks and traditional machine learning for pattern learning to classify typical connection modes such as single ring network, double ring network, double chain, double radiation, and network type. Through verification, the method proposed in this paper outperforms both the single GNN method and traditional graph theory methods in terms of accuracy and recall. The method shows varying degrees of improvement in accuracy and recall, particularly in the recognition of complex lattice structures. Finally, this paper explores potential research directions for combining deep reinforcement learning and intelligent optimization algorithms in the future.

title: Optimization Design of Uplift Safety Reserve Factor for Transmission Line Foundations in Collapsible Loess Sites
authors: Shaokang Han, Liping Huang, Shaohui Wang, Fan Wen, Lei Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The design of transmission line foundations in collapsible loess regions is critically constrained by the coupled effects of soil collapse-induced softening, differential settlement, and dynamic loading. Conventional design approaches—predicated on the assumption of homogeneous soil behavior—fail to capture the transient and path-dependent hydro-mechanical response of collapsible loess under wetting and loading, leading to arbitrary or empirically calibrated safety reserve factors that compromise either structural reliability or economic efficiency. To address this gap, this study develops a moisture-content-dependent hydro-mechanical coupling constitutive model; uncovers the progressive degradation mechanism at the foundation-soil interface through high-fidelity finite element simulations; proposes a reliability-based multi-objective optimization framework for determining rational safety reserve factors; and validates the integrated methodology via comprehensive in-situ tests conducted across representative collapsible loess sites. The resulting design paradigm achieves an explicit trade-off between safety and economy, resolves the fundamental inconsistency between static safety-factor conventions and dynamic soil behavior, and provides both theoretical grounding and implementable guidance for uplift-resistant foundation design in collapsible loess environments.

title: From the Society: President's Message
authors: Shervin Shirmohammadi
abstract: Dear members, colleagues, and friends of the IEEE Instrumentation and Measurement Society (IMS)—Welcome to the June 2026 issue of the Magazine. I would like to update you on three specific activities.

authors: N/A

title: Impedance-Spectrum Driven Frequency Weighted Functional Fault Location in Undersea Observation Network Power Systems
authors: Erjia Zhang, Mouduo Yu, Wentao Huang, Nengling Tai
abstract: Fault location in the power supply systems of undersea observation networks is constrained by limited observability and the frequency-dependent characteristics of the electrode–seawater–cable loop. This paper proposes a frequency-domain fault location method based on impedance spectrum analysis, in which the identification of fault parameters is formulated as a minimization problem of a frequency-weighted residual functional. A physical model of the electrode–cable–fault path is constructed to compute theoretical input impedance spectra under different fault distances and fault impedances. These spectra are then compared to the measured impedance spectrum obtained at the shore station under swept-frequency excitation. The residual functional is constructed by separating the real and imaginary components of the spectral difference, reflecting the deviation of complex input impedance at each frequency point. Considering the influence of impedance sensitivity across frequencies, independent weighting functions are introduced for the real and imaginary components to enhance contributions from informative bands and suppress unreliable regions. This yields a frequency-weighted residual functional with physical significance, characterizing the correspondence between fault parameters and spectral discrepancy. The functional is minimized via a gradient-based optimization strategy, enabling simultaneous determination of fault distance and fault impedance based solely on shore-side measurements. Simulation studies across various fault conditions validate the method's performance in terms of location accuracy, robustness under high impedance scenarios, and numerical stability over long transmission distances. The proposed framework fuses spectral sensitivity with physical transmission modeling to infer spatial fault characteristics solely from frequency-domain observations, without relying on internal measurements or reflectometry.

title: Urban Environmental Challenges and Health: Environmental Determinants of Respiratory Illnesses in Samarkand
authors: Bahodirhon Safarov, Ana Cornelia Pereş, Nargiza Mansurova, Monica Costea, Bekzot Janzakov, Nandor Koteles, Habibullo Hasanov, Alexandru Bogdan Ilies
abstract: Rapid urban development and changing environmental conditions increasingly challenge public health in historic cities such as Samarkand, Uzbekistan. In recent years, respiratory illnesses have risen in the region, raising concerns about the role of environmental factors, particularly air quality. This study examines the influence of atmospheric dust and ambient temperature on asthma and other respiratory illnesses in Samarkand using monthly data from 2022–2023. The analysis applies regression-based time series technique, namely distributed lag model to capture both immediate and delayed environmental effects. The results show that short-term fluctuations in dust and temperature do not significantly affect asthma incidence. However, the distributed lag analysis suggests that atmospheric dust contributes to broader respiratory morbidity when delayed effects are considered, indicating that pollution-related health impacts may develop gradually over time. Temperature variations did not show a meaningful association with respiratory diseases. These findings emphasize the importance of strengthening air-quality monitoring, pollution mitigation strategies, and environmental health management in Samarkand.

title: Multi-Objective UAV–HAP Collaborative Offloading for Hotspot Region Computing in Urban Non-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Hai Huang, Zhiyong Wu, Cong Liu, Yulong Han, Runrulin He, Yuancan Li
abstract: With the proliferation of urban vehicles, traditional static edge computing faces significant challenges in ensuring service quality under high mobility. To address this issue, this paper investigates Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs) enabled by high-altitude platforms (HAPs) and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), and proposes a Hotspot-Guided Collaborative Matching Offloading (HGCMO) framework for task hotspot regions (THRs). The objective is to jointly optimize energy consumption and computational latency. Specifically, we develop a stable matching-based UAV-Hotspot algorithm (UHRMA) and a density-delay-driven positioning scheme (DDUPO) to optimize resource allocation and deployment. Furthermore, a Dynamic Weighted Load-Aware Offloading Algorithm (DWLAO) is introduced to balance task delay and energy consumption under resource constraints. DWLAO dynamically balances latency and energy according to system load. The framework is evaluated under varying traffic intensity, user density, and UAV computing resources. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed HGCMO-based scheme achieves significant performance gains compared with baseline methods, validating its effectiveness in hotspot-driven task offloading scenarios.

title: CCSFusion: A Hierarchical Semantic Chain-of-Thought Reasoning Architecture for Infrared-Visible Image Fusion and Captioning
authors: Miaoshan Lin, Guoheng Huang, Jietao Yang, Jiehao Zheng, Xiaochen Yuan, Yan Li, Xiaofeng Zhang, Kim-Fung Tsang, Chi-Man Pun
abstract: Infrared-Visible Image Fusion (IVIF) aims to generate a single, information-rich image for downstream tasks. However, prevailing methods exhibit two key limitations. First, many approaches lack explicit hierarchical semantic decoupling, failing to effectively integrate semantic features across different levels, which restricts their ability to capture complex scene structures. Second, task-driven fusion frameworks typically adopt a cascaded design, with unidirectional supervision provided by geometry-centric downstream tasks like detection. This architecture not only limits mutual reinforcement between the fusion and task networks, but also creates a ”supervision bottleneck” by lacking interaction with the linguistic modality that captures richer scene relationships. To tackle these challenges, we propose CCSFusion, the first framework that leverages Chain-of-Thought captioning as supervision, redirecting IVIF optimization from narrow geometric accuracy to multimodal scene comprehension. It establishes a mutually reinforcing coupling between the fusion network and the captioning task. Specifically, we introduce a Segmentation Mask Calibration Unit (SMCU) to refine coarse semantic priors, providing precise pixel-level guidance. Subsequently, the calibrated features are fed into Chained Semantic Fusion Module (CSFM) which explicitly decomposes the semantic priors into three hierarchical levels, and then feeds them into the Hierarchical Semantic Attention module. Finally, a bidirectional knowledge distillation mechanism transfers the reasoning ability of the teacher network to the student. Experiments show that CCSFusion achieves superior fusion performance and generates more semantically coherent images for high-level cognitive tasks. The code is available at: https://github.com/Snaillms/CCSFusion.

title: High Voltage Power Lines Conductor Anomaly Detection using Magnetic Field
authors: Francesco Messina, Emad Samuel Malki Ebeid, Marcello Chiaberge
abstract: This paper introduces a contactless, fully passive approach for locating faults in transmission line conductors. The method relies on the magnetic fields generated by the line current and captures them using low-cost, three-axis magnetic sensor integrated into a custom-designed drone payload. Unlike traditional inspection techniques, the proposed system performs rapid, multi-position magnetic scanning around the conductors within a short time window. By analyzing the spatial variations and temporal signatures of the measured magnetic field, the method can detect and characterize both external and internal anomalies, including potential structural degradations or irregular current distributions. Identified anomalies can be georeferenced accurately using the RTK-enabled GPS of the drone, allowing operators to determine their precise position along the transmission line. This capability supports targeted, close-range inspections and greatly enhances both the efficiency and safety of transmission line maintenance activities. This paper presents the experimental validation of the proposed method using a laboratory prototype. The prototype was tested on a 30mm diameter All-Aluminum Alloy Conductor energized through a current transformer operating at 50 Hz and 350 A. These tests were designed to evaluate the system’s ability to capture magnetic field signatures under realistic operating conditions, assess sensor performance, and verify the method’s capability to detect and localize conductor anomalies in a repeatable, safe and controlled environment.

title: Performance Analysis of Multiple User Average BLER in Downlink NOMA Short-Packet Communications over $\alpha - \kappa - \mu$ Shadowed Fading
authors: Phu Tran Tin, Minh-Sang Van Nguyen, Tran Trung Duy, Symeon Chatzinotas, Byung-Seo Kim, Miroslav Voznak
abstract: The spectral efficiency of sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks is anticipated to experience large improvements through the implementation of non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) technology. The integration of short-packet communications (SPC) into NOMA networks enables low-latency operation and high spectral efficiency. The present study investigates the performance of multiple users in a NOMA downlink SPC system operating over an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha - \kappa - \mu$</tex-math></inline-formula> shadowed fading channel. Precise and asymptotic closed-form approximations for the average block error rate (BLER), reliability, throughput, goodput, and overall BLER were derived using approximate Gaussian-Chebyshev quadrature. The analytical results were validated through numerical simulations, providing insights into the impact of fading parameters on system performance. The study's findings indicate that the proposed downlink NOMA SPC system is highly suitable for ultra-reliable and low-latency communications (URLLC), achieving reliability levels of 99.99% for multiple users. The study also determined the optimal transmission bit rate required to maximize throughput and goodput while minimizing the BLER. The proposed downlink NOMA SPC system operating with an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha - \kappa - \mu$</tex-math></inline-formula> shadowed fading channel demonstrates high potential for improving Internet of Things (IoT) network performance over conventional downlink orthogonal multiple access (OMA) approaches. Most of the analysis adopts perfect successive interference cancellation (pSIC) and perfect channel state information (pCSI) as theoretical benchmarks, while additional results explicitly quantify the performance degradation caused by residual interference and channel estimation errors (CEE). The results reveal that imperfect SIC (ipSIC) dominates the BLER floor at high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), whereas imperfect CSI (ipCSI) primarily affects the moderate-SNR regime, highlighting distinct impairment-driven performance bottlenecks. Therefore, we have extended the study by including two additional scenarios: pCSI combined with ipSIC, and ipCSI combined with ipSIC. This extension allows us to compare the performance of these systems and clearly shows that the system with pSIC and pCSI achieves the best performance. Finally, the results were validated with Monte Carlo simulations.

title: Foundations of Efficient Model Reconstruction Using Counterfactual Explanations_supp1-3695917.pdf
authors: Pasan Dissanayake
abstract: With the ever-growing computational demands of AI, extracting richer information from fewer data points is of utmost importance. This work proposes data-efficient model reconstruction and compression by leveraging an explainability technique called counterfactual explanations. Counterfactual explanations are traditionally used to explain black-box machine learning models by providing ways of achieving a favorable model outcome with minimum input perturbation (essentially, the closest point on the other side of the decision boundary). Interestingly, such counterfactuals can also be used to reconstruct a model by training a surrogate model on them to give similar predictions as the original model. In this work, we analyze how counterfactual explanations can improve model reconstruction by specifically utilizing the fact that the counterfactuals lie quite close to the decision boundary. Our main contribution is to derive novel theoretical relationships between the error in model reconstruction and the number of counterfactual queries required by leveraging polytope theory. We also derive additional bounds considering multiple model families such as ReLU networks and Lipschitz-continuous models. Our results establish that counterfactual explanations can act as information-rich probes for model reconstruction, which has significant implications for energy-efficiency, sustainability, and also privacy.

title: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
authors: N/A

title: Participating in Designing Sustainable Futures: The Challenges of Imagination and Envisioning
authors: Juan Alfonso De La Rosa, Stefana Broadbent, François Jégou

title: DuSS-PET: Self-Supervised Low-Dose PET Reconstruction Using Pseudo-Dual-Domain Consistency Without Paired Data
authors: Shuijin Lin, Yuzhu He, Yongbo Wang, Jianhua Ma, Fan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) is pivotal in medicine and healthcare, but requires high radiation doses for quality imaging. Although deep learning has shown promise for low-dose PET reconstruction/enhancement, existing methods primarily rely on fully supervised learning, which requires paired low- and full-dose scans that are clinically impractical to obtain. To overcome this fundamental limitation, we propose DuSS-PET, a novel self-supervised PET reconstruction framework that does not require paired low-/full-dose images as supervised training targets in the main reconstruction stage. Our method is built upon a computational Noisier2Noise (Nr2N) paradigm, featuring a Bernoulli corruption strategy that serves as an image-space approximation to emulate low-count noise patterns in image-derived pseudo-sinogram representations. The DuSS-PET framework integrates three core components: an adaptive Swin transformer for pseudo-sinogram restoration, a plug-and-play module for image enhancement, and a consistency-constrained SSL strategy that enforces agreement between the pseudo-sinogram and image representations. Extensive experiments on multi-center datasets demonstrate that DuSS-PET achieves superior reconstruction performance, outperforming state-of-the-art SSL methods and achieving comparable or even better results than fully supervised approaches across various dose levels. In particular, it exhibits exceptional generalization across different scanner vendors. This work is among the first self-supervised low-dose PET reconstruction frameworks that enforce consistency between reconstructed images and pseudo-sinograms, where the pseudo-sinogram generated in the image space serves as a computational consistency constraint for enhanced imaging without paired full-dose training targets. The source code will be publicly available at https://github.com/ladderlab-xjtu/DuSS-PET.

title: Sensor Fusion with Neural Virtual Measurements for Aerial GNSS/INS Integration
authors: Ning Liu, Lixuan Zhu, Kejun Hu, Junfang Fan
abstract: High-spinning aerial platforms operating under high-speed rotation and strong electromagnetic interference experience severe GNSS signal degradation, causing periodic outages and measurement gaps. Such conditions force the navigation system into pure inertial mode, resulting in rapidly accumulating position errors. To address sensor measurement gaps, this paper devel-ops a physics-informed mapping between IMU observa-tions and flight-phase states derived from 7-DOF dynam-ics equations. A Transformer-CNN-based virtual sensor is developed for real-time flight-phase identification and synthetic position-increment measurements, dynamically adapting the Kalman filter's measurement covariance matrix and measurement model. This dual-constraint sensor fusion framework improves GNSS/INS integration robustness during signal degradation. Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) sensor simulations demonstrate that under sporadic electromagnetic interference (short-term GNSS outages), position and velocity RMSEs decrease by 63% and 16%, respectively; under sustained interference in electromag-netically contested environments (prolonged outages), reductions reach 42% and 11%. The approach mitigates pure inertial navigation error accumulation, substantially enhancing multi-sensor fusion accuracy in GNSS-challenged ex-treme environments. Comparative validation across multiple neural network architectures confirms the Transform-er-CNN's superiority in adaptive flight-phase recognition. This work provides a deployable virtual sensor-enhanced framework for multi-sensor fusion systems operating under severe electromagnetic interference, offering both theo-retical rigor and engineering viability for practical implementation.

title: Physical Realization of Mathematics Geometric Fractals in Metasurface Filters
authors: Baiying TaiShi, Di Wu, Yuke Huang, Yiyuan Hao, Huiming Yao, Jiaxin Li, Jianchun Xu, Ke Bi
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: With the rapid development of wireless communication systems, there is an urgent demand for multi-band and broadband electromagnetic devices to improve spectrum utilization. Metasurfaces, as planar metamaterials, provide an ideal platform for high-performance filter design. A Ku-band metasurface filter is designed using self-affine fractal sets. Through systematic mathematical modeling, the inherent space-filling and self-similar properties of fractals are leveraged to enhance electromagnetic performance. Two order fractal unit cells are designed and analyzed. Simulations confirm theoretical predictions, showing that the second order structure preserves the first-order resonant peaks at 12.65 GHz and 13.86 GHz while introducing a new broadband peak at 16.01 GHz, resulting from the overlap of multi-scale resonant modes. Fractal dimension analysis guides parameter optimization, enabling precise tuning and shifting of resonant frequencies. Specifically, modifying the factor k primarily regulates the second order frequency offset, while altering the period p facilitates comprehensive frequency adjustment. The appropriate parameters range for high transmission across 12-18 GHz are mathematically identified. The fabricated 3 × 3 PCB prototypes exhibit measured responses in agreement with simulations, validating the advantages of geometric fractals in metasurface design. This work establishes a rigorous foundation linking fractal theory to electromagnetic functionality, opening new avenues for advanced multi-band and broadband metasurface design with strong potential for future communication devices.

title: Transceive Beamforming in FDA-MIMO-Based Dual-Function Radar-Communication Systems for Low Probability of Interception
authors: Haozheng Wu, Chenguang Shi, Jianjiang Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Low probability of interception (LPI) technology is critical for the self-protection of dual-function radar-communication (DFRC) systems in hostile environments. However, the range-dimensional uncontrollability of the beampattern imposes a bottleneck limitation on LPI development. This paper investigates a transceive beamforming for frequency diverse array (FDA)-multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO)-based DFRC systems. Initially, we innovatively construct the signal model for the FDA-MIMO DFRC system, which surmounts the inseparability between frequency offsets and transmitting antennas. Subsequently, the equivalent transmitted beampattern and communication channel are derived into two-dimensional range-angle expressions, aiming to implement joint range-angle beamforming. The communication symbols are strategically placed in predetermined range-angle positions of each user through the beampattern modulation technique. The LPI optimization problem is formulated as the minimization of transmitting beamforming power, subject to the constraints of radar SINR and communication sum-rate. To tackle the non-convex problem with high-dimensional nonlinear constraints, a distributed alternating algorithm is developed for fast solving, involving sequence convex approximation (SCA), weighted mean-square error minimization (WMMSE), and alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM). Simulation results demonstrate that our method achieves punctate multi-target detection and multi-user communication in two-dimensional range-angle planes, enormously facilitating the LPI capability.

title: Knowledge-Enhanced Contrastive Learning for Unsupervised Signal Modulation Clustering
authors: Jie Chen, Shilian Zheng, Luxin Zhang, Keqiang Yue, Zhijin Zhao
abstract: The adaptability of modulation recognition in open environments is challenging, making unsupervised modulation clustering an increasingly prominent research focus. However, traditional unsupervised clustering methods that rely only on data-driven learning have difficulty maintaining stable clustering performance under complex channel conditions. In this paper, we propose a knowledge-enhanced contrastive clustering (KECC) method for unsupervised signal modulation clustering, which introduces a new paradigm of clustering guided by expert knowledge in signal processing. KECC consists of two stages: first, in the knowledge-enhanced contrastive pre-training stage, expert knowledge is embedded into the shared feature extraction network by constructing an auxiliary expert knowledge branch and a data augmentation branch. Then, in the knowledge-screening contrastive fine-tuning stage, the neighborhood relationship guided by expert knowledge is utilized for feature optimization, and a clustering head is directly trained to achieve unsupervised clustering. Experimental results on multiple public modulation datasets show that KECC significantly outperforms existing baseline methods in terms of ACC, ARI and NMI metrics, achieving state-of-the-art clustering performance.

title: DRL-Enabled Polymorphic Acceleration Framework for Flexible and Energy-Efficient Hybrid-Float Deep Learning Inference in Mobile Computing
authors: Qunkang Meng, Jie Hu, Zhihan Zhang, Zhan Li, Xingyu Shi, Jun Luo, Qijun Huang, Hao Wang, Sheng Chang
abstract: Hybrid-float quantization has emerged as a promising solution for efficient deep neural network inference on mobile platforms, but its practical deployment is still limited by three challenges: compute–transmission imbalance, rapidly growing mapping complexity, and the tradeoff between intermediate-data movement and hardware cost under tight memory constraints. To address these issues, we propose a DRL-enabled polymorphic acceleration framework for hybrid-float inference in mobile computing. The framework integrates three tightly coupled modules: a Graph Builder that constructs a compute–transmission aware parameterized DAG to expose workload imbalance, a ScaleCore Designer that provides a retargetable hybrid-float hardware template with flexible low-bit floating-point support and a three-level memory hierarchy, and a DRL Scheduler that jointly optimizes deployment and hardware-parallelism decisions under feasibility constraints and normalized multi-objective costs. Experiments on three FPGA platforms across complex CNNs and Bert show that the proposed framework achieves 16.36%–20.25% lower EPF or 19.92%–25.44% higher energy efficiency than state of-the-art accelerators. These results demonstrate a practical path toward flexible, energy-efficient, and scalable hybrid-float deployment for future mobile intelligence systems.

title: Aging Behaviors of Ester-Insulated Oil–Paper Composite Systems under Combined Electrical and Thermal Stresses – Part I: Evolution of Oil Properties and Molecular Microscopic Mechanism
authors: Jiaming Yan, Yixin He, Wenyu Ye, Guangliang Liu, Tianxiang Ding, Xinhan Qiao, Jianwen Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Ester-based insulating oils have been proposed as alternatives to mineral oil, yet their electrothermal aging behavior in oil-paper insulation systems remains insufficiently understood. In this study, accelerated electrothermal aging experiments at 130 °C and 6 kV were conducted on oil-paper insulation systems based on soybean-based natural ester insulating oil and palm fatty acid ester insulating oil (PFAE), together with reactive molecular dynamics simulations. Experimental results show that the increases in kinematic viscosity and acid value of PFAE insulating oil during electrothermal aging are smaller, demonstrating its superior oxidation stability and thermal stability. Although the physicochemical and electrical properties of both insulating oils deteriorate with aging, PFAE maintains a higher breakdown voltage throughout most of the aging process, reflecting better dielectric performance under combined electrical and thermal stress. In addition, the PFAE-based oil-paper insulation system exhibits significantly lower furfural generation, indicating reduced cellulose degradation. Unlike previous studies, this work establishes a clear correlation between macroscopic aging parameters, including moisture content, kinematic viscosity, acid value, dielectric properties, and furfural content, and the underlying microscopic reaction mechanisms. The results further demonstrate how the intrinsic molecular structures of different esters influence degradation pathways and by-product evolution, thereby providing a mechanistic basis for understanding electrothermal aging behavior in ester-based insulation.

title: Coding Agents in the Wild: Failure Modes and Rejection Patterns of AI-Generated Pull Requests
authors: Mahd Hindi, Yasir Mahmood, Linda Mohammed, Salah Bouktif, Mohammed Mediani
abstract: Autonomous coding agents increasingly submit pull requests (PRs) to real software repositories, making it important to evaluate their behavior under actual review, testing, and governance workflows rather than only through synthetic benchmarks. This paper investigates how agent-generated pull request (APR) rejection evolves over time, which visible failure modes most often explain rejected APRs (RAPRs), and how these findings inform more effective coding-agent deployment and design. We follow a three-phase methodology. First, we construct an APR cohort from the AIDev-POP subset of the AIDev dataset, covering 12,433 APRs from 1,495 popular repositories and five coding agents. Second, we develop a socio-technical taxonomy of rejection reasons and validate a large language model (LLM)-based classifier on a manually labeled ground-truth subset of commented RAPRs, achieving 85.5% accuracy in identifying the main bucket reasons of the rejection. Third, we model temporal rejection patterns and conduct stratified analyses across agents, deployment settings, and reviewer types. Observational results show a significant decline in APR rejection odds over time after accounting for differences in change size, documentation and test updates, and agent type. This decline is not limited to a single agent in particular. Empirical results show that documentation co-changes are associated with lower rejection odds, whereas test-touch and patch size provide weaker differentiation. Most RAPRs are silent, with 84.2% closed without inline reviewer feedback. Among commented RAPRs, functional failures are the dominant visible rejection pattern, especially specification mismatch and logic defects. Overall, APR outcomes reflect interactions among agent behavior, repository guardrails, and developer practices. We translate these findings into evidence-informed design principles and advocate reflexive coding agent as a new design pattern that emphasizes context acquisition, local validation, and closer alignment with repository expectations. Our replication package is available at: https://github.com/mahdhindi/coding-agents-wild.

title: ST-GCN and Reinforcement Learning-Assisted Dynamic Multi-Strategy Task Offloading in Edge-IoT Vehicular Networks
authors: Chuang Li, Xi Chen, Gang Liu, Jianhao Wei, Yanhua Wen, Junyan Hu, Qingyu Shi, Zhao Tong
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) enables intelligent transportation services by connecting vehicles with roadside infrastructure and generating time-sensitive data. To support low-latency processing, edge-IoT vehicular networks deploy distributed edge servers near mobile users. However, high vehicular mobility and heterogeneous edge resources make it difficult for existing approaches to effectively exploit spatio-temporal mobility patterns and to support real-time offloading decisions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes TPADO, a Trajectory Prediction-Aware Dynamic Offloading framework that integrates a Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network (ST-GCN) with a multi-agent decision mechanism based on Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). TPADO employs ST-GCN to perform high-fidelity trajectory prediction by explicitly modeling the graph structure of vehicular networks, thereby enabling proactive candidate-node selection and mobility-aware result delivery. Based on the predicted mobility information, we further design a hierarchical multi-strategy offloading framework, where a DRL-based policy layer adaptively selects offloading strategies, and a rule layer performs fine-grained node assignment and task partitioning. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that TPADO achieves the best overall performance among the compared methods. Compared with the centralized DQN baseline, it reduces global average latency by 6.4% and system saturation by 3.01 percentage points, while also delivering higher throughput and task success rate. These results validate the effectiveness and generalizability of the proposed framework.

title: Technical and economic feasibility study of a grid-connected solar power plant in northwest Algeria for charging Electric Vehicles
authors: Ghizlene Cheikh, Fatima Boutlilis, Jânio Monteiro, Cristian-Győző Haba, Hadj Adda Bentounes
abstract: The ongoing expansion of electric vehicles requires the development of charging infrastructures, preferentially powered by clean energy sources. This study investigates the optimal design of a hybrid photovoltaic/battery/grid system to supply an electric vehicle charging station in Mesra, Mostaganem province, Algeria. The approach integrates 22 years of high-resolution climate data analysis, the development of a representative charging load profile based on three EV models, and a techno-economic optimization performed with HOMER Pro software. The proposed station is dimensioned to satisfy the expected daily charging load, corresponding to about 97 vehicles per day. Results highlight an optimal configuration combining photovoltaic panels, a battery storage system, and grid backup, ensuring full supply reliability. The energy mix achieves a well-balanced contribution between solar generation, battery storage, and grid electricity. Economic analysis demonstrates strong feasibility, characterized by a highly competitive levelized cost of energy and a reasonable initial investment, confirming the economic attractiveness of the proposed system. From an environmental perspective, the system achieves complete elimination of critical air pollutants and a substantial reduction in CO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> emissions. This research provides both a scalable framework for integrating renewable energy into EV charging infrastructures and a strategic contribution to Algeria’s transition toward a cleaner and more resilient energy mix.

title: Robust Multi-Level Forecast-Based Anomaly Detection for Smart Grid Overload Attacks
authors: Thamidu Naveen, Malka N. Halgamuge, Sisil Kumarawadu, Shahriar Kaisar, V. Logeeshan
abstract: Smart grids rely on advanced metering infrastructure (AMI) for real-time usage data, yet this connectivity introduces vulnerabilities. One critical threat is grid overloading cyberattacks, where an adversary manipulates demand to exceed safe limits and trigger blackouts. Such attacks can harness compromised smart meters to drastically raise the load and destabilize the network. Despite their severity, AMI data manipulation for overloading attacks has received limited attention. We propose a novel anomaly detection framework that combines household and neighborhood-level load predictions. Using deep learning models, our system predicts the electricity usage of each customer, as well as the aggregate community load, allowing detection of sudden deviations that signal an attack. To quantify anomalies, we introduce two metrics: an Abnormality Index (capturing the magnitude of peak deviations) and a Regularity Index (measuring energy consumption pattern consistency). These indices are fed into an ensemble model to detect threats. Crucially, our ensemble model combines multiple classifiers to mitigate adversarial manipulation, making it robust to data poisoning and evasion attempts. Extensive evaluation with a real dataset demonstrates our proposed model achieves a detection rate of 93% and a false alarm rate of 9% in the most challenging baseline conditions, and maintains 81% detection even under the hardest adversarial setting using white-box AutoAttack scenarios, outperforming existing contemporary methods. This integrated solution advances smart grid cybersecurity by combining predictive load modeling, novel anomaly metrics, and robust classification.

title: Lightweight Power Quality Disturbance Recognition for Edge Monitoring in Distribution Networks
authors: Zihao Peng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of distributed generation and power electronic loads, distribution networks exhibit stronger nonstationary behaviors and high-frequency disturbances, leading to growing power quality issues at feeder and transformer levels. For edge monitoring scenarios, disturbance recognition methods must achieve stable and realtime performance under limited computational resources. This paper proposes a power quality disturbance recognition approach based on time-frequency representation and a lightweight convolutional network. Short-time Fourier transform is employed to construct spectrograms that explicitly capture time-frequency structures of disturbances, followed by a lightweight convolutional network for classification. A noise-view constraint is introduced during training to enhance output stability under varying signal-to-noise ratios. Experimental results based on synthetic data demonstrate that the proposed representation effectively differentiates typical disturbance types, and stratified SNR evaluation quantitatively reveals the impact of noise on discriminative structures. The proposed framework provides a deployment-oriented solution for edge-level power quality monitoring in distribution networks.

title: Research on Multi Factor Coupling Modeling and Prediction Optimization Method for Distributed Photovoltaic Power Station Generation Power
authors: Qifeng Qian, Honghou Zhang, Qiansheng Qiu, Lingchao Xiao
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Under the dual-carbon strategy, the scale of distributed photovoltaic power generation continues to expand, and its output volatility poses severe challenges to grid dispatch. To address this, this article constructs a coupled modeling and prediction optimization framework for PV power generation based on multi-source data. The study quantifies the coupling weights of key factors such as irradiance and temperature; establishes a bidirectional long short-term memory network integrated with an attention mechanism (Bi-LSTM-Attention), which adaptively allocates the importance of multi-dimensional meteorological factors via a self-attention layer. Furthermore, an improved adaptive particle swarm optimization algorithm (IPSO) is introduced to perform global optimization of the network depth, number of nodes, and learning rate, while a composite loss function is designed to mitigate overfitting. Validation using measured data from a 30 MW photovoltaic power plant demonstrates that the proposed model achieves a coefficient of determination <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}})$</tex> of 0.953, a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 3.45%, and reduces the root mean square error (RMSE) to 98.7 kW. Compared with other benchmark models, the prediction accuracy improves by more than 20% and 33%, respectively. These findings provide reliable technical support for the high-penetration integration of distributed generation, enabling accurate power forecasting and dispatch decisionmaking under complex weather conditions.

title: A Study on the Mechanism and Coordinated Control of the Conflict Between Excessive Short-Circuit Current and Insufficient Short-Circuit Ratio at the “Desert, Gobi, and Wasteland” New Energy Base
authors: Pengfei Song, Wenhui Zhai, Minghui Huang, Xiaoyu Tian, Wenchao Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Desert, Gobi, and wasteland regions possess extremely abundant wind and solar resources, offering immense potential for renewable energy generation. Once these “desert, Gobi, and wasteland” new energy bases are connected to the power grid, they will increase the short-circuit current of nearby 750 kV substations to levels approaching the circuit-breaking capacity of the switches. At the same time, on the new energy side, issues such as insufficient short-circuit ratio due to low short-circuit capacity and excessive transient voltage rise following AC and DC faults coexist, necessitating the deployment of a large number of distributed phase-shifting reactors. Based on the current contradiction between excessive short-circuit currents and insufficient short-circuit ratios in the “desert, Gobi, and wasteland” new energy bases, this paper first investigates typical power source organization schemes for such bases in the Northwest Power Grid. It analyzes the mechanisms by which voltage-source and current-source equipment enhance shortcircuit currents, concluding that the base provides approximately 20 kA of short-circuit current to the system. Based on the initial current level on the system side, the paper provides recommendations for the selection of constant-voltage and flexible DC systems; Next, the study analyzes and concludes that the high impedance resulting from step-up transformation from the renewable energy generation units to the collection stations is the fundamental cause of low short-circuit capacity at the grid connection points. Based on the two primary challenges faced by renewable energy sites-insufficient short-circuit ratio and transient overvoltage-the paper proposes rapid estimation methods and distributed phase-shifting reactor configuration schemes, respectively; Finally, using a specific operational DC transmission line in the Northwest Power Grid as a case study, the accuracy of the proposed method is verified, and safety assessments of short-circuit current, short-circuit ratio, and transient overvoltage are conducted for DC projects under the 15th Five-Year Plan.

title: Analysis of a UHV Converter Transformer Electrostatic Ring Discharge Failure
authors: Jin Wang, Peijie Luo, Xin Wang, Pengxiang Zhou, Jingyi Dong
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This article analyzes a UHV converter transformer acetylene sudden increase fault. Combining offline and online oil chromatography data, it is preliminarily determined that there is a discharge fault involving solid insulation inside the converter transformer. Through diagnostic tests, it is confirmed that there is an obvious discharge signal inside the converter transformer. Further internal inspection and disassembly inspection are carried out. It is clear that the cause of the fault is the breakage of the electrostatic ring equipotential line of the lower lead of the coil on the grid side of column 2, which triggers the electrostatic ring suspension discharge and causes abnormal oil chromatography. Based on this, improvement measures such as optimizing the margin control of the lead-out wire of the electrostatic ring, strengthening welding quality control, and strengthening oil chromatography monitoring are proposed to provide reference for the prevention of faults in similar equipment.

title: Analysis and Damping Control of Low-Frequency Oscillations in Renewable-Integrated Power Systems: A Brief Review
authors: Ze Gong, Guangqiang Peng, Jiyang Wu, Zhidi Huang, Xi Zhang, Qinhao Yang, Longjie Tao, Minyu Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Low-frequency oscillation (LFO) is a critical challenge threatening the stability of power systems amidst the rapid transition toward a “dual-high” paradigm-characterized by high penetration of renewable energy sources (RES) and power electronic devices. This paper presents a comprehensive review of the mechanisms, analysis techniques, and damping control strategies for LFO in renewable-integrated power systems. First, the LFO mechanisms are categorized into linear and nonlinear theoretical frameworks. Subsequently, three primary analysis methodologies for LFO analysis are discussed, including the model-based methods, the measurement-based methods, and the energy function-based methods. The review further investigates the impact of RES integration on LFO, identifying power flow redistribution, system inertia reduction, and converter-grid dynamic interactions as pivotal influencing factors. Finally, existing damping control strategies are classified into physical-model-based and data-driven approaches. While traditional model-based methods offer strong interpretability, they face challenges regarding model accuracy and complexity. Consequently, this paper highlights the growing significance of data-driven control techniques, which utilize real-time measurement data to overcome the limitations of physical modeling in renewable-dominated power systems.

title: Multi-Source Spatio-Temporal Prediction of EV Charging Demand Based on Spatial Behavior Clustering
authors: Weichang Wang, Shuxin Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the rapid proliferation of urban electric vehicles (EVs), the impact of EV charging loads on distribution networks has intensified. Accurate spatio-temporal demand prediction is a prerequisite for effective Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) coordination and active power rescheduling. However, existing multi-feature spatio-temporal prediction models often suffer from spatial homogeneity bias, neglecting the underlying heterogeneity of regional functions and user behaviors. Furthermore, the forced dimensionality reduction when fusing unnormalized multidimensional covariates frequently leads to feature distortion and gradient instability, resulting in the over-smoothing of predictions during extreme periods. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Context-Aware Gated Attention Spatio-Temporal (CAGA-ST) model. First, leveraging the real-world UrbanEV dataset, node-level physical features are extracted to construct four Heterogeneous Spatial Profiles (HSP), providing an orthogonalized contextual representation. Second, CAGA-ST introduces a dynamic gating network utilizing the Hadamard product to perform channel-wise attention recalibration. Experimental results based on 1,362 charging stations in Shenzhen demonstrate that CAGA-ST effectively stabilizes training gradients, achieving a record-low MSE of 15.00% and RMSE of 38.73% in percentage mode. Most importantly, it successfully breaks the over-smoothing bottleneck, significantly improving the tracking accuracy of extreme peak and valley fluctuations compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

title: A Two-Layer Architecture of BiLSTM Prediction and Nonlinear MPC for Coordinated Multi-Unit PEMWE Hydrogen Production
authors: Peng Yang, Mi Lv, Jingguan Zhang, Minghao Yan, Tingting Gao
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Precise current regulation and temperature management are critical for the long-term efficiency and safety of photovoltaic-driven hydrogen production systems. This paper proposes a two-layer control framework for a three-unit proton exchange membrane water electrolyzer system powered by photovoltaic. A Bidirectional Long Short-Term network provides PV power forecasts at a 5-minute time interval, which are imposed as hard constraints in the control layer. Three strategies are evaluated under identical predicted PV power. Results show that the proposed S3 strategy achieves the highest hydrogen yield, eliminates temperature violations, and delivers the best economic performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining physics-based nonlinear MPC with data-driven forecasting.

title: Variable-Temperature Magnetic Properties Measurement and Improved Temperature-coupled Bertotti Core-Loss Modeling of Fe-Based Amorphous Alloy
authors: Jialin Li, Yu Dou, Jiapeng Zhou, Shuaichao Yue, Tao Chen, Yongjian Li
abstract: Fe-based amorphous alloys are important for medium- and high-frequency magnetic devices. This work investigates the variable-temperature magnetic properties and core loss of Fe-based amorphous ribbon strips and develops a temperature-coupled Bertotti model for loss prediction under coupled frequency–temperature conditions. In this paper, the magnetic properties and core loss of Fe-based amorphous alloy were measured from 25 to 100 °C and from 50 Hz to 3 kHz by the Epstein-frame method. The experimental results show that temperature significantly affects the magnetization and loss characteristics. With increasing temperature, the magnetization curve shifts rightward, the peak permeability increases, and the hysteresis-loop area as well as the total loss generally decreases. Based on the Bertotti loss-separation framework, a temperature-coupled core loss model is established and temperature dependence is incorporated at the loss components. The accuracy and effectiveness of the proposed model are verified using experimental results under varying temperatures and a wide frequency range.

title: Routing of LEO Satellite Networks Based on Characteristic Graph Embedding Heuristic Approach
authors: Yuanzhi He, Shanshan Feng, Zhiqin Cao, Huajun Fu, Xiang Chen
abstract: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks have emerged as a pivotal component of sixth-generation (6 G) communication systems, offering substantial advantages in latency reduction, extensive coverage, and enhanced reliability. Nevertheless, their inherent heterogeneity, dynamic topologies, and frequent inter-satellite link handovers present significant challenges to conventional routing approaches. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes an intelligent routing optimization framework, the Characteristic Graph Embedding Heuristic Approach (CGEHA). This method utilizes the Node2vec model with graph feature information to learn the topology of LEO satellite networks, generating low-dimensional embedding vectors that capture the node-edge structure and the relationship of link information, thereby effectively simplifying the complexity of satellite networks topologies. A novel node-edge feature information walk strategy enhances structural representation capability, enabling precise capture of node-edge relationships. Furthermore, the graph embedding Node2vec model with graph feature information is co-optimized with the heuristic algorithm, which significantly improves the path search efficiency and optimizes the end-to-end data transmission performance. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared to baseline algorithms, the proposed CGEHA routing method effectively reduces end-to-end delay and packet loss rate while increasing throughput, which verifies the effectiveness and availability of the method.

title: A Hybrid Cascaded Active Power Quality Conditioner for Railway Power System
authors: Kai Yang, Minghao Wang, Junyu Chen, Yinbo Ge
abstract: Series-type hybrid railway power conditioners (S-HRPCs) have been proposed to compensate the negative sequence currents (NSCs) and reactive power in the railway power systems (RPSs) with reduced converter capacity. However, their practical application is limited by mismatched intermediate DC voltages. To address this issue, a novel thyristor-controlled LC-coupled (TCLC) hybrid cascaded active power quality conditioner (HC-APQC) is proposed in this article. Owing to its distributed DC capacitor configuration, the HC-APQC inherently eliminates the DC voltage mismatch issue. Furthermore, leveraging the adaptive impedance of the TCLC, the HC-APQC significantly reduces both the required operating DC voltage and the capacity of the cascaded converter. The operating principle of the TCLC is first analyzed, followed by an investigation of the DC voltage mismatch mechanism in S-HRPCs. Then, a two-stage power flow distribution strategy is developed to determine the HC-APQC reference currents for NSC and reactive power compensation under fluctuating traction loads and unbalanced grid conditions. Considering converter capacity constraints, the proposed strategy ensures real-time cascaded submodules (SMs) capacitor voltage equalization and current limitation within the rated range. Finally, experimental results are presented to validate the effectiveness of the proposed system and its control strategy, demonstrating that the HC-APQC achieves a 45% reduction in the required DC voltage compared with conventional APQCs.

title: Adaptive Phase Selection for Stator High-Impedance Grounding Fault Considering Winding Parameter Asymmetry and Nonlinear Potential Distribution
authors: Zixiong Wan, Hongzhong Ma, Baowen Liu, Zhenfei Chen, Chi Zhang, Chijian Jiang
abstract: Accurate faulty phase selection is a prerequisite for reliable operation of stator grounding protection functions in pumped storage generator-motor (PSGM). Conventional methods overlook the natural asymmetry of the three-phase grounding parameters and the nonlinear distribution characteristics of phase potential, which causes coupling between the system inherent imbalance and the grounding fault feature. This oversight frequently leads to phase misjudgment, particularly in high-impedance grounding fault (HIGF). To address this issue, an adaptive phase selection method for stator grounding fault is proposed. First, a zero-sequence model considering parameter asymmetry of stator winding is established. An online measurement technique for asymmetric grounding parameters is developed to quantify the inherent imbalance. Then, based on winding potential distribution under different topologies, the dynamic range of phase angle for the faulty winding potential is defined. On this basis, a phase selection characteristic quantity capable of eliminating interference from random parameter asymmetry is constructed, and its mapping to the faulty phase is established. Finally, the faulty phase selection method that automatically adapts to the winding parameters and topologies is formed. Simulation, experiment and field fault data verify the feasibility of the method and its adaptability in engineering applications.

title: FOF-X: Towards Real-time Detailed Human Reconstruction from a Single Image_supp1-3696142.pdf
authors: Kun Li
abstract: We introduce FOF-X for real-time reconstruction of detailed human geometry from a single image. Balancing real-time speed against high-quality results is a persistent challenge, mainly due to the high computational demands of existing 3D representations. To address this, we propose Fourier Occupancy Field (FOF), an efficient 3D representation by learning the Fourier series. The core of FOF is to factorize a 3D occupancy field into a 2D vector field, retaining topology and spatial relationships within the 3D domain while facilitating compatibility with 2D convolutional neural networks. Such a representation bridges the gap between 3D and 2D domains, enabling the integration of human parametric models as priors and enhancing the reconstruction robustness. Based on FOF, we design a new reconstruction framework, FOF-X, to avoid the performance degradation caused by texture and lighting. This enables our real-time reconstruction system to better handle the domain gap between training images and real images. Additionally, in FOF-X, we enhance the inter-conversion algorithms between FOF and mesh representations with a Laplacian constraint and an automaton-based discontinuity matcher, improving both quality and robustness. We validate the strengths of our approach on different datasets and real-captured data, where FOF-X achieves new state-of-the-art results.

title: Fractional and Fractal Theory for Progressively Scaled Pharmacokinetic Compartment Models with Anomalous Diffusion
authors: K B Shadiya Nasrin, J. Kokila, Yong-Ki Ma
