abstract: Accurate characterization of drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination (ADME) remains a fundamental challenge in pharmacokinetics, as conventional integer-order compartment models often neglect memory effects and anomalous diffusion inherent in biological systems. To overcome this, we develop and analyze fractional-order oral and intravenous pharmacokinetic models with exact solutions in terms of Mittag-Leffler functions that reflect increasing physiological complexity. In addition, parameter estimation is carried out to quantify the underlying kinetic processes and validate the proposed formulations using experimental data. Numerical results show improved fitting of drug concentration profiles. The other part of this study focuses on the application of fractal kinetics to the aforementioned models. The numerical analysis and parameter estimation highlight the enhanced flexibility and descriptive power of fractal approaches, providing a generalized framework for optimizing drug dosing and therapeutic strategies.

title: Transition-Region Co-Optimization for Enhanced dV/dt Ruggedness in 4H-SiC MOSFETs
authors: Zihan Zhang, Lei Yuan, Kaiyu Chen, Xiaowen Wang, Xuesong Liu, Tongxiao Hou, Enhuai Guan, Ruijie Yang, Guibao Wang, Miao Yu, Yuming Zhang, Renxu Jia
abstract: Under high-speed switching conditions, silicon car bide (SiC) metal–oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistors (MOSFETs) are susceptible to reliability failures induced by high dV/dt stress, which has emerged as a critical bottleneck limiting their high-frequency and high-efficiency applications. This article presents a systematic investigation of the failure behavior and underlying physical mechanisms of 1200 V 4H SiC MOSFETs under high dV/dt conditions. Transient electric field concentration across the thin gate oxide in the transition region is identified as the primary trigger of dV/dt-induced failure, based on double-pulse testing combined with EMMI, FIB, and SEM failure analyses. Further TCAD transient simulations reveal that both an excessive thin-gate-oxide length and dynamic incomplete-ionization effects contribute to severe transient gate oxide electric-field intensification during fast voltage ramping. Based on these insights, a co-optimization strategy centered on electric-field redistribution is proposed and experimentally validated by shortening the thin-gate-oxide length and reducing the surface doping concentration in the transition region. The optimized device can operate reliably at dV/dt exceeding 330 V/ns, with no failure observed even under the extreme condition of Rg = 0 Ω, representing more than a threefold improvement over the original device. Furthermore, continuous-pulse stress tests demonstrate stable gate leakage current after 2.5 × 106 high-speed switching cycles under dV/dt stress above 300 V/ns, indicating no observable gate-oxide degradation. Large-scale statistical measurements on 1200 V devices confirm that key static parameters, including threshold voltage, on-resistance, and breakdown voltage, remain unchanged, while HTRB testing at 175°C for 1000 h verifies that the proposed optimization does not compromise long-term static reliability.

title: Orchestrating Data Collection and Computation in Green IoT Networks
authors: Junfei Zhan, Tengjiao He, Kwan-Wu Chin, Benyu Chen, Fei Song
abstract: Future Internet of things (IoT) networks will host applications that involve data collection and computation tasks on one or more servers. To this end, this paper proposes the first mixed integer linear program (MILP) to schedule and embed applications on energy harvesting nodes, where it optimizes (i) the sampling time of devices, (ii) whether to run an application, and (iii) the energy usage of devices, gateways and servers. To ensure applications are run often, we adopt the maximum age of service (AoS) metric, and set the MILP’s objective to minimize the maximum AoS or min-max AoS of applications. This paper also proposes two novel solutions: (i) a receding horizon control (RHC) based method, and (ii) a solution that greedily embeds applications according to their AoS. The results show that the min-max AoS of RHC and greedy approach is respectively 1.07x and 1.13x higher than MILP.

title: Communication-Efficient Dual-Branch Transmission with Semantic Masks and Quantized Features for V2X Cooperative Perception
authors: Di Wu, Jiankun Peng, Yuming Ge, Shuangzhi Yu, Ke Xu, Chunye Ma
abstract: In IoT-enabled V2X cooperative vehicle–infrastructure perception, roadside units must transmit informative representations to vehicles over bandwidth-limited and latency-sensitive links, making it challenging for single-modality transmission to reduce communication payload while preserving detection accuracy. This paper presents a communication-efficient V2X cooperative 3D object detection framework, termed QMDet (Quantization-and-Mask based Detector). In the proposed design, the roadside unit jointly transmits a low-bit compressed feature map and a semantic mask to the vehicle. The feature branch performs learnable joint channel–spatial compression followed by INT8 quantization. In parallel, the semantic branch conducts contextual modeling and generates a semantic mask, providing explicit semantic priors to compensate for information loss caused by compression and quantization. Furthermore, a gated fusion module is introduced to adaptively integrate vehicle-side features, decompressed roadside features, and semantic priors via dynamic weighting, thereby mitigating cross-view/domain discrepancies. Experiments on the DAIR-V2X-C dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework reduces the RSU-to-vehicle communication payload by 24% while maintaining detection accuracy, achieving gains of 0.11% on AP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3D</sub>@0.5 and 0.13% on AP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BEV</sub>@0.5. These results indicate that QMDet improves communication efficiency without sacrificing cooperative detection accuracy.

title: Transformer-Enhanced Machine Learning Framework for Advanced Palm Biometric Recognition
authors: SYeda Adan Fatima Gilani, Muhammad Fasih Uddin Butt, Zirwa Khan, Arshad Ali Khan, Wadood Abdul
abstract: Palmprint (PPR) and palmvein recognition (PVR) have emerged as reliable biometric modalities due to their rich structural information and resistance to impersonation. In this study, we design a dual-modality recognition pipeline that evaluates both palm-based traits using a combination of conventional machine learning (ML) classifiers and recent deep feature extractors. Classical models, including Support Vector Machines (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Random Forests (RF), and the lightweight MobileNetV2 network, serve as strong baselines, producing accuracies of 98.8%, 98.8%, and 99.4% for PPR; and 95.5%, 97.5%, and 96.8% for PVR. To explore more expressive feature representations, we further employ EfficientNet-B3, Swin Transformer, and Vision Transformer (ViT). EfficientNet-B3 achieves a training accuracy of 94.8% and a validation accuracy 92.3%. The Swin Transformer further improves performance reaching 98.5% validation accuracy with a precision of 98.7%, an AUC-ROC of 0.996, and an inference time of 12 ms per image, indicating efficient inference performance. ViT also demonstrates stable validation performance under the adopted protocol, achieving 99.44% accuracy during training and 98.11% on validation data. To leverage the strengths of both CNN and Transformers models, a hybrid design is developed in which EfficientNet’s spatial feature maps are combined with Swin Transformer’s hierarchical attention features, resulting in more stable and discriminative representations. Finally, the complete recognition system is deployed through a Streamlit-based interface for real-time palm verification, demonstrating the feasibility of proposed approach under the adopted evaluation setting. Overall, the results indicate that merging traditional ML pipelines with contemporary CNN and Transformer-based approaches leads to strong closed-set identification performance of both PPR and PVR systems.

title: CMPCC: Cross-modal Point Cloud Completion Model Fusing Single-view Images and Partial Point Clouds
authors: Liming Zhang, Chenxi Sun, Yuxing Shi, Tingcheng Zhang, Jinfeng Jia, Lu Gao
abstract: Point cloud completion is a core task in 3D computer vision, aiming to recover the complete 3D shape of an object from partially incomplete point clouds collected by 3D scanning sensors. In practical scenarios, 3D scanning sensors are affected by factors such as occlusion, insufficient illumination, and resolution limitations, leading to frequent regional missing in the collected point clouds. However, most existing main-stream methods rely on unimodal shape priors and directly infer missing regions from local point clouds through encoder-decoder architectures. This easily results in the loss of local geometric details and inconsistency in global structure, making it difficult to meet the requirements of high-precision applications. To address the above issues, we reconstruct point cloud completion as a cross-modal collaboration task and propose a cross-modal point cloud completion model fusing single-view images and partial point clouds (called CMPCC). The model integrates complementary information from monocular images and local point clouds to achieve high-quality completion with global structure fidelity and refined local details. CMPCC consists of two stages: (1) the first stage is coarse-grained point cloud reconstruction, which uses CNN to extract global features from single-view images and generates a coarse-grained complete point cloud through deconvolution to ensure consistency between the overall contour and the real object. Then, a probability-based patch segmentation strategy is adopted to divide the coarse point cloud into several local patches, and patch-level features are extracted to lay the foundation for detail generation. (2) The second stage is dense point cloud generation, which uses DGCNN to extract fine-grained geometric features of local point clouds, capturing the surface texture and structural relationships of objects. Through a cross-attention fusion module, image features and point cloud features are fused to generate a global style code that constrains the consistency of the global structure. A multi-patch generator is used to fuse coarse-grained patch features with fine-grained geometric information, generating local patch point clouds with rich details. Finally, the patches are stitched together and sampled via FPS to obtain the complete point cloud. Experiments on the ShapeNet-ViPC and ModelNet-MPC datasets show that CMPCC significantly outperforms unimodal/cross-modal baseline methods: on ShapeNet-ViPC, the average F-Score reaches 0.869 (7.3% higher than XMFNet), and the average CD is 1.066×10⁻³ (22% lower than XMFNet); on ModelNet-MPC, CMPCC achieves an average CD of 1.267×10⁻³ and an average F-Score of 0.845, reducing the average CD by 16.4% and increasing the F-Score by 6.8% compared with XMFNet, and reducing the average CD by 9.0% and increasing the F-Score by 5.8% compared with EGIInet, demonstrating strong cross-dataset generalization and performance superiority of the model.

title: LMFF-Net: Lightweight Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Object Detection for Underwater IoT Networks
authors: Xiushuai Xu, Zhibin Xie, Qian Li, Xin Shu, Changbin Shao
abstract: With the advancement of Underwater Internet of Things (U-IoT) networks, underwater object detection has become crucial. However, detection performance is severely hindered by underwater image degradation, small object scales and weak textures, as well as the stringent resource limitations of deployment platforms. Meanwhile, existing detectors rely on heavy backbones and standard downsampling, which not only increases computation but also discards fine-grained spatial information, resulting in low precision for small objects and suboptimal deployability. To address these challenges, a lightweight multi-scale feature fusion network named LMFF-Net is proposed. Utilizing RepGhostNet as the feature extraction network and incorporating an enhanced bidirectional feature pyramid structure, the proposed LMFF-Net effectively reduces the number of parameters and computational cost while maintaining detection accuracy. In the feature pyramid, a multi-scale object enhancement (MSOE) module is designed, which synergistically combines spatial multi-scale convolutions with spatial-frequency attention mechanisms. This synergy enhances feature discriminability and improves small object representation. Furthermore, a channelized spatial fusion (CSF) module is constructed to achieve lossless downsampling of feature maps by leveraging the spatial-to-depth transformation principle, thereby maximizing the retention of fine-grained spatial information in deep networks. Experimental results on the DUO dataset show that LMFF-Net achieves an mAP@0.5 of 84.7% with only 1.73 M parameters, significantly outperforming other classic object detection models. Additionally, generalization experiments on the URPC2020 and RUOD datasets also demonstrate the excellent generalization ability of the proposed model.

title: Detection Method for Mechanical Deformation of Autotransformer Winding Based on Frequency Response and DATN Algorithm
authors: Lijun Zhou, Hutao Dai, Anzhi Fu, Xiaobo Zhang, Weiheng Liu, Guochao Qian, Dongyang Wang, Lei Guo
abstract: Autotransformer is one of the core electromechanical equipment in high-speed railway, of which winding mechanical deformation is the primary cause of operation failure. Frequency response analysis (FRA) is an effective method for detecting winding mechanical deformation. For improving generalization capability of detection method based on FRA, this paper studies a discrepancy analysis based on deep learning and result interpretation method for FRA curves. Firstly, a differential signal extraction network is developed to capture the discrepancy information between healthy and faulty curves, improving the effective utilization of FRA curve data. Subsequently, a combination of Multi Kernel Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MK-MMD) and adversarial training is incorporated into the network to guide the feature extractor in learning domain invariant features. Finally, the differential adversarial transfer network (DATN) diagnostic algorithm is proposed to achieve identification of winding mechanical deformation faults. Experimental results demonstrate that the DATN algorithm achieves an accuracy of 96% in the source domain and 90% in the target domain.

title: Hypersharpening Using Inter-cluster Sample Equalization and Intra-cluster Low-rank Constraints
authors: Jiaxun Li, Xiaolin Han, Huan Zhang, Weidong Sun
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Hyperspectral remote sensing plays an irreplaceable role in fine earth observation tasks, where the complete preservation of fine and rare spectral features is the core premise for subsequent quantitative applications. Hypersharpening, which enhances the spatial resolution of low-spatial-resolution hyperspectral (LH) images using the corresponding panchromatic (Pan) image over the same scene, is a critical technique to bridge the inherent spatial-spectral tradeoff of hyperspectral imaging. Dictionary learning based hypersharpening is a powerful way to achieve this goal by optimizing an objective that minimizes the sum of representation errors for all pixels. However, it may lead to larger fusion errors for small-sized clusters, which is not conducive to subsequent applications, such as the small and weak spectral target detection or classification. Faced with this issue, a Sample Equalized Hypersharpening method is proposed in this letter. Specifically, an inter-cluster sample equalization model is proposed to balance the number of spectral samples across clusters. Then, a subpixel-shift decomposition strategy is introduced to generate successive equalized subpixel-shifted sub-Pan images consistent with the LH resolution, and to transform the hypersharpening problem into a series fusion problems between the above sub-PAN and LH images. Finally, a joint optimization process, driven by spatial continuity and spectral similarity, is applied under intra-cluster low-rank constraints, to accurately extract the spectral information from the spectral dictionary within the LH image and the spatial information from its coefficient matrix within the LH and Pan images. Extensive experiments against related leading-edge methods indicate that, our proposed method can reconstruct high-spatial-resolution hyperspectral images with superior performance. Notably, for small-sized clusters, it achieves 0.89dB higher peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) 0.80° and lower spectral angle mapper (SAM), effectively preserving the rare fine spectral features of small targets.

title: Learning for Statistical Channel Fingerprint-Based Positioning in Massive MIMO System: Static Mapping and Dynamic Tracking
authors: Xinrui Gong, Xiaofeng Liu, Xu Dong, Chen Sun, Xiqi Gao, Xiaohu You
abstract: Accurate, low-latency, and infrastructure-light positioning is a core requirement for 6G integrated positioning and communications (IPAC), yet remains challenging when robustness to fast fading and applicability to both static and dynamic scenarios are required. This paper proposes a unified massive MIMO positioning framework built on Beam-Domain Statistical Channel Fingerprints (BDSCFs), which are long-term, location dependent angular–delay energy matrices that are temporally stable and naturally available at the base station. BDSCFs serve as a unified representation that bridges fingerprint-based localization and continuous tracking. For static localization, we reformulate positioning as a conditional distribution learning problem and develop a BDSCF-Feature Extraction Conditional GAN (BFGAN), in which the generator maps latent noise and BDSCFs to 2-D coordinates while the discriminator enforces data-consistent sampling. The training objective combines ad versarial loss with a geometric regularizer; we show theoreti cally that the adversarial term minimizes the Jensen-Shannon divergence between generated and true conditional distributions, while a Lipschitz-type bound on the generator preserves spatial consistency. For dynamic tracking, we design a Transformer based tracker that operates on short temporal sequences of BDSCF tensors. By applying patch embedding and multi-head self-attention, the tracker learns motion-aware spatio-temporal features and enables low-latency regression under an offline training/online-inference paradigm. Geometry-based simulations demonstrate that BFGAN surpasses deterministic regression baselines and benefits from denser reference grids and more antennas; the Transformer attains 95% of errors ≤ 2 m and shows the expected sensitivity to sequence length. The BDSCF (BFGAN + Transformer) pipeline bridges fingerprinting and continuous tracking, offering a practical path toward accurate positioning in 6G IPAC systems.

title: Robust Target-Sensitive Region Segmentation via Adaptive Frequency Fusion and Dynamic Optimization
authors: Hua Lu, Guoheng Huang, Qiaorui Che, Bingo Wing-Kuen Ling, Zhipeng Zheng, Xiaochen Yuan, Xuhang Chen, Zhoule Feng, Chi-Man Pun, Qingjian Ye
abstract: Target-sensitive region segmentation plays a crucial role in environmental understanding and perceptual decisionmaking. Its accuracy and robustness directly affect the reliability of smart devices in consumer electronics, such as autonomous vehicles and augmented reality glasses, as well as in medical consumer electronics, like intelligent endoscopic lesion segmentation systems. However, in complex scenarios, common cross-domain challenges, such as boundary ambiguity, semantic confusion, and high-frequency interference, often arise. Current mainstream methods overly rely on spatial-domain feature modeling, which limits their ability to explore implicit cross-scale semantic correlations in the frequency domain, thereby reducing segmentation robustness under heterogeneous interference. To address these challenges, we propose the Adaptive Frequency Fusion Dynamic Optimization Network (AFFDO-Net), which integrates three key modules: 1) The Frequency-Adaptive Enhancement Fusion Module (FAEFM), which dynamically fuses high- and low-frequency image information through a frequency-domain decoupling strategy, improving both global localization and edge-sharpening capabilities; 2) The Dynamic Response Optimization Module (DROM), which utilizes a Progressive Channel Self-Attention Mechanism (PCSM) and a Positional Information Mapping Module (PIMM) to dynamically optimize the fused features, alleviating intra-class inconsistency and boundary offset caused by the fusion of high- and low-frequency information; 3) The class-large kernel attention wavelet convolution module (SWC-LKA) embedded in the decoder, which mitigates noise interference, such as reflection artifacts, through its frequency-space dual-domain filtering characteristics, significantly enhancing the detail retention capability of the segmented regions. We conducted experiments on five publicly available datasets and one private dataset (Colps), and the results demonstrate that our network outperforms existing methods.Our code is available at https://github.com/LLH45/AFFDO.

title: Uncertainty Modeling for Domain Generalization in Cross-Scene Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Bin Pan, Lan Ma, Ruiying Li, Xia Xu, Zhenwei Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Domain generalization is a promising approach for cross-scene hyperspectral image classification. Existing domain generalization-based methods usually neglect uncertainty in feature statistics, which may make the trained model vulnerable to statistical changes. Uncertainty modeling may solve this problem by explicitly characterizing the distribution of feature statistics. However, introducing it directly faces two obstacles: properly describing uncertainty is challenging, and the convergence of the entire model is not guaranteed. To address the above issues, in this paper, we propose an uncertainty modeling method for domain generalization (UMDG) in cross-scene hyperspectral image classification, which consists of a newly designed framework and a convergence theorem. The framework develops two strategies to model uncertainty in input and label spaces, respectively. In the input space, we propose a variational style augmentation approach to model the explicit distribution of feature statistics across domains by sampling from a learned Gaussian distribution. To maintain consistency, in the label space, we introduce a label mixup strategy that leverages the same uncertainty to increase label diversity. Furthermore, to ensure convergence of the entire model, we establish a convergence theorem by demonstrating that the model converges in expectation to a stationary point. The major contributions of UMDG include designing an uncertainty modeling method for domain generalization in cross-scene hyperspectral image classification and providing a convergence theorem. Our experiments on three popular datasets have substantiated the significance of our work.

title: Critically Damped Output-Feedback Positioning System for PMSM Servo Drives With Model-Independent Observer and Disturbance Observer via Pole-Zero Cancellation
authors: Seok-Kyoon Kim, Chao Xu, Choon Ki Ahn
abstract: This study develops a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{q}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-axis current loop-free positioning technique for permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs), integrating a specially structured proportional-integral (PI) controller, a model-independent cascade observer, and a fourth-order disturbance observer (FO-DOB). The method effectively handles PMSM parameter and load uncertainties and remains practical for industrial use. The core contribution lies in applying pole-zero cancellation (PZC) to both estimation and control loops: 1) the tailored gains of the observers induce PZC, yielding first-order estimation error dynamics independent of the PMSM model; 2) the filtered position output by the observer drives a FO-DOB to achieve triply-damped disturbance estimation; and 3) the combined observer and FO-DOB construct the PI-type controller, whose gain structure assigns a critically damped closed-loop response through PZC. Experimental validation is conducted on a 1-kW PMSM dynamometer.

title: A High-Gain Loop-Shaping-Based Real-Time Iterative Compensation Control Applied to Ultraprecision Wafer Stages
authors: Ze Wang, Peng Fang, Taotao Chen, Wenxin Qi, Min Li, Chuxiong Hu, Yu Zhu
abstract: In the semiconductor industry, lithography machines require improved throughput and precision under high dynamic motion conditions, where the accuracy of the scanning stage directly impacts lithography quality. Real-time iterative compensation (RIC) offers significant potential for reducing tracking errors due to its high control precision and online iteration capabilities. However, traditional RIC methods are highly sensitive to high-frequency components due to their online nature, making it challenging to properly adjust control loop gains and achieve higher precision. Additionally, for the fourth-order trajectories commonly used in semiconductor applications, different trajectory phases have distinct frequency characteristics, and the conventional RIC method employs a single feedforward strategy, limiting its control performance. To address these challenges, this article proposes a high-gain loop-shaping-based real-time iterative compensation (HGLS-RIC) method with time-varying feedforward parameters. This method utilizes a model-injection feedforward framework and enhances control gain through a loop shaping structure, effectively avoiding high-frequency errors. Furthermore, by setting appropriate real-time compensation parameters for the constant-velocity and acceleration/deceleration phases of the trajectory, compensation accuracy is further improved. Experiments on a gantry-type linear motor motion platform, across three scenarios, demonstrate the high control precision and robustness of the proposed online method.

title: Automated Refactoring for Conditional Branching
authors: Yang Zhang, Xi Zhang, Chunhao Dong, Chaoshuai Li, Grant Meredith
abstract: Conditional branching provides a fundamental structure for executing a specific branch based on the value of a boolean expression at run-time. However, repeated or nested conditional branching can lead to increased complexity. Furthermore, fall-through semantics in conditional branching result in uncontrollable jumps. Refactoring conditional branching manually is error-prone, time-consuming, and tedious. There is a critical need to provide automated refactoring support for conditional branching. To this end, this paper presents ReBrancher, an automated refactoring approach to eliminate repeated or nested conditional branching. Firstly, ReBrancher parses source code into an abstract syntax tree and walks through conditional branching statements. Secondly, it removes redundant fall-through semantics by static program analysis and an automaton. The automaton is constructed from a control flow graph to match patterns of conditional branching. Finally, it converts a conditional branching into a switch expression and removes the fall-through semantics. ReBrancher was evaluated on nine real-world projects involving 25,137 conditional branchings. Experimental results show that a total of 1,790 conditional branching constructs are refactored within an average of 18.62 seconds per project. Furthermore, ReBrancher reduced the average cyclomatic complexity by 4.41% and removed 1,249 code smells, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving code quality.

title: Fast Robust Dual Dynamic Programming for Multi-Stage Robust Intra-Day Microgrid Scheduling
authors: Zhilu Liu, Jianfeng Zheng, Jinpei Lu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Microgrid (MG) plays a vital role in integrating renewable energy and improving local energy management. However, the presence of renewable generation and energy storage introduces both uncertainty and inter-temporal coupling into the MG's operation. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multi-stage robust optimization model for intra-day MG scheduling. The proposed framework captures the sequential decision-making process and incorporates the non-anticipativity property, ensuring that decisions at each stage rely only on currently available information. To efficiently solve the problem, a fast robust dual dynamic programming algorithm (FRDDP) is employed. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Hierarchical Coordinated Control of Source-Grid-Load-Storage in Highly Resilient Power Grids
authors: Xuejiao Jiang, Lei Zhong, Jingxu Yang, Lei Gao, Zheng Fu, Yansong Sun, Lukun Zeng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The integration of large-scale renewable energy sources (RESs) and the widespread application of power electronic devices have posed significant challenges to the resilience and stability of modern power systems. To address these issues, this paper proposes a hierarchical coordinated control for source-grid-load-storage (SGLS) systems in high-resilience grids. Within this framework, a dual-layer optimization model is established. The source and grid layers are optimized to minimize total operational costs while proactively incorporating a resilience margin factor to enhance system adaptability, and the load and storage layers focus on mitigating power fluctuations. A comprehensive set of technical constraints-including power balance, nodal voltage limits, state of charge, and stochastic output limits for wind and photovoltaic power-is incorporated to ensure system security. The model is solved using an improved Particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm to derive the optimal SGLS dispatch strategy. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed method improves nodal power flow balance, achieves rapid voltage recovery under fault conditions, and enhances computational efficiency by over 57 % compared to conventional methods.

title: Research on Transmission Line Image Monitoring Technology Based on Ultra-High-Pixel Imaging
authors: Minghao Zhang, Mengxuan Li, Peng Li, Bin Zhao
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Current transmission line monitoring devices generally adopt CMOS imaging modules with a resolution not exceeding <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 6}$</tex> megapixels. Limited by lens performance parameters, they can only realize condition monitoring and inspection of transmission line corridors via visual monitoring. At a typical span distance of 300 meters, it is difficult to accurately identify defects on the opposite tower body, such as vibration damper slippage and insulator damage. This research takes the fully domestic, independently controllable Vertical Charge Transfer Imaging Device (VPS) as the core, focusing on the application of hundred-megapixel camera modules. Through chip integration, lens design, device integration and field tests, a novel intelligent image monitoring device for transmission lines is developed. It achieves 1 cm resolution at a distance of 300 meters, and innovatively realizes tower body condition inspection based on on-tower image monitoring.

title: A Continuous Wavelet Transform Framework with Frequency-Enhanced Convolutional Attention for Bearing Fault Classification
authors: Yang Wang, Xinzhe Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Motor bearing fault classification is a critical component of intelligent operation and maintenance for industrial equipment. Traditional methods rely on handcrafted features and shallow models, leading to insufficient utilization of frequency-domain information and inherent limitations that restrict classification accuracy. This paper proposes a fault classification method based on continuous wavelet transform (CWT) and a frequency-domain enhanced channel attention convolutional neural network (FECAM-CNN). Specifically, CWT is employed to obtain time-frequency representations of the signals; discrete cosine transform (DCT) and channel attention mechanisms are then used to enhance key frequency-domain features. The extracted features are subsequently fed into a least squares support vector machine (LSSVM) optimized by ISABO for classification. Experimental results on the Jiangnan University bearing dataset demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a classification accuracy of 98.12% under complex operating conditions.

title: Power Optimization Allocation Strategy for Wind-Storage Frequency Regulation Considering Stability and Economic Efficiency
authors: Zhixuan Zhao, Jiajun Ma, Qiancun Chen, Ning Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy reduces system inertia and exacerbates frequency fluctuations. Traditional frequency regulation strategies often overlook economic costs, making it difficult to coordinate stability with efficiency. To address this, this paper proposes a power optimization allocation strategy for wind-storage frequency regulation. Frequency regulation allocation coefficients are introduced to transform the direct optimization of regulation outputs into a coupled “coefficient-power” relationship, thereby reducing model complexity. An improved whale optimization algorithm incorporating nonlinear convergence factors, adaptive weights, and Levy flight is developed to enhance adaptability and convergence. Simulations under step/continuous load disturbances demonstrate that the proposed strategy effectively improves system frequency regulation capability while reducing costs, with the improved algorithm exhibiting faster convergence and stronger adaptability.

title: Proactive Rolling Optimization-Based Scheduling Method for Off-Grid Flexible Synthetic Ammonia System
authors: Jiaqing Zhou, Ziyue Pan, Zhe Wu, Yufei Pan, Wenjie Huang, Muyang Li
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Off-grid scalable synthetic ammonia system is a potential solution that can be used to curb the irregularities of the production of renewable energy sources to help foster the greater use of renewable energy. This paper aims at streamlining the operational plan of such a system, by suggesting a preemptive rolling optimization-based scheduling plan. The methodology uses hourly outputs and consumption rates of wind power, photovoltaics, hydrogen production, hydrogen storage, energy storage and synthetic ammonia as variables of the decision. The optimization problem is solved by using a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) algorithm and the constraints of equipment and minimizing electricity curtailment and maximizing the production of synthetic ammonia. The model is tested with the real data on the wind and solar within a typical week in one of the regions in Northeast China which shows that it is adaptable. The offered strategy allows optimizing the system planning on the level of intelligence, thus offering a solid technical support to the massive implementation of renewable energy and the green transformation of the chemical industry.

title: Truncated Sparse Regularization Adjustment Method for Radar Forward-Looking Azimuth Super-Resolution Imaging
authors: Jie Han, Wenping Song, Kang Huang, Yang Wu, Shiyue Shen, Dongsheng Liu, Haiyong Ding
abstract: The ill-posed problem in radar forward-looking azimuth super-resolution imaging severely impairs the reliability of parameter estimation, which in turn impacts the application accuracy of marine detection and imaging, marine disaster rescue, ship navigation, and so on. For sparse ill-posed problem in radar forward-looking imaging, popular regularization methods lack adaptive regularization parameter selection based on MSE minimization that accounts for the joint effect on variance and bias. The truncated singular value decomposition (TSVD) method tends to misjudge medium-frequency signal as high-frequency noise, resulting in unnecessary signal truncation and the loss of valuable potential information. To address the above-mentioned problems, this study proposes a novel Truncated Sparse Regularization Method (TSRM) that integrates TSVD with sparse regularization. An equivalent weight matrix is constructed to simplify the proposed model. We derive the truncation condition equations for identifying the set of singular values to be truncated by comparing the MSE of the regularized solution with that of the TSVD solution for effective discrimination of medium- and high-frequency signals. Furthermore, a TSRM solution with the partial bias-correction is derived. According to the MSE minimization criterion, the bisection method is employed to adaptively determine the optimal regularization parameter. Extensive high-noise simulation experiments confirm the effectiveness and outperformance of TSRM, offering a feasible solution for ill-posed radar super-resolution imaging.

title: From Dual-Stream Manifold-Aligned Tokenizer to Latent Sensor-Invariant Consistency Model: Rapid Generative Super-Resolution for Cross-Sensor Remote Sensing Imagery
authors: Yujie Mao, Guojin He, Guizhou Wang, Ranyu Yin, Hongxuan Zhang, Ziying Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Diffusion models have established state-of-the-art visual realism in remote sensing Super-Resolution (SR) but suffer from high inference latency. Although accelerated approaches such as latent consistency models (LCMs) enable efficient one-step sampling in a compressed space, their effectiveness in cross-sensor scenarios is hindered by latent manifold misalignment. This limitation arises from the magnified distributional discrepancy within the compressed latent space, which undermines the stability of consistency training and hinders convergence on heterogeneous data. To address this challenge, we propose a unified framework that explicitly decouples cross-sensor SR into sensor-invariant manifold learning and single-step latent residual generation. First, to effectively bridge the domain gap, we introduce the Dual-Stream Sinkhorn-Contrastive Masked Autoencoder (DSSC-MAE). Functioning as a domain-aligned tokenizer, it employs a novel Hierarchical Alignment Strategy that integrates reconstruction objectives with global Sinkhorn optimal transport and local patch-wise contrastive constraints. This design aligns heterogeneous distributions within a unified, structure-preserving latent manifold, effectively mitigating sensor-specific discrepancies. Second, building upon this aligned latent space, we propose the Latent Sensor-Invariant Residual Consistency Model (LSIRCM). Leveraging the sensor-invariant latent representation as a structural anchor, LSIRCM reformulates SR as a controllable latent residual generation task. By incorporating cross-attention mechanisms to integrate this aligned context, it utilizes an Anchor Diffusion Transformer (AnchorDiT) backbone with a hybrid consistency training strategy to condense the iterative sampling trajectory into a deterministic single-step mapping. Extensive experiments on public and newly constructed datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves a superior efficiency-quality trade-off. It surpasses the perceptual quality of state-of-the-art diffusion models while maintaining high structural fidelity, matching the inference speed of regression-based networks. Consequently, our framework offers a robust and computationally efficient solution for cross-sensor remote sensing SR tasks. The code will be publicly available at https://github.com/afomyj/LSIRCM.

title: Quantum Hardware-in-The-Loop for Optimal Power Flow in Renewable-Integrated Power Systems
authors: Zeynab Kaseb, Rahul Rane, Aleksandra Lekić, Matthias Möller, Amin Khodaei, Peter Palensky, Pedro P. Vergara
abstract: Quantum computing has emerged as a promising computational paradigm to address unresolved challenges in the modeling and control of modern power systems. However, most existing studies focus on offline simulations, and a practical framework for validating quantum algorithms in real-time operational environments remains lacking. This study proposes a quantum hardware-in-the-loop framework that integrates a real-time digital simulator with quantum and quantum-inspired hardware to solve combinatorial power flow and optimal power flow formulations under dynamic operating conditions. The proposed framework is validated using the IEEE 9- bus test system and a modified version with integrated solar and wind farms. The results confirm successful integration and convergence within a predefined tolerance. The study also identifies key limitations and challenges, such as limited access to quantum and digital annealers and current scalability limitations, that must be considered in future developments. Nevertheless, the results highlight the potential of quantum computing to significantly enhance the modeling and control of future power systems with high penetration of renewable energy sources.

title: A Novel INFORM Strategy Based on Bidirectional Voltage-Vector Injection for Low-Speed Sensorless PMSM Control Considering Armature Reaction
authors: Minggang Sheng, Anwen Shen, Huan Wang, Xin Luo, Qipeng Tang, Jie Ye, Jinbang Xu
abstract: The INFORM (INdirect Flux detection by Online Reactance Measurement) method is widely adopted for sensorless control of permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs) at low-speed. However, existing approaches either require additional voltage vector injection, increasing the switching frequency of power devices, or neglect the influence of stator current armature reaction, resulting in enlarged rotor position estimation errors. To address these issues, this paper proposes an INFORM-based rotor position estimation method that takes armature reaction into account without altering the switching frequency. By analyzing the mathematical expressions of stator current slopes under the action of voltage vectors, this method employs the current slope differences induced by the bidirectional voltage vectors of three phases for rotor position estimation, which can effectively reduce the position estimation errors caused by stator current armature reaction. Furthermore, a bidirectional voltage vector injection method was proposed, which generates six active voltage vectors within two or three PWM cycles without changing the switching frequency of the standard space vector pulse width modulation (SVPWM) method, thereby further improving the position estimation accuracy. The proposed method features a simple vector processing procedure and easy implementation for low-speed operations. An experimental platform is built to conduct experimental analysis and verification of the proposed algorithm. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively improve the rotor position estimation accuracy.

title: An Improved Affine Optimization Method for Lowcarbon Dispatch in Multi-Energy Microgrids Considering Source-Load Correlation
authors: Yi Sun, Haotian Yang, Aoqi Gegen
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Under the dual influence of source-load uncertainty and correlation, multi-energy microgrids face challenges in achieving effective energy allocation during operation, thereby compromising operational safety and economic efficiency. To address these issues, this paper proposes an affine real-time optimization dispatch method that accounts for source-load correlation. By extending the deterministic economic dispatch model to the affine domain through affine arithmetic theory, the approach comprehensively considers uncertainties on both sides of the source-load relationship, overcoming the conservatism inherent in traditional interval optimization methods. To account for dependencies between sources and loads, identical noise elements are introduced to characterize source-load correlations, accurately depicting the true scope of their uncertainties. Case studies demonstrate that by incorporating source-load correlations, the proposed method resolves interval dependencies and the conservatism of traditional approaches. It more precisely characterizes uncertainty ranges, yielding more economical and less conservative scheduling solutions with significantly narrowed operational cost intervals.

title: Wasserstein Distributionally Robust Optimization for Park-Level Integrated Energy Systems
authors: Xinyu Yang, Yu Guo, Sile Hu, Yuan Gao, Bingqiang Liu, Jiaqiang Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the multiple sources of uncertainty in park-level integrated energy systems (PIES), including the stochastic outputs of wind and photovoltaic generation as well as diversified multi-energy loads, a two-stage distributionally robust optimal scheduling model based on a Wasserstein ambiguity set is proposed. First, detailed mathematical models are established for the electrolyzer, the combined heat and power (CHP)-hydrogen fuel cell (HFC) thermoelectric coupling system, the methane reactor (MR) integrated with carbon capture and storage (CCS), diversified energy storage systems, and multi-energy loads, enabling an accurate representation of energy flows within the system. On this basis, Wasserstein ambiguity sets are constructed for renewable energy generation and multi-energy loads, leading to a two-stage distributionally robust optimization framework capable of characterizing multiple uncertainties. Finally, by leveraging strong duality theory, the proposed model is reformulated into a tractable mixed-integer linear programming problem. Case study results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively balances economic performance and robustness, and is capable of handling extreme operational scenarios in system operation.

title: A Lithium-Ion Battery SOC Estimation Method Based on an Attention-Residual Bidirectional LSTM Network
authors: Bowen Hu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: State of Charge (SOC) is a critical variable in Battery Management Systems (BMS), and its estimation accuracy directly impacts energy management and safety. To address nonlinear dynamics, measurement noise, and long-range temporal dependencies during charging/discharging, this paper proposes an attention-residual bidirectional LSTM (AR-BiLSTM) for SOC estimation. The method takes voltage, current, and temperature as inputs, enhances key temporal representations via time-step attention and input residual projection, and outputs SOC through a regression head. Experimental results demonstrate that this method outperforms others in RMSE, MAE, MAPE, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex> metrics, providing a reproducible and lightweight solution for SOC estimation.

title: Physics-Informed BiLSTM with Adaptive Gated Fusion for Lithium-Ion Battery State-of-Health Estimation
authors: Zhenkai Yan, Bin Yi, Xuanlin Zhang, Yuqi Ke
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Accurate state of health estimation is critical for reliable battery management, but purely data driven models often generalize poorly and may violate physical degradation trends. This paper proposes a physics informed bidirectional long short term memory framework with adaptive gated fusion for lithium ion battery state of health estimation. The model combines health indicators from routine cycling data with a data branch and a physics branch, and learns to adjust their contributions according to the current degradation stage. A physics informed loss encourages monotonic and physically plausible predictions. Experiments on two public benchmark datasets show robust performance under both data scarce and data rich conditions. The results demonstrate that adaptive fusion improves accuracy, strengthens physical consistency, and provides interpretable degradation behavior.

title: Finite Element Simulation Analysis of Tritium Transport in Lithium-Lead Droplets Inside Vacuum Sieve Tray
authors: Bin Zhang, Yongpeng Lv, Peng Ran, Shixin Ruan, Zhaoting Huang, Cheng Yang, Zhihui Zheng, Kai Huang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Safe, efficient, and large-scale hydrogen isotope (tritium) extraction technology is one of the key prerequisites for the commercial application of fusion energy. In this study, a single-nozzle vacuum sieve tray (VST) device was modeled and simulated using the dilute-phase transport - multiphase flow level-set coupling model in COMSOL Multiphysics 6.3. Four nozzle models with diameters of 0.4 mm, 0.6 mm, 0.8 mm, and 1.0 mm were constructed. The internal gas-liquid two-phase flow behavior and tritium transport processes were systematically analyzed, and the tritium extraction efficiency was calculated. The results indicate that, under given operating conditions, the extraction efficiencies reached 96.4%, 92.7%, 86.6%, and 77.1%, respectively.

title: Federated Contrastive Diffusion Prototypes for Robust Private Learning
authors: Jiao Zhang, Xiong Li, Wei Liu, Changyun Li, Muhammad Khurram Khan, Jinjun Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The secure deployment of Federated Learning (FL) is critically undermined by statistical data heterogeneity and a profound vulnerability to adversarial attacks, these weaknesses are exacerbated by FL’s privacy-preserving preclusion of large-scale, centralized data for robust training. Existing proto-typebased methods suffer from representation collapse when naively aggregating from non-IID clients, while generative approaches often lack a principled mechanism for synthesizing features that confer adversarial resilience. We introduce Federated Contrastive Diffusion Prototypes (Fed-CDP), a novel paradigm that transforms the server from a passive aggregator into an active synthesis hub for robust features. Fed-CDP aggregates lightweight client prototypes to serve as semantic anchors, guiding a server-side diffusion model via a contrastive objective. This process synthesizes a high-fidelity feature space explicitly optimized for maximal inter-class separability, a property intrinsically linked to robust generalization. These server-generated features are then distributed to clients as a potent regularizer, aligning disparate local models and directly mitigating client drift. Our extensive evaluations across multiple challenging datasets establish that Fed-CDP outperforms existing state-of-the-art baselines. For instance, on CIFAR-100 under severe heterogeneity (α = 0.1), Fed-CDP surpasses leading methods by nearly 5% in standard accuracy and over 9% in robust accuracy under Projected Gradient Descent attacks. Fed-CDP provides a new blueprint for building secure and high-performance collaborative AI, laying the foundation for trustworthy systems in critical sectors like finance and multi-institutional healthcare.

title: Cross-Center Surgical Step Recognition in Standardized Training Tasks: Dataset, Baselines, and Transfer Analysis
authors: Georg Wolf, Alexandra Eberenz, Georg Männel, Felicia Bader, Michael Thomaschewski, Freschta Stöhrmann-Malekzada, Tobias Keck, Liv Arning, Nazila Esmaeili, Dennis Kundrat, Jannis Hagenah
abstract: Objective: Accelerating surgical training and improving patient safety requires automated assessment that delivers actionable, step-specific feedback. Standardized box-trainer exercises, with their inherently comparable action steps, offer a natural route towards this goal. Reliable detection of surgical steps is a necessary prerequisite, yet it remains largely unexplored for box-trainer exercises. Crucially, practical deployment requires methods that are invariant to center-domains, i.e. platform, instrument, and camera differences. Methods: We (i) formalize surgical steps for three box-trainer exercises and publicly release the first Multi-center Surgical Training dataset MiST-STEP with step annotations; (ii) benchmark cross-center step recognition with a prevalent two-stage pipeline; (iii) study two self-supervised pretraining schemes—MoCo v2 and a lightweight temporal-order task; and (iv) analyze three finetuning strategies while varying the amount of labeled target center data. Results: Using MiST-STEP (i), we find: (ii) single-center models lose up to 48 percentage points (pp) in macro-F1 on unseen centers; (iii) temporal-order pretraining outperforms MoCo v2 and cuts this deficit by two-thirds; and (iv) finetuning with just two labeled videos per target center removes most of the remaining gap. Conclusion: Our study provides the first comprehensive, cross-center benchmark for step recognition in surgical training and shows that the benefit of self-supervision is highly task-dependent. Our dataset is available at https://gitlab.cc-asp.fraunhofer.de/imte-public/liros/mist. Significance: Center-robust step recognition in box-trainer exercises is essential towards the development of automated feedback systems that aim to standardize training quality and ultimately improve patient outcomes.

title: User-Guided Visual Analytics of Genome-wide DNA Methylation Data Based on Self-Organizing Maps
authors: Ignacio D´ıaz, Jos´e M. Enguita, Abel A. Cuadrado, Diego Garc´ıa, Sara Roos-Hoefgeest, Tamara Cubiella, Nuria Vald´ es, Mar´ ıa D. Chiara
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: DNA methylation is a key epigenetic modification with diagnostic and prognostic relevance across a wide range of diseases, particularly cancer. Modern array-based technologies enable high-throughput quantification of methylation states at hundreds of thousands of CpG sites, yielding high-dimensional datasets that pose significant challenges for exploratory analysis and feature prioritization. Existing visualization tools often lack interactivity, integration with machine learning methods, or flexible mechanisms for dynamic dimensionality reduction and biological interpretation. This work presents an interactive analytical framework that extends the Self-Organizing Map approach for epigenomic data exploration. Our method introduces meta sites—representative prototypes of CpG site clusters—enabling interpretable, real-time visualization and machine learning over reduced feature spaces. Through conditional sample projections (e.g., via PCA, t-SNE, or UMAP), user-driven region selection, and the integration of sparsity-controlled logistic regression, we generate metasite relevance maps that reveal discriminative epigenetic patterns and guide downstream analysis. The proposed approach supports iterative, visually driven discovery of co regulated modules and disease-associated methylation signatures, offering a powerful and intuitive interface for multidimensional exploration of complex methylation landscapes. Its utility is demonstrated through the analysis of DNA methylation in pheochromocytomas and paragangliomas, focusing on SDHB mutation status and the role of protocadherine gene clusters.

title: Large Deviation Analysis for the Reverse Shannon Theorem
authors: Shi-Bing Li, Ke Li, Lei Yu
abstract: We consider the problem of simulating a noisy channel using noiseless channels with unlimited shared randomness. This can be interpreted as the reverse problem of Shannon’s noisy channel coding theorem. In contrast to previous works, we employ Rényi divergence (with the parameter α ∈ [0,∞]) to measure the level of approximation, and we obtain the reverse Shannon theorem under this measure, which characterizes the Rényi simulation rate, the minimum communication rate required for the Rényi divergence vanishing asymptotically. Our derivation is done by a precise large-deviation analysis. When the communication rate is above the Rényi simulation rate, we provide a complete characterization of the convergence exponent for the Rényi divergence, called the reliability function. When the communication rate is below the Rényi simulation rate, we determine the linear increasing rate for the Rényi divergence, which implies the strong converse exponent for the order-α fidelity.

title: Metasurface-Assisted Ultra-Wideband Radar for High-Sensitivity Non-Contact Respiratory Detection
authors: Nurhayati Nurhayati, Usman Rizqi Iman, Fitri Yuli Zulkifli, Dewiani Dewiani, Azuwa Ali, Mohd Najib Mohd Yasin, Lilik Anifah
abstract: Non-contact respiration monitoring using impulse-radio ultra-wideband (IR-UWB) radar is attractive because of its low transmitted power, fine range resolution, and clutter-rejection capability; however, practical performance at meter-scale standoff is often limited by weak radar–thorax coupling and clutter-dominated echoes. This paper presents a wearable, flexible metasurface that passively enhances the chest-return signature without modifying the radar hardware or increasing transmit power. The proposed metasurface is implemented as a 6 × 6 array of subwavelength unit cells on a 1-mm-thick polyimide substrate and is optimized for low-GHz IR-UWB operation centered at 7.29 GHz under multilayer tissue loading. Full-wave simulations confirm strong impedance matching, low insertion loss, stable broadside field reinforcement, and bending robustness under practical conformal conditions. In addition, the simulated 1-g averaged specific absorption rate (SAR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1g</sub>) is only 0.0013W/kg under 1Waccepted power, which is far below commonly adopted exposure limits. Experimental validation using a commercial XeThru X4M06 IR-UWB radar at a 150 cm standoff demonstrates that the metasurface-assisted configuration yields more stable respiration-rate tracking and a clearer, more periodic breathing waveform than the baseline case without metasurface. The improvement is maintained for both standing and supine postures under identical radar settings. These results indicate that conformal, fully passive metasurfaces can improve the robustness and practical usability of IR-UWB respiration sensing at meter-scale distances while preserving the inherent low-power and safety advantages of UWB radar.

title: Refined State-error-based Model Predictive Control and Flight Testing of Eagle of Taihu 10: A Canard-Configured Tail-Sitter Technology Demonstrator
authors: Zhixiong Xu, Guangwei Wen, Yixin Hu, Sijia Li, Li Fan, Chao Xu
abstract: As the size of tail-sitter electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft increases, significant challenges have arisen due to a lower thrust-to-weight ratio, more nonlinear aerodynamics during transition, and slower control response. To support the design and implementation of the flight control system for the large-scale tail-sitter eVTOL Eagle of Taihu 100 (ET100), a one-third-scale, 13 kg subscale technology demonstrator, ET10, was developed. This paper presents the control framework tailored for ET series. In this framework, a refined state-error-based model predictive control (MPC) scheme is proposed. By reformulating the translational dynamics, the coupling between translation and rotation is explicitly captured. Aerodynamic feedforward and efficiency-aware control allocation are integrated to mitigate model uncertainties. A PX4 software-in-the-loop (SITL) platform was constructed by integrating data from computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations and experiments to ensure a high-fidelity aerodynamic modeling. Flight simulations were first conducted to evaluate the improved algorithm in terms of tracking performance across various tasks. Next, flight tests of the ET10 were conducted, demonstrating the reliability and robustness of the scheme across the full flight envelope.

title: Design and Analysis of a High Torque Density FSPM Machine with Enhanced Low Pole-Pair Working Magnetic Field
authors: Shuangshuang Luo, Yu Zhao, Dawei Li, Guangyu Wang, Ronghai Qu
abstract: Benefited from the flux modulation effects, flux-switching permanent magnet (FSPM) machines have the advantage of high torque density. However, constrained by the flux barrier effects caused by the spoke-array PMs, the low pole-pair magnetic fields that feature stronger flux modulation effects and torque generation capability have been restricted. This paper aims to realize the efficient utilization of low pole-pair working magnetic fields by enhancing the excitation source and improving the transmission paths. On the one hand, the asymmetric stator teeth and PMs are adopted, which contain rich magnetomotive force (MMF) and permeance harmonics. These harmonics interact synergistically to produce the desired low pole-pair working magnetic fields. On the other hand, the flux bridges is introduced, which provides the low-magnetic reluctance paths for the working magnetic fields. In detail, the analytical model and working principle of the proposed FSPM machine are established and elaborated. Subsequently, the influence of key parameters on torque performances are investigated via finite element analysis (FEA). Furthermore, comprehensive performances comparison between the proposed FSPM machine and a conventional counterpart are conducted. The torque contributions of 1-pole-pair and 5-pole-pair harmonics are increased from 0 Nm and 3.82 Nm to 1.27 Nm and 4.38 Nm, respectively, leading to a 23.7% improvement in torque density. Finally, a 12/17 stator/rotor pole prototype is fabricated and tested.

title: Full-period one-shot prediction of Li-ion battery aging trajectories via multi-view encoding
authors: Jie Huang, Yi Yu, Shangkun Liu, Kuijie Li, Yu Zhou, Liqun Chen
abstract: Data-driven methods for battery aging trajectory prediction are limited in performance due to feature redundancy. To conquer this defect, this paper proposes a prediction framework via multi-view encoding. The method introduces three complementary views: capacity decay, material activity, and polarization shift, and uses independent encoders to extract aging information from each view. It then employs a multi-head attention mechanism to adaptively aggregate features, and finally generates the aging trajectory using a BiLSTM decoder. Experimental results show that, compared with early-fusion baselines, the proposed framework reduces MAPE by 47.5%, achieving a value of 1.05%. In addition, an R2 of 0.87 confirms that the model can accurately reconstruct the aging trajectory. Moreover, the model demonstrates excellent adaptability across aging stages, with prediction error decreasing from 1.16% in the early stage to 0.65% in the later stage. This work establishes a principled multi-view encoding paradigm and provides novel insights for achieving high-reliability state estimation in battery management systems.

title: A Unified Perceptual-Physical Framework for Real-Time Underwater Motion Simulation of Immersive Agents and Large-Scale Crowds
authors: Jong-Hyun Kim
abstract: This paper proposes a unified perceptual–physical underwater motion simulation framework that simultaneously supports immersive user interaction and large-scale crowd behaviors. In underwater environments, various fluid forces, including drag, buoyancy, gravity, and Magnus effects induced by rotation, act on the human body in a coupled manner. However, conventional virtual reality systems have limitations in realistically conveying such forces due to restricted hardware input and the absence of force-feedback devices. To address this problem, we model underwater forces not as direct force feedback but as the temporal evolution of velocity states, and present a unified motion generation framework that can be applied consistently to both user-controlled avatars and non-player characters (NPCs), as well as large-scale crowd simulations. The proposed method explicitly computes buoyancy, drag, and Magnus effects at the level of joints and body segments to generate physically plausible full-body motion, while biomechanically valid joint range constraints prevent unrealistic postures caused by external forces. For user interaction, a controllable perceptual coupling between the simulated physical state and the actual user input intentionally introduces visual discrepancies, allowing users to intuitively perceive underwater resistance without additional haptic devices. The same physical model is further used to naturally produce diverse underwater behaviors in large-scale crowds through variations in initial conditions and body parameters. A real-time implementation in Unity validates the efficiency and scalability of the proposed framework, and experimental results demonstrate stable and consistent underwater motion representation in both immersive interaction scenarios and large-scale crowd simulations. The proposed approach provides a practical solution for integrating physical realism and perceptual experience in underwater environments without requiring additional hardware, and shows potential for applications in virtual reality training, simulation, games, and digital twin environments.

title: OMARF: An Orchestrated Multi-Agent Resilience Framework for Autonomic Software-Defined Offshore Wind Industrial Networks
authors: Agrippina Mwangi, Mikkel Gryning, Elena Fumagalli, Madeleine Gibescu
abstract: Industrial networks are growing rapidly in scale and complexity, making them increasingly vulnerable to random faults and cyberattacks. Traditional resilience approaches often struggle to keep up, motivating the adoption of autonomic software-defined solutions that can detect, respond to, and adapt to disruptions in real time. This paper introduces the Orchestrated Multi-Agent Resilience Framework (OMARF), which integrates self-healing and self-defense agents to manage failures in the control-plane and data-plane components, mitigates thermally-induced network congestion, and defends against persistent cyber threats. Simulations demonstrated that OMARF-enabled networks achieve up to 28.3% lower path latency and higher throughput across diverse scenarios. Even under degraded conditions, the framework maintained a resilience index, RI(t), above 0.625, indicating stable and adaptive performance. Key mechanisms such as controller autoscaling, reinforcement-learning-based path recomputation, and adaptive cyber defense work in concert to sustain a resilient, self-adaptive network state. These results highlight the potential of OMARF to enhance the reliability and security of modern industrial networks.

title: Few-Shot Cross-Domain Indoor Localization via Multi-Modal Feature Refinement
authors: Kaixuan Huang, Junyi Zhou, J. Andrew Zhang, Guangjin Pan, Shunqing Zhang
abstract: Fingerprint-based indoor localization is a critical enabling technology for Internet of Things (IoT) applications, where the primary challenges stem from complex environmental variability and prohibitive costs of data collection and labeling. This paper introduces a cross-domain multi-modal indoor localization framework that effectively combines visual and WiFi signals using few-shot learning techniques, achieving improved localization performance with minimal training data. We derive an upper bound on the generalized transfer localization error. Based on this bound, our learning-based approach applies feature-level knowledge distillation from pre-trained localization models. This process systematically calibrates discrepancies in feature distributions between the source and target environments. As a result, the proposed method significantly reduces the dependence on large labeled datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method achieves substantial improvements over state-of-the-art localization models, with a mean localization error of 0.247 meters across diverse indoor environments, while requiring substantially fewer labeled samples in the target domain.

title: End-to-End Through-Wall Respiratory Waveform Reconstruction for 60-GHz MIMO FMCW Radar
authors: Abdel-Kareem Moadi, Chandler Bauder, Vijaysrinivas Rajagopal, Aly E. Fathy
publish: IEEE Journal of Electromagnetics, RF and Microwaves in Medicine and Biology
abstract: This paper presents an end-to-end deep-learning framework that reconstructs respiratory waveforms for a seated subject in through-wall scenarios using 60 GHz MIMO FMCW radar. Conventional through-wall vital-sign pipelines that include localization, spatial filtering, phase extraction, and downstream biomarker analysis, are fragile under wall-induced attenuation and dispersion. This usually promotes lowering the operating frequency yielding inaccurate localizations and noisy waveforms. To address these limitations while retaining the spatial resolution of mmWave radar, we develop a single deep neural network that operates directly on multi-channel range-domain I/Q data and outputs the corresponding time-domain respiration waveform, implicitly performing localization, spatial filtering, and temporal reconstruction. The model is trained and evaluated on 720 minutes of data from three separate subjects positioned at ranges of 1–2 m and azimuths within <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm \mathbf{60}^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> behind drywall, cement board, glass, and wood panels of varying thicknesses. Across all wall types, ranges, and angles, the network attains a mean cosine similarity of 0.92 between reconstructed and ground-truth waveforms and maintains 0.91 on a held-out glass wall material, indicating robustness to moderate changes in wall composition within this experimental setting. Relative to baseline methods, the proposed approach reduces waveform mean-squared error by at least 43% and decreases errors in derived biomarkers, breathing rate, inter-breath interval, and inhalation/exhalation ratio, by up to 69%. These results demonstrate that end-to-end learning on 60 GHz MIMO FMCW radar can provide accurate through-wall respiration waveforms suitable for downstream biomarker analysis while avoiding hand-engineered localization and phase-extraction stages.

title: Energy-aware Adaptive Topology Control for UOWSNs_supp1-3693790.pdf
authors: Chi Lin
abstract: Underwater Optical Wireless Sensor Network (UOWSN) is a promising technology as it can achieve high-speed communication in underwater environment. However, affected by the uncertainty of complex underwater environment, the network topology of UOWSN is highly dynamic, making it difficult to quantify flexibility or further optimize the topological structure. Additionally, node mobility and energy constraints pose significant challenges to reliable communication. In this paper, we propose a mobility-aware and energy-efficient flexibility-based network topology evaluation model (ME-FEM) for UOWSNs. Then, a reinforcement learning model, termed ME-FEM-DRL, for optimizing the network topology based on ME-FEM is developed, which enables UOWSN to maintain an optimal topology when working in harsh underwater environments. Theoretical analysis proves the NP-hardness of the optimization problem and demonstrates that our algorithm achieves an approximation ratio of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(\log N)$</tex-math></inline-formula> with optimal parameter boundaries. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method can significantly improve the network flexibility. Compared with the five baseline algorithms in simulations, ME-FEM-DRL reduces normalized topology optimization time cost by 64% and extends network lifetime by 95% on average. Test-bed experiments verify the applicability and effectiveness in practical applications for detecting emergent events.

title: False Alarm Rate-Controllable Maritime Target Detection Via Cross-Attention Transformer Network
authors: Qingsong Jiang, Wenjing Zhao, Weiqing Zhao, Guolong Cui
abstract: To address degraded maritime target detection and false alarm rate (FAR) control under strong sea clutter, this paper proposes FARC-CAT, a FAR-controllable detector based on a dual-encoder cross-attention Transformer. An energy-frequency dual-branch encoder is designed for feature extraction, followed by bidirectional cross-attention and gated fusion to enhance feature interaction. In addition, a hybrid loss with a learnable threshold is introduced to constrain FAR during training. Experiments on publicly available measured datasets show that FARC-CAT outperforms representative methods in low-SCR scenarios while maintaining a low FAR. In particular, under a preset FAR of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{-4}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, it achieves an average detection probability of 91.97%, outperforming the existing representative methods.

title: Sensor Data Quality Scoring for MEMS Accelerometers Via Spectral Anomaly Detection
authors: Tim Ruhland, Kilian Müller, Maximilian Lübke, Norman Franchi
abstract: Low-cost MEMS accelerometers are widely deployed for industrial condition monitoring, yet their data streams lack intrinsic quality metrics. This can lead to false alarms caused by sensor artifacts rather than genuine process anomalies, and to diminished trust in condition monitoring systems. This work introduces a lightweight, unsupervised framework for computing a continuous Quality of Sensing (QoS) score. A feedforward autoencoder learns a spectral fingerprint from power spectral density (PSD) and time-domain features during nominal operation – the reconstruction error is then mapped to a normalized QoS parameter. The proposed approach isolates intrinsic MEMS degradation mechanisms. The framework is validated against physically grounded noise simulation: thermo-mechanical noise, vibration rectification error (VRE), and flicker-induced bias instability. The autoencoder detects all three degradation mechanisms, with VRE producing the steepest response. Experiments under laboratory shaker excitation, electric motor vibration, and idle conditions show that real operational data yield the optimal detection sensitivity. The lightweight architecture supports future edge-oriented deployment and improves the trustworthiness of Industrial IoT sensor systems.

title: Research on Dual-Frequency Penetrating Radio Wave Detection Method and Application in Fault Structures of Coal Seam Working Face
authors: Rongxin Wu, Yao Pang, Zean Hu
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Radio wave penetration technology is widely employed for geological anomaly detection in coal seam working faces; however, conventional single-frequency techniques exhibit limitations in fault detection, including insufficient sensitivity to weak fault anomalies and ambiguous fault boundary characterization. Although multi-frequency radio wave exploration has been attempted, data processing and imaging methodologies remain confined to single-frequency approaches, failing to fully exploit the potential advantages of multi-frequency data. To address these constraints and achieve more precise detection of fault structures in coal seam working faces, a radio wave penetration data processing method utilizing the dual-frequency absorption factor γ is proposed. Theoretical derivation elucidates the variation laws between attenuation constants of coal/rock media and radio wave frequency, establishing a computational model for the dual-frequency absorption factor γ based on the ratio of dual-frequency attenuation constant difference to frequency difference. The model demonstrates that γ exhibits more pronounced variation rates in the low-frequency band; consequently, employing γ for data processing and imaging requires at least one low-frequency band to yield stronger anomaly responses. COMSOL multiphysics simulations validate that γ exhibits comparable variation trends to single-frequency attenuation coefficients in coal seam, yet the anomaly amplitude in fault zone doubles relative to high-frequency attenuation coefficient, significantly improving detection sensitivity and response intensity for fault exploration, thereby substantiating the efficacy of utilizing γ for multi-frequency data processing. Field tests conducted at a fully mechanized working face in Zhangji coal mine of Huainan Mining Area, Anhui Province, demonstrate that γ enables precise delineation of fault-affected zone extent for faults with throw less than one-third of seam thickness. The experimental results reveal that, compared with single-frequency imaging, this method achieves more accurate fault boundary characterization and enhanced recognition precision of fault influence zones, showing high consistency with mining exposure results. This method effectively overcomes the deficiencies of single-frequency detection and provides a novel technical approach for fine detection of fault structures and multi-frequency data processing in coal seam working faces.

title: Hearing Materials, Feeling Participation, Caring for Personhood: Situated Lessons from Participatory Design with Nonverbal Participants
authors: Amber De Coen, Ralf Vetter, Sanne Nieuwenhuis
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: Enacting Utopias: A Participatory Design Approach to AI
authors: Christian Dindler, Ole Sejer Iversen, Claus Bossen, Magnus Høholt Kaspersen, Peter Dalsgaard
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: Participatory Design through the Lenses of Women Architects and Designers
authors: Alana Yasmim Lampert, Leandro Miletto Tonetto, Claudia de Souza Libânio
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
