title: Frequency-Selective Adaptive Generalized Orthogonal Matching Pursuit for DME Interference Mitigation in Aeronautical Communications
authors: Siqi Huang, Yanbo Zhu, Zhipeng Wang, Kai Guo, Yiming Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The L-band Digital Aeronautical Communication System (LDACS) experiences severe interference from legacy Distance Measuring Equipment (DME) system, which significantly degrades its communication performance. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a Frequency-Selective Adaptive Generalized Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (FS-AGOMP) method, which integrates frequency-domain interference isolation with adaptive sparse reconstruction. The method begins with frequency-selective processing module to separate DME-dominated spectral components, followed by precise time-domain localization and extraction of interference-rich signal segments. Finally, an adaptive generalized orthogonal matching pursuit (AGOMP) module reconstructs and suppresses the interference, with its key sparsity parameter adaptively determined by a data-driven model based on the estimated interference intensity. Extensive evaluations including numerical simulations and real flight-test data experiments demonstrate that the proposed method substantially outperforms conventional techniques. The FS-AGOMP method achieves a significant reduction in bit error rate (BER), confirming its efficacy in ensuring robust LDACS operation in spectrally congested aeronautical environments. Future work will extend the framework to more challenging scenarios, optimize its computational efficiency for real-time implementation and explore data-driven intelligent adaptation for dynamic environments.

title: Modeling and Stability Analysis of the ISOP-Type Cascaded Hydrogen Converter
authors: RUI ZHOU, FEI XIAO, JILONG LIU, YUFAN LI, SHANXU DUAN
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Industrial Electronics Society
abstract: The ISOP cascaded hydrogen converter is a key device for interfacing the medium-voltage DC (MVDC) bus with the electrolyzers in off-grid hydrogen production systems. However, existing ISOP cascaded hydrogen converters are unable to simultaneously meet the comprehensive performance requirements, including wide input voltage range, high reliability, broad output power regulation capability, and the ability to supply power to multiple electrolyzers concurrently. Moreover, the stability analysis based on the closed-loop two-port small-signal model (CTSM), as well as the impact of multiple controller gains on system stability, has not been thoroughly investigated. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel two-stage ISOP cascaded converter topology along with a stability analysis method. First, the CTSMs are developed for both the switched-capacitor (SC) submodules and the output-stage phase-shift full-bridge (PSFB) converter interfaced with three PEM electrolyzers. Based on these models, the closed-loop transfer functions are derived, and a set of new stability criteria grounded in the Nyquist Criterion are formulated. Furthermore, a systematic sensitivity analysis is performed to evaluate the influence of multiple controller gains on the proposed criteria, providing theoretical guidance for multi-loop controller tuning in cascaded converters. The proposed stability evaluation method can be further extended to the stability assessment of the other two-stage cascaded converters. Finally, both simulation and experimental results are presented to validate the effectiveness of the proposed stability assessment approach.

title: Pre- and During-Disaster Co-Optimized Pre-Scheduling Strategy for Distribution Networks
authors: Dongwei Guo, Xue Li, Jian Wang, Fubo Zhang, Guiming Wang, Haijian Gao
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To mitigate power outages caused by extreme weat her, this paper proposes a multi-stage coordinated resilience-enha ncement strategy for distribution networks. Traditional methods relying on static fault assumptions and isolated dispatch reduce re source efficiency. To address this, we integrate spatiotemporal dy namic fault scenario generation with a two-stage stochastic optimi zation model. The pre-disaster stage optimizes Mobile Energy Sto rage Systems (MESS) deployment, while the during-disaster stage dynamically reconfigures topology and coordinates MESS with D istributed Generation (DG) for active islanding. Incorporating a load importance model, simulations on an IEEE 33-bus system de monstrate significant reductions in expected load shedding costs a nd enhanced practical resilience.

title: Research on Daily Power Generation Prediction of Photovoltaic Power Station Considering Meteorological and Dust Factors
authors: Wenping Qiu, Shuhong Fan
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Photovoltaic power generation is characterized by inherent instability, and improving the accuracy of its daily power generation prediction has long been a research focus. Comprehensively considering meteorological factors and dust accumulation factors is of great significance for enhancing the accuracy of daily photovoltaic power generation prediction. Firstly, an improved SE ResNet algorithm based on convolutional neural network is proposed, which realizes the intelligent identification of dust accumulation state by using the graphical features formed by daily irradiance and dispersion rate data. Secondly, a photovoltaic dust simulation model is constructed to quantitatively analyze the dust impact index of photovoltaic panels. Finally, in view of the time-varying characteristics of meteorological parameters and the cumulative characteristics of dust accumulation indicators, a single model is difficult to accurately capture the complex characteristics of such heterogeneous data; thus, a prediction model combining the improved similar day algorithm with CNN-GRU-Attention is proposed. Experimental results show that the improved SE ResNet algorithm achieves a dust recognition accuracy of 95%, and the proposed hybrid prediction model can significantly improve the accuracy of daily photovoltaic power generation prediction.

title: Loss Modeling and Parameter Co-Optimization of GaN QR Flyback Converter for High-Frequency and High-Efficiency Applications
authors: Yida Wu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the limitations of traditional Silicon (Si)-based flyback converters regarding low efficiency and insufficient power density, this paper presents a design and optimization study of Quasi-Resonant (QR) flyback converters utilizing Gallium Nitride (GaN) wide-bandgap devices. Firstly, a QR topology incorporating a self-driven active snubber is adopted. Secondly, a simulation model is constructed on the PSIM platform to thoroughly analyze the multi-stage resonance mechanism. A comprehensive loss model is established, encompassing device conduction losses, switching losses, and magnetic component losses. Based on this, a co-optimization strategy for component and driving parameters is proposed. By matching the clamp capacitor, optimizing the output capacitor, and adjusting the switching frequency and duty cycle, the converter performance is significantly improved. Simulation results indicate that the optimized output voltage ripple is reduced to within 0.1 V, representing an improvement of 71.4%, while the system efficiency is enhanced by approximately 2% compared to the initial design. The research results validate the application advantages of GaN devices in high-frequency and high-efficiency converters, providing theoretical reference and technical support for the design of low-to-medium power highdensity power supplies.

title: Adaptive Detection of Early Abnormal States in Inverters via a Mechanism Combining Attention and Transfer Learning
authors: Huixuan Yang, Ruizhao Zhang, Jincan Wang, Jinhui Liu, Dandan Peng, Bo Yan
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the issues of low accuracy and poor adaptability in traditional detection methods caused by weak early-stage anomaly features and complex, variable operating conditions in inverters, this paper proposes an adaptive detection model that integrates attention mechanisms with transfer learning. First, a high-dimensional feature set is constructed through sliding window sampling and multi-dimensional feature extraction (timedomain statistics, frequency-domain energy entropy, and transient waveform distortion);second, a dual-branch attention network (channel attention + temporal attention) is designed to enhance the representation of key features and suppress noise interference; finally, a domain adaptation strategy from transfer learning is introduced, using the MMD (Maximum Mean Divergence) metric to measure the difference in feature distributions between the source domain (normal operating conditions) and the target domain (abnormal operating conditions), thereby achieving crosscondition adaptive detection. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves an early anomaly detection accuracy of 96.7% on the PV grid-connected inverter dataset, a significant improvement over traditional SVM and LSTM models, while reducing the false positive rate to 3.1% under various load fluctuation scenarios.

title: Research on Hierarchical Decoupling and Service Permission Control Technology for SCD Files in Smart Substations
authors: Jin Zhang, Renhui Dou, Tao Zheng, Hui Ren
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Substation Configuration Description (SCD) files in smart substations are prone to triggering global anomalies during modification and maintenance due to localized changes. This paper proposes an SCD file management approach combining hierarchical decoupling with service permission control. Based on the structural characteristics of SCD files, the approach decomposes them into three major modules - equipment, relationships, and others - to achieve granular decoupling at the physical layer. In accordance with operational requirements for protection, automation, and metering in smart substations, this approach maps the relationships between various services and their corresponding configuration information. It establishes clear boundaries for operational permissions across different services. This methodology effectively reduces modification risks in SCD files, narrows validation scopes, and enhances the operational efficiency of secondary systems within smart substations.

title: Site - Selection and Capacity - Planning Method of Soft Open Points Adaptation to Distributed New Energy Access
authors: Yiqiong He, Guojun Lan, Lijun Zhao, Xiulu Zhang, Yonghui Sun, Xia Miao, Yong Shi, Jian Le
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The integration of high-proportion distributed new energy (DNE) presents a key challenge in modern power system planning. The inherent uncertainty, volatility, and low-inertia characteristics of DNE create significant operational complexities, requiring transformative upgrades to traditional distribution network frameworks. This paper proposes a planning methodology for the optimal sitting and sizing of Soft Open Points (SOPs) in distribution networks. The approach focuses on minimizing annual comprehensive system costs as its main goal. Furthermore, it analyzes and quantifies the temporal output characteristics of DNE alongside their voltage impacts, developing a sensitivity analysis method that considers DNE output features. Using second-order cone relaxation, the mixed-integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) problem is reformulated into a convex model, greatly improving computational efficiency. By scientifically planning SOP capacity and locations, this method maximizes operational cost savings while improving power quality. Case studies show that strategically placed SOPs reduce annual operational costs by 22 % and decrease voltage fluctuations by 59 %, confirming their effectiveness in high-DNE-penetration scenarios.

title: Weakness Analysis of Distribution Network Based on Multi-Source Heterogeneous Data Collaborative Driving
authors: Xin Lin, Guangjin Wu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The weak point analysis of distribution network with DG is often limited by the scarcity of real-time measurement data, the uncertainty of distributed energy generation power and the existence of bad data. Therefore, this paper establishes a weak point recognition system based on multi-source heterogeneous data. Firstly, the quasi-Monte Carlo sampling of the improved Sobol sequence is used to generate a random DG capacity coefficient matrix. Then, combined with the forward-backward sweep power flow calculation and data-driven feature screening technology, the effective convergence sample set is extracted efficiently. Then, through the probability distribution mapping and error optimization fitting algorithm, the probability distribution parameters in the voltage over-limit scenario are obtained. Finally, the double index evaluation system of over-limit voltage difference and variance is introduced to quantify the degree of node voltage weakness. The simulation results of the improved IEEE 33 system based on the measured data show that the proposed method can significantly improve the calculation accuracy while ensuring the efficiency.

title: Transfer Learning Enhanced Multi-Scale Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring with Dual-Branch Decomposition
authors: Jianmeng Zhang, Runze Li, Xuyong Shang, Minghua Xu, Lianji Liang, Qing Ma
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Non-intrusive load monitoring (NILM) aims to disaggregate aggregate household power consumption into appliance-level signals without additional sensors. Existing deep learning methods achieve high accuracy but often rely on large labeled datasets and considerable computational cost. To address these issues, this paper proposes a transfer learning enhanced multi-scale NILM framework with dual-branch decomposition. The model integrates multi-scale temporal feature extraction, shared representation learning, and parallel regression/classification branches to capture both transient and steady-state appliance characteristics. Transfer learning is introduced to improve adaptability under limited labeled data. Experiments on the UK-DALE dataset with four representative appliances show that the proposed method outperforms CNN- and GRU-based baselines in monitoring accuracy while reducing dependence on large-scale annotations. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining multi-scale fusion and parameter transfer for practical NILM applications.

title: Coordinated Control of Active Preheating and Intelligent Balancing for Lithium-Ion Battery Packs Under Low-Temperature Conditions
authors: Yiran Wang, Chen He
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: A coordinated control strategy integrating active preheating and intelligent balancing is proposed to address performance degradation, consistency deterioration, and insufficient coordination in lithium ion battery packs under low temperature conditions. First, a distributed preheating method based on nonlinear power allocation and multi objective optimization is developed to achieve rapid temperature rise and suppress cell to cell temperature differences while considering heating efficiency, temperature uniformity, and energy consumption. Then, a temperature threshold based cooperative control mechanism is established according to the principle of preheating first and balancing later, enabling adaptive coordination between the two subsystems. An electro thermal coupled simulation platform is built in MATLAB, and four operating cases are designed for comparative evaluation under low temperature conditions. The results show that the proposed method can effectively improve heating performance, reduce temperature differences among cells, and enhance temperature uniformity. Meanwhile, the coordinated strategy significantly improves state of charge consistency and overall operating performance of the battery pack. The proposed method provides an effective solution for battery management system design in cold environments.

title: MFANet: LiDAR-Camera Extrinsic Calibration via Multi-Features Association with Dual-Branch Cross-Attention
authors: Zhenyu Yang, Qing Wang, Xiaolin Meng, Zhongyuan Wang, Yihan Fu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In the fields of autonomous driving and robotic navigation, the integration of 3D LiDAR and 2D camera data for reconstruction and perception tasks has become increasingly prevalent. The effectiveness of such multi-sensor fusion relies heavily on accurate extrinsic calibration. Traditional methods usually require specific targets, elaborate environmental setups, and laborious manual operations, resulting in time-consuming and labor-intensive processes. Furthermore, in practical deployments, sensor systems are prone to drift due to unforeseen mechanical movements, which necessitate frequent recalibration.We propose an online LiDAR-Camera extrinsic calibration(LCEC) network named MFANet. First, to compensate for the lack of 3D geometric and topological information in depth maps, an independent point cloud branch is introduced, which local fine-grained features of projected points and large-scale point cloud features based on pillar partitioning are extracted, constructing a multimodal input. Furthermore, a symmetric dual-branch multi-head cross-attention module is designed to jointly model the structured relationships among RGB features, depth maps, and point-cloud pseudo-images, enabling deep cross-modal feature alignment and fusion. Finally, the translation and rotation extrinsics are directly regressed, and an iterative refinement strategy is introduced to further enhance the accuracy of the calibration results. Extensive experiments on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets show that MFANet exhibits excellent performance, with strong generalization ability and time efficiency.

title: High Resolution Optical Displacement and Polar Axis Deviation Angle Detection Method for Superconducting Magnetic Levitation Rotor
authors: Xu Cui, Xinning Hu, Chunyan Cui, Yuan Zhang, Feifei Niu, Xian Yi, Hao Li, Bowen Yang, Qiuliang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Micro displacement and polar axis deviation angle detection play a crucial role in high-precision measurement of superconducting magnetic levitation rotor (SMLR). A dual channel reflective optical sensor is proposed in this paper for detecting micro scale suspension displacement and deviation angle of SMLR. The high precision detection was mainly realized through structural design and quality improvement of fiber bundles. The dual channel reflective optical sensor ensures that the detector is not affected by external factors and can increase detection accuracy. An optical 3D calibration platform was built for sensors, and the experiments of measurement system showed that the suspension displacement accuracy was 1μm in the range of 1mm, and the extreme deviation angle detection accuracy reached to 0.1"within a small angle range.

title: Coverage Performance Analysis of FAS-enhanced LoRa Wide Area Networks under both Co-SF and Inter-SF Interference
authors: Gaoze Mu, Yanzhao Hou, Mingjie Chen, Yuanyu Hu, Yongan Zheng, Qimei Cui, Xiaofeng Tao
abstract: This paper presents an analytical framework for evaluating the coverage performance of the fluid antenna system (FAS)-enhanced LoRa wide-area networks (LoRaWANs). We investigate the effects of large-scale pathloss in LoRaWAN, small-scale fading characterized by FAS, and dense interference (i.e., packet collisions under the ALOHA protocol) arising from randomly deployed end devices (EDs). Both co-spreading factor (co-SF) interference (with the same SF) and inter-SF interference (with different SFs) are introduced into the network, and their differences in physical characteristics are also considered in the analysis. Additionally, simple yet accurate statistical approximations of the FAS channel envelope and power are derived using the extreme-value theorem. Based on the approximated channel expression, the theoretical coverage probability of the proposed FAS-enhanced LoRaWAN is derived. Numerical results validate our analytical approximations by exhibiting close agreement with the exact correlation model. Notably, it is revealed that a FAS with a normalized aperture of 1×1 can greatly enhance network performance, in terms of both ED numbers and coverage range.

title: OOBA: Object Offset Backdoor Attack for Remote Sensing Object Detection
authors: Jielun Huang, Shenglong Zhou, Qiaorui Che, Guoheng Huang, Xiaochen Yuan, Wenyun Li, Xuhang Chen, Guo Zhong, Bingo Wing-Kuen Ling, Chi-Man Pun
abstract: The growing adoption of consumer electronic Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) systems in outdoor military and civilian scenarios is inseparable from the rapid development of remote sensing object detection (RSOD) technologies. However, the security of RSOD has not yet received sufficient attention, particularly with respect to backdoor attacks. In such attacks, the backdoor model behaves normally on benign data but can be maliciously manipulated using pre-defined triggers, posing a serious threat to the reliable detection of UAV systems. Nevertheless, existing backdoor methods designed for natural images fail to adequately capture the characteristics of remote sensing imagery, and their single data modification mode limits their effectiveness on RSOD tasks. In this paper, we explore mislocalization attacks and propose the first RSOD-specific backdoor method named Object Offset Backdoor Attack (OOBA). The proposed OOBA embeds the backdoor through data poisoning and has two data modification modes: Position Offset and Class Modification. Specifically, Position Offset leverages redundant background content in remote sensing images by offsetting the bounding boxes to induce mislocalization by the detector. Meanwhile, this additional background information contained in the bounding boxes can assist the detector in fully embedding backdoors on tiny objects. Class Modification aims to induce misclassification by the detector, overcoming limitations of single data modification mode. Extensive experiments on the NWPU VHR-10 and DIOR datasets demonstrate the state-of-the-art attack performance of OOBA. The code is available at: https://github.com/WongJaylen/OOBA.

title: BiasField: Interactive Bias Probing of Machine Learning Datasets
authors: Zhen Li, Weikai Yang, Xinhuan Shu, Jiangning Zhu, Hui Zhang, Shixia Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Bias in machine learning datasets occurs when certain attributes are unfairly associated, e.g., serious males being mostly linked with law enforcement officers in job-related image datasets. Training models on biased datasets will degrade model performance and lead to fairness issues, particularly for underrepresented groups. Existing bias detection methods mainly focus on male explicit biases associated with predefined attributes (e.g., gender and ethnicity) while overlooking implicit biases associated with subtler, dataset-specific attributes (e.g., facial expressions and attire). To address this gap, we present BiasField, an interactive tool that offers a closed-loop workflow for detecting, analyzing, and mitigating bias. Central to BiasField is the adaptive detection of both explicit and implicit biases, a process facilitated by the automatic extraction of the dataset-specific attributes. It then employs a plant-growth metaphor to visualize these biases, enabling structured analysis to identify similar biases and track how they strengthen with additional attributes. Finally, confirmed biases are mitigated through targeted generative data augmentation. A user study, two case studies, and an expert study are conducted to demonstrate its capability to detect, analyze, and mitigate complex biases.

title: BrushEdit: All-In-One Image Inpainting and Editing_supp1-3695011.pdf
authors: Yaowei Li
abstract: Image editing has advanced significantly with the development of diffusion models using both inversion-based and instruction-based methods. However, current inversion-based approaches struggle with big modifications (e.g., adding or removing objects) due to the structured nature of inversion noise, which hinders substantial changes. Meanwhile, instruction-based methods often constrain users to black-box operations, limiting direct interaction for specifying editing regions and intensity. To address these limitations, we propose BrushEdit, a novel inpainting-based instruction-guided image editing paradigm, which leverages multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and image inpainting models to enable autonomous, user-friendly, and interactive free-form instruction editing. Specifically, we devise a system enabling free-form instruction editing by integrating MLLMs and a dual-branch image inpainting model in an agentcooperative framework to perform editing category classification, main object identification, mask acquisition, and editing area inpainting. Extensive experiments show that our framework effectively combines MLLMs and inpainting models, achieving superior performance across seven metrics including mask region preservation and editing effect coherence.

title: BEMF-Based PMSM Sensorless Control Using Finite Position Set-PLL Considering Speed Reversal and Computational Burden
authors: Shuo Chen, Xiaoyue Wang, Xiang Wu, Zhixun Ma
abstract: For back electromotive force-based permanent magnet synchronous motor sensorless drives, the conventional phase-locked loop (PLL) has the drawbacks of limited dynamic response, laborious parameter tuning, and inability to speed reversal. This article proposes a novel finite position set-PLL (FPS-PLL) scheme considering speed reversal and computational burden. First, the mechanisms and speed reversal ability of conventional PLL, FPS-PLL, and their variants are analyzed. Second, a novel equivalent position error is designed based on complex operation, enabling the derivation of a novel iteration formula with convergence across the entire computational domain. Moreover, a state machine of motor steering recognition is proposed to revise the Newton iteration formula, and the rotor speed is extracted by a linear tracking differentiator (LTD)-based speed observer without using the differentiator. Compared to reported FPS-PLLs, the proposed FPS-PLL has a lower computational burden and speed reversal ability. Eventually, the effectiveness of the proposed FPS-PLL scheme is verified by experiments.

title: DSA-NRP: No-Reflow Prediction from Angiographic Perfusion Dynamics in Stroke EVT
authors: Shreeram Athreya, Carlos Olivares, Ameera Ismail, Kambiz Nael, William Speier, Corey Arnold
abstract: Following successful large-vessel recanalization via endovascular thrombectomy (EVT) for acute ischemic stroke (AIS), some patients experience a complication known as no-reflow, defined by persistent microvascular hypoperfusion that undermines tissue recovery and worsens clinical outcomes. Although prompt identification is crucial, standard clinical practice relies on perfusion magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) within 24 hours post-procedure, delaying intervention. In this work, we introduce the first-ever machine learning (ML) framework to predict no-reflow immediately after EVT by leveraging previously unexplored intra-procedural digital subtraction angiography (DSA) sequences and clinical variables. Our retrospective analysis included AIS patients treated at UCLA Medical Center (2011–2024) who achieved favorable mTICI scores (2c or 3) and underwent pre- and post-procedure MRI. No-reflow was defined as a > 15% reduction in relative cerebral blood volume or flow within the infarct core compared to the contralateral hemisphere. From DSA sequences (anteroposterior and lateral views), we extracted statistical and temporal perfusion features from the target downstream territory to train ML classifiers for predicting no-reflow. Our preliminary results demonstrate that this novel method out-performed a clinical-features baseline (AUROC: 0.9330 vs. 0.7768 (p = 0.006)), suggesting that real-time DSA perfusion dynamics may encode clinically relevant information related to microvascular integrity. This approach establishes a preliminary foundation for immediate, accurate no-reflow prediction, enabling clinicians to proactively manage high-risk patients without reliance on delayed imaging, though it warrants validation in larger, independent cohorts.

title: Fluid Antennas: An Efficient Approach to Mitigating Packet Loss in Broadband Wireless Communication Systems
authors: Daniel Dinis, Luis Bernardo, Rui Dinis, Risto Wichman
abstract: In uplink broadband multiple access systems, packet collisions and limited channel diversity across retransmissions can severely degrade multiuser detection performance. We propose the use of fluid antenna system (FAS) at the mobile terminal (MT)s to introduce spatial diversity by switching antenna ports between retransmission slots. The resulting weakly correlated channel realizations improve the conditioning of the multiuser channel matrix, enhancing both diversity combining and packet separation at the base station (BS). Simulation results show that the proposed scheme approaches the performance of uncorrelated retransmissions and significantly outperforms the equal-channel-with-shifted packet (SP) techniques.

title: Cascaded Stimulated Raman Soliton Frequency-Shift Amplification Tunable Into the 1.7-μm Spectral Region Based on Erbium-Ytterbium Co-Doped Fiber and Phosphorus-Doped Fiber
authors: Jiaxuan Zhang, Minglie Hu
abstract: This paper reports a cascaded stimulated Raman soliton frequency-shift amplification (CSR-SFSA) scheme based on the combination of an erbium-ytterbium co-doped fiber (EYDF) and a phosphorus-doped fiber (PDF) for the generation of femtosecond soliton pulses in the 1.7-μm spectral region. The EYDF functions as a narrowband pre-amplifier, preserving spectral coherence while suppressing incoherent components and reducing the effective Raman threshold in the subsequent PDF stage. The cascaded configuration enables a reduced Raman fiber length, mitigates walk-off effects induced by group-velocity mismatch, and supports stable single-soliton operation. With appropriate pump power and optimized fiber parameters, Raman-shifted solitons with center-wavelength tunability from 1.6 to 1.7 μm are obtained, accompanied by a spectral tail extending toward 1.75 μm. The system delivers an average output power of up to 150 mW and a maximum pulse energy of 2.17 nJ. At a pump power of 0.8 W, long-term measurements over two hours show a wavelength drift below 0.12 nm and a root-mean-square (RMS) power fluctuation of approximately 0.27%. The radio-frequency spectrum exhibits a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of 37 dB, indicating stable and low-noise soliton operation. The demonstrated CSRSFSA scheme provides a compact and efficient approach for generating high-coherence ultrashort pulses in the 1.7-μm spectral region, which is of interest for applications in biomedical imaging, nonlinear spectroscopy, and related photonic systems.

title: Quantum-Driven State-Reduction for Reliable UAV Trajectory Optimization in Low-Altitude Networks
authors: Zeeshan Kaleem, Muhammad Afaq, Chau Yuen, Octavia A. Dobre, John M. Cioffi
abstract: This letter introduces a graph-condensed quantum-inspired placement (GC-QAP) framework for reliability-driven trajectory optimization in uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV)–assisted low-altitude wireless networks. The dense waypoint graph is condensed using probabilistic quantum-annealing to preserve interference-aware centroids while reducing the control state space and maintaining link-quality. The resulting problem is formulated as a priority-aware Markov decision process and solved using ε-greedy off-policy Q-learning, considering UAV kinematic and flight corridor constraints. Unlike complex continuous-action reinforcement learning approaches, GC-QAP achieves stable convergence and low outage with substantially and lower computational cost compared to baseline schemes.

title: Psych-Air: Predictive Smog Analytics for Psychotic Disorder Patients’ Risk Assessment in Smart Cities
authors: Sweta Dey, Abhinandan S. Prasad, Sudeepta Mishra, Dharavath Ramesh
abstract: The issue of smog in smart cities (SCs) poses serious health risks due to the rising concentrations of air pollutants (APs), including particulate matter, carbon, and sulfur. Their complex and dynamic behavior makes data-driven analysis challenging. Thus, accurate source identification and forecasting of those APs are vital for assessing psychotic disorder risks in hospitalized patients. To address this, a model named Psych-Air has been proposed. The proposed Psych-Air model employs federated learning (FL) with a customized multivariate bidirectional GRU (BGRU) architecture featuring cross-variable gated attention and parallel temporal encoders. Unlike conventional FL schemes, Psych-Air incorporates heterogeneity-aware and pollutant-sensitive federated optimization, enabling stable and communication-efficient learning across non-IID smart city environments. It also models pollutant and meteorological time series as an interdependent tensor stream, with dynamic gating and synchronized bidirectional states capturing pollutant-specific patterns. A shared fusion layer learns spatiotemporal signatures predictive of psychotic disorder onset while operating securely in a decentralized environment. Psych-Air effectively isolates latent triggers from noisy environmental data, achieving clinically relevant forecasting. FL-based BGRU identifies key pollutant sources, accounting for 46% of Air Quality Index impact in SCs. The model outperforms traditional ML, DL, and FL methods by 20%, 15%, and 10%, respectively, and supports the assessment of psychotic disorders through statistical analysis. Additionally, Psych-Air promotes sustainable, energy-efficient smart cities by reducing annual costs and CO emissions, proving adaptable across various urban settings.

title: Plasticity-Enhanced Multi-Agent Mixture of Experts for Dynamic Objective Adaptation in UAV-Assisted Emergency Communication Networks
authors: Wen Qiu, Zhiqiang He, Wei Zhao, Hiroshi Masui
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles serving as aerial base stations can rapidly restore connectivity after disasters, yet abrupt changes in user mobility and traffic demands shift the quality of service trade-offs and produce strong non-stationarity. Deep reinforcement learning policies suffer from plasticity loss under such shifts, as representation collapse and neuron dormancy impair adaptation. We propose plasticity-enhanced multi-agent mixture of experts (PE-MAMoE), a centralized training with decentralized execution framework built on multi-agent proximal policy optimization. PE-MAMoE equips each UAV with a sparsely gated mixture of experts actor whose router selects a single specialist per step. A non-parametric Phase Controller injects brief, expert-only stochastic perturbations after phase switches, resets the action log-standard-deviation, anneals entropy and learning rate, and schedules the router temperature, all to restore policy plasticity without destabilizing safe behaviors. We derive a dynamic regret bound showing the tracking error scales with both environment variation and cumulative noise energy. In a phase-driven simulator with mobile users and 3GPP-style channels, PE-MAMoE improves normalized interquartile mean return by 26.3% over the best baseline, increases served-user capacity by 12.8%, and reduces collisions by approximately 75%. Diagnostics confirm persistently higher expert feature rank and periodic dormant-neuron recovery at regime switches.

title: Dynamic Resource Scheduling for Time-triggered Flows With Deep Reinforcement Learning Algorithm in TSN-5G Network
authors: Xiaohuan Zhang, Yiqin Lu
abstract: Integrating Fifth Generation Communication Network (5G) with Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) is essential for deterministic Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). It is a challenge to provide ultra-reliable and deterministic services for time-triggered (TT) flow in the integrated TSN-5G network. In this paper, we propose PTFRS, a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)-based time–frequency resource scheduling approach that provides ultra-reliable and deterministic services for TT flow in 5G channel quality fluctuations. PTFRS applies a residual neural network (ResNet)-based convolutional neural network (CNN) (ResNet-based CNN) as a shared state encoder to extract high-dimensional features from the environment state. Then, based on these embeddings, the model jointly optimizes the allocation of sending time offsets, queue shifts, and resource blocks (RBs) using PPO. The auction expert algorithm serves as a guide in the PTFRS, helping the agent develop a more effective resource block allocation strategy for the UEs. The experimental results show that PTFRS reduces end-to-end delay by 26.16%, 14.17% over PPO-RB and DetTsch DRL algorithms and improves TT scheduling success by 20.3%, 53.4%, and 20.9% over PPO-Sym, PPO-RB, and DetTsch DRL algorithms, respectively. The PTFRS algorithm significantly reduces end-to-end delay and significantly enhances the scheduling success ratio and reliability performance. Our proposed PTFRS stabilizes and accelerates the model learning compared to the other existing deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithms. Consequently, the proposed PTFRS can provide ultra-reliable, low-latency, and deterministic services for TT flows in the integrated TSN-5G network.

title: Metal Integrated Suspended Line Technology: New Paradigm for Low-Loss RF and Millimeter-Wave Components
authors: Yu Zhan, Yi Wu, Kaixue Ma, Feng Feng, Wei Liu, Ruipeng Liu, Dongqi Gu
abstract: In modern RF and microwave systems, the electromagnetic environment is increasingly congested. Demand for massive data throughput and ultra-high-resolution sensing has driven systems to higher frequency bands. Beyond 30 GHz (subterahertz and above), dielectric loss becomes the primary bottleneck limiting system noise figure improvement and power efficiency. At these frequencies, traditional planar transmission lines, such as microstrip lines and substrate-integrated waveguides, degrade significantly due to increased dielectric loss and radiation leakage. Traditional metal waveguides offer excellent power-handling capability and near-zero dielectric loss. However, they are highly dependent on high-precision computer numerical control milling, resulting in bulky, heavy, high-cost structures that are incompatible with miniaturization of modern satellite payloads and mobile terminals.

title: Can LoRA teach? Cross A -matrix in Single Space for Low-Rank Knowledge Distillation
authors: Hyeon-ki Jo, Yuri Seo, Eui-Nam Huh
abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) reduces fine-tuning costs by freezing the pre-trained parameters and updating only lightweight low-rank parameters. However, because the LoRA still relies on frozen pre-trained parameters, additional Knowledge Distillation (KD) methods are required for deployment in hardware-constrained environments. Existing KD approaches primarily focus on transferring the output distribution or representation of the entire model. On the other hand, in LoRA-applied models, fine-tuned knowledge is embedded only in low-rank parameters. Consequently, applying traditional KD to LoRA is suboptimal because it unnecessarily processes the vast frozen parameters rather than focusing on the low-rank modules where the actual fine-tuned knowledge resides. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel distillation method, the Cross A-matrix in Single Space (CASS), which effectively transfers fine-tuned knowledge embedded in the LoRA adapter. Our method enables KD across different pre-trained weights between teacher and student models by sharing the teacher’s LoRA A-matrix rather than the prediction space. We evaluate CASS across multiple language models, including GPT-2, LLaMA-2, and Qwen1.5, demonstrating its generality and robustness. On GPT-2, CASS achieves an average 5.73% ROUGE-L improvement across various distance metrics. We further validate our method under the LoRA-only KD scenario, where access to teacher pre-trained weights is restricted. CASS achieves a 3.64% ROUGE-L gain using only the LoRA A-matrix, requiring only 0.0004% of the parameters compared to full-model transfer.

title: Understanding the Role of Future Visions as Infrastructure for Sustainable Living
authors: Simran Chopra, Adrian K. Clear, Rachel E. Clarke
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers

title: IRS Aided Non-linear Energy Harvesting with Harvester Sensitivity in CRN: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Vishwas Srivastava, Binod Prasad
abstract: Energy harvesting from radio frequency (RF) signals has emerged as a promising solution for power-constrained cognitive radio networks (PC-CRNs), where devices often operate without a permanent power source and have limited battery capacity. However, most existing studies rely on simplified energy harvesting models or ignore the sensitivity limitations of practical energy harvesting circuits, and thus fail to capture the combined impact of non-linear harvesting behavior and hardware sensitivity on spectrum sensing and energy harvesting performance. This study introduces the integration of an intelligent reflecting surface (IRS) with a PC-CRN to address these challenges. In addition, we employ a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approach to jointly optimize the IRS phase shifts and transmit power, where the learning objective is to maximize the received signal strength (mean reward) and minimize the interference through a penalty mechanism, thereby improving spectrum sensing performance and energy harvesting efficiency. To ensure practical relevance, we consider a realistic energy harvesting model that incorporates the non-linearity and sensitivity of RF harvesters. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of applying DRL to the IRS-assisted PC-CRN. The proposed DRL-based optimization solves the problem approximately 1.5× and 1.2× faster than existing DRL-based approaches, and about 6.6× faster than nature-inspired optimization methods, while achieving the same level of sensing accuracy. It is also observed that, after optimization, the number of required IRS elements is reduced by half to reach the harvester sensitivity threshold and activate the device.

title: Evaluating the Capability of InSAR-Derived Surface Deformation to Characterize the Spatio-temporal variability of Permafrost Active Layer Thickness in Svalbard
authors: Qianhui Liu, Yonghong Yi, Tian Chang, Arthur Endsley, John Kimball
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate assessment of active layer thickness (ALT) dynamics is essential for understanding permafrost evolution and its response to climate change. Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) has shown great potential in regional ALT mapping through detecting thaw-induced surface deformation, yet its performance under varying surface and climate conditions remains uncertain. In this study, we assessed the capability of InSAR-derived seasonal deformation to characterize the spatial and temporal variability of ALT in Svalbard based on simulations from an improved Stefan model. The model integrates the snow-insulation mechanism from the Kudryavtsev framework into the traditional Stefan formulation, enabling more realistic characterization of ALT variability in snow-dominated permafrost regions. Validation against in situ observations shows enhanced model performance, with an R of 0.9 and an RMSE of 0.19 m. Across Svalbard, the mean ALT is 1.17 ± 0.44 m, with an average increasing trend of 1.4 ± 0.9 cm/yr. The seasonal deformation in three subregions representing a diverse range of land cover types and surface conditions from 2016 to 2024 was derived using the Sentinel-1 C-band SAR data and the Small Baseline Subset method and evaluated against the simulated ALT. Larger deformation occurs in low-lying, moist, and vegetation-covered surfaces, where sensitivity to ALT variability is highest. By introducing an empirical edaphic factor representing vegetation, soil moisture and texture conditions, we show that the correspondence between deformation and ALT weakens under drier or sparsely vegetated terrains. Our results demonstrate that the integration of improved analytical ALT modeling and InSAR observations provides new opportunities for large-scale assessment of permafrost active layer changes in a rapidly warming Arctic.

title: Adaptive Inertia of DC-link Voltage Synchronization
authors: Ziqiang Wang, Yingping Cao, Hong Tan, Muhammad Muzammal Islam, Marco Liserre, Massimo La Scala, Chi Yung Chung
abstract: DC-link voltage synchronization (DCVS) can be the virtual synchronous generator (VSG) strategy for grid-forming converter, but its virtual inertia configuration problem remains unsolved. Therefore, this paper proposes a universal adaptive virtual inertia strategy for DCVS. The desired virtual inertia adaptively decreases with the rise of DC-link voltage deviation (frequency deviation), which is entirely opposite to that of the traditional VSG ignoring DC-link voltage dynamic. To configure the desired adaptive virtual inertia, the AC frequency of the interfaced inverter is correlated with the energy stored in DC-link capacitor by the linear proportional and power-law logic. It can effectively alleviate the trade-off problem between DC-link voltage fluctuation and power angle overshoot under large power variation for better dynamic performance. Moreover, it can improve the dynamic performance (less DC-link voltage fluctuation) not in the cost of the steady-state performance (low-frequency-resonance suppressing capability) by not increasing the loop gain around the equilibrium point. The real-time simulation based on NI-PXI and power hardware-in-the-loop (PHIL) experiment validate its effectiveness and advantages compared to traditional methodology.

title: SDMCC: Sample-wise Debiased Multilevel Contrastive Clustering for Single-cell Gene Expression Data
authors: Han Xiao, Dayu Hu, Fengyue Zhang, Shrooq Alsenan, Por Lip Yee, Jing Yang, Xiaoyu Cui
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Single-cell gene expression profiling has emerged as a powerful technology for dissecting complex tissues at unprecedented resolution. Accurate cell clustering is a fundamental computational prerequisite for cell type identification. In recent years, numerous single-cell contrastive clustering algorithms have been developed to more effectively identify individual cells and characterize cellular heterogeneity. Despite these advances, the presence of noise and data sparsity in single-cell datasets continues to impede algorithmic performance. Mainstream clustering methods face three principal limitations: (i) most clustering methods process data in mini-batches, and batch-related value shifts can destabilize feature extraction; (ii) existing contrastive learning strategies fail to achieve multilevel, coordinated information fusion, making them prone to collapsed solutions; and (iii) noise and sparsity can cause centroid shifts, yet existing methods lack effective mechanisms for dynamic centroid updating. To address these challenges, we propose sample-wise debiased multilevel contrastive clustering with shrinkage risk regularization (SDMCC). Instead of directly learning from the original sparse data, SDMCC applies a sample-wise correction module in conjunction with a batch-size adaptation module to mitigate distortion. Furthermore, we introduce a multilevel contrastive learning strategy that integrates instance-level and cluster-level information, enabling the capture of fine-grained cellular variations while maintaining coarse-grained semantic consistency across cell subpopulations. In addition, a shrinkage risk regularization term prevents convergence to trivial solutions. Extensive experiments conducted on multiple single-cell datasets demonstrate that SDMCC not only achieves superior clustering accuracy but also effectively uncovers biologically meaningful patterns in gene expression, thereby highlighting its potential for broader biomedical applications.

title: Deadbeat Direct Flux Control: Plug-and-Play Torque Controllers for Electrically Excited Synchronous Motors
authors: Sandro Rubino, Alessandro Ionta, Federica Graffeo, Radu Bojoi
abstract: Permanent magnet synchronous machines (PMSMs) have long been regarded as the benchmark technology for electric powertrains due to their superior torque density and efficiency. However, the growing demand to reduce reliance on rare-earth materials has drawn attention to electrically excited synchronous machines (EESMs) as a competitive alternative for traction applications. EESMs offer greater control flexibility, as their magnetic flux can be actively regulated and optimized via the rotor field current. However, limited control solutions are reported in the literature, primarily based on current vector controllers and only partially addressing optimal torque regulation. Furthermore, the cross-coupling introduced by the magnetic interaction between stator and rotor increases the complexity of the inner loops' design, whose performance is significantly affected by the magnetic saturation. As a consequence, EESM drives for traction applications often require the execution of demanding calibration and certification procedures. Therefore, this article proposes cutting-edge self-calibrated torque controllers for traction EESMs based on a deadbeat direct flux control (DB-DFC) approach. The proposed solution eliminates the need for any tuning efforts for the inner control loops while achieving high-performance of torque regulation and minimizing the overall copper losses, thus resulting in minimum-tuning torque controllers. The experimental validation is carried out on a 100 kW commercial EESM used in the Renault Zoe EV R135, confirming the effectiveness and the added value of the proposed torque controllers in traction applications.

title: A Low-Latency Three-Phase ROI Cropping Architecture for Real-Time Single-Object Localization on FPGA
authors: In A Gwon, Sang Yoon Park
abstract: For real-time recognition of single objects captured by a camera, numerous studies have explored Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)-based architectures for image cropping and resizing. However, most existing approaches mitigate the high variability of camera inputs by applying static Region of Interest (ROI) cropping or uniform resizing across all frames. Although these methods are fast and resource-efficient, their recognition accuracy varies significantly with the object’s size and position. This paper presents a three-phase cropping and resizing architecture optimized for single objects exhibiting distinct color contrast against a uniform background. The proposed design performs all cropping and resizing phases in parallel, thereby minimizing the latency of dynamic ROI generation and improving memory utilization efficiency. Implemented on a Xilinx Zybo Z7-20 FPGA integrated with an OV5640 camera core, the proposed system completes object localization, bounding-box generation, and user-defined image output within approximately 21.87 μs after image capture. Compared with existing FPGA-based camera recognition systems under the same hardware configuration, the proposed method exhibits enhanced autonomy in object position detection, achieves about 2.01× lower BRAM utilization, reduces latency by 746×, and consumes less overall power.

title: High-Frequency Current Transformers in Rotating Machines: A Critical Review
authors: Kaynan Maresch, Christoph Andres, Martin Fritsch, Martin Wolter, Ghendy Cardoso, Aécio L. Oliveira, Luiz F. Freitas-Gutierres, Maurício Sperandio
abstract: High-frequency current transformers (HFCTs) are widely used for online partial discharge (PD) monitoring in rotating machines due to their non-intrusive nature and high sensitivity to fast current transients. Unfortunately, the scientific literature often lacks detailed descriptions of the physical principles governing HFCT operation and the influence of sensor design parameters on PD detection are considered insufficiently. This paper presents a focused review of HFCT operating principles and their use in PD monitoring of rotating machines. The electromagnetic behavior of the sensor is revisited, highlighting the role of core geometry, material properties, air-gap configuration, and transfer impedance on the frequency response. In addition, the propagation of PD pulses along power cables is discussed to clarify the combined effects of signal attenuation and sensor bandwidth. The analysis contributes to a clearer understanding of HFCT behavior and supports more informed application of this technique in online PD diagnostics in rotating machines.

title: Normal-Variation-Aware Cross-Domain Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection via Multi-Prompt Learning with CLIP
authors: Masahiro Tsuchiya, Tsubasa Hirakawa, Takayoshi Yamashita, Hironobu Fujiyoshi
abstract: Cross-domain zero-shot anomaly detection, in which models are trained on a source domain and directly applied to unseen target domains without using any target-domain normal or abnormal samples, has gained increasing attention in image anomaly detection. Most existing zero-shot methods based on Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining estimate anomaly regions by comparing image features with predefined normal and abnormal textual prompts. Although effective in structured industrial inspection tasks, these approaches often degrade in medical imaging, where normal appearances exhibit large intra-class variability that is often difficult to represent by a single normal concept. To address this limitation, we propose a Normal-Variation-Aware cross-domain zero-shot anomaly detection framework that explicitly accounts for intra-normal variability. In addition to normal and abnormal prompts, the proposed method introduces layer-specific Normal-Variation prompts to represent ambiguous yet non-anomalous regions. By explicitly modeling normal, abnormal, and Normal-Variation states, the framework redistributes prompt probabilities more appropriately in the presence of natural normal variability, thereby reducing false positives and stabilizing anomaly decision boundaries in unseen domains. Experiments on eight medical imaging datasets demonstrate that the proposed method improves average pixel-level localization performance on datasets with pixel-level annotations, while also improving image-level detection on datasets with both normal and anomalous test samples.

title: Robust and Explainable Physics-Informed Transformer-Based Thermal Parameter Identification of Lithium-Ion Battery Systems
authors: Sung-Eun Lee, Junghyun Lee, Jiwon Yoon, Hyunjun An, Haeseok Suh, Youngwoo Kim, Joungho Kim
abstract: This article presents a robust, explainable physics-informed transformer (PI-Trans) for lithium-ion battery thermal parameter identification, addressing critical challenges posed by limited sensors and the inherent lack of explainability and robustness in conventional data-driven approaches. PI-Trans integrates a physics-constrained parameter identification framework with a transformer architecture, built upon a discretized lumped thermal model, to dynamically identify internal physical coefficients (α, β, γ, ε) governing irreversible heating, cooling, and entropic effects. Rather than directly predicting temperature, the proposed framework infers time-varying parameters and enforces thermodynamic consistency through a physics residual loss, enabling stable and physically consistent estimation under highly stochastic and previously unseen operating conditions. Experimental validation demonstrates a robust overall average root mean squared error (RMSE) of 0.56°C. Under the Pacific Northwest National Laboratory (PNNL) frequency regulation profile, the proposed model demonstrates a low average cell-wise RMSE of 0.276°C, outperforming conventional baselines such as extended Kalman filter (EKF) (0.624°C) and recursive least squares (RLS) (0.728°C). Beyond improving prediction accuracy, PI-Trans provides data-efficient learning from limited operational data and identifies physically meaningful parameters, supporting interpretable thermal diagnostics and early indication of hot-spot risk before clear surface temperature anomalies emerge. These capabilities support practical deployment in safety-critical battery management systems under dynamically varying thermal boundary conditions.

title: Superimposed Pilot-Based Adaptive Semantic Communications for Wireless Image Transmission
authors: Jian Zou, Jian Xiao, Wenwu Xie, Fanyang Meng, Renhai Feng, Liang Yang, Yongsheng Liang
abstract: The non-orthogonal superimposed pilot (NOSIP) scheme significantly improves the spectrum efficiency of semantic communication (SemCom) systems by effectively reducing pilot overhead. However, existing SemCom systems based on NOSIP still face challenges, including strong model-channel coupling and limited adaptability to heterogeneous channels. To address these issues, this paper proposes a flexible, channel-adaptive digital SemCom (D-SemCom) architecture based on the NOSIP scheme. Specifically, we design a lightweight semantic codec, termed ShiftViT, and a semantic receiver, termed ShiftRx, which employ time- and frequency-domain shift mechanisms to decouple pilot and data and suppress multi-user interference, thereby enabling image transmission under complex channel conditions. Furthermore, a lightweight channel adaptation algorithm based on first-order meta-learning is proposed to facilitate rapid adaptation and mitigate the strong coupling between semantic models and channel environments. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed D-SemCom system achieves approximately 25.14% and 1.16% goodput improvements over traditional receivers and existing state-of-the-art methods, respectively, while reducing the computational complexity in terms of FLOPs by approximately 40.37%. In addition, the proposed channel adaptation algorithm is shown to rapidly adapt to diverse channel scenarios within the D-SemCom system.

title: Verifiable Error Bounds for Physics-Informed Neural Network Solutions of Lyapunov and Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman Equations
authors: Jun Liu
abstract: Many core problems in nonlinear systems analysis and control can be recast as solving partial differential equations (PDEs) such as Lyapunov and Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman (HJB) equations. Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have emerged as a promising mesh-free approach for approximating their solutions, but in most existing works there is no rigorous guarantee that a small PDE residual implies a small solution error. This paper develops verifiable error bounds for approximate solutions of Lyapunov and HJB equations, with particular emphasis on PINN-based approximations. For both the Lyapunov and HJB PDEs, we show that a verifiable residual bound yields relative error bounds with respect to the true solutions as well as computable a posteriori estimates in terms of the approximate solutions. For the HJB equation, this also yields certified upper and lower bounds on the optimal value function on compact sublevel sets and quantifies the optimality gap of the induced feedback policy. We further show that one-sided residual bounds already imply that the approximation is a valid Lyapunov or control Lyapunov function. We illustrate the results with numerical examples.

title: Capacity Optimization of CSP Plants Considering Nonlinear Temperature-Efficiency Interdependence
authors: Yan Xu, Zhengfeng Shuai, Feng Tian, Yongnian Yang, Lin Shi, Hongshan Liu, Huan Ma, Qun Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Concentrating solar power (CSP) systems represent a cornerstone of dispatchable renewable energy; however, conventional capacity configuration methods often rely on nominal design points, overlooking the dynamic operational complexities and nonlinear interactions among subsystems. This misalignment frequently leads to sub-optimal capacity configuration and diminished operational efficiency. This research proposes a dual-layer iterative optimization framework that integrates multi-typical-day operational simulations with capacity configuration to bridge the gap between design and practical performance. By decoupling the global capacity configuration problem into an upper-layer capacity configuration optimization model and a lower-layer multi-condition simulation model, this research enables the synergetic global optimization of solar field, thermal energy storage, and power generation block parameters through dynamic feedback. Compared with a simplified external-characteristic model, the configuration obtained from the proposed framework increases the plant's lifecycle profit by approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5. 7 \%}$</tex>. Multi-typical-day simulations demonstrate that the optimized configuration maintains robust power generation and superior economic performance across diverse meteorological conditions. The sensitivity analysis further shows that, in contrast to the capacity of HTF pump for heat release, the solar field area and the capacity of dual tanks emerge as the primary economic trade-offs. The framework enhances design precision and operational adaptability, offering a scalable strategy to maximize lifecycle efficiency and profitability of CSP plants.

title: Research on Evaluation and Optimization of MarketBased Regulation for Charging Resource Clusters Based on Multi-Source Data Fusion
authors: Yan Han, Hui Zhou, Liyang Jiang, Zhi Zhu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the rapid growth of electric vehicle ownership, cluster-based operation of charging resources has become crucial for resolving supply-demand conflicts and enhancing system efficiency. This paper proposes a framework for evaluating and optimizing the effectiveness of market-based regulation of charging resources based on multi-source data fusion. By integrating charging facility operation data, power market transaction data, user behavior data, and environmental data, a dynamic evaluation model and optimization decision-making system are constructed. The study employs the Entropy WeightTOPSIS method to quantify regulatory effectiveness and utilizes deep reinforcement learning algorithms to achieve dynamic optimization of regulatory strategies. Empirical results demonstrate that the proposed method can increase charging resource utilization by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2. 7 \%}$</tex> and reduce user charging costs by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9. 3 \%}$</tex>, validating the effectiveness of multi-source data fusion in market-based regulation.

title: Multi-Scenario Optimization and Key Factor Assessment of Renewable Energy Utilization Under High Penetration Levels
authors: Jinfang Zhang, Fuqiang Zhang, Meng Wang, Mingxin Zhao, Yinan Hao, Xiong Wu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Under the background of large-scale renewable energy development and slowing load growth, power systems are gradually entering a load-saturation stage. The challenge of renewable energy accommodation is shifting from insufficient total capacity to spatiotemporal mismatch and limited flexibility. To address this issue, this study focuses on typical accommodation paradigms, including generation-grid-load-storage integration and integrated multi-energy renewable bases with bundled transmission. A unified mathematical optimization model is constructed to analyze the operational characteristics, accommodation capability, and evolution of key constraints under high, medium, and low renewable penetration levels. From the perspectives of resource endowment, system flexibility, transmission capacity, load characteristics, and generation mix, the study identifies the key factors affecting renewable energy utilization. Based on scenario data and optimization results for the milestone year 2035, the impacts of these factors on renewable utilization under load-saturation conditions are quantitatively evaluated, and their relative importance is ranked accordingly.

title: Sensorless Control of SPMSM with Unknown Flux Linkage Based on Switching from Standard-Type to Nonlinear-Type Flux Observer
authors: Zheng Li, Yutao Huang, Qiang Sun, Huakang Xia
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: For sensorless control of surface permanent magnet synchronous motor (SPMSM), the nonlinear flux observer offers advantages including implementation simplicity, robustness, and satisfactory control performance. However, its operation relies on prior knowledge of the motor flux linkage, which is not always provided by manufacturers. This limitation requires experimental measurement of the flux linkage before the nonlinear flux observer can be applied. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel observer-switching strategy that enables the direct implementation of the nonlinear flux observer without requiring a priori flux information. Specifically, the system initially operates using a standard flux observer while simultaneously measuring the motor's flux linkage. Once sufficient flux data are acquired, the system seamlessly switches to the nonlinear flux observer based on the measured flux information. Experimental results demonstrate that the flux linkage is identified with an error range of -2.17 % to 7.67 %, and the speed fluctuation during the transient period of observer switching is limited to within 6.25 %, which verifies the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed strategy.

title: An Advanced NanocompositeInsulation Framework for High-Voltage Power Equipment
authors: Xiaonan Cheng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the growing demand for large-capacity, highreliability high-voltage power equipment, conventional insulating materials such as polyimide (PI) are increasingly challenged to meet the combined requirements of dielectric strength and thermal management. This paper presents a high-temperature dielectric energy storage material based on polyimide doped with alumina <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$</tex>. Through a multi-scale design strategy that optimizes filler dispersion and interfacial characteristics, the resulting composite exhibits significantly enhanced dielectric and thermal properties. Experimental results show that the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} / \text{PI}$</tex> composite achieves a breakdown strength of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$682 \text{MV} / \mathrm{m}$</tex>, representing a substantial improvement over pure polyimide. In addition, the thermal conductivity reaches approximately 0.7 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{W} /(\mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{K})$</tex>, while the dielectric loss remains below 0.013. These improvements provide important technical support for enhancing the safety and reliability of next-generation high-voltage power equipment.

title: The Effects of Relaxing Price Limits on Spot Electricity Markets Under High Renewable Penetration
authors: Nan Jiang, Zeyuan Liu, Zhu Li, Xian Zhang, Ziming Ma, Yuanji Cai, Hongye Guo
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In the context of high renewable energy (RE) penetration, existing narrow price-limit mechanisms often fail to accurately reflect the scarcity value of electricity. This paper proposes a power spot market clearing model that balances the revenues of multiple market participants, including coal-fired units, RE entities, and energy storage systems (ESS). First, the methodology integrates RE Green Certificate (GC) revenue and traditional capacity compensation to construct a comprehensive bidding model covering energy, certificates, and capacity remuneration. Second, an innovative approach-characterized by ‘constraining the average grid-wide price while relaxing spot price limits’-is introduced. By utilizing the average electricity price as a benchmark constraint, the study quantitatively analyzes the incentive effects of price-limit relaxation on participant bidding behavior. Simulation results, based on the IEEE 30-bus system and empirical data from a Chinese province, demonstrate that relaxing price limits effectively expands peak-valley spreads and releases critical scarcity signals. Furthermore, the negative price mechanism incentivizes coal-fired units and ESS to proactively participate in system regulation, enhancing the RE utilization rate by 5.6%. Finally, the relaxation of price limits facilitates a transition from pure energy value to flexible regulation value, driving a cost-transfer ratio of 1.32% and significantly optimizing resource allocation within the modern power system.

title: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
authors: N/A

title: Retention Characteristics of the Synaptic Pass-Transistor Dependent on the Position of the Load Resistor for the Long-Term Plasticity_supp1-3694348.pdf
authors: Sungsik Lee
abstract: The synaptic pass-transistor (SPT), which integrates a synaptic thin-film transistor (Syn-TFT) with a load resistor (R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</sub>), enables low-power neuromorphic operation in the subthreshold region by suppressing transient burst currents. However, the role of R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</sub> placement in governing retention characteristics under subthreshold operation has remained unclear. Here, we quantitatively investigate this effect by comparing a conventional SPT and an inverted SPT (i-SPT) that share an identical Hf-ZnO channel and Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>/Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> gate-insulator stack but differ in R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</sub> placement. For the facilitation-programmed state, relocating R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</sub> yields a substantial retention enhancement, with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\tau _{\text {eff}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> reaching <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.36\times 10^{{5}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> s, which is <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4.6\times 10^{{3}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> times longer than that of the conventional SPT, accompanied by a twofold reduction in programming speed. Stretched-exponential-function fitting and inverse-Laplace-transform (ILT) analysis further reveal that the dominant trap activation energy deepens from 0.984 eV in the SPT to 1.269 eV in the i-SPT. These results provide a device- and circuit-level physical explanation of the speed-retention trade-off in subthreshold SPTs and establish R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</sub> placement as a practical design parameter for selecting either fast, volatile learning or stable, long-term memory in energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware.

title: A Spiking Few-Shot Learning Framework for Drone RF Signal Recognition
authors: Weibin Lu, Shilian Zheng, Jiakai Liang, Chao Wang, Mayue Wang, Keqiang Yue, Wenjun Li
abstract: Radio-frequency (RF)-based drone recognition plays an increasingly important role in security surveillance and airspace management with the rapid development of drone communications and intelligent sensing technologies. However, most existing deep learning methods rely on large annotated datasets and energy-intensive artificial neural networks (ANN), which limits their applicability in sample-scarce and resource-constrained scenarios. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a spiking few-shot learning framework for RF-based drone identification that combines the data-efficient learning capability of Few-Shot Learning (FSL) with the inherent energy efficiency of Spiking Neural Networks (SNN). The proposed framework employs a pretrained SNN backbone and a Feature-Integrated Multi-Gated Attention (FIMA) module, where a multi-gated mechanism enables adaptive feature weighting and multi-head attention models cross-sample relationships, followed by task-adaptive fine-tuning to optimize FIMA and prototype representations for improved discriminative capability in few-shot tasks. Experiments conducted on the DroneRFb-Spectra dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves accuracies of 99.27%, 98.47%, and 97.28% on the 3-way 1-shot, 5-way 1-shot, and 8-way 1-shot tasks, respectively, outperforming recent methods by approximately 2–5%. Meanwhile, the SNN-based feature extractor significantly reduces inference energy consumption to 41.9% of that of ANN-based counterparts, while also improving accuracy by 1–3%. Ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of the multi-gated multi-head attention mechanism and the fine-tuning stage, contributing approximately 1–4% and 4–9% performance improvements, respectively.

title: Simplifying Robotic Ultrasound Calibration Via Conic Sections Geometry_supp1-3695989.mp4
authors: Zheng Li
abstract: Robotic ultrasound (US) systems represent an emerging frontier in medical imaging. A fundamental component of these systems is the rigid body transformation between the robot flange and the attached US probe, which enables mapping of visual data from image space to the robot's reference frame. Traditionally, calibrating this transformation has been a tedious process, complicated by equipment demands and operational constraints arising from the probe's narrow field of view. This work presents a novel calibration strategy based on conic sections geometry, which offers several key simplifications over existing approaches: 1). It requires no external equipment beyond a single cone phantom; 2). It operates with a small input size and imposes no strict alignment or motion constraints on the US scan plane during calibration; and 3). It employs a straightforward pattern analysis pipeline to process images acquired from phantom scans. Experimental validation results show that the proposed method achieves accuracy comparable to existing state-of-the-art approaches while delivering superior precision, thereby demonstrating enhanced calibration reproducibility enabled by its streamlined workflow. These advantages make this method particularly suitable for application in clinical scenarios that require frequent and efficient calibration.

title: Multiple Fault Calculation for Distribution Networks with Inverter-Based Distributed Generator Based on Fault Component Network
authors: Yongchen Wang, Yuan Li, Fang Peng, Linli Zhang, Wei Cong, Min Huang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: When simultaneous multiple-point faults occur, the coupling of sequence components between different fault locations poses incorrect operation risks to traditional protection based on single-point fault theory. Existing methods for multiple-fault analysis typically fail to account for transition resistance and diverse fault types. To address these limitations, this paper proposes an equivalent modeling and calculation method for complex faults in distribution networks using fault component network theory. Based on the superposition principle, the faulted network is decoupled into a steady-state network and a fault component network. Integrating the controlled-source model of the inverter, the boundary conditions and analytical expressions of the superimposed sources are derived. Specifically, considering the open-circuit equivalent characteristic of the inverter in the negative-sequence network under asymmetrical faults, the fault electrical quantities are solved by formulating loop current equations within the positive and negative sequence superimposed topologies. Simulation tests conducted on a 10kV active distribution network model in PSCAD/EMTDC demonstrate that the magnitude and phase angle errors for fault voltages and currents under two-point short-circuit conditions remain within 5% and 7°, validating the high accuracy and reliability of the proposed method.

title: Watermarking for Model Ownership Verification:Invisible at Deployment, Activated by Updates
authors: Hewang Nie, Jue Xiao, Zhi Lu, Renfei Shen, Chunqiang Yu, Zhenjun Tang
publish: ACM Transactions on Privacy and Security
