title: Research on dynamic load and trajectory detection method of maize threshing based on the bionic electronic maize ear
authors: Caiyuan Zhang, Chenlong Fan, Tao Cui, Ying Liu, Mengmeng Qiao, Yongkun Qiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Real-time inversion of motion and force parameters during maize threshing is central to deciphering the mechanism of low-damage threshing. In this study, an instrumented bionic electronic maize was integrated with ultra-wideband (UWB), inertial measurement unit (IMU), and flexible thin-film pressure sensors. It was developed to enable real-time detection of impact forces, rubbing pressures, and motion trajectories of maize ears during threshing, and to reveal threshing damage mechanisms. The error calibration methods for IMU and UWB were developed, and UWB/IMU coupled positioning algorithms (KF, EKF, UKF filtering algorithms) were employed to improve positioning accuracy in complex environments. The performance of the detection system was verified through the bench test. Results showed that the average impact force detection error was 0.92 N with an accuracy of 96.94%, and the average rubbing pressure detection error was 0.48 N with an accuracy of 97.96%. In the positioning test, the UKF algorithm demonstrated optimal performance, followed by the EKF and KF algorithms. In the bench test, the electronic maize ear accurately measured impact force and positioning trajectories, validating its effectiveness in the digital reconstruction of the simulated threshing process. The relevant research provides a theoretical basis for achieving low damage harvest of maize kernels.

title: CoGFormer: A Context-Gated Spatial Transformer With Temporal-Spatial Gated Graph Convolution for Traffic Flow Forecasting
authors: Jianhang Liu, Tonghao Xing, Haibo Wu, Shanchen Pang, Shibao Li, Yunhao Bu
abstract: Traffic flow forecasting is a critical component of intelligent transportation systems, yet its reliability still faces challenges due to the inherent complex spatio-temporal dependencies of traffic data. Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) have been widely applied in traffic flow prediction because they effectively model spatial structures based on road networks. However, GCN-based models often struggle to capture the dynamic and global dependencies within traffic networks. In contrast, Transformer-based models are good at capturing global relationships. Yet, most of these methods treat all attention heads equally, making them fail to adaptively adjust the output weights of different attention heads based on real-time traffic conditions. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a hybrid architecture, CoGFormer, which combines a Context-Gated Spatial Transformer with a Temporal-Spatial Gated Graph Convolution Network (TSGGCN) through residual connections. The TSGGCN module captures fine-grained local spatio-temporal patterns, while the Spatial Transformer models long-range, global spatial dependencies among all traffic nodes. Our paper introduces a lightweight and efficient Attention Pooling Gate (APG) mechanism within the multi-head self-attention architecture of Transformers. This mechanism generates a global context vector via learnable query probes, dynamically recalibrating the output contribution of each attention head based on this vector. It enables the model to adaptively match the most relevant spatial patterns according to real-time traffic conditions, thereby enhancing prediction accuracy. Comprehensive experiments conducted on four publicly released real-world highway datasets demonstrate that CoGFormer achieves highly competitive performance levels, confirming that CoGFormer provides a robust and effective solution for highway traffic flow forecasting.

title: A High-Band Phase Synchronization Approach for Distributed UAV SAR
authors: Junjie Yan, Linghao Li, Pucheng Li, Zegang Ding, Yibo Gao, Yibo Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: To achieve multistatic phase synchronization of distributed unmanned aerial vehicle synthetic aperture radar (UAV SAR), the two-way direct wave links are established between every two radar units and the time-varying phase fluctuations in bistatic echoes can be eliminated utilizing the traditional two-way phase synchronization method. However, the vibration environment of the UAV platform causes the degradation of the frequency source stability. Especially at high carrier frequencies, the phase fluctuations doubling to RF terminal break the phase unwrapping condition, and the time-varying π-ambiguity is introduced in phase synchronization error estimation value which causes the SAR image defocused. In addition, to satisfy the general baseline design requirements, there may be direct wave link obstruction between stations, resulting in interruption of direct wave reception and subsequent failure of phase synchronization. Even if the direct wave link remains unobstructed, potential multipath effects can degrade synchronization accuracy. To address the above issues, a reliable distributed UAV SAR high-band phase synchronization approach is proposed, in which a coarse-to-fine two-stage phase synchronization is performed based on two-way direct waves and echoes, respectively. In the coarse synchronization stage, the time-varying π-ambiguity after unwrapping is effectively suppressed using two-way multiplication and local unwrapping techniques, and full-link phase synchronization is achieved utilizing the phase triangle under conditions of partially obstructed direct-wave links. In the fine synchronization stage, the two-way echo of the calibration target is utilized instead of the full scene echo to avoid multipath effects in the direct-wave link, thereby achieving higher phase synchronization accuracy. This approach has been applied in the high-band distributed UAV SAR system, and the real measured data shows the effectiveness of the approach.

title: Modeling of multi-service queueing systems with traffic overflow
authors: Mariusz Głąbowski, Sławomir Hanczewski, Damian Kmiecik, Maciej Stasiak, Joanna Weissenberg
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: This article proposes an analytical model of a multi-service hierarchical system with multi-service overflow traffic. To model the primary and secondary resources of this system, the state-dependent queue service discipline was used. In order to model the secondary resources with the dedicated queue for overflow traffic, the Hayward’s approach was generalized and applied. To evaluate its accuracy, the results of analytical modeling were compared with the data obtained during the simulation experiments carried out in the study. Both the data presented in the article and the results obtained by the present authors in numerous comparative studies clearly indicate that the proposed model makes it possible to evaluate the values of the blocking probability with the accuracy that provides its reliable practical application at the stage of network dimensioning. The overflow mechanism has particular significance in networks with limited resources, such as mobile networks.

title: Ultra-compact Ultra-low-loss 1 × 2 Power Splitter for Broadband SiN-LNOI Photonics via DBS Inverse Design
authors: Dingqi Ma, Lin Cheng, Pengfei Cao, Peng Gong
abstract: We propose and numerically demonstrate an ultra compact, polarization-insensitive 1 × 2 power splitter on a heterogeneous silicon nitride-lithium niobate-on-insulator (SiN LNOI) platform. Taking advantage of the excellent etchability and CMOS compatibility of the SiN layer, the device effectively alleviates the fabrication challenges inherent in conventional thin film lithium niobate devices. Optimized using the direct binary search (DBS) inverse-design algorithm, the splitter occupies an ultra-compact footprint of only 2.6×2.6 μm2. Simulation results show that the device achieves a state-of-the-art average insertion loss of 0.09dB and a power imbalance below 0.015dB over an ultra-broad 200nm bandwidth (1500–1700nm) for both TE and TM polarizations. The calculated polarization-dependent loss (PDL) remains around 0.1dB across the entire operating band. Moreover, the design strictly adheres to a minimum feature size of 130nm, ensuring high robustness for large-scale, high-density photonic integration.

title: Photonics Breakthroughs 2025: Parallel Photonic Machine Learning With a Nonlinear Amplifying Loop Mirror
authors: L. Di Lauro, A. Aadhi, I. Alamgir, B. Fischer, P. Dmitriev, C. Mazoukh, N. Perron, E. A. Viktorov, A. V. Kovalev, A. Eshaghi, S. Vakili, M. Chemnitz, P. Roztocki, B. E. Little, S. T. Chu, D. J. Moss, R. Morandotti
abstract: Neuromorphic photonics has emerged as a promising route for brain-inspired information processing, with optical neural networks offering a path beyond key bandwidth and energy bottlenecks of conventional electronic hardware. Within this landscape, photonic reservoir computing has attracted growing interest because it reduces training complexity while retaining the rich dynamics needed for machine-learning tasks. Recent progress has expanded the field through improved integration, better control of memory and nonlinearity, and new strategies for parallel information processing. Yet delay-based single-node reservoirs still face major limitations, since virtual nodes are generated sequentially and throughput remains tied to cavity latency. Here, we present and discuss our recent work on a tunable neuromorphic photonic reservoir based on a nonlinear amplifying loop mirror and place it within the broader context of recent advances in photonic reservoir computing. The device combines controllable nonlinearity and fading memory with concurrent wavelength processing and dense temporal encoding, allowing multiple data streams to be processed within the same physical cavity. We also review recent developments in the field and discuss current challenges, emerging opportunities, and future directions toward scalable, low-latency, and energy-efficient photonic machine-learning hardware.

title: Beyond Sequential Scanning: A Synergistic Mamba Paradigm with Adaptive Receptive Recalibration for Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Cheng Chen, Tao Wang, Jiping Cao, Qiying Wang, Yanzhao Su, Yao Ding
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: State Space Models (SSMs) have emerged as a compelling paradigm for long-range dependency modeling with linear computational complexity. However, their intrinsic 1D scanning process inevitably disrupts the local topological continuity of high-dimensional image structures. This limitation is particularly pronounced in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification, where existing Mamba-based methods frequently struggle with intricate spatial-spectral coupling and the deficiency of local inductive bias, resulting in suboptimal feature representation. To surmount these bottlenecks, we propose SynerMamba, a novel synergistic Mamba-based framework designed to reconstruct local structural integrity within the sequential modeling paradigm. Specifically, a Spatial-Spectral Disentangler (SSD) is first introduced to explicitly decouple highly correlated spatial-spectral features via local dynamic graphs, establishing a structurally conscious manifold prior to SSM processing. To rectify the perception bias in sequential scanning, an Adaptive Receptive Mamba (ARM) is developed to inject multi-scale spatial priors into the selective scanning process, effectively capturing fine-grained textures. Furthermore, a Mamba Synergizer (MS) is engineered to orchestrate the sophisticated nonlinear interplay between spatial contexts and spectral signatures, transcending the representation constraints of conventional linear aggregation strategies. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that SynerMamba achieves state-of-the-art classification performance while maintaining high computational efficiency.

title: Model-Data Co-Driven Detection of False Data Injection Attacks for Two-Stage Volt/Var Control in Distribution Networks
authors: Zhigang Ren, Xianzhuo Sun, Lei Ding, Jiaqi Ruan, Yuechuan Tao, Jing Qiu
abstract: False data injection attacks (FDIAs) present hurdles to the efficiency of communication-dependent Volt/Var control (VVC) in distribution networks. This paper proposes a novel FDIA targeting the two-stage combined central and local VVC scheme. Two distinct attack scenarios, namely economic-driven attacks and voltage security-driven attacks, are designed and their adverse impacts on VVC are studied. The attacks are launched by manipulating the transmitted data from a central controller (CC) to local controllers (LC) and do not necessitate knowledge of system parameters. A model-data co-driven detection method is then developed to locate and distinguish FDIAs based on the detection function. This function is obtained through model-based analytical derivation of power flows and data-driven linear least squares (LS) regression, with the procedure only demanding limited online measurements and being straightforward to implement in practice. As for countermeasures after the detection of FDIAs, we also design adjusted Q(V) curves with specific rules to mitigate local voltage violations until cyber-attack concerns are fully addressed. Numerical simulations are conducted on the 33-bus distribution system to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in detecting FDIAs, mitigating voltage violations and reducing economic losses.

title: An Hybrid Photovoltaic Power Prediction Model Based on VMD-ICPO-LSTM
authors: Shenxiang Wang, Zhenggang Han
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: A hybrid PV power prediction model combining K-means clustering, VMD, improved CPO algorithm, and LSTM networks is proposed in this study. Historical data are preprocessed by outlier removal and mean imputation, then clustered into three weather types using K-means. PV power series are decomposed into 5 components via VMD, with LSTM hyperparameters optimized by improved CPO. Experimental results show that the VMD-ICPO-LSTM model achieves higher prediction accuracy and better generalization ability than baseline LSTM and VMD-LSTM models Experimental results based on real operational data from a PV plant in China show that the proposed VMD-ICPO-LSTM model outperforms conventional LSTM and VMD-LSTM models under all weather conditions. For example, on sunny days, the proposed model reduces RMSE to 0.73733 and MAPE to 1.9897%, demonstrating superior prediction accuracy and generalization capability.

title: Fuzzy Event-Driven Optimal Quantized Control for USVs under Thruster and Positioning Constraints: An Adaptive Dynamic Programming Method
authors: Jun Ning, Xiangjie Cui, Xiaoyang Gao, Tieshan Li, Shaocheng Tong, C. L. Philip Chen
abstract: This paper presents a fuzzy event-driven optimal quantized feedback control strategy for unmanned surface vehicle (USV) trajectory tracking under multiple practical constraints, including limited maritime communication resources, model uncertainties, thruster saturation, and positioning constraints. To address the communication limitation, a signal quantization and event-driven mechanism is introduced, where the thruster quantization is characterized by a linear analytical model. A fuzzy adaptive observer (FAO) is used to estimate the quantized feedback signal, while a finite-time disturbance observer (FDO) estimates environmental disturbances. Additionally, a dynamic auxiliary system compensates for thruster saturation effects. A logarithmic barrier function-based constraint mechanism ensures positioning variables remain within predefined safety bounds. The adaptive quantized feedback controller is designed using the backstepping framework and dynamic surface control techniques, with an optimal compensation term based on adaptive dynamic programming (ADP) to minimize the cost function of the tracking error system. The proposed control strategy ensures that all closed-loop signals remain within specified limits. Simulation results confirm the effectiveness and advantages of this approach.

title: Modality-Guided Feature Alignment and Complementary Enhancement for Infrared-Visible Object Detection
authors: Hao Zhu, Yaming Chen, Xiao Pan, Yi He, Jinjian Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Infrared-Visible object detection shows excellent potential in all-weather scenarios. The key challenge lies in effectively extracting and fusing cross-modal complementary information while bridging the modal gap. To address this challenge, we propose a cross-modal fusion detection framework based on feature alignment learning and enhancement. A Cross-modal Feature Alignment Representation Learning (CFAR) method is designed to learn the representation of specific and interactive features according to the designed disentangled feature alignment function. It achieves the goal of bridging the modal gap while fully characterizing the cross-modal complementary information. In addition, to enhance the fusion representation, a Cross-modal Complementary Enhancement Fusion Module (CCEF) is proposed. On the one hand, it integrates gradient and local contrast priors to enhance specific feature representation. On the other hand, it jointly explores local and global information and gradually removes redundant information from interactive features. The discriminability of fused features is improved by enhancing the representation of specific features and removing the redundancy from interactive features. Our experiments on the M3FD, LLVIP, and DroneVehicle datasets demonstrate the superiority of our proposed method and improve the detection accuracy under different conditions. Our code is available at https://github.com/w-o-w-1/AlCE-FusionNet.

title: Cross-Domain Radar Gesture Generation via Diffusion Models with Transfer Guidance and Physical Priors
authors: Fubiao Yang, Yuanyuan Wang, Yanhua Yin, Xiaoxu Han, Yi Zhang
abstract: Radar-based gesture recognition has shown great potential in human-computer interaction due to its strong privacy-preserving capabilities. However, practical deployment is severely constrained by factors such as device installation limitations, environmental variability, and high data acquisition costs. These challenges lead to sample scarcity and cross-domain distribution shifts, which significantly degrade both generation and recognition performance. Existing generative methods suffer from mode collapse under extreme data scarcity and produce samples lacking kinematic physical constraints, compromising structural plausibility. To address these challenges, this paper proposes Transfer-Guided Diffusion Process with Physical Prior (TGDP-P), a diffusion-based radar gesture generation method that synergistically integrates transfer guidance with physical priors. During the reverse denoising process of the diffusion model, TGDP-P incorporates a transfer-guidance mechanism and physical prior constraints. Specifically, a density-ratio correction term is introduced to efficiently bridge the distribution gap between source and target domains, while time-frequency ridge regularization enforces kinematic consistency in the generated samples. Ablation studies confirm that the physical prior is critical to generation quality, and the synergy between distribution guidance and physical constraints enables TGDP-P to generate samples with both high fidelity and strong physical plausibility. Experiments on a simulated dataset and the public UWB-Gestures benchmark demonstrate that the proposed method maintains superior performance under extreme data scarcity. With 100 samples from the target domain, TGDP-P achieves an F1-score of 0.7970 and improves classification accuracy by 2.60 percentage points compared to the baseline. Furthermore, quantitative evaluation across four representative cross-domain scenarios validates the strong generalization capability of TGDP-P, offering a high-fidelity and robust data generation solution for radar-based gesture recognition under resource-constrained conditions.

title: Difference-Based Recovery for Modulo Sampling: Tightened Bounds and Robustness Guarantees
authors: Wenyi Yan, Zeyuan Li, Lu Gan, Hongqing Liu, Guoquan Li
abstract: Conventional analogue-to-digital converters (ADCs) suffer from clipping when inputs exceed their dynamic range. Modulo (unlimited) sampling mitigates this by folding the signal prior to digitisation. However, existing recovery methods are either computationally intensive or require high sampling rates due to loose oversampling factors. Moreover, prior analyses do not account for sampling jitter, which is unavoidable in practice. This paper revisits the difference-based recovery and establishes refined theoretical and practical guarantees. In particular, we first establish an improved Bernstein-type inequality for N-th order divided differences of bandlimited signals. We then revise the baseline difference-based recovery algorithm through an N-dependent window length. Based on this refinement, we derive recovery conditions for both noiseless and noisy settings. In the noiseless case, the sufficient oversampling factor is reduced from 2πe to 3, while in the noisy case, explicit robustness guarantees are obtained. We further extend the analysis to non-uniform sampling and establish recovery conditions under bounded sampling jitter. Finally, we identify the second-order setting (N = 2) as a practically favourable design choice, offering an effective trade-off between sampling rate and robustness. An FPGA-based prototype demonstrates reliable reconstruction with amplitude expansion up to ρ = 108, validating the framework and highlighting the potential of low-complexity, high-performance unlimited sensing.

title: Constellation Selection and Power Control for OFDM-based ISAC: From Theory to Prototype
authors: Kaitao Meng, Kawon Han, Christos Masouros, Fan Liu
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) techniques can leverage existing, wide-coverage communication networks to perform sensing tasks, enabling large-scale and low-cost target sensing. However, the inherent randomness of communication data payloads introduces undesired sidelobes in the ambiguity function that may degrade target detection and parameter estimation performance. This paper develops a communication-centric ISAC framework that is standards-compliant and compatible with existing devices. Specifically, we propose a low-complexity constellation selection scheme over a finite, off-the-shelf alphabet, achieving an efficient sensing-communication trade-off without custom waveforms or frame-structure changes. To this end, we analyze two classical sensing receivers including matched filtering (MF) and reciprocal filtering (RF) for ranging measurements, and derive closed-form sensing laws that link constellation statistics to sensing performance. Under any finite-alphabet constellation combination, MF sidelobes depend on the weighted sum of the kurtosis values of the per-subcarrier constellations, while RF noise enhancement depends on the inverse second moment of the transmit symbol, providing a tractable expression for tuning the sensing-communication trade-off. Guided by these insights, we propose a low-complexity design that retains a finite constellation set and shapes power under communication quality constraints. We prove that in flat-fading channels, there exists a Paretooptimal solution that activates no more than three constellations. For frequency-selective channels, a bilevel algorithm with closed-form inner updates attains near-optimal performance while sharply reducing computational complexity. We validate the entire theoretical pipeline with numerical simulations as well as experimental results. The results corroborate the theory, constellation mixing and coherent multi-symbol processing reliably reveal markedly weaker targets at practical signal-to-noise ratios.

title: Reentrancy Vulnerability Detection in Smart Contracts Based on Adversarial Examples
authors: Fei Tong, Haoxuan Zang, Fang Jiang, Guodong Li, Hongyi Qiu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Reentrancy Vulnerabilities (RVs) in smart contracts pose severe financial risks to blockchain systems. While deep learning-based detection methods have shown promise, they suffer from critical limitations: (1) vulnerability to adversarial examples, (2) inadequate handling of Cross-Function RVs (CFRVs), and (3) reliance on limited training data. To address these challenges, we present SCAEG, the first adversarial example generation framework specifically designed for RV detection. SCAEG introduces five novel perturbation actions that preserve code functionality while exposing model vulnerabilities, including targeted modifications for CFRV scenarios. Our comprehensive evaluation demonstrates that SCAEG-generated examples sig nificantly degrade the performance of state-of-the-art detectors (e.g., reducing AWDNN's accuracy by 27.51 percentage points), exposing their fragility to adversarial attacks. Building on these insights, we propose VoteRD, an ensemble detection model that combines semantic, rule-based, and positional features through a multi-model voting mechanism. Experimental results show VoteRD achieves superior robustness, outperforming existing approaches by 3.7% in recall and 7.4% in F1-score (compare with AWDNN), while effectively detecting both traditional RVs and CFRVs. This work not only advances RV detection capabilities but also establishes a new benchmark for evaluating model robustness in smart contract security analysis. SCAEG and VoteRD form a synergistic framework: SCAEG systematically evaluates model robustness, while VoteRD leverages adversarial insights to enhance detection accuracy. Our datasets and tools are publicly released to support future research in adversarial testing and vulnerability detection.

title: Trans-MAML: Meta-Learning Enhanced Transformers for Human Activity Recognition in WiFi CSI Sensing
authors: Khairunnisa Rifdah, Vibekananda Dutta, Takafumi Matsumaru, Xin He
abstract: Recognizing unseen activities that are absent during training poses significant challenges for conventional neural networks in the recognition of human activity based onWiFi Channel State Information (CSI). Existing approaches are difficult to generalize new activities without extensive retraining—a critical limitation for real-world applications such as smart homes and healthcare monitoring. We present Trans-MAML, the first integration of Transformer architectures with Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) for unseen human activity recognition in WiFi CSI sensing. The proposed method leverages the Transformer’s effectiveness to capture long-range temporal dependencies in CSI data, while MAML enables fast adaptation to unseen activity class from minimal samples. Using leave-one-activity-out evaluation, our approach achieves up to 99.9% accuracy on the adaptation task, significantly outperforming non-Transformer backbones such as CNN and LSTM. The method maintains robustness across different antenna configurations (99.9% accuracy with 9 antennas and 98.9% with 3 antennas), suggesting its potential for practical real-world deployments where collecting large amount of data for every activity is impractical.

title: A Dual Asynchronous-synchronous Relation Graph Method for Sensor-based Group Activity Recognition
authors: Ai Bo, Ruohong Huan, Ke Wang, Peng Chen, Ronghua Liang
abstract: The fast advancement of the Internet of Things (IoT) has laid a foundation for group activity recognition (GAR) in smart environments, which aims to understand complex activities composed of multiple individual actions. However, existing methods primarily focus on recognizing synchronous actions and exhibit significant limitations in handling asynchronous actions, which are prevalent in dynamic real-world IoT scenarios. In asynchronous actions, temporal-delayed individuals perform or respond to actions with temporal lags that are not synchronized with those of real-time individuals, forming complex temporal-delayed relations. To address this issue, this paper proposes a dual asynchronous-synchronous relation graph method (DASRG) for sensor-based GAR. By introducing an asynchronous relation graph, the temporal-delayed relations between the temporaldelayed individuals and real-time individuals are captured, and the features of temporal-delayed individuals are synchronized with those of the real-time individuals through feature shifting. Meanwhile, a synchronous relation graph is constructed to enhance the modeling of interactions and action differences between individuals by integrating spatial interaction relation features and residual relation features, thereby reducing the interference of non-critical individuals on recognition results. To achieve the coupling between asynchronous and synchronous relation graphs, a graph aggregation module based on the multi-head attention mechanism is designed. By leveraging weighted feedback from attention heads, this module optimizes node information updating and enables the mutual feedback of information between the two graphs in the spatial domain, improving the model’s ability to handle asynchronous actions and mitigate interference from non-critical individuals. Extensive experimental results further demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in complex scenarios involving asynchronous actions and interfering individuals.

title: Home-Charging Sufficiency for EVs in Hot Desert Climates: A Real-World GPS Case Study from Qatar
authors: Irfan Alp Gurkaynak, I. Safak Bayram, Sertac Bayhan
abstract: In hot desert regions such as Qatar, planning electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure requires methodologies that account for local driving patterns and extreme climate conditions. This study presents a pioneering exploratory simulation-based framework to evaluate the feasibility of residential EV charging in Qatar, using high-resolution (ten-second) GPS traces from seven conventional petrol vehicles used as surrogates for local EV driving behavior. This framing reflects the Qatari context: the country is resource rich and carbon intensive, private mobility remains petrol dominated, and private EV penetration is still extremely low, partly because petrol prices are highly subsidised. The statistical analysis of telematics data collected over four months (September–December), capturing seasonal variations in travel and climate is presented.Aclustering and labeling methodology, based on a Bayesian-optimized composite of Silhouette, Davies–Bouldin, and Calinski–Harabasz indices, is presented to characterize representative spatio-temporal mobility profiles. By employing the open-source emobpy platform, EV operations are simulated for two extreme driver profiles (high- and low-usage), three EV types (small sedan, mid-size SUV, and truck), and four charging strategies. Hourly local climate variables (temperature, dew point, and pressure) are further integrated into simulation study to quantify climatic impacts on energy use. Electric mobility time series are statistically validated against observed travel patterns. Results indicate that home charging can satisfy daily energy requirements in most simulated cases, while hot-weather operation significantly increases specific energy use. For the low-usage driver profile, average consumption increases from 0.193, 0.280, and 0.366 kWh/km in December to 0.282, 0.366, and 0.449 kWh/km in September for the Nissan Leaf, KIA EV9, and Ford F-150, respectively. Across the simulated combinations of two driver profiles, three EV types, four charging strategies, and two representative seasonal months, the results further show that charging strategy can offset part of the battery-size requirement by enabling more frequent home-based replenishment. The findings are therefore interpreted as an initial data-driven basis for EV charging planning in Qatar and neighbouring hot-climate regions, rather than as a comprehensive national mobility survey.

title: 3D SLAM and Deep Learning for Real-Time Fire Localization in Ship Engine Rooms
authors: Seon Deok Kim
abstract: This study proposes a fire monitoring system for complex and confined ship engine room en-vironments using a mobile robot. Conventional fire detection systems in such environments provide limited spatial information, which restricts effective situational awareness and response. To address this limitation, this study integrates 3D Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) with fire detection using an RGB-D camera, enabling real-time perception and 3D spatial localiza-tion. Fire detection is performed using a deep learning model trained on a fire dataset collected from an actual ship engine room, and its performance is evaluated under different model configurations on a workstation. The trained models are then deployed on an embedded platform to assess real-time feasibility within the integrated robotic system. Experimental results demonstrate that a lightweight model achieves stable real-time performance while maintaining reliable detection accuracy. In addition, 3D mapping accuracy is vali-dated through quantitative comparison, showing improved performance over 2D mapping approaches. The integration of perception and mapping enables accurate localization of fire regions in a global coordinate frame, as verified through repeated experiments in both ship engine room and laboratory environments. The results indicate that the proposed system provides an effective solution for real-time fire detection and 3D localization, contributing to improved situational awareness and safety in maritime environments.

title: Comparative Study Between On- and Off-grid Photovoltaic to Reduce Peak Demand in Residential Houses
authors: Chaiyan Jettanasen, Chayanut Sottiyaphai, Sulee Bunjongjit, Panapong Songsukthawan, Natthanon Phannil, Atthapol Ngaopitakkul, Santipont Ananwattanaporn
abstract: This study proposes a programmable logic controller (PLC)–based energy management system integrated with an off-grid photovoltaic (PV) system and battery storage to reduce residential peak demand. The proposed system dynamically manages power supply between the distribution grid, PV generation, and battery storage based on real-time power demand measurements. When the measured power exceeds a predefined threshold, stored renewable energy is utilized to support high-load conditions and mitigate peak demand. Experimental results obtained from a residential-scale test system demonstrate that the proposed off-grid PV system with PLC control can reduce peak demand by up to 29.68% and 15.57% under office-working and work-from-home scenarios, respectively, compared with conventional grid supply and on-grid PV systems without storage. In addition, the system achieves electricity cost reductions of up to 13.63% under Time-of-Use tariffs and up to 11.42% under normal electricity rates, depending on load behavior. These results indicate that integrating PLC-based control with off-grid PV and battery storage can effectively mitigate residential peak demand and reduce electricity expenses under realistic operating conditions.

title: CASSIS: An LLM-Driven Framework for Sensitive API Identification in Cyber–Physical System Software Repositories
authors: Zhefan Chen, Baolin Zhang, Na Wu, Dan Li, Yuhong Nan
abstract: As cyber–physical systems (CPSs) are increasingly deployed in critical infrastructures, the misuse of CPS-sensitive (safety-critical) data can compromise device safety and even endanger human life. In this paper, we focus on identifying repository-specific Sensitive APIs that manipulate CPS-sensitive data along sensor–to–actuator data paths in CPS software repositories (“CPS repos”). These APIs are critical to downstream tasks related to CPS-sensitive data operations, such as vulnerability detection, access control, and privacy protection. Existing static and dynamic program analysis tasks typically assume a repository-specific list of Sensitive APIs as input. However, such lists are usually hand-crafted or derived from ad hoc pattern-matching rules. Therefore, they are difficult to reuse across repositories. Moreover, in large CPS repositories, where physical semantics are often abstract and documentation is scattered, these lists are prone to omissions. To address this issue, we propose CASSIS, an LLM-driven framework that automatically identifies Sensitive APIs in CPS repos without model fine-tuning. CASSIS constructs a CPS-aware code–documentation index, prunes unlikely candidates, and iteratively labels the remaining functions with an LLM without updating model parameters. On two real-world CPS repos—Genesis and ArduCopter, CASSIS reduces the number of functions requiring LLM-based classification by more than 80%, while preserving high recall for I/O-related functions. Overall, CASSIS achieves function-level Accuracy/Macro-F1 of 80.4%/77.8% on Genesis and 82.0%/80.8% on ArduCopter, demonstrating its effectiveness for practical CPS code auditing and security assessment.

title: Creating a University-based Accessible Makerspace for Use by Local Community Members through Participatory Design Workshops
authors: Erin Higgins, John Magee, Foad Hamidi
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers

title: Participants on Participation
authors: Amanda A. Geppert, Maurizio Teli

title: PCB-QA: A Task-Specific Question Answering Framework for Text-Centric Chip Datasheet Queries in PCB Design
authors: Binwu Zhu, Bei Yu
publish: ACM Transactions on Design Automation of Electronic Systems
abstract: None

title: RHFormer: A Residential Electricity Load Forecasting Model
authors: Hongwei Luo, Wei Zhao, Chujia Yan, Tongle Xie, Yanyouyou Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Accurate long-term residential load forecasting is critical for modern power grid scheduling and demand response. To address the challenges of non-stationary distribution shifts and the limitations of single-scale pattern capturing, this paper proposes RHFormer, an enhanced long-sequence load forecasting model built upon a deep decomposition framework. The model integrates Reversible Instance Normalization (RevIN) to mitigate the effects of non-stationarity and introduces a hybrid correlation module. By combining global auto-correlation with local multi-scale depthwise separable convolutions (MS-DSC), RHFormer enables collaborative modeling of complex load patterns. Experimental results on a public electricity dataset demonstrate that the proposed model consistently outperforms mainstream baselines across multiple forecasting horizons.

title: An Improved Zonotope-Based Method for Flexible Resource Aggregation
authors: Yuanmeng Zhu, Anjia Mao
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The extensive integration of distributed energy resources (DERs), particularly high-penetration renewable energy and flexible loads, poses significant challenges to the secure, stable, and economic operation of power systems. In particular, the flexibility regulation capability of the traditional power grid struggles to keep pace with the rapid growth and inherent volatility of wind and solar power generation. Consequently, coordinating massive DERs in an orderly manner to fully unlock their flexibility potential has become critical, necessitating an efficient resource aggregation method. To address these issues, this paper proposes an improved Zonotope aggregation model that explicitly accounts for time value and dispatch potential. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed model improves economic efficiency while maintaining aggregation accuracy, thereby validating the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method.

title: Fault Analysis and Handing of a Converter Transformer Overheating Gas Production
authors: Jin Wang, Wenfeng Liao, Xin Wang, Sen Li, Jingyi Dong
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This study analyzed a converter transformer oil chromatography abnormality, used oil chromatography data tracking, three-ratio coding, system test verification and other means to analyze that the gas production was caused by overheating faults, and positively correlated with the load, and initially determined the fault site is located in the converter leads or other through-flow circuits. Further internal inspection found that there were many traces of ablation in the valve winding parallel lead shielding tube, indicating that the root cause of overheating gas production was vibration insulation local damage of the valve winding parallel lead, which causing a local short circuit. Under the combined effect of leakage-induced loop current and lead current shunt, the equipotential line terminals and connected leads heated and product gas.

title: Investigations of magnetic properties of nanoribbon arrays based on Co and Cu
authors: Vorobiov, B. Stropkai, V. Latyshev, M. Lisnichuk, D. Kondrakhova, I. Shpetnyy, O. Tkach, E. Čižmár, V. Komanický
abstract: This work presents the fabrication and magnetic characterization of one-dimensional (1D) Co75Cu25 nanoribbon arrays (NRs) obtained by electron-beam lithography and electrochemical deposition. To evaluate the effect of dimensional reduction, the magnetic properties of NRs were compared with those of thin films of identical composition. Structural analyses (SAED, STEM) revealed the formation of a metastable Co(Cu) solid solution in the as-prepared state and significant grain growth upon thermal annealing up to 750 K without phase transformation. MFM measurements demonstrate a transition from a stripe-domain structure in thin films to a bamboo-like domain configuration in nanoribbons, indicating the dominant role of shape anisotropy in 1D systems. Magnetometry confirms the presence of a well-defined longitudinal easy axis and reveals an increase in coercivity after annealing, accompanied by a reduction of local anisotropy contributions. Thermomagnetic ZFC-FC measurements show a pronounced shift of the irreversibility temperature toward higher values, reflecting enhanced magnetic correlations and an increase in the effective magnetic volume. These results demonstrate that dimensional reduction and geometric structuring provide an effective route for controlling domain configuration, magnetic anisotropy, and thermomagnetic stability in Co-Cu systems, with strong potential for applications in spintronic devices and nanoscale magnetic elements.

title: A CGAN-LSTM-Based Framework for Time-Varying Non-Stationary Channel Modeling
authors: Keying Guo, Ruisi He, Mi Yang, Yuxin Zhang, Bo Ai, Haoxiang Zhang, Jiahui Han, Ruifeng Chen
abstract: Time-varying non-stationary channels exhibit complex dynamics and temporal evolution, which pose critical challenges for accurate modeling and performance evaluation of wireless communication systems. Different from most existing approaches that mainly focus on static channel generation or short-term prediction, our core objective is not precise future prediction, but the generation of synthetic channel sequences that statistically replicate the temporal non-stationary characteristics of real-world channels. To this end, we propose a hybrid deep learning framework that integrates conditional generative adversarial networks (CGAN) with long short-term memory (LSTM) networks. A temporal stationarity-constrained mechanism is further introduced to enforce correlations across consecutive snapshots, ensuring realistic channel evolution and preventing non-physical fluctuations. The framework is validated on ray tracing-based datasets under various non-stationary scenarios. Experimental results show that the generated channels closely match ground-truth simulations in terms of key statistical features.

title: How Far Are We from Generating Missing Modalities with Foundation Models?
authors: Guanzhou Ke, Bo Wang, Guoqing Chao, Weiming Hu, Shengfeng He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Multimodal foundation models have demonstrated impressive capabilities across diverse tasks. However, their potential as plug-and-play solutions for missing modality re construction remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we identify and formalize three potential paradigms for missing modality reconstruction, and perform a comprehensive evaluation across these paradigms, covering 42 model variants in terms of reconstruction accuracy and adaptability to downstream tasks. Our analysis reveals that current foundation models often fall short in two critical aspects: (i) fine-grained semantic extraction from the available modalities, and (ii) robust validation of generated modalities. These limitations lead to suboptimal and, at times, misaligned generations. To address these challenges, we propose anagentic framework tailored for missing modality reconstruction. This framework dynamically formulates modality-aware mining strategies based on the input context, facilitating the extraction of richer and more discriminative semantic features. In addition, we introduce a self-refinement mechanism, which iteratively verifies and enhances the quality of generated modalities through internal feedback. Experimental results show that our method reduces FID for missing image reconstruction by at least 14% and MER for missing text reconstruction by at least 10% compared to baselines. Code is released at: https://github.com/Guanzhou-Ke/AFM2.

title: Simultaneous Sensing Data Acquisition and Sharing in Low-Altitude Wireless Networks: Fundamental Limits and Signaling Design
authors: Fuwang Dong, Fan Liu, Yifeng Xiong, Yuanhao Cui, Wei Wang, Shi Jin
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing
abstract: In the low-altitude wireless networks, the simultaneous sensing data acquisition and sharing (SDAS) through an ISAC signaling strategy becomes a typical application scenario. In this paper, we mainly investigate three primary aspects of the SDAS system, namely, the information-theoretic framework, the distribution of ISAC channel input, and the waveform design for Gaussian signaling. First, we establish the information-theoretic framework and develop a modified source-channel separation theorem (MSST) tailored for the SDAS systems. The proposed MSST elucidates the relationship between achievable distortion, coding rate, and communication channel capacity in cases where the distortion metric is separable for sensing and communication (S&C) processes. Second, we present a channel input distribution design for dual-functional signaling, which aims to minimize SDAS distortion under the constraints of the MSST and resource budget. We then conceive a two-step Blahut-Arimoto (BA)-based search algorithm to numerically solve the functional optimization problem. Third, to provide practical insights, we further propose a waveform design method for Gaussian signaling in multi-input multi-output (MIMO) SDAS systems. The associated covariance matrix optimization problem is addressed using a successive convex approximation (SCA)-based waveform design algorithm. Finally, we provide numerical simulation results to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms, which characterizes the unique performance tradeoff between S&C processes.

title: A Universal and Lightweight Automated Design Framework for Multi-Band Power Amplifiers
authors: Jinyu Wang, Jingyang Gao, Shengwei Meng, Yuchen Wei, Wenchao Peng, Ruihong Fang, Yang Wang, Cong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: In power amplifier (PA) design, the repeated trial-and-error process and time-consuming electromagnetic (EM) simulation are major obstacles to achieving efficient design. In this study, based on a small microstrip line dataset, a lightweight deep neural network (DNN) is trained to accurately predict the S-parameters of layout-level matching networks (MNs). Leveraging the DNN’s high-speed prediction capabilities and an ordinary particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm, the entire optimization process is completed without the involvement of EM simulation software, enabling highly efficient automated design of PAs. This method is applied to the automated synthesis of narrowband, tri-band, and broadband PAs, demonstrating high flexibility. Among these, the broadband PA (a 0.5-5 GHz GaN PA) is fabricated for validation. Measurement results show that within the operating frequency range, the output power reaches 39-42.2 dBm, and the drain efficiency (DE) ranges from 47.2% to 74.6%, with an average of 56.5%.

title: APT: Optimal Tree for Diagnosis Simulation and Various Diagnosis Applications
authors: Wu-Tung Cheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Performing a scan test failure diagnosis is necessary for advanced semiconductor manufacturing technologies to improve yield. A pre-silicon diagnosis simulation is essential to avoid low diagnosis resolution during volume production. Just as fault simulation identifies test quality problems, diagnosis simulation identifies diagnosis quality problems. The root causes of low diagnosis quality can be classified into three areas: (1) Insufficient data collected from the automatic test equipment (ATE) to support diagnosis. To fix this problem, ATE needs to have more extensive fail data storage; (2) Production scan test patterns are not adequate for diagnosis, such that better test pattern selection or new diagnosis-specific test patterns should be created; (3) Designs are not friendly to creating these diagnosis-specific test patterns, so design-for-diagnosis enhancements should be used to modify the designs to be friendly for diagnosis-specific test patterns. We propose using an Asymmetric Partition Tree (APT) to extract useful information from fault simulations for diagnosis simulation usage. APT can be used to calculate diagnosis coverage and identify areas with diagnosis quality issues. Furthermore, APT can be used to analyze the impact of ATE’s failed data storage limit on diagnosis coverage. APT can be proven to have the minimum size for diagnosis simulation. This paper provides a detailed explanation of how to collect the necessary information and construct APT trees effectively and efficiently for various applications.

title: Sentiment Hypergraphs with Hierarchical Reasoning: A Novel Framework for Advanced Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis
authors: Anusha P. Anilkumar, Yeo-Chan Yoon, Kyu-Hyung Kim, Dae-Heon Park, Ki-Duck Kim
abstract: We introduce SHHR (Sentiment Hypergraphs with Hierarchical Reasoning), a novel framework that unifies structural modeling, semantic enrichment, and reasoning-based inference for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on shallow pairwise attention or static syntactic trees, SHHR constructs dynamic hypergraphs to en code higher-order dependencies between aspect terms, opinion tokens, and latent sentiment paths. These graphs are further infused with external knowledge from ConceptNet and WordNet, forming hybrid structures that anchor sentiment interpretation in both contextual and commonsense understanding. A multi hop reasoning path mechanism traverses these fused graphs to aggregate semantically interpretable reasoning paths with component-level attribution, while a hierarchical attention module seamlessly combines local and global context. Together, these components enable SHHR to perform sentiment propagation over both structurally ambiguous and semantically rich utterances. Empirical results on SemEval-2014 (Laptop and Restaurant) and the MAMS benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements over leading transformer-based, graph-based, and knowledge augmented ABSA models. Ablation studies, statistical tests, and detailed error analysis highlight the robustness and interpretability of SHHR, affirming its utility in modeling complex sentiment flows beyond surface-level cues.

title: Hybrid Grid-Forming Control for Enhanced Active Support and DC Voltage Stability in MMC-HVDC Systems
authors: Weiye Diao, Jun Mei, Guanghua Wang, Yiwei Xu, Yiming Shao
abstract: Grid-forming control based on power synchronization and DC voltage synchronization is two typical approaches for enabling modular multilevel converter-based high-voltage direct current (MMC-HVDC) systems to operate in weak grids. However, the inherent limitations of their synchronization mechanisms make it difficult to simultaneously ensure system stability and economic efficiency. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a two-stage hybrid grid-forming control strategy. In the first stage, a decoupled matching control is designed to exploit the energy stored in submodule (SM) capacitors for active damping support, which achieves nonlinear indirect matching between DC voltage and frequency while maintaining the economic operation of the sending-end grid. Meanwhile, a voltage stabilization method based on redundant SMs is proposed to decouple SM capacitor voltage from the DC voltage, thereby enhancing DC voltage stability. In the second stage, once the average voltage of the SMs exceeds a predefined threshold, virtual synchronous generator control mobilizes the reserved energy from renewable plants and the rotor kinetic energy of wind turbines, further strengthening inertia and damping support. Moreover, a small signal model is developed to reveal the parameter evolution, and a unified two-stage framework is established through control correction and parameter optimization, thereby improving system dynamic performance and stability. Finally, PSCAD/EMTDC simulations validate the correctness and effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Spectral Diagnostics of Emergence: A Random Matrix Baseline and a Mesoscopic Regime from Competing Scales
authors: Alain Tcheukam Siwe
abstract: Understanding how large-scale organization emerges from interacting components remains a central challenge across physics, biology, and artificial intelligence. In this work, we establish a spectral framework that both identifies and reproduces emergent structure using random matrix theory (RMT) as a quantitative baseline. We employ the ratio of level spacings (r-statistic) and its variance as unfolding-free observables to characterize spectral organization across scales. Numerical experiments on classical random matrix ensembles (GOE, GUE, and Poisson) demonstrate immediate universality: spectral statistics converge at small system sizes (N ≥ 100), remain stable across scales, and exhibit no intermediate mesoscopic regime. This confirms that RMT provides a null model devoid of emergent behavior. We then introduce a minimal correlated random matrix model with three competing components: noise, structured correlations, and a low-rank signal tuned to the Baik–Ben Arous–Péché (BBP) transition. This controlled setting reveals a genuine mesoscopic regime characterized by a finite-size peak in the variance of the edge statistic Var(r), accompanied by persistent restructuring of the bulk spectrum. The effect is maximized at criticality and disappears asymptotically, establishing emergence as a finite-size phenomenon driven by competing scales. These results provide a concrete and operational definition of emergence as a deviation from spectral universality. The proposed framework transforms spectral analysis into a diagnostic tool: stable RMT-like behavior indicates absence of structure, while mesoscopic variance peaks signal partial organization, criticality, and ongoing reorganization. This approach is general and directly applicable to complex systems, including neural networks, biological data, and correlated physical systems, where it offers a new lens for detecting structure formation and dynamical transitions.

title: Analysis and Experiment of Isolated DC-DC Converter Using Multiple Cascaded Chopper Cells with Phase-Shift Application
authors: Lucas P. Morales, Makoto Hagiwara
abstract: The rise of renewable energy sources and DC power grids demands the use of reliable and efficient converters to interface them with energy storage systems. This paper introduces an improved control strategy for an isolated DC-DC converter suited to low-voltage DC grid systems. Based on the three-phase dual active bridge (TP-DAB) topology, an auxiliary converter formed by cascaded choppers is introduced between the primary bridge and the primary windings of the transformer. This auxiliary converter generates a compensating voltage that reduces the reactive current and, therefore, lowers power losses and peak currents. Compared with other techniques, the proposed method achieves higher efficiency under certain operating conditions determined by the voltage ratio and load. By applying a phase shift between the switching signals of the primary and secondary bridges, the power and ac current can be controlled. This simplifies the control strategy of the auxiliary converter, enabling the implementation of a two-level voltage waveform. Consequently, a higher switching frequency can be used in the main bridge compared with previous works, resulting in smaller passive components. The circuit topology, operation and control strategy are introduced. Current and power loss comparisons are carried out through simulation. Finally, the proposed converter is verified by experimental results with a downscaled 2.5 kW, 150 V prototype. Compared to previous works, the main bridge frequency is increased from 450 Hz to 1350 Hz, while the capacitor voltage of the choppers is reduced by 27%.

title: Robust Real-Time Human Activity Recognition via 2D Tensor CNN with Change Point Detection and Confidence-Based Prediction Filtering
authors: Ghufron Andriansyah, Fiky Y. Suratman, Istiqomah
abstract: Falls are a serious health issue, particularly among the elderly, where delayed detection can lead to severe injuries or even death. Conventional fall detection systems based on cameras or wearable devices suffer from limitations in terms of privacy, comfort, and reliability. To overcome these challenges, this study proposes a real-time Human Activity Recognition (HAR) system using mmWave FMCW radar integrated with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), combined with Confidence Based Prediction Filtering (CBPF) and Change Point Detection (CPD) algorithms. Radar point cloud data are transformed into two-dimensional representations, namely Doppler Range, Doppler Time, and Range Time, which are then classified into four main activities: sitting, standing, walking, and falling. The current CNN-based HAR radar approach still faces limitations in computational efficiency and prediction stability because classification is performed on each frame without considering significant temporal changes in activity. In this paper, CBPF algorithm is designed to improve classification stability by applying a confidence threshold and enforcing temporal consistency (consecutive prediction count) before updating the final label. Meanwhile, the CPD module detects significant changes in Doppler patterns that indicate transitions in activity and only forwards radar frames that show such significant changes to the CNN for classification, thereby improving computational efficiency. In addition, a stride windowing parameter analysis was conducted to support real-time processing with minimal delay while maintaining high accuracy. Experimental results show that integrating CBPF and CPD significantly enhances real-time classification accuracy, increasing accuracy from 75% (without CBPF and CPD) to 93.33%, while maintaining a maximum system latency of 0.83 s, which is suitable for real-time deployment. The proposed system was successfully implemented in a real-time graphical user interface (GUI) displaying point cloud, heatmap, and stable classification outputs. These findings demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively delivers accurate and reliable activity detection, making it highly applicable for real-world scenarios such as elderly monitoring and smart healthcare systems.

title: Machine Learning for Network Optimization: An Empirical Performance Analysis Across Diverse Traffic Scenarios
authors: Ansa Niaz, Muhammad Saqib Niaz, Kazim Raza Talpur, Bandeh Ali Talpur
abstract: The objective of this scientific study is to determine under which traffic conditions machinelearning-based routing control provides sufficient benefit over conventional equal-cost multi-path routing to justify its additional operational complexity. To address this objective, routing is formulated as a sequential decision-making problem and evaluated through a controlled empirical comparison in Network Simulator 3 using a fat-tree data-center topology. The study compares three controllers: a conventional equal-cost multi-path baseline, a supervised learning controller based on a temporal convolutional network, and a deep reinforcement learning controller based on a deep Q-network. Traffic workloads are derived from public Internet backbone, long-haul, and campus-network trace repositories, with additional distributed-denial-of-service traffic injected from a public attack dataset to construct four operating regimes: Core Internet, Long-Haul Diurnal, Enterprise Campus, and Stress/Attack. Performance is assessed using average and 99th-percentile flow completion time, receiver goodput, packet loss rate, and inference latency under heterogeneous trace-driven traffic conditions. The results show that both learning-based controllers improve latency and reliability relative to the conventional baseline, with the largest gains under volatile and adversarial traffic. In the aggregate medium-load evaluation, the deep reinforcement learning controller reduces average flow completion time by 33.4% and packet loss by 80.7% relative to equal-cost multi-path routing. The supervised controller is more competitive in stable regimes and delivers about half the inference latency of the reinforcement controller (0.8 ms versus 1.6 ms). These findings indicate that supervised learning is attractive when traffic is predictable and operational simplicity is important, whereas reinforcement learning is better justified when traffic non-stationarity, burstiness, and attack-driven stress make long-horizon adaptation more valuable.

title: Artificial Intelligence-Driven Design Automation of RF and Millimeter-Wave Circuits and Systems
authors: Fábio Passos
abstract:
The growing complexity of radio frequency (RF) and millimeter-wave (mm-wave) integrated circuits (ICs) has widened the gap between design productivity and system requirements. While artificial intelligence (AI)-based techniques have recently emerged as promising enablers of automation, many approaches remain limited to schematic-level optimization or isolated circuit blocks.

This article presents a hierarchical, layout- and variability-aware methodology for AI-driven RF and mm-wave circuit and system synthesis. The proposed framework integrates multiobjective optimization; automatic layout generation; parasitic extraction; and process, voltage, and temperature (PVT)-aware evaluation directly within the design loop, ensuring robustness by construction.

Device-, circuit-, and system-level examples, including passive components, amplifiers, oscillators, and a 77-GHz receiver (Rx) front end, demonstrate scalability and silicon-consistent performance. The results highlight how hierarchical automation can expose rich tradeoffs, accelerate exploration, and complement expert RF designers in the development of next-generation microwave systems.

title: Joint Beamforming and Deployment Optimization for Active STAR-RIS Enabled Covert Communications
authors: Yawen Wang, Jiancun Fan, Hengbo Xu, Qingze Yan, Jie Luo, Feng Yan, Xiaolin Jia
abstract: This paper introduces a novel system architecture that integrates an active simultaneous transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (aSTAR-RIS) with rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) to achieve wireless covert communication in the presence of a multi-antenna warden. Departing from conventional approaches that deploy STAR-RIS at fixed positions, we treat the aSTAR-RIS location as a design variable to be optimized for maximizing the covert transmission rate. Our objective is to jointly optimize the aSTAR-RIS placement, transmit beamforming, transmission/reflection coefficients (TRCs), and common rate allocation so as to maximize both the common and private rates for the covert user. The resulting multi-variable optimization problem is addressed via an alternating optimization (AO) framework, which decomposes the problem into four tractable subproblems. For the multi-ratio fractional programming subproblem in the context of transmit beamforming and TRCs optimization, we adopt a Lagrangian dual formulation and quadratic transformation to reformulate the objective into a difference of convex (DC) form. The rank-one constrained beamforming design is then resolved using a penalized successive convex approximation (SCA) method combined with Gaussian randomization. A closed-form solution is derived for the common rate allocation subproblem, while the aSTAR-RIS location optimization is efficiently tackled via the SCA technique. Simulation results validate that the proposed scheme substantially enhances covert transmission rates while preserving communication covertness and guaranteeing reliable common signal reception. Importantly, the results highlight that optimizing the aSTAR-RIS location plays a critical role in improving the overall covert performance of the system.

title: Enhancing Service Function Chain Performance in SDN/NFV-Enabled Space-Air-Ground Integrated Vehicular Networks: A Three-Sided Stable Matching Approach
authors: Mengqiu Tian, Xiaoming Yuan, Ni Tian, Hongwei Ding, Celimuge Wu, Zhu Han
abstract: Leveraging network function virtualization and software-defined networking, 6G space-air-ground integrated vehicular networks (SAGIVNs) are envisioned to provide efficient and customized services on a global scale. However, on-demand resource allocation for vehicle users (VUs) remains challenging due to the rigidity of service chains, the diversity of user demands, and the complexity of network environments. Existing studies also suffer from inaccurate scene recognition and inefficient virtual resource scheduling. To address these issues, this paper proposes a scene-recognition-based on-demand resource scheduling strategy. Specifically, we first develop a scene recognition mechanism to identify VU demands by analyzing the impact of scenes. Then, the multi-criteria services of multi-dimensional resources are characterized through a polychromatic-set-based description, which supports a unified and extensible representation of heterogeneous resource attributes. Meanwhile, the on-demand matching degree is introduced to measure the satisfaction level of multi-criteria demands. Next, resources are scheduled in the form of groups to improve the efficiency of virtual resource mapping and deployment. Furthermore, a three-sided matching problem among SAGIVN equipment, VU groups, and VUs is formulated, based on which a SAGIVN-equipment-oriented resource scheduling algorithm and a VU-oriented resource scheduling algorithm are developed to achieve accurate service-demand matching. Simulation results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed schemes over benchmark methods.

title: Making Policy Work: Navigating Organisational Inertia Through Participatory Design
authors: Sissel Olander, Nicky Nedergaard
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: Theatre of the Tech-Oppressed: Exploring Algorithmic Injustice Through Participatory Performances
authors: Najme Babai, Eva Durall Gazulla, Netta Iivari, Marianne Kinnula, Ole Sejer Iversen
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: SwinLoAttn-DIP: A Hybrid Transformer-Enhanced Deep Image Prior for Microscopic EIT Sensing
authors: Xuefei Chen, Zhongye Chen, Wenbo An, Zekun Chen, Shili Liang, Lei Zhang, Suqiu Wang
abstract: Applying Electrical Impedance Tomography (EIT) at the microscopic scale is a promising approach for label-free cellular monitoring in the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT). However, practical deployment faces challenges such as ill-posed inverse problems, low Signal-to-Noise Ratios (SNRs), and the lack of labeled training data. To address these issues, this paper proposes SwinLoAttn-DIP, an unsupervised reconstruction framework designed for a custom miniature sensing system. The core network, SwinLoAttnNet, combines Swin Transformer blocks with a hybrid attention mechanism. Unlike standard convolutional methods that may blur fine details, this architecture captures global dependencies to reconstruct complex microstructures without requiring pre-training. We validated the proposed method using a self-developed 15-mm sensor node. Through systematic comparisons with recent architectures, including pretrained Transformers and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)-based models, the results demonstrate that our framework achieves superior boundary fidelity and reduced artifacts across simulations, ex vivo tissues, and public datasets. This work establishes a robust and data-efficient paradigm for high-precision microscopic imaging, facilitating the implementation of reliable remote biosensing in data-scarce IoMT environments.

title: Simultaneous Temperature and Salinity Sensing via FBS and FWM
authors: Hongcheng Ni, N. Guo, Wujun Zhang, Wei Geng, Yongheng Ma, Yuangang Lu
abstract: A novel method is proposed for simultaneous measurement of temperature and salinity, using forward Brillouin scattering (FBS) and four-wave mixing (FWM) interactions within a single-mode fiber (SMF). A novel linear relationship is theoretically established and experimentally validated between the frequency shift of FWM-induced beat frequency components and ambient temperature surrounding the sensing fiber. This phenomenon enables superior signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) while maintaining high frequency shift-temperature coefficient. Moreover, the corresponding coefficient exhibits a sign opposite to that of the FBS temperature sensitivity coefficient, and remains insensitive to variations in salinity. Building upon these characterized phenomena, we propose a method for simultaneous temperature and salinity measurement that achieves a lower measurement uncertainty. In a proof-of-concept experiment, the measurement uncertainties for temperature and salinity were found to be as low as 0.04 ℃ and 0.25%, respectively.

title: Development of Columnar Tin-Doped Gallium Oxide Films Using an RF/DC Co-Sputtering Technique of Dual-procedure for Hydrogen Sulfide Sensing Applications
authors: Ming-Fong Tsai, Chia-Lin Wu, Ting-Jen Hsueh
abstract: This study presents the development of a highly sensitive hydrogen sulfide (H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>S) gas sensor that features Tin-doped Gallium Oxide (Sn-d-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>) nanorod films with a micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS) platform using a dual RF/DC co-sputtering process. The dual-procedure deposition and the high-thermal treatment allow controlled nanorod formation, enhanced carrier transport and improved electrical conductivity, compared with undoped and single-procedure films. Thermal calibration demonstrates that there is a highly linear relationship between applied voltage and operating temperature (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9988), so there is accurate temperature control for the microheater. Structural analyses confirm Sn incorporation, increased surface area and a polycrystalline nanorod morphology. The optimized sensor, which is fabricated at a high-thermal temperature of 197.2 °C and operated at 282.8 °C, exhibits a strong response to H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>S and high selectivity against interfering gases. The sensor achieves rapid response/recovery times of 12.1 s and 32.2 s, respectively, and demonstrates stable detection at sub-ppm concentrations, with a calibration linearity (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) of 0.994. Aging tests show that dual-procedure deposition significantly suppresses impedance drift and improves long-term stability. These results show that the synergistic combination of MEMS integration, Sn donor doping and dual RF/DC sputtering increases sensing performance, reproducibility and the stability of Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>-based gas sensors, which are eminently suited to practical environmental monitoring applications.

title: Truthful Online Preference Aggregation for LLM Fine-Tuning in Mobile Crowdsourcing
authors: Shugang Hao, Lingjie Duan
abstract: To better serve users' demands in mobile applications (e.g., navigation), mobile crowdsourcing platforms can iteratively align large language model (LLM)-generated content (e.g., AI-generated traffic condition predictions) with human feedback collected from crowdsourcing workers (e.g., mobile users). However, workers may strategically misreport their online preference feedback to maximize their influence or payment. Existing pipelines in mobile crowdsourcing (e.g., EM-based weight estimation) fail to identify the most accurate worker in this online setting, resulting in a linear regret <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(T)$</tex-math></inline-formula> over <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$T$</tex-math></inline-formula> time slots. In this paper, we study truthful online preference aggregation for LLM fine-tuning in mobile crowdsourcing. We formulate a new dynamic Bayesian game to model the multi-agent online learning process between the platform and strategic mobile workers. We propose a novel online weighted aggregation mechanism that dynamically adjusts each worker's weight in the preference aggregation according to their feedback accuracy. We prove that our mechanism ensures truthful feedback from strategic workers and achieves a sublinear regret <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(\sqrt{T})$</tex-math></inline-formula> over <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$T$</tex-math></inline-formula> time slots. We further extend our mechanism to a challenging scenario with limited worker feedback per time slot, still guaranteeing a sublinear regret <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(\sqrt{T})$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Experiments on LLM fine-tuning with real-world datasets further demonstrate significant performance gains of our mechanisms over benchmark schemes.

title: Effect of Annealing on the Microstructure and the Magnetostrictive Property of Nanogranular Fe-B-N Alloy Thin Film
authors: Naoki Isogai, Kosuke Imamura, Yuta Nakamura, Takahiro Yamazaki, Yuko Kaneda, Kohei Yamagami, Masato Kotsugi, Mitsuru Ohtake
abstract: (Fe100–xBx)0.89N11 (at. %) films with x = 18 and 25 are formed on thermally oxidized Si substrates at room temperature and then annealed at 200 or 400 °C. The influences of annealing on the microstructural, electrical, magnetic, and magnetostrictive properties are investigated. Granular structure is realized in the films both without and with annealing, where Fe-B-N grains of about d = 5 nm diameter are surrounded by boundaries consisting of amorphous B-N phase. The grains are composed of amorphous phase for the films without and with annealing at 200 °C. On the other hand, parts of the grains are crystallized and tend to coalesce together by annealing at 400 °C. Furthermore, formation of the B-N phase is enhanced by employing a B-richer content of x = 25 and a higher annealing temperature of 400 °C. The Fe-B-N films without and with annealing exhibit high electrical resistivities of ρ = 230–1550 μΩ·cm, when compared with the cases of nanocrystalline materials such as Fe-B-Si-Nb-Cu and Fe-B-Si-P-Cu. However, by annealing at 400 °C, the resistivities of films with x = 18 and 25 respectively decrease from ρ = 500 to 230 μΩ·cm and from ρ = 1550 to 930 μΩ·cm due to the partial coalescence of grains. By contrast, the saturation magnetizations of films with x = 18 and 25 are respectively improved from Ms = 0.9 to 1.2 kemu/cm3 and from Ms = 0.5 to 0.8 kemu/cm3, possibly because the Fe content in grains increases through the segregation of B and N atoms to the grain boundaries. Moreover, the saturation magnetostrictions of films with x = 18 and 25 are respectively suppressed from λs = 21×10–6 to 3×10–6 and from λs = 9×10–6 to 5×10–6 due to the partial crystallization of grains and/or due to the variation of the B and N contents within the grains. The present study has shown that granular Fe-B-N alloy is one of the candidates for soft magnetic materials with high resistivity and low magnetostriction.

title: Progressive Point Cloud Denoising Guided by Unsigned Distance Function
authors: Peng Du, Xingce Wang, Haichuan Zhao, Zhongke Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Point cloud denoising is a fundamental task in 3D geometric processing. Existing learning–based methods typically supervised training on synthesized noise, aiming to recover the perturbation paths. However, these approaches often lack explicit constraints on the movement direction during projection, which can lead to deviations from the desired directions and result in uneven point distributions on the underlying surface. To address this issue, we propose a novel progressive point cloud denoising approach that is guided by unsigned distance function (UDF), which leverages both moving distance and movement direction to accurately guide point projections onto the underlying surface. Specifically, we perform the denoising process in two steps while enforcing a consistent directional constraint. First, to improve the robustness of direct UDF-guided point projection under high noise, we adjust noisy points at different scales to lower-scale intermediate points.
