After obtaining these noise-adjusted points, two independent branches are employed to learn the required moving distance and movement direction, respectively. We explicitly define the intermediate point in the first step as a linear interpolation between the noisy point and its corresponding clean point, which facilitates enforcing displacement constraints along this interpolation direction in the second step. Our method achieves stable movement directions without requiring ground-truth UDF values or normals. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and scanned datasets demonstrate that our approach produces more uniformly distributed points compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our source code is available at https://github.com/ricodp54/UDFDenoising.

title: Topology-Guided Knowledge Transfer for Multiobjective Multitask Optimization
authors: Tingyu Zhang, Xinyi Wu, Wenyin Gong, Shuijia Li, Yanchi Li, A. K. Qin
abstract: In single-task multiobjective optimization, exploiting the low-dimensional manifold structure of the Pareto set is effective for guiding the search. However, extending this idea to the more complex multiobjective multitask optimization (MMTO) raises two key challenges: i) how to acquire relevant manifold knowledge across different tasks, and ii) how to transfer it effectively. To address these issues, we propose MMTO-TGT, an MMTO algorithm with topology-guided knowledge transfer. For the first challenge, MMTO-TGT employs a self-organizing map (SOM) method on the combined source and target populations to construct inter-individual topological neighborhoods, where neighbors are defined as structurally similar individuals from both populations. For each target parent, MMTO-TGT exploits the learned topology to identify its neighborhood and extract task-relevant manifold knowledge. To address the second challenge, MMTO-TGT effectively exploits the acquired knowledge by employing multiple offspring generation strategies to improve transfer performance. Moreover, to mitigate negative transfer, MMTO-TGT dynamically adjusts the transfer probability by comparing self-evolution and knowledge transfer with respect to both convergence and diversity indicators. Extensive experiments on three benchmark suites, together with two real-world applications, demonstrate the superiority of the proposed MMTO-TGT over several state-of-the-art algorithms.

title: Evolving Robot Morphology with Manipulation Learning Inspired by Human Hand Evolution
authors: Bangchu Yang, Li Jiang, Wenhao Wu, Ming Cheng, Kening Gong
abstract: The evolution of human hands is intricately linked to their morphologies, behaviors, and selection pressures. This biological evolution process provides critical insights for robot design, facilitating robot evolution through environmental interaction and skill learning. In this context, we propose a Baldwin-effect-inspired framework that endows robots with the capacity for long-term enhancement in response to environmental changes. This framework integrates large-scale population evolution and morphology-aware manipulation learning. Each robotic hand undergoes extensive testing of its grasp and manipulation abilities with various objects. Leveraging this framework, these hand agents validate several prior hypotheses related to morphological embodied intelligence: the relative advantage of human-like segment models in manipulative potential, the impact of the environment on the final evolved morphologies, and the influence of morphology difference on the agent's learning ability. These phenomena are not independent, as multiple agents with different segment models exhibit convergent evolution and morphological specialization. Furthermore, this natural evolutionary capability has been implemented in three-, four-, and five-fingered reconfigurable robotic hands, each following unique evolutionary trajectories that evolve towards higher bilateral symmetry. The evolved prototypes have demonstrated superior performance, surpassing anthropomorphic designs by human experts.

title: Fully Automated Schematic and Layout Design of Millimeter-Wave CMOS Power Amplifiers Using Prior Knowledge-Guided Decomposition and Machine Learning-Assisted Optimization
authors: Hanyu Liu, Yawen Wei, Qi Wu, Guangyi Lu, Haiming Wang, Wei Hong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: The RF CMOS technology is preferably used in the development of mass-market millimeter-wave (mmWave) integrated circuits because of its cost-effectiveness and integration capabilities. However, conventional design methods, limited by fixed topologies, struggle to achieve optimal performance, power, and area (PPA) trade-offs. A fully automated method is proposed for rapid mmWave CMOS power amplifier (PA) design that uses prior knowledge-guided decomposition and machine learning-assisted optimization (MLAO). Following hybrid topology configuration through schematic simulation, the proposed approach decomposes the entire circuit into multiple optimizable subproblems using microwave network theory. Then, these subproblems are solved sequentially and iteratively. In particular, a multibranch MLAO algorithm is applied to on-chip transformers to enable broadband matching and high-fidelity impedance synthesis. Post-layout simulation results for five different PA design cases, spanning 17 to 41 GHz, demonstrate that the proposed method significantly accelerates the design process compared to conventional approaches while maintaining a high PPA.

title: Time-Frequency Feature-Guided Progressive Attention DenseNet for Audio Magnetotelluric Noise Detection
authors: Dian Hong, Jin Li, Jiayu Wang, Qunbin Qi, Jianmin Li, Jingtian Tang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The audio-magnetotelluric (AMT) sounding method has been widely applied in mineral exploration and engineering geology. Unfortunately, field-observed signals are often contaminated by various random noises, causing the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) to change dynamically over time. Most existing time-domain denoising methods process the entire observation sequence uniformly and do not adaptively distinguish signals according to temporal variations in signal quality. However, certain time segments inherently possess high SNR and thus require no additional denoising. Uniform processing not only increases computational cost but may also overprocess useful signals. To address this issue, we propose a time-frequency feature-guided progressive attention-dense network (TF-PADenseNet) for AMT noise detection. The method first employs the continuous wavelet transform to convert time-domain signals into time-frequency feature maps within a unified representation space, thereby revealing the energy distribution characteristics across both time and frequency. Next, a progressive attention-dense network is constructed by integrating squeeze-and-excitation and convolutional block attention module mechanisms to progressively refine and reconstruct feature weights, enabling the network to distinguish between high-quality and noisy signals. Finally, a classification layer produces the recognition results, achieving precise identification of noisy and useful signals across different time segments. Simulation results show that the proposed TF-PADenseNet achieves an accuracy of 98.01% on the test dataset, outperforming several mainstream noise detection methods. Field experiments further demonstrate that TF-PADenseNet exhibits strong robustness under complex electromagnetic conditions. When combined with any denoising algorithm, its detection results can further improve data quality, yielding apparent resistivity and phase curves that are both smooth and continuous while preserving key signal features.

title: Personalized Diabetes Care: Discovering Diabetes Mindset Disparities From Metaphorical Cognition
authors: Wang Zhao, Rui Mao, Shuangyan Deng, Erik Cambria
abstract: Diabetes self-management is shaped not only by knowledge and resources but by implicit cognitive architectures that guide how patients interpret symptoms, data, and control. Existing questionnaire-based measures and surface-level NLP analyses capture explicit constructs and topics, yet they overlook how patients conceptually structure their illness experience. Grounded in Conceptual Metaphor Theory, we propose a scalable framework that reconstructs diabetes self-management cognition from naturalistic patient discourse via computational metaphor analysis. We extract concept mappings from diabetes posts on Reddit using MetaPro 2.0, and summarize them into an interpretable multi-layer taxonomy, spanning embodied grounding, clinical event reasoning, social negotiation, psychological sense-making, instrumental treatment, and long-horizon control. We quantify sociocognitive homophily by correlating demographic and cognitive distance matrices, and test subgroup disparities. Association-rule mining further identifies indicative metaphorical markers that tend to differentiate patient subgroups in this dataset. Beyond demographics, latent class analysis reveals adversarial, managerial, and transactional coping logics. Finally, we explain how key cognitive themes evolve with age and disease duration, highlighting shifts from metric shock to embodied burden and toward resource-based integration. Overall, the results inform the design of precision diabetes education and future cognitively matched interventions beyond demographics.

title: Emoji-Free Sarcasm Detection in Code-Mixed Text via Emotion-Polarity Divergence
authors: Falak Yusuf Malik, Pramod Bide, Sudhir N. Dhage
abstract: Detecting sarcasm in informal, code-mixed Hinglish remains a significant NLP challenge, especially without explicit cues like emojis. Existing models often miss the critical interplay between emotional expression and contextual polarity. To ad dress this, we propose the Emotion-Polarity Divergence Sarcasm Detection (EPD-SD) framework, a novel multi-task cross-lingual Transformer system. EPD-SD simultaneously predicts emotion type, intensity, and sentiment polarity, fusing these signals to detect sarcasm through their logical divergence. We evaluate the framework on a novel 5,000-sentence custom-generated dataset, and crucially, confirm its real-world generalizability on a newly curated, human-authored test set. Benchmarking against standard Transformers, a sequential Bi-LSTM, an experimental Hybrid architecture, an ablation model, a fine-tuned LLaMA 3.2-1B, and a state-of-the-art Large Language Model (LLaMA 3-8B-Instruct), our results show that the single-encoder EPD-SD model (F1: 0.8085) significantly outperforms all standard and hybrid baselines. While matching the fine-tuned LLaMA-1B, it remains highly competitive with the 8-billion parameter LLaMA 3 (F1: 0.8510), achieving these results with nearly 4.5 and 30 times fewer parameters than these respective benchmarks. This confirms that explicitly modeling emotion-polarity contradiction provides a superior, interpretable, and highly resource-efficient strategy for detecting emoji-free, code-mixed sarcasm.

title: Ultra-Flat Gain Profile With Raman Amplification Employing Combined Coherent and Incoherent Pumps
authors: Zoltán Papp, Daichi Ogata, Shigehiro Takasaka, Ryuichi Sugizaki, András Cserteg, Zoltán Várallyay
abstract: Distributed Raman amplification is essential for enhancing capacity in wideband optical networks; yet achieving an ultra-flat gain profile remains a significant design challenge. This work presents a validated numerical model incorporating both coherent pump and spectrally broad incoherent pump sources. Model accuracy was rigorously confirmed by demonstrating excellent agreement (average deviation <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< 0.1\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) between simulation and experiment across various forward, backward, and multi-directional pumping schemes. Leveraging this validated model, we employed an improved simplex optimization algorithm to target ultra-flat amplification across <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$95 \rm { nm}$</tex-math></inline-formula> C+L bands in a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$40 \rm { km}$</tex-math></inline-formula> fiber. By implementing a hybrid nine-pump scheme (four first-order incoherent and five second-order coherent pumps) to meet practical power limits, we achieved an unprecedented peak-to-peak gain flatness of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\le 0.04$</tex-math></inline-formula> dB over the 95nm bandwidth, with a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\leq 0.01\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula> standard deviation at different target gain values of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$9\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Furthermore, we demonstrate the versatility of the system in generating arbitrary gain profiles with high precision. The best performing scheme was thoroughly tested by a Monte Carlo-based stability investigation, resulting in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\leq 0.04\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula> standard deviation of gain for practical power and wavelength variations. This result confirms the superior capability of optimally configured hybrid incoherent/coherent pumping for minimizing gain ripple and enabling reconfigurable gain shaping in future high-capacity WDM systems.

title: Analysis of Carbon Reduction Strategies for ParksConsidering Dynamic Carbon Emission Factors and Energy Storage Optimization
authors: Yurui Wang, Shengjie Zhou, Yutong Wang, Qiang Fang, Ye Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Aiming at the challenge of quantifying dynamic indirect carbon emission factors at the park level, a park-level power system model is developed on the MATLAB/Simulink platform in this study, enabling the dynamic simulation of power distribution across various park components. Subsequently, the carbon emission factors of the park on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5}$</tex>-minute time scale are calculated, which reveals the dynamic propagation characteristics of carbon emissions in both spatial and temporal dimensions. Furthermore, by optimizing the operational strategy of the energy storage system, the overall carbon footprint of the park is significantly mitigated. Simulation results indicate that the proposed method can rationally quantify dynamic carbon emission factors incorporating the effects of energy storage, thereby offering a practical control strategy reference for the lowcarbon operation of parks.

title: Identification of Electricity Load Fluctuation Characteristics in the Textile and Apparel Industry Based on CatBoost Algorithm Model
authors: Zhexin Lin, Han Lin, Chenhan Zhang, Yuqing Cai, Xinying Zheng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Simply capturing the surface-level patterns of electricity load changes leads to low accuracy in identifying electricity load fluctuation characteristics. Therefore, this study investigates the identification of electricity load fluctuation characteristics in the textile and apparel industry based on the CatBoost algorithm. The CatBoost algorithm's target encoding and ordered boosting mechanism transforms high-cardinality categorical features into continuous numerical features, avoiding dimensionality explosion and overfitting. The CatBoost algorithm is then used to process categorical features. Feature importance is calculated based on the CatBoost model, and key features are selected by information gain ranking to achieve the identification of electricity load fluctuation characteristics in the textile and apparel industry. Experiments show that the proposed method significantly reduces the identification error for various load types compared to comparative methods, maintains higher accuracy stability across different feature dimensions, and improves the accuracy of electricity load fluctuation characteristic identification.

title: Hydro-Thermal Optimization of Battery Thermal Management Based on 3D Composite Variable Cross-Section Channels Under Low Pump Power Conditions
authors: Rui Zhang, Zhijun Liang, Yanxin Hu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Suppressing heat accumulation in lithium-ion batteries under extremely low coolant flow conditions is a key challenge for reducing power consumption in thermal management systems and extending the range of electric vehicles. This paper proposes a novel three-dimensional composite variable cross-section (3D-CVC) liquid cooling plate design for pouch batteries, aimed at optimizing hydro-thermal performance under limited pump power. Numerical simulations at an extremely low flow rate of 0.003 kg/s were performed to evaluate temperature distribution and pressure drop characteristics. The results show that the 3D-CVC structure effectively suppresses downstream hotspots by reconstructing local flow fields. With an extremely low pump power of approximately 0.37 mW (124.8 Pa pressure drop), the maximum contact surface temperature was reduced to 29.1° C. Compared to serpentine channels, the maximum temperature difference was reduced to 74% of the original, significantly enhancing thermal uniformity. This research demonstrates that refined 3D spatial topology optimization can effectively compensate for insufficient fluid kinetic energy at low flow rates, offering a promising solution for energy-efficient battery thermal management systems (BTMS).

title: Air Handling Unit Fault Diagnosis via Bayesian Network Parameter Optimization Under Data-Scarce Conditions
authors: Yu Du, Hua Meng, Yingjun Ruan, Dong Zhao, Yuting Yao, Tingting Xu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Fault detection and diagnosis (FDD) of air handling units (AHUs) remains challenging under data-scarce conditions. This study develops a Bayesian-network-based FDD framework using the ASHRAE RP-1312 dataset. First, model input features are pre-screened using Pearson correlation and further refined using SVD-PCA, reducing the feature subset to 10 while maintaining diagnostic accuracy and lowering model complexity. An improved expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm with a confidence-based sample screening strategy is then proposed for Bayesian network parameter optimization. Error propagation analysis shows that the proposed method suppresses the influence of low-quality samples and improves parameter estimation stability. Experimental results demonstrate that under extremely data-scarce scenarios with label missing rates of 95% and 98%, the proposed method achieves diagnostic accuracies of 88.45% and 72.88%, respectively, outperforming the standard EM algorithm by 3.56% and 7.54% and the Hard-EM algorithm by 33.7% and 24.43%. These results indicate that the proposed method provides robust AHU fault diagnosis under highly incomplete data conditions.

title: A Short-Term Multivariate Load Forecasting Model Based on ILSO-WTC-BILSTM
authors: Qingzheng Li, Kuo Ren, Xingyu Jiang, Qingde Li, Kuisheng Li, Lixin Yuan
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The collaborative management and prediction of multi-energy loads (including electricity, cooling, and heating loads) has become a core link in ensuring regional energy supplydemand balance and improving energy utilization efficiency. Therefore, achieving high-precision short-term multi-energy load prediction is of great significance for optimizing energy system dispatch strategies, enhancing energy supply reliability, and reducing system operation and energy storage configuration costs. First, grey correlation analysis and Pearson correlation coefficient analysis are used to screen the input feature data of multi-energy loads. Then, a neural network model based on WTC-BiLSTM is constructed, taking the screened feature data together with the corresponding historical multi-energy load data as inputs. Finally, an improved lion swarm algorithm with elite reverse learning is employed to optimize the hyperparameters of the WTC-BiLSTM model, with prediction accuracy and robustness as the dual optimization objectives.

title: Text-Image Guided Retrieval Network for Triple-modal Images Few-Shot Semantic Segmentation
authors: Fengshuo Chen, Liuxin Bao, Juting Miao, Chao Tao, Xiaofei Zhou
abstract: This letter presents a novel framework for triple-modal few-shot semantic segmentation involving visible, depth, and thermal modalities. While multi-modal approaches aim to provide complementary data, existing models often suffer from a heavy reliance on the visual appearance of support samples and lack effective semantic guidance during cross-modal fusion. To address these issues, we build a Text-Image Guided Retrieval Network (TIGRNet), where we leverage a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to inject semantic priors into the segmentation process. Specifically, we propose a Text Guided Fusion (TGF) module that dynamically regulates modality weights using global semantic cues, thereby suppressing noise and enhancing reliable features. Furthermore, we introduce a dual-branch retrieval strategy consisting of an Image-Guided Retrieval(IGR) module to capture visual appearance and a Text-Guided Retrieval(TGR) module to retrieve regions consistent with target semantics, reducing the burden of precise visual matching. Experiments on the VDT-2048-5<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{i}$</tex-math></inline-formula> dataset demonstrate that our TIGRNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art models in both 1-shot and 5-shot settings. The dataset and code are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/FengshuoChen/TIGRNet</uri>.

title: LiDAR Fusion-based Optimized Path Selection for Autonomous Vehicles using the Verification Model
authors: Muhammad Nawaz Khan, Sokjoon Lee, Mohsin Shah, Arshad Khan, Abdul Malik, Ajab Khan
abstract: Autonomous vehicles use LiDAR sensors to navigate 3D maps of their surroundings, enabling improved decision-making and road control systems. It utilizes laser light to measure the speed, distance, shape, direction, and perturbation of any 3D object in a point cloud. Data on point clouds is in high resolution and used to identify different objects of regular or irregular shapes. It is also used in safe path planning, path optimization, and object avoidance routes. Although point cloud data is robust and beneficial for safe navigation, errors and inconsistencies in the interpretation lead to erroneous judgments. Processing wrong information potentially resulting in hazardous circumstances with severe consequences of casualties and road accidents. In contrast, each path planning and path optimization policy should be thoroughly tested and validated before being applied to real-world scenarios. To provide a safer and collision-free path and to verify optimized path policy, we have proposed a technique called ”LiDAR Fusion-based Optimized Path Selection (LOPS) for Autonomous Vehicles using the Verification Model.” The proposed method uses an object detection strategy and instructs autonomous vehicles to use optimized paths for safe travel and collision-free path selection. The proposed scheme is formally modeled and verified for defined states in UPPAAL, and each state with defined properties has been checked. LOPS has been evaluated with different parameters, including the jerking rate, which ranges from 1.0% to 0.06% in various densities of autonomous vehicles. We have also calculated the effect of optimization of the path on the distance covered in unit time, and it is found that 50 vehicles require 20% more time to cover 50 km than 10, 5, or 2 vehicles. The lane usage is calculated in unit time and shows that using optimization in the lane scenario, lane 2 is 5-19 times more used in 5 minutes. LOPS is evaluated with two datasets, CARLA and NuScenes for different parameters, including path length, path smoothness, object avoidance success rate, path tracking accuracy, and decision delay comparisons.

title: Low-cost Parallel Transmission for Dense Indoor Data Collection with LoRaWAN: Time Synchronization and Resource Allocation
authors: Junxiao Liu, Xinyu Fan, Luping Xiang, Kun Yang
abstract: LoRaWAN is a compelling low-cost solution for large-scale indoor Internet of Things (IoT) data backhaul, owing to its strong penetration capability and low power consumption. However, its default pure ALOHA access mechanism leads to severe channel contention, substantial packet loss, and reduced throughput under dense, concurrent transmissions. To overcome this, we propose a lightweight out-of-band (OOB) synchronization scheme that integrates a time division multiple access (TDMA) mechanism into commercial LoRaWAN Class A networks. Unlike approaches requiring gateway scheduling, frequent downlink signaling, or custom hardware, our method introduces a single low-cost node providing millisecond-level alignment via a dedicated OOB synchronization channel. End devices seamlessly access this channel by briefly retuning their existing LoRa transceivers. Consequently, the scheme imposes zero downlink overhead during the steady-state reporting phase, requires no hardware modifications to gateways or end devices, and remains fully backward-compatible. This design enables collision-free scheduled channel access within the configured nominal resource capacity, thereby improving throughput and reducing contention. Real-world experiments using an indoor positioning prototype demonstrate that the proposed TDMA-LoRaWAN architecture improves system throughput by over 30% and reduces the packet loss rate from 25.8% to 5.02% in a 20-node indoor deployment. Furthermore, large-scale simulations corroborate these empirical findings, support the scalability analysis under larger network sizes, and indicate improved energy efficiency per successful packet in dense network settings. These combined results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach for dense indoor IoT data collection and indicate its practical potential under high uplink reporting demands.

title: Deep Learning Framework for Breast Tumor Detection and Segmentation Using CT Imaging
authors: Zainab Pervaiz, Tallat Jabeen, Ruqaiyya Adil, Muhammad Waleed Khan, Aaysha Tariq, Muhammad Irfan Zafar, Ishrat Jabeen
abstract: Breast cancer remains the leading malignancy among women globally, yet AI-based detection systems focus predominantly on mammography while whole-body CT imaging is routinely performed for oncological staging thus, remains critically underexplored. Existing CT-based studies are limited by small datasets, geographical bias, and lack of spatial localization capabilities. This study introduces a radiologist-verified CT scan dataset of 9330 annotated slices from three Pakistani hospitals, approved by institutional ethics committees. Data was partitioned into training (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%) sets to develop a two-stage deep learning framework. The training data includes 6,106 whole-body CT slices (3,053 tumor, 3,053 normal). The perfectly balanced training dataset eliminates class-level imbalance, while detection-level imbalance is addressed through distributional focal loss mechanisms. We systematically evaluated seven transfer learning architectures (VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50V2, InceptionV3, DenseNet121, DenseNet201, EfficientNetB0–B5), revealing that VGG16 achieved optimal performance (82% accuracy, 0.80 AUC-ROC) compared to deeper architectures (ResNet50V2: 49%, EfficientNet: 50%), demonstrating that simpler models exhibit better inductive bias for constrained medical imaging datasets. Building upon this, we developed an integrated YOLOv11-Seg framework exploiting architectural innovations: C3K2 bottleneck modules for hierarchical feature extraction, Spatial Pyramid Pooling Fast (SPPF) for multi-scale context aggregation, and Pixel Spatial Attention (C2PSA) for enhanced boundary delineation. YOLOv11-Seg achieved 84.6% precision, 90% recall, 0.84 mAP@50, 0.839 Dice coefficient, and 0.724 mean IoU. Error analysis revealed false negatives (3.83%) primarily from low-contrast lesions and small tumors (<8 mm), while false positives (6.3%) resulted from fibrous tissue misclassification. Real-time video inference on >10,000 frames demonstrated 10 FPS processing, 93.1% sensitivity, 97.9% specificity, and 94.3% temporal consistency, enabling clinical workflow integration. Limitations of the current study include single-radiologist annotation, geographical homogeneity, retrospective design, and binary classification scope, warranting prospective multi-center validation, multi-class classification extension, and explainable AI development. This work establishes foundation for AI-assisted opportunistic breast cancer screening in whole-body CT imaging, particularly valuable for resource-constrained settings, with clear pathways toward comprehensive staging and diagnostic support systems.

title: Model Analysis and Research of Equivalent Capacitance of Multi-segment Induction Coil in Aviation Electromagnetic Measurement System
authors: Hongzhen Yang, Xinyu Cao
abstract: In aviation electromagnetic measurement systems, the performance of induction coils as core sensors directly determines the quality of exploration data. Existing coil parameter models exhibit limited fidelity to physical structures, resulting in irregular deviations in the calculated parameters and making it difficult to effectively guide engineering design. This study proposes a segmented coil capacitance parameter analytical model that accurately reconstructs the true winding structure of coil skeletons. The model comprehensively considers the impacts of winding techniques, mechanical stress, and interlayer misalignment structures on electrical parameters. Comparative analysis with experimental data demonstrates the model's practical applicability and accuracy. Systematic performance evaluations of key coil structural parameters provide theoretical foundations for designing high-quality induction coils with broadband and low-distortion reception characteristics. These advancements significantly shorten R&D cycles for airborne electromagnetic measurement systems while delivering robust technical support for aviation electromagnetic measurement operations.

title:
C
                    <sup>3</sup>
                    DG: Conditional Domain Generalization for Hyperspectral Imagery Classification with Convergence and Constrained-risk Theories
authors: Zhe Gao, Bin Pan, Zhenwei Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral imagery (HSI) classification faces the challenge of hyperspectral-monospectra, where distinct classes exhibit similar spectral signatures. The spatial-spectral approach is a common solution but often inflates accuracy due to test pixels overlapping with training patches. While domain generalization shows promise in addressing hyperspectral-monospectra, it still struggles to distinguish spectrally similar samples from different domains. In this work, we address hyperspectral-monospectra using spectral information alone and propose a Convergence- and Error-Constrained Conditional Domain Generalization method (C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>DG) for HSI classification. The main contributions of this work are twofold: (1) the Conditional Revising Inference Block (CRIB), and (2) the theoretical guarantees on model convergence and generalization error. CRIB forms the core of our method, employing a shared encoder and multi-branch decoders to fully leverage conditional label distributions during training. This design achieves structural decoupling aligned with the generation mechanisms of HSI. To ensure stable optimization and bounded generalization error, we establish an optimization convergence theorem and a risk upper bound theorem. The convergence theorem shows that the gradients of the loss components consistently align, ensuring stable model optimization. The risk bound theorem establishes a concrete error bound by connecting test-time training with recent developments in sharpness-aware minimization. Experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate the superior performance of C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>DG. The code is available at github.com/Lab-PANbin/C3DG.

title: CSEM3DINV: A Scalable 3-D parallel inversion framework for multi-source CSEM data
authors: Jian Li, Xulong Wang, Xiaoyan Zhao, Rongwen Guo, Yasuo Ogawa, Pingyang Fan, Tianyu Guo, Jingdao Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In this article, we present a MATLAB-based three-dimensional (3-D) controlled-source electromagnetic (CSEM) inversion and interpretation framework that couples an efficient forward/adjoint modelling engine with a source–frequency parallelization (SFP) strategy. A checkerboard model and a step–thrust fold model are used to evaluate the impacts of source configuration, data type, parallelization, and modelling method. Notably, data–type selection is critical: incorporating the Hz component markedly suppresses artifacts and improves resistivity recovery, whereas adding Hy provides little or no additional benefit. Compared with single-wire-source (SWS) inversions, the proposed orthogonal-wire-source (OWS) inversions improve resolution without compromising convergence and may even accelerate it. Across all OWS data types, spurious artifacts are markedly suppressed. In terms of computational efficiency, SFP reduces the inversion time to 57%–58% of that under frequency parallelization (FP), and replacing divergence correction (DC) with a regularized grad–div (GD) constraint further lowers the runtime to 53%–60%. Overall, SFP with GD achieves a speedup of 1.8–2.3, with nearly identical data fits and inverted models. The proposed framework is practical for large-scale 3-D CSEM inversion involving multiple sources, frequencies, and data type, and can be extended to other applications, such as joint magnetotelluric and CSEM inversion.

title: Two-Stage Optimization for Multi-Objective Dynamic Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows under Uncertainty_supp1-3696098.pdf
authors: Zhenan He
abstract: As a fundamental optimization problem in real-world logistics and transportation, the Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (VRPTW) typically faces uncertainty in the operational process (e.g., travel times and demands), as well as dynamically arriving customer requests. Pursuing robustness requires preserving sufficient buffers to absorb uncertainties, but inserting new customer requests inevitably consumes these buffers, which means the adaptability is achieved at the cost of robustness. Conversely, strictly maintaining robustness will reduce possible insertion positions that satisfy this requirement, thereby restricting adaptability to dynamic customer requests. Unfortunately, existing methods typically deal with both challenges separately, ignoring their inherent conflict and thus failing to achieve a balance between robustness and adaptability. To address this issue, this paper formulates a two-stage mathematical model for the Multi-Objective Dynamic VRPTW under Uncertainty, including a robust stage and a dynamic stage. To solve the model, a Two-Stage Optimization algorithm is developed. The robust stage employs a dual-population mechanism with a novel local search strategy to generate a high-quality and proactively flexible initial plan. During the dynamic stage, a rapid response procedure is proposed to efficiently accommodate new requests. Extensive experiments on benchmark and real-world problems demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms competitive state-of-the-art algorithms in terms of robustness and solution optimality.

title: LLM-Guided Multiobjective Optimization for Physically-Plausible and Diverse 3D Human Motion Generation
authors: Xu Gui, Hua Yu, Dongsheng Zhou, Yaqing Hou
abstract: Text-driven 3D human motion generation aims to synthesize diverse motion sequences that are semantically aligned with textual descriptions. Most existing generative approaches encode the entire sentence into a single semantic embedding, which inevitably compromises the semantic consistency of the generated motions. Furthermore, in long sequences, the lack of explicit physical constraints often leads to physically implausible motions, particularly in the later segments. To address these limitations, we propose LMPGen, a novel framework that formulates text-driven 3D human motion generation as a multiobjective optimization problem. Without introducing additional parameters, LMPGen enhances both the diversity and quality of the generated motions. Leveraging the semantic understanding capabilities of a large language model (LLM), we slice the input text into fine-grained action segments, each guiding the construction of semantically grounded objective functions. To effectively balance conflicting objectives, we adopt an evolutionary algorithm (EA) augmented by a pretrained generative model to initialize and guide population evolution. Each action segment serves as a semantic anchor, steering the search process toward diverse yet semantically faithful motion sequences. Furthermore, we incorporate physical criterion into the optimization process to ensure that the generated motions are smooth, stable, and physically plausible. Experimental results on mainstream human motion datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches in terms of both semantic consistency and diversity, providing a robust solution for generating high-quality, text-aligned human motion sequences.

title: DCT: Dimensional Collapse Tiling for Tensor Operators on Attention Accelerators
authors: Lei Xu, Chen Yin, Zelong Yuan, Yuxuan Huang, Lifu Cheng, Qin Wang, Jianfei Jiang, Naifeng Jing
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: On accelerators, data reuse is critical, prompting optimal tensor operator dataflow analyses. However, conventional tensor dataflow analyses mainly focus on square-shaped tensors, whereas in attention-based models, especially in long sequence tasks, tensors often exhibit narrow-and-long shapes, necessitating analyses that differ from conventional approaches. In this study, we first identify the guiding principles inherent in conventional dataflow optimizations. Then we propose dimensional collapse tiling (DCT), a novel tensor tiling scheme to exploit data reuse in narrow-and-long tensors under limited buffer sizes. We introduce the DCT method and explain why DCT is superior in dataflow optimization by using attainable data movement analysis. DCT is well-suited for the latest fused tensor operator dataflows and can establish a novel inter-operator dataflow that enables efficient fusion of multiple operators connected at any length. Finally, we broaden the scope of dataflow exploration by incorporating DCT and develop an exploration framework that supports operator fusion, aiming to identify the optimal dataflow on spatial accelerators with a given on-chip buffer size. Experimental results show that DCT delivers 4.69×, 1.99×, and 2.12× speedup and 55.3%, 24.7%, and 16.3% energy savings on average for tiny, edge, and cloud accelerators, respectively, compared with the state-of-the-art dataflow FLAT.

title: A Robust Synchronization Method for Enhancing Stability of IBR-Dominated Grids
authors: Huawei Yuan, Balarko Chaudhuri, Siew-Chong Tan, Shu Yuen Ron Hui
abstract: Inverter-based resources (IBRs) operating in grid following (GFL) mode are particularly vulnerable to instabilities in weak grid conditions. This is largely due to the inherent limitations of synchronization with a phase locked loop (PLL). This paper presents a novel voltage magnitude-based synchronization (VMSync) method that ensures stable and well damped response even in very weak grid conditions, without compromising dynamic performance or adding any stabilization control components. Through comprehensive frequency- and time-domain analysis, VMSync is shown to outperform conventional synchronous reference frame PLL (SRF-PLL) for both small- and large-signal disturbances. Systems with multiple GFL inverters employing VMSync demonstrate significantly improved damping and stability. Furthermore, mixed deployment of VMSync alongside legacy SRF-PLL-based GFL inverters exhibits superior dynamic performance compared to deployment of solely SRF-PLL-based GFL inverters. The results highlight the potential of VMSync as a robust synchronization strategy to mitigate PLL-driven instabilities in power grids with high IBR penetration.

title: Hybrid p-n Junction/Ohmic Gate p-GaN HEMT with Enhanced Threshold Voltage Stability and Large Gate Swing
authors: Kuo Zhang, Kai Liu, Chong Wang, Yuhan Luo, Jiahui Ren, Wentao Zhang, Yunlong He, Xuefeng Zheng, Xiaohua Ma, Yue Hao
abstract: This work proposes a hybrid p-n junction/ohmic gate p-GaN HEMT (HPNO-HEMT) that incorporates spaced ohmic contact regions to p-GaN within the stacked n-GaN/p-GaN junction gate. This central ohmic/p-GaN gate regions acts as the charge release paths, suppressing the charge storage effect in the p-GaN layer and thereby significantly improving threshold voltage (VTH) stability under both gate and drain stress. Furthermore, under high gate bias, the depletion regions from the flanking p-n junctions extend laterally and deplete the central p-GaN layer, thereby suppressing the dominant gate leakage path. As a result, the HPNO-HEMT achieves low gate leakage, a high gate breakdown voltage of 13.5 V, and excellent gate reliability. The proposed structure successfully breaks the typical trade-off by simultaneously achieving stable VTH and a large gate swing, offering a promising design strategy for p-GaN HEMTs in power electronics applications.

title: Operationalizing Urgent Computing for Wildland Fire: A Case for Readiness
authors: Ilkay Altintas
abstract: Wildfire management is a persistent, time-critical, and data-intensive challenge that includes emergency response, strategic planning, and long-term landscape management. Urgent computing has traditionally emphasized rapid allocation of scalable computing resources during disasters, but speed alone is insufficient. In wildfire contexts, readiness for sustained integration of sciencedriven tools with operational decision making is equally critical. This paper reframes urgent computing for wildland fire as a readiness problem enabled by composable cyberinfrastructure that can be continuously exercised, adapted, and reused across the full wildfire lifecycle. It argues that enabling platforms, powered by urgent computing, are essential for such readiness. Drawing on the Wildfire Science and Technology Commons, the paper presents three contrasting examples: Firemap for real-time fire response, BurnPro3D for prescribed fire planning, and Points2Pano for continuous vegetation analysis. These examples demonstrate how composability across data, models, workflows, and compute resources supports both emergency response and mission-critical planning, enabling operational readiness.

title: A Study on the Modeling of Energy-Intensive Industrial Loads and Their Control Potential in Production Processes
authors: Xiang Zhang, Kejian Shi, Hengyu Liu, Zhaochen Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Energy-intensive industrial loads, especially in the steel industry, have high electricity use and production processes that can be closely controlled. These features make them important resources for demand-side regulation in modern power systems. This paper focuses on short-process steel production, using a resource-task network to describe how materials and equipment are linked between processes. It builds physics-based power models for electric arc furnaces and rolling mills, creates a multidimensional index to evaluate regulatory potential, and uses the Grey Relational Degree-Approximation to the Optimal Solution (GRA-TOPSIS) method to measure this potential in typical loads. Simulation results show the proposed model captures both power fluctuations and the sequence constraints in steel production, providing a theoretical foundation for grid operators to use industrial load resources.

title: Wind Power Forecasting via MSTL Decomposition and Complexity-Driven Dual-Branch Deep Learning
authors: Siyu Ge, Xin Guo, Xinyu Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the limited prediction accuracy caused by the strong stochasticity of wind power and its multi-scale seasonal fluctuations, this paper proposes a dual-branch deep learning model (MSTL-TCN-TR) that integrates Multiple Seasonal-Trend decomposition. The model employs MSTL to decompose the power time series into trend, seasonal, and residual components, the decomposed components are subsequently evaluated and partitioned into stationary-regular and high-frequency stochastic subsequences. Based on this classification, a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) is adopted to efficiently extract local dependencies from low-predictability stationary sequences, while a Transformer (TR) is employed to deeply decode the long-range nonlinear correlations of high-predictability abrupt-change sequences. Experiments on real wind farm data demonstrate that the proposed model is acutely sensitive to transient mutation characteristics, achieving an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> of up to 0.9889, with RMSE and MAE reduced to 65.50 MW and 45.31 MW, respectively. The prediction accuracy and robustness significantly outperform conventional hybrid models, providing effective support for high-precision wind power forecasting under complex meteorological conditions.

title: STWFormer: Spatial-Temporal Sparse Transformer with Adaptive Gating for Multi-Location Wind Power Forecasting
authors: Hongda Jin
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Accurate short-term wind power forecasting is essential for reliable grid integration, energy market optimization, and operational scheduling of renewable power systems. However, existing deep learning approaches for wind power prediction face three critical limitations: (1) conventional graphbased models rely on static spatial topologies that fail to capture the dynamic wake interactions among turbines under varying wind regimes; (2) standard Transformer architectures suffer from quadratic computational complexity, making them impractical for large-scale wind farms with high-resolution temporal data; and (3) most models neglect the complementary information embedded in multi-height meteorological measurements, leading to suboptimal feature utilization. To address these challenges, we propose STWFormer (Spatial-Temporal Wind Power Transformer), a novel framework that integrates three key innovations: (1) a spatial-temporal sparse attention mechanism that restricts each token to attend only to topologically adjacent nodes and temporally local windows, reducing complexity from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{O}\left(L^{2}\right)$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{O}(L \cdot w \cdot|\overline{\mathcal{N}}|)$</tex>; (2) an adaptive gating module that dynamically balances attention-enhanced representations against residual signals based on wind condition stability; and (3) a dualheight fusion encoding strategy that jointly embeds 10 m and 100 m meteorological features with periodic temporal encodings tailored to diurnal and seasonal wind patterns. Extensive experiments on four real-world wind farm datasets demonstrate that STWFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing MAE by up to 1.34 % and RMSE by up to 2.31 % compared to nine competitive baselines, while maintaining superior computational efficiency.

title: Physical-Data Dual-Driven Method for Power Scheduling Auxiliary Decision: PDEVR Closed-Loop Framework with Constrained Large Language Model
authors: Dayong Xu, Weixin Chen, Yuqian Fang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: As novel power systems featuring high penetration of renewable energy and power-electronic devices continue to expand rapidly, the complexity of grid operation risks has increased significantly. Traditional scheduling auxiliary decision methods often struggle with slow computation speeds and inconsistent preplan quality. Although existing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N}-1 / \mathrm{N}-1-1$</tex> contingency analysis algorithms can accurately identify high-risk scenarios through detailed physical modeling, they continue to face the persistent “last-mile” challenge: converting numerical results into executable preplans that dispatchers can directly apply. In this paper, we propose a physical-data dual-driven framework that tightly integrates N-1/N-1-1 fault anticipation at the physical layer with a constrained large language model at the data-driven layer, enabling automatic preplan generation. Our core innovation positions the LLM as a “physically constrained decision expressor” rather than an independent physical-state reasoner. We introduce a Perception-Decision-Execution-Verification-Review (PDEVR) closed-loop framework that formalizes verification and review as independent modules. For domain adaptation, we employ layered LoRA together with curriculum learning and physics-informed augmentation. Additionally, our LLM + RAG dual verification integrates over 200 regulations and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5000+$</tex> historical cases, achieving 99.9% compliance in tested scenarios. Experiments conducted on a real 200 -node provincial grid show that our method delivers <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8.75 \times$</tex> faster preplan generation time, 98 % completeness, and 94 % accuracy. Overall, we believe this approach opens a promising new paradigm for intelligent dispatching.

title: Low-Communication Adaptive Volt/Var Control for PV-Rich Distribution Networks
authors: AoJie Hu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: High photovoltaic penetration is transforming distribution grids from passive networks into active distribution systems, where operational scheduling must jointly coordinate voltage and reactive power in real time in addition to balancing active power. Under fast load and PV fluctuations and limited sensing and communication resources, centralized Volt/VAR optimization is often difficult to deploy, while fixed Volt/VAR settings may become either insufficient for voltage regulation or overly aggressive in reactive power actuation across operating conditions. This paper addresses distribution operation scheduling with inverter-based reactive power support and proposes a low-communication adaptive Volt/VAR control scheme. Using only local inverter measurements, the method online identifies the local voltage-reactive response and self-tunes the control aggressiveness, while enforcing capability and smoothing constraints to ensure implementability and stable actuation. Closed-loop power-flow simulations on standard distribution test feeders with representative daily load and PV profiles show that the proposed approach reduces voltage violations without requiring system-wide state estimation and yields more robust performance across operating conditions in terms of reactive power usage and actuation frequency compared with fixed Volt/VAR strategies.

title: A 6–16 GHz Reflectionless Downconverter Using Fast-Settling Digital-Coarse Feed-Forward AGC
authors: Qingxian Li, Bingzheng Yang, Aoran Han, Yiyang Shu, Xun Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a 6–16 GHz downconverter featuring precise gain control and short settling time, enabled by the digital-coarse feed-forward automatic gain control (DCF-AGC) and the reflectionless IF technique. To improve the gain control accuracy within a wide control range, the DCF-AGC incorporates three parallel-switching AGC (PS-AGC) modules for coarse gain control and one feed-forward AGC (FF-AGC) for fine gain control. Meanwhile, the DCF-AGC enables a short settling time for wideband signals. An absorptive IF buffer is used to mitigate in-band intermodulation (IM) interference of the downconverter, thereby ensuring the flatness of the wideband gain. The clean spectrum obtained by the reflectionless IF technique reduces the linearity error of the detector, which consequently enhances the precision of AGC. In addition, a wideband amplifier incorporating a linearity-enhanced (LE) active inductor is implemented in the proposed AGC. Verified in a conventional 40-nm bulk CMOS technology, the proposed downconverter achieves a dB-linear gain range of 40.5 dB (−9.5~31 dB) with a gain error of less than ±0.25 dB. It exhibits a noise figure (NF) of 11.4 dB and an IIP3 of 12.8 dBm, with a baseband bandwidth of 3 GHz. Meanwhile, the settling time of the proposed downconverter is 42.4~75.9 ns. Benefited from the proposed architecture, the downconverter supports 3 Gb/s 64-QAM modulation and 8-channel 4.8 Gb/s 64-QAM carrier aggregation (CA) modulation, which is attractive for 5G-A/6G wireless and satellite communication (SATCOM) applications.

title: PCB-Embedded SiC MOSFET Packaging Using Shield to Suppress Near-Field Magnetic Radiation
authors: Zifan Li, Wei Liu, Yixuan Wang, Laili Wang, K. T. Chau
abstract: Wide-bandgap semiconductor printed circuit board (PCB)-embedded packaging offers a small size and low parasitic parameters, potentially improving the power density and switching performance of power electronic converters. However, due to the high switching frequency and fast speed of wide-bandgap semiconductors, coupled with the high device density of PCB-embedded packaging, reducing electromagnetic interference poses a critical challenge. This paper newly proposes a shielded PCB-embedded packaging that utilizes a copper layer in the PCB to shield the near-field magnetic radiation induced by silicon carbide devices. Finite element analysis shows that the proposed shielded PCB-embedded packaging reduces magnetic field interference significantly by 96.2% compared with an unshielded PCB-embedded packaging.

title: Optimization Analysis of Core Loss and Rotor Eddy Current Loss in Aircraft Excitation Built-In Hybrid Excitation Brushless Machine
authors: Longxin Du, Zhuoran Zhang, Chen Wang
abstract: An excitation built-in hybrid excitation brushless machine (EBI-HEBM) is investigated as a brushless solution for aircraft high-voltage dc power systems. The structural characteristics and operating principles of the EBI-HEBM are introduced, and the relevant calculation formulas for losses are provided. Then, the loss optimization under high-speed conditions is analyzed. The PM rotor adopts a segmented design with 6 segments per pole and 10 segments per axial section to reduce eddy current losses; the loss of the FE rotor is concentrated near the air-gap surface of the claw pole and increases with the increase of field current and armature current. The stator selects a yoke thickness of 15 mm to balance core loss and power density, and the core loss is the lowest when the slot width is 1.1 mm. To verify the design effectiveness, a 90 kW prototype is manufactured and tested. The test results are in good agreement with the finite element analysis results.

title: DRoPE: Directional Rotary Position Embedding for Efficient Agent Interaction Modeling
authors: Jianbo Zhao, Taiyu Ban, Zhihao Liu, Hangning Zhou, Xiyang Wang, Qibin Zhou, Lei Liu, Bin Li
abstract: Accurate and efficient modeling of agent interactions is essential for trajectory generation in autonomous driving. Existing methods, however, present an “impossible triangle,” failing to simultaneously optimize accuracy, computational time, and memory efficiency. To break this limitation, we first pro pose adapting Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) from natural language processing to improve relative position encoding in the autonomous driving domain. RoPE's design effectively resolves the time and space complexity overhead of prior query-centric methods. However, we identify that RoPE, while effective for spatial positions, inherently fails to model agent headings due to the periodicity of angular information. To solve this, we propose Directional Rotary Position Embedding (DRoPE), a novel and theoretically-grounded adaptation designed specifically for angles. DRoPE introduces a uniform identity scalar into the 2D rotary transformation, creating a principled mapping that correctly and naturally encodes relative angular information. Our final framework employs a hybrid strategy: using the original RoPE for spatial positions and our proposed DRoPE for agent headings. This combined approach is the first query-centric method to achieve optimal efficiency. Empirical evaluations against state-of-the-art models confirm our framework's practical effectiveness, achieving comparable performance while offering significant computational and memory advantages. The video documentation is available at https://drope-traj.github.io/.

title: Correlation-Driven Extended Target Detection in the Frequency Domain: High-Speed Waveform Design
authors: Yongqiang Zhang, Zhiyong Song, Yifu Hu, Ping Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Radar systems are confronted with increasingly demanding performance requirements, where waveform design stands as a pivotal approach to enhancing their capabilities. Conventional waveform design methods, which rely on fixed prior knowledge, suffer from significant performance degradation when dealing with high-speed dynamic targets due to the rapid variations in their frequency-domain responses. To address this issue, this paper proposes an adaptive waveform design algorithm. The core idea of the algorithm is to leverage the strong correlation of the target frequency-domain responses between adjacent radar pulses. By employing a Kalman filter to track response variations in real time, the algorithm achieves dynamic waveform-target matching. Simulations and measurements with real data demonstrate that, compared to traditional methods, the proposed algorithm effectively improves detection probability and robustness, offering a feasible solution to address the challenges in modern radar detection.

title: Asymmetric Antenna for Mobile Platforms with Common-Mode Current Suppression
authors: Mengyao Hu, Zibin Weng, Xingbao Lin, Yihong Qi, Yong-Chang Jiao
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: An asymmetric antenna for mobile platforms is proposed, which introduces a novel application of the self-balancing balun concept to asymmetric structures for effective common-mode current (CMC) suppression. The antenna adopts a three‑element structure and realizes CMC suppression using the self‑balancing balun principle based on the voltage‑zero point. Quantitative evaluation of the suppression effect is performed within the operating band. Experimental results show that the prototype antenna achieves stable impedance matching over the 5.76–7.16 GHz band (|S₁₁| < −10 dB) and significantly suppresses CMC across the entire operating band. This work provides a reliable and novel CMC suppression scheme for asymmetric mobile platform antennas.

title: Multiple Optical Waveguide Tactile Sensor for Differentiating Micro- and Macro-Scale Tangential Displacements
authors: Lionel Fliegans, Sylvain Blayac, Marc Ramuz
abstract: In an era where robotics rapidly advances toward ubiquitous integration, achieving human-like tactile perception remains a central challenge. Tactile sensing extends beyond simple contact detection, enabling robots to intuitively interact with complex and dynamic environments. This paper presents the design of flexible, stretchable optical waveguide-based sensors. By embedding light-emitting diodes into waveguides and leveraging frustrated total internal reflection, multi-guide optical sensors convert mechanical deformations into optical responses to applied pressure with high fidelity. This work focuses specifically on distinguishing between normal and tangential applied forces in a multi–optical-waveguide sensor. We have demonstrated their use in a fully stretchable matrix design, which allows for higher spatial resolution and the capability to detect both normal and shear forces. These advancements pave the way for next-generation robotic and prosthetic systems with refined perception, adaptability, and dexterity.

title: Schrödinger Bridges of Averaged Systems
authors: Daniel Owusu Adu, Yongxin Chen
abstract: We consider a Schrödinger bridge problem where the Markov process is subject to parameter perturbations, forming an ensemble of systems. Our objective is to steer this ensemble from the initial distribution to the final distribution using controls robust to the parameter perturbations. Utilizing the path integral formalism, we demonstrate that the optimal control is a non-Markovian strategy, specifically a stochastic feedforward control, which depends on past and present noise. This unexpected deviation from established strategies for Schrödinger bridge problems highlights the intricate interrelationships present in the system's dynamics. From the perspective of optimal transport, a significant by-product of our work is the demonstration that, when the evolution of a distribution is subject to parameter perturbations, it is possible to robustly deform the distribution to a desired final state using stochastic feedforward controls.

title: Reshaping Wireless Channel using UAV Swarms: A User-Centric Approach with Practical Constraints
authors: Fatma Gamal, Mohamed AbdelRaheem, Mohamed Atef, Mahmoud Elsaadany
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper proposes a user-centric cell-free massive MIMO framework supported by cooperative unmanned aerial vehicle swarms with adaptive three-dimensional deployment. By leveraging controlled UAV mobility, the system actively reshapes the air-to-ground propagation environment to enhance spectral efficiency, user fairness, and energy efficiency under realistic mobility, power, and kinematic constraints. A joint UAV placement, mobility, and energy-aware deployment optimization is formulated that incorporates user mobility, altitude bounds, and propulsion energy consumption. To address the resulting non-convex and time-varying problem, we propose a family of genetic-inspired algorithms, namely: a memorized constrained genetic-algorithm that ensures smooth, kinematically feasible UAV adaptation; a power-aware user-centric genetic-algorithm that selectively repositions only UAVs serving the worst-performing users to reduce energy consumption; and a multi-objective genetic-algorithm that balances communication performance and tracking accuracy. Simulation results show that the proposed methods achieve near-optimal spectral efficiency while reducing total UAV energy consumption by up to 65-75% compared to baseline genetic-algorithm approaches, without degrading minimum user rate guarantees.

title: Distributed Triangulation Filter for Target Motion Estimation via Bearing-Only Measurements_supp1-3695606.mp4
authors: Xuyang Gao
abstract: This paper introduces the novel consensus-based distributed spatial-temporal coordinated triangulation filter (CD-STCTF), a unified framework designed to overcome the fundamental challenge of achieving reliable and high-precision motion estimation of non-cooperative targets using bearing-only measurements in multi-agent systems. First, our approach centers on the spatial-temporal coordinated triangulation (STCT) model, which optimally integrates historical and spatial bearing information to transform the nonlinear problem into a robust, pseudo-linear state estimation problem. Second, this powerful geometric linearization is embedded within a consensus-on-information-matrix distributed architecture, ensuring robust operation and network consistency across multi-agent systems with sparse communication topologies. Third, a key technical feature is the incorporation of a dynamic measurement noise covariance matrix, which correctly models the distance-dependent uncertainty in angular measurements. Furthermore, we prove the global bounded stability of the CD-STCTF dynamic error. Simulation and real-world experimental results against existing distributed algorithms and the centralized Kalman filter (CKF) benchmark demonstrate that the CD-STCTF achieves superior estimation accuracy, faster convergence rates, and enhanced robustness and is comparable to the CKF, establishing its efficacy for high-performance cooperative bearing-only target motion estimation.

title: A Fusion Algorithm for Ground Roll Suppression Methods based on Adversarial Matching Filtering
authors: Ning Wang, Siyuan Chen, Zhiwei Li, Wei Shi, Xingyu Liu
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: While existing ground roll suppression methods have respective strengths, a single method often struggles to achieve both accurate suppression and primary reflections preservation. To address this issue, we have proposed a fusion algorithm based on the adversarial matching filtering (AMF) to integrate the strengths of ground roll suppression methods. The algorithm constructs the joint inversion equation under the framework of morphological component analysis (MCA), with dual inputs: the primary reflection and noise gathers obtained from two different ground roll suppression methods. The matching filters for both components are calculated by small-scale matrix inversion. Regularization weights are introduced to control the similarity ratios of the primary reflections and the noise with respect to the input data. The “adversarial” interaction between the primary reflections and the noise enables optimal matching during the calculation of their filters. This algorithm can serve as a secondary optimization of the existing noise suppression methods, which is designed to preserve the complementary strengths while mitigating the limitations of different methods. Tests on both synthetic model and field seismic data validate that the proposed algorithm can enhance the suppression accuracy and preserve primary reflections.

title: Seeking Safety From Uncertainty: Probabilistic Vessel Trajectory Prediction With a Flow-Based Generative Model
authors: Kaisen Yang, Yuxu Lu, Dong Yang
abstract: Vessel trajectory prediction remains a critical focus in maritime safety, serving as a pivotal component in vessel traffic service and maritime autonomous surface ship. However, unmeasurable factors, such as human intentions and vessel failure, pose challenges to the reliability of predictions, particularly in multivessel encounters. In response to this, we propose the sequence generative flow (SGlow), a flow-based deep generative model that describes future motions of the vessels with a multimodal probability distribution. It mitigates navigation uncertainty by multiple probabilistic inferences, mapping a single observation to multiple possible predictions. By combining sequential modeling with flow-based distribution learning, SGlow provides more reliable and comprehensive trajectory forecasts for maritime risk warning and navigation decision-making. Compared to existing deep generative models, SGlow excels in multimodal prediction of vessel trajectory with two distinct advantages: first, precise modeling of trajectory distributions through exact log-likelihood optimization, and second, a mathematically traceable learning process that aligns with trajectory evolution dynamics. Experimental results show that SGlow achieves favorable performance compared with 11 benchmark deep learning models in both prediction accuracy and plausibility, enhancing maritime operations safety.

title: Interpretable Undergraduate Dropout Prediction via Explainable Boosting and Gaussian Hidden Markov Models
authors: Edson José Pacheco, Carlos Marcelo Pedroso
abstract: Student dropout remains a persistent challenge in higher education, particularly within engineering and technology undergraduate programs. Although several machine learning approaches achieve high predictive accuracy, their inherent lack of interpretability often constrains pedagogical teams from designing and justifying evidence-based interventions. To address this issue, this paper presents a model that integrates Explainable Boosting Machines (EBM) for cross-sectional risk scoring with Gaussian Hidden Markov Models (G-HMM) for temporal trajectory modeling. The EBM component produces inherently interpretable shape functions to quantify nonlinear relationships between academic indicators and dropout risk. Complementarily, the G-HMM captures transition dynamics by modeling the temporal evolution of these scores across five latent states. The experimental evaluation was conducted using a 15-year dataset from an electrical engineering program. Starting from an initial set of 71 academic indicators, a multi-phase selection algorithm identifies the most discriminative features for each program, ensuring model parsimony and performance. Based on these selected features, the EBM achieved an F1-score of 97.81% with 100% sensitivity. The G-HMM complemented this result by modeling temporal trajectories across five latent states, achieving a mean detection lead time of 2.60 semesters prior to dropout. Unlike traditional baselines, the proposed methodology generates interpretable early-warning signals that facilitate the design and monitoring of targeted interventions. This approach enables institutions to quantify the impact of corrective actions across successive academic cycles.

title: Risk-Aware Rulebooks for Multi-Objective Trajectory Evaluation under Uncertainty
authors: Tichakorn Wongpiromsarn
abstract: We present a risk-aware formalism for evaluating system trajectories in the presence of uncertain interactions between the system and its environment. The proposed formalism supports reasoning under uncertainty and systematically handles complex relationships among requirements and objectives, including hierarchical priorities and non-comparability. Rather than treating the environment as exogenous noise, we explicitly model how each system trajectory influences the environment and evaluate trajectories under the resulting distribution of environment responses. We prove that the formalism induces a preorder on the set of system trajectories, ensuring consistency and preventing cyclic preferences. Finally, we illustrate the approach with an autonomous driving example that demonstrates how the formalism enhances explainability by clarifying the rationale behind trajectory selection.

title: EA-APO: A Universal Proactive Defense Against Facial Manipulation
authors: Lizhi Xiong, Jun Li, Ziqiang Li, Weiwei Jiang, Zhangjie Fu, Zhihua Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The advent of deep learning has accelerated the development of facial manipulation techniques, particularly face-swapping and face attribute editing, raising serious concerns about privacy and identity-related misuse. Existing proactive defense methods predominantly target attribute editing and often generalize poorly to face-swapping models, making it difficult to provide effective protection across both tasks within a unified framework. To bridge this gap, we propose a generalized defense framework, Epoch-Adaptive Adversarial Perturbation Optimization (EA-APO). Specifically, EA-APO introduces a proactive defense mechanism that establishes optimal adversarial paths by optimizing perturbations on a white-box surrogate model to enhance adversarial transferability, and applies the resulting perturbations to source face images to disrupt both face swapping and face attribute editing, even against previously unseen target models in black-box settings. This approach mitigates identity feature tampering while adapting to changes in visual attributes and preserving high-quality adversarial examples. Experimental results show the generalization of our method across multiple face-swapping and attribute-editing models, including commercial ones, while also maintaining strong defense under various common post-processing operations and real-world social media transmission conditions, underscoring its potential for real-world deployment.

title: Definitional-Closure-Constrained Multi-Task Retrieval of Leaf LMA, EWT, and FMC From Hyperspectral Spectra
authors: Jinlong Liu, Xuanwen Wang, Quan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Leaf mass per area (LMA) and equivalent water thickness (EWT) are characteristics of leaf structure and water status. The ratio of these two traits, fuel moisture content (FMC), reflects the balance between water and structural components, involving a series of ecological processes. Simultaneously retrieving these traits from hyperspectral reflectance should facilitate disentangling water and structural components, enabling per-sample physical consistency checks via definitional closure. This enhances the credibility of cross-species and cross-environment interpretations compared to traditional single-trait or stepwise retrievals. In this study, we propose a physically consistent, multitask framework based on a temporal convolutional network (TCN) that uses a shared spectral encoder to learn synergistic information across three traits. The framework uses a soft-physics formulation of "physical baseline + residual correction" to unify predictive accuracy and physical self-consistency. Experiments on the composited EcoSIS dataset demonstrate stable retrieval performance for all three traits (test-set R² ≈ 0.89–0.93) and show that 3-task joint learning significantly outperforms stepwise-based derivation (derived FMC RMSE ≈ 47.63%). Physics regularization reduces the symmetric relative difference (SRD) — a measure of internal physical inconsistency — from ~4.71% to ~0.45%, effectively mitigating the "high-accuracy illusion" of unconstrained models. The model maintains stable accuracy and consistency across spectral resolutions ranging from 1 to 50 nm. These results suggest that the proposed framework could serve as a general retrieval paradigm for composite traits with explicit analytics and provide a more interpretable and transferable solution for generalized leaf-trait retrieval across sensors and ecological domains.

title: Personalized Privacy-preserving Task Allocation in Spatial Crowdsourcing
authors: Jianhao Wei, Xiaolong Li, Jun Cai, Xin Yao, Yixiao Li, Jin Zhang, Yanhua Wen, Yu Liu, Chuang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: As a popular service management system, the spatial crowdsourcing (SC) server is responsible for allocating nearby workers to perform tasks based on outsourced locations. However, protecting the sensitive information contained in these outsourced locations is crucial. Traditional differential privacy (DP) methods suffer from two limitations: 1) they usually rely on a trusted third party, failing to protect both worker and task location privacy simultaneously, thus risking privacy breaches; 2) they ignore the personalized privacy demands of different users. In this paper, we propose a personalized local DP-based location obfuscation (PLDPLO) scheme, thereby providing personalized privacy-preserving both worker and task locations locally while allocating high-quality tasks. To achieve this, we introduce a personalized location indistinguishability (PLI) model, a new personalized Laplace mechanism achieving local DP, to jointly provide the protection of worker locations and different privacy levels for different workers. To address task privacy, we present a spatial mapping indistinguishability (SMI) algorithm to obfuscate task locations based on a random response mechanism, thereby ensuring data utility. Additionally, we propose a Zipf-Poisson model-based task allocation graph (ZPTAG) algorithm to perform one-task-multiple-workers allocation and achieve a high competitive ratio, which reduces the move distance of workers. Our PLDPLO scheme guarantees ϵ-LDP. Extensive experiments over real datasets demonstrate that our scheme achieves over 89% data utility for task allocation and outperforms state-of-the-art methods while providing personalized privacy levels.

title: NOVA: A Self-Supervised Graph Framework for Real-Time Anomaly Detection in Internet of Vehicles
authors: Arash Heidari, Jamal N. Al-Karaki
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: The Internet of Vehicles (IoV) enables cooperative driving and real-time Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication but remains vulnerable to behavioral and structural anomalies due to its dynamic, decentralized nature. Existing deep learning methods either overlook topological inconsistencies or ignore communication feature fidelity, while random-walk sampling introduces contextual noise. In this paper, we propose Network Observation for Vehicular Anomalies (NOVA), a self-supervised graph-based framework that detects both behavioral and structural anomalies in IoV networks without labeled data. NOVA models vehicular communications as attributed graphs and employs intimacy-guided subgraph sampling to extract meaningful neighborhoods.
