A Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)–based generative module reconstructs node attributes to reveal behavioral deviations, while a contrastive module validates structural coherence through embedding comparisons of real and perturbed contexts. Their hybrid anomaly score enables accurate, scalable, and real-time detection of compromised nodes. Performance results show that NOVA achieves state-of-the-art performance (98.7% accuracy, 98.1% F1), real-time throughput (~4.7k events/s at 5k msg/s), and strong robustness (AUROC 0.99, AUPRC 0.98, FAR 0.05) with near-linear scalability (≤40 ms latency for 50k vehicles). By integrating generative and contrastive self-supervised learning with context-aware sampling, NOVA significantly enhances IoV security, reliability, and adaptability.

title: FASE: FPGA-Assisted Syscall Emulation for Rapid Early-Stage Processor Performance Evaluation
authors: Chengzhen Meng, Xiuzhuang Chen, Bingcai Sui, Zhenyu Zhao, Tun Li, Hongjun Dai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: The rapid advancement of AI workloads and domain-specific architectures has led to increasingly diverse processor microarchitectures, whose design exploration requires fast and accurate performance validation. However, traditional workflows defer validation process until RTL design and SoC integration are complete, significantly prolonging development and iteration cycle. In this work, we present FASE framework, FPGA-Assisted Syscall Emulation, the first work for adapt syscall emulation on FPGA platforms, enabling complex multi-thread benchmarks to directly run on the processor design without integrating SoC or target OS for early-stage performance evaluation. FASE introduces three key innovations to address three critical challenges for adapting FPGA-based syscall emulation: (1) only a minimal CPU interface is exposed, with other hardware components untouched, addressing the lack of a unified hardware interface in FPGA systems; (2) a Host-Target Protocol (HTP) is proposed to minimize cross-device data traffic, mitigating the low-bandwidth and high-latency communication between FPGA and host; and (3) a host-side runtime is proposed to remotely handle Linux-style system calls, addressing the challenge of cross-device syscall delegation. Experiments were conducted on Xilinx FPGA with open-sourced RISC-V SMP processor Rocket. With single-thread CoreMark, FASE introduces less than 1% performance error and achieves over 2000× higher efficiency compared to Proxy Kernel due to FPGA acceleration. With complex OpenMP benchmarks, FASE demonstrates over 96% performance validation accuracy for most single-thread workloads and over 91.5% for most multi-thread workloads compared to full SoC validation, significantly reducing development complexity and time-to-feedback. All components of FASE framework are released as open-source <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: Optimization Method for Safety Domain of Electric Vehicle Cluster and Distributed Photovoltaic Collaborative Operation Based on Graph Reinforcement Learning
authors: Meng Ye, Longbo Luo, Zhenhuang Chen, Lanqian Yang, Shaoyu Zeng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This paper proposes a graph reinforcement learning-based collaborative safety domain optimization method to mitigate power fluctuations and voltage violations from large-scale electric vehicle and photovoltaic integration. A deep graph convolutional network models real-time interactions among vehicles, charging stations, and road networks for dynamic charging and routing decisions. A hybrid Analytic Hierarchy Process-Entropy Method framework balances subjective and objective factors in multi-objective optimization. A Voronoi-improved Particle Swarm Optimization strategy refines safety boundaries, enhancing economy while ensuring security. Results show significant improvements: voltage offset reduced by 33.78% at peak, daily offset by 40.67%, power loss to 2.849 MW, and maximum voltage deviation to 5.263%.

title: A Rapid Method for Evaluating Power Grid Capacity by Integrating Graph Neural Networks and Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Huixuan Yang, Dandan Peng, Jinhui Liu, Bo Yan
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of distributed photovoltaic (PV) systems, the assessment of distribution network carrying capacity faces challenges such as low computational efficiency, difficulties in modeling time-series characteristics, and delays in dynamic optimization. This paper proposes a hybrid simulation framework that integrates Graph Neural Networks (GNN) with Deep Reinforcement Learning (PPO). By using GNN to accelerate power flow calculations, LSTM to predict load and renewable energy output, and PPO to optimize operational strategies, the framework enables rapid carrying capacity assessment under dynamic conditions. Using a real-world 10 kV distribution network as a case study, experiments demonstrate that this method requires only 2.1 seconds for a single 24 -hour simulation (a 6.0 -fold acceleration compared to the Newton method), with prediction errors for load and renewable energy output below 5% and 10%, respectively. The research results significantly outperform single AI models and static evaluation methods, providing technical support for the safe operation of distribution networks under high renewable energy penetration scenarios.

title: Research on Dark Fermentation-Driven Biological Hydrogen Production Systems
authors: Minyu Liu, Xinqi Cui, Meitong Li, Hongqi Yan, Zhiyi Mo, Jiawen Ni, Haoran Sun, Bohan Song
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Against the backdrop of escalating energy demands and sustainable development imperatives, dark fermentation emerges as a pivotal technology for biohydrogen production, leveraging organic waste to integrate waste management with clean energy generation. This review synthesizes recent advancements addressing key bottlenecks, including suboptimal yields and operational instability. Critical operational parameters—pH, temperature, and organic loading rate—are optimized to favor hydrogen-producing bacteria while suppressing competitors like methanogens. Microbial regulation strategies, such as thermal/chemical pretreatment and substrate-specific domestication, enrich dominant consortia, boosting yields by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{3 0}-\text{4 0 {\%}}$</tex>. Reactor innovations, including electrochemical integration and membrane-assisted systems, enhance mass transfer and gas separation. Nanomaterial additives further improve enzyme activity and electron transfer. Despite challenges like high energy costs, these advancements bridge laboratory efficiency and industrial scalability. Future research should focus on multi-stage system integration, AI-driven controls, and microbial engineering to unlock dark fermentation's full potential in the circular bioeconomy.

title: Research on IGBT Open-Circuit Fault Diagnosis for Grid-Connected Photovoltaic Inverters Based on EVR-RF
authors: Qingjie Wei, Xianglian Xu, Xinrong Gao, Kangning Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) inverters are critical components in PV systems, where the reliability of their power switching devices is paramount. Undiagnosed IGBT open-circuit faults can severely compromise system stability. This paper proposes a synergistic fault diagnosis framework coupling Extreme Value Ratio (EVR) with Random Forest (RF). The innovation lies in their co-design: EVR's dimensional reduction enables RF's millisecond inference, while RF's ensemble robustness compensates EVR's noise sensitivity. A simulation model of a two-stage three-phase grid-connected PV inverter is established in MATLAB/Simulink. A two-stage three-phase gridconnected PV inverter model is established in MATLAB/Simulink. The EVR method extracts concise fault features while reducing data dimensionality, and the RF classifier categorizes 21 open-circuit fault types plus normal condition. Under severe noise conditions (5 dB additive white Gaussian noise), the proposed method achieves a diagnostic accuracy of 95%, surpassing the SVM-based approach by 4.5 percentage points, which validates its robustness and effectiveness.

title: Research on Data Simulation and Energy Analysis Based on Multi-Stage Membrane Separation Process
authors: Yunqi Wang, Haoyue Zhang, Jiaxin Fu, Sitong Ren
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the challenges of large flue gas flow fluctuations in the coal-fired power industry and the low efficiency and high energy consumption of traditional CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> capture methods, this study develops a multi-stage PVAm membrane separation process model, aimed at progressively improving CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> separation purity and efficiency through the increase in the number of membrane stages. Based on the PengRobinson equation of state, Aspen HYSYS was employed to simulate and analyze the capture performance and energy consumption characteristics of the multi-stage membrane separation system. The study systematically evaluated the variations in CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> purity, capture rate, and energy consumption under different membrane stage conditions. Results indicate that as the number of membrane stages increases, both CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> purity and capture rate in the system improve significantly. After threestage membrane separation, the CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> purity and capture rate are greatly increased. At the same time, the compressor power consumption and the cooler's thermal load gradually decrease. These research findings provide an effective technical pathway for the design and operation of CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> membrane separation capture systems in coal-fired power plants.

title: Experimental Study on the Threshold of Loss of Vacuum in an NBI Cryopump Under Liquid-Helium Flow-Loss Conditions
authors: Lunhuan Xia, Yuanlai Xie, Qianxu Wang, Yuanzhe Zhao, Shihua Song, Wei Yi, Caichao Jiang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: A reduction in liquid-helium supply to an NBI cryopump was simulated on an NBI test platform by adjusting the opening of a liquid-helium control valve, and the response of the vacuum system was investigated. During the experiment, the system remained stable at a valve opening of 57%; when the opening was reduced to 54%, the vacuum signals deteriorated continuously and the system eventually lost vacuum. The results indicate that, under the present experimental conditions, the vacuum stability of the NBI cryopump is highly sensitive to variations in liquid-helium supply, and that the stable operating boundary lies between valve openings of 57% and 54%. Preliminary analysis suggests that the reduction in liquid-helium supply weakens the effective pumping capability of the cryopump, disturbs the original vacuum balance of the system, and thereby accelerates vacuum deterioration. The present results provide an experimental basis for subsequent fault analysis and the development of operating protection strategies.

title: Co-Optimization of Capacity Configuration and Operational Scheduling for a Park-Level Integrated Energy System
authors: Kaiqi Xing
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Integrated energy systems play an important role in the low-carbon transition of industrial parks. This paper develops a co-optimization framework for equipment sizing and day-ahead operation of a park-level integrated energy system integrating electricity, heat, cooling, gas, and multi-type storage, and formulates a MILP model with total cost minimization as the objective. Results show that the gas turbine and heat pump are the dominant supply units, with optimal capacities of 782.45 kW and 690.44 kW, while the absorption chiller is 216.67 kW. Electrical storage reaches 3000 kWh and provides peak shaving, whereas thermal and cold storage mainly perform fine adjustment, improving renewable energy utilization and heat cascade efficiency.

title: A Subdivision Analytical Model for Accurate Prediction, Rapid Positioning and Degree Evaluation of Irreversible Demagnetization of Permanent Magnets in Surface-Mounted Permanent Magnet Motors
authors: Zezhi Xing, Xiuhe Wang, Yubo Yang
abstract: In response to the drawbacks of long time consumption of finite element method (FEM) and poor universality of traditional analytical models for irreversible demagnetization of permanent magnets (PMs) in surface-mounted permanent magnet motors (SPMMs), a subdivision analytical model (SAM) is proposed. The proposed analytical model not only has strong universality and high accuracy in analyzing irreversible demagnetization of PMs of SPMMs, the calculation errors of fundamental amplitudes of no-load back electromotive forces (EMFs) before and after irreversible demagnetization of PMs are less than 1.40% and 3.00%, but also can greatly reduce calculation time compared to the FEM. Afterwards, a fast fault location and degree evaluation method for local irreversible demagnetization of PMs based on coil no-load back EMF is proposed, which can quickly locate the local irreversible demagnetization and evaluate demagnetization degree under common demagnetization conditions, including local irreversible demagnetization of a single magnetic pole and all magnetic poles. The width angle of local irreversible demagnetization of the trailing edge of PMs can be quickly determined by combining the waveform of the coil no-load back EMF and the phase of the typical component, and the average demagnetization rate can be quickly estimated based on the amplitude of the typical component.

title: A Comprehensive Study on the Evolution of Different Motivations and Architectures of YOLO
authors: May Altulyan, Mokhtar Mohammadi, Mohammad Hossein Shakoor, Reza Boostani
abstract: YOLO (You Only Look Once) is a popular real-time object detection method that has been widely used in most computer vision applications recently. YOLO is a fast and lightweight deep neural network that can provide the output in a single-stage operation. This paper is a comprehensive survey of more than 200 papers that have been published over the last two years about YOLO applications. This study explains the applications of YOLO and their motivations to change the architecture to enhance their operations. In this research all of the comparison criteria of different YOLO versions have been compared and the YOLO components have been revised comprehensively. The advantages and limitations of each YOLO versions have been discussed and the statistical comparisons of different YOLO versions in different applications have been illustrated. These applications include industrial, medical, agriculture, agronomy, transportation, security, monitoring, remote sensing, and animal-related. These applications are related to the classification, segmentation, detection, recognition, and localization of different objects in the grayscale, colored, X-ray, Computed Tomography (CT), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), Infrared (IR), Thermal, and synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images. For all of the revised papers the motivation, the improvement and the specifications of that application have been discussed and for some of these applications the results have been shown in some figures. In terms of the motivations of each reviewed paper, some researches replaced the backbone of the original YOLO with a lightweight network to reduce the parameters, FLOPs (floating point operations), and model size. Some of them, proposed an attention mechanism to enhance feature extraction capabilities of backbone. Diffusion mechanism of features and dimension integration of the neck are other innovations in most of the YOLO-based approaches to optimize the detection of small and overlapped objects. Furthermore, some of these researchers improved the loss function and head enhancement of YOLO to boost detection accuracy.

title: A Robust Tamper-Resistant and Location-Aware Authentication Protocol for Securing Charging Services in V2G Environments
authors: Muhammad Umer, Muhammad Farooq, Syed Asad Ali Naqvi, Khalid Mahmood, Bander A. Alzahrani, Ashok Kumar Das, Shehzad Ashraf Chaudhry
abstract: The rapid increase in electric vehicles (EVs) and the widespread deployment of charging stations have made secure authentication a critical requirement in Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) environments. The growing interconnection among EVs, charging stations, and grid infrastructure introduces serious security and privacy challenges, including impersonation, replay, ephemeral secret leakage, and physical tampering attacks. Although several authentication protocols have been proposed for EV charging services, many existing schemes lack robust tamper-resistant and location-aware authentication capabilities and remain unsuitable for dynamic and resource-constrained V2G conditions. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a robust, tamper-resistant, and location-sensitive authentication protocol for securing EV charging services in V2G environments. The proposed protocol integrates configurable Arbiter Physical Unclonable Functions (A-PUFs) to provide device-level protection against physical tampering and unauthorized charging access. It also employs lightweight cryptographic primitives and techniques, including one-way hash functions, XOR operations, concatenation operations, and timestamp-based freshness verification, to support efficient mutual authentication and secure session key agreement. The security of the proposed protocol is evaluated through informal analysis and formal verification under the Random Oracle Model (ROM). Furthermore, comparative analysis demonstrates that the proposed protocol achieves a 17.16% reduction in communication cost and a 7.49% reduction in average computation cost compared with existing authentication protocols while maintaining strong security features. The results confirm that the proposed scheme enhances the security, efficiency, and practical deployability of EV charging authentication for next-generation V2G networks.

title: Agentic PD: Rethinking Participatory Design through a Transformative Agency Lens
authors: Ekta Shokeen, Netta Iivari, Ole Sejer Iversen, Christian Dindler, Leena Ventä-Olkkonen, Heidi Hartikainen, Sumita Sharma

authors: Naftali Ndeapo Indongo, Rachel Charlotte Smith, Heike Winschiers-Theophilus, Shorty Kandjengo, Uariaike Mbinge

authors: Jörn Christiansson

title: Outlier Detection of Electricity Load Anomalies in the Leather Manufacturing Industry Based on the Isolation Forest Algorithm
authors: Zhijun Jiang, Wuxiao Chen, Nan Lin, Yihao Zou, Kexin Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Electricity load data in the leather manufacturing industry is not a simple time-series signal; its abnormal fluctuation characteristics are complex, making it difficult to analyze the load change rate. Therefore, this paper studies an outlier detection method for electricity load anomalies in the leather manufacturing industry based on the Isolation Forest algorithm. Given the problems of missing values, noise, and dimensional differences in the original data, methods such as mean imputation, smoothing filtering, and standardization are used to process the data, while ensuring data acquisition security to obtain a standardized dataset. The Isolation Forest algorithm is used to calculate the outlier scores of the dataset; the closer the score is to 1, the greater the probability of an anomaly. When determining anomaly features, an anomaly score threshold is set based on business experience to filter out anomalies. Simultaneously, the load change rate is calculated by combining time series analysis, and a change rate threshold is set. Combining these two indicators more accurately determines anomaly features and filters out misjudged data. Experimental results show that the fitting error is low in single vacuum dryer detection; in terms of detection accuracy, the precision reaches 94.2%, recall 92.7%, and F1-score 93.4, showing significant advantages; In terms of stability, the method has the highest AUC value curve and the smallest fluctuation.

title: Topology Optimization Design of Pemfc Cathode Flow Field Based on Composite Objective Function
authors: Pengcheng Zhao, Qiu Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Aiming at the problems of low efficiency of traditional design and difficulty in achieving consistent gas distribution in the cathode flow field design of Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells, this paper proposes a flow field topology optimization design method based on the variable density method. A composite objective function incorporating flow velocity uniformity and flow field pressure drop indices was established, and the Method of Moving Asymptotes was adopted to perform structural optimization on the flow channels in the distribution zone. The evolution laws of topological configurations of the fuel cell distribution zone under different optimization objectives were systematically studied. The results show that with the increase of uniformity weight, the flow field exhibits a bionic tree-like structure with multi-level branching. The optimal configuration balancing flow uniformity and low flow resistance was determined, which significantly maim proves the gas distribution quality in the distribution zone compared with traditional designs.

title: Effect of Gradient Distribution of Platinum Content and Perforation Structure on Reaction Transport Processes in the Catalytic Layer
authors: Tao Li, Jiadong Liao, Zhiya Zhang, Xiaobin Guo, Riming Lin
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The effects of double-perforation structure and gradient distribution of platinum (Pt) content within the catalytic layer (CL) of proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) on oxygen transport and electrochemical reaction process are investigated at the pore scale by using the lattice Boltzmann method (LBM). Based on the fixed double perforation structure, three cases of uniform distribution of Pt content, positive gradient and negative gradient distribution along the thickness direction are comparatively analyzed. According to the simulation findings, when the Pt content is arranged in a negative gradient pattern, the ionomer phase records its peak average oxygen concentration at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.269 \text{mol} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{-3}$</tex> and the highest average electrochemical reaction rate at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.438 \times 10^{-14} \text{mol} \cdot \mathrm{s}^{-1}$</tex>. In contrast to the uniform platinum distribution design, these two key parameters are enhanced by 2.836% and 3.867% in turn. The study demonstrates that the combination of Pt negative gradient distribution and double perforated structure can synergistically optimize the reaction transport process within the CL and improve the Pt utilization.

title: Spatio-Temporal Feature Fusion Network with Multi-Head Attention for Electricity Spot Price Forecasting
authors: Guoyang Wang, Liye Xie, Jiayi Wang, Qingkai Sun, Zhao Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Accurate electricity spot price forecasting is indispensable for market clearing and risk management in modern power systems. However, the increasing integration of intermittent renewable energy introduces extreme non-linearity and volatility, rendering traditional time-series models less effective. This paper proposes an advanced hybrid forecasting framework that enhances the representation capability of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. By incorporating a Multi-Head Attention (MHA) mechanism, the model adaptively weighs critical temporal dependencies and spatial correlations across diverse market variables. We further employ a multi-layer architecture with Dropout regularization to mitigate overfitting during high-volatility events. Real market operation data is difficult to obtain due to issues such as commercial privacy protection, and publicly available data is often outdated and incomplete. Extensive case studies using simulated market data, which accounts for seasonal and trend components, demonstrate that the proposed model achieves a significant reduction in forecasting error compared to benchmark models, providing a robust decision-support tool for market participants.

title: Standardized Commercial Building Cooling Audits for Low-Carbon Power Systems: An ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 211 Framework to Operationalize National Cooling Action Plans
authors: Godfrey Olivier A. Bautista, Aldrin D. Calderon
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Cooling energy demand in buildings is rising rapidly, intensifying energy security, affordability, and climate risks in fast-urbanizing tropical economies. National Cooling Action Plans (NCAPs) provide policy roadmaps for “low-carbon building energy systems,” but implementation often stalls at the building level due to inconsistent diagnostics, weak baselines, and limited verification of savings. This paper presents an original framework that maps NCAP implementation needs to standardized commercial building energy audit outputs, using ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 211 as a unifying methodology. A PRISMA-guided literature review was conducted, covering 20102023 and including international standards, national guidelines, and recent research on building energy control and audit innovations. Five key NCAP needs are identified - from portfolio benchmarking to measurement and verification - and linked to audit deliverables across ASHRAE Level 1-3 audits. The framework, introduced as an “NCAP-to-Audit” mapping table, synthesizes evidence from 44 unique sources to demonstrate how standardized audits can translate high-level cooling targets into actionable measures at the facility level. The results show that an “audit-to-policy” pathway grounded in ASHRAE 211 improves data consistency, comparability, and investment credibility of cooling efficiency measures. It also aligns with ISO 50001 energy management processes and integrates emerging tools to accelerate NCAP implementation.

title: Thermoeconomic Study on Low-Carbon Steam System Retrofitting in a Pulp and Paper Mill
authors: Shuwen Zhu, Wenwu Ding, Danyang Wu, Qifeng Jiang, Fang Chen, Weibin Zhang, Zhenggui Li
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The pulp and paper industry is the fourth-largest energy-intensive sector globally. Steam generation consumes substantial amounts of fossil fuels and is associated with significant energy destruction, thereby constraining low-carbon transition pathways. Based on operational data from a pulp and paper mill in Iran, this study comparatively investigates two low-carbon retrofit options for steam production: a heat pump (HP) integrated with photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T) collectors and an electric boiler (EB) coupled with photovoltaic (PV) power, both partially replacing the original boiler system. A thermoeconomic methodology is applied to systematically evaluate exergy costs and performance. The results show that the steam network in the original system exhibits the highest unit exergy cost <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(6.43 ~\text{kW} / \text{kW})$</tex>, primarily due to exergy destruction caused by steam quality mismatch and the consequent increase in effective fuel cost. After retrofitting, the unit exergy cost of Paper Mill 3 (PM3) decreases to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.32 ~\text{kW} / \text{kW}$</tex> under the HP+PV/T configuration, which is lower than that of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{E B}+\mathbf{P V}$</tex> system <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{4. 6 3 ~ k W} / \mathbf{k W})$</tex> and slightly below the original PM3 value (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.38 ~\text{kW} / \text{kW}$</tex>). In terms of exergy performance, the specific energy consumption of the original system—accounting for fossil fuels and grid electricity—is 41.92 GJ/tp. Following retrofitting, and considering fossil fuels, grid electricity, and photovoltaic electricity as energy inputs, the specific energy consumption is reduced to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$34.09 \text{GJ} /$</tex> tp for the HPbased scheme and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$38.61 \text{GJ} / \text{tp}$</tex> for the EB-based scheme. Meanwhile, carbon emissions decrease by approximately 652 kg <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> per ton of paper, corresponding to an annual reduction of about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 4, 8 0 0}$</tex> t CO<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$_{\mathbf{2}} \boldsymbol{(} \mathbf{1 5. 0 2 \%) }$</tex>.

title: Fundamental Limit of Discrete Distribution Estimation under Utility-Optimized Local Differential Privacy
authors: Sun-Moon Yoon, Hyun-Young Park, Seung-Hyun Nam, Si-Hyeon Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: We study the problem of discrete distribution estimation under utility-optimized local differential privacy (ULDP), which enforces local differential privacy (LDP) on sensitive data while allowing more accurate inference on non-sensitive data. In this setting, we completely characterize the fundamental privacy–utility trade-off. The converse proof builds on several key ideas, including a generalized uniform asymptotic Cramér–Rao lower bound, a reduction showing that it suffices to consider a newly defined class of extremal ULDP mechanisms, and a novel distribution decomposition technique tailored to ULDP constraints. For the achievability, we propose a class of utility-optimized block design (uBD) schemes, obtained as nontrivial modifications of the block design mechanism known to be optimal under standard LDP constraints, while incorporating the distribution decomposition idea used in the converse proof and a score-based linear estimator. These results provide a tight characterization of the estimation accuracy achievable under ULDP and reveal new insights into the structure of optimal mechanisms for privacy-preserving statistical inference.

title: Decentralizing Land Registry Infrastructure: Consumer-Run Shared Blockchain for Sustainable Agricultural Applications
authors: Gunasekaran Raja, Sai Ganesh Senthivel, Selva Ragul Kumar, Pronoy Kundu, Mohammad Tabrez Quasim, Kapal Dev
abstract: Land records are unclear and highly scattered in today’s digital world. A centralized digital land registry system, while usable, provides a single high-value target for security attacks. While the vulnerability of such a system can be mitigated through decentralization, the cost of running an extensive nationwide decentralized system can be prohibitive. An AI-Enabled Sharded (AIES) Blockchain is proposed to address this challenge by incorporating a novel Genetic Cluster Sharding (GCS) mechanism, which significantly improves the scalability and throughput of the underlying blockchain network. Furthermore, the cost of running the AIES Blockchain is offset by moving to a consumer-supported deployment. This enables individuals to actively participate in network validation while aligning with sustainable practices, reducing operational costs, and minimizing reliance on centralized infrastructure. Finally, the AIES Blockchain uses machine learning to embed decision-making capabilities, leveraging data stored on the blockchain. The simulation results demonstrate that the AIES Blockchain accelerates the land registry process by processing transactions more efficiently, thereby enhancing the system’s overall throughput and scalability. Additionally, the land registry learning model in AIES Blockchain predicts land value with 95% accuracy.

title: A Hierarchical Deep Reinforcement Learning Framework for Dependency-Aware Task Offloading in Edge Computing
authors: Yanping Du, Yan Ding, Haotian Wang, Chubo Liu, Keqin Li, Kenli Li
abstract: How to efficiently schedule a set of dependent tasks has always been a key challenge for edge computing. However, traditional heuristic methods often fail to provide the adaptability to respond to time-varying environments. Meanwhile, pure deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods frequently encounter problems such as state-space explosion and convergence instability in high-dimensional scheduling spaces. To address the above issues, we propose a Greedy-based Dueling Double Deep Q-Network (GD3QN), which is a dependency-aware hierarchical scheduling framework for edge computing to optimize the system completion delay. GD3QN decomposes the scheduling problem into two collaborative stages. First, an end-side local greedy partitioning algorithm (LGPA) is designed to perform task partitioning by constructing a virtual reference server and employing an adaptive threshold mechanism. Then, an intelligent scheduling algorithm based on Dueling Double DQN (D3QN) is proposed to optimize edge resource allocation. The second stage algorithm learns long-term load balancing strategies among heterogeneous servers by aggregating multi-dimensional task features and real-time queue states. Simulation results demonstrate that GD3QN reduces the system makespan by up to 21.72% compared to the genetic algorithm in high-concurrency scenarios. It maintains load balance and alleviates severe queuing congestion under resource-constrained conditions.

title: Is Geometry Enough? An Evaluation of Landmark-Based Gaze Estimation
authors: Daniele Agostinelli, Thomas Agostinelli, Andrea Generosi, Maura Mengoni
abstract: Appearance-based gaze estimation frequently relies on deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). These models are accurate, but computationally expensive and act as ‘‘black boxes’’, offering little interpretability. Geometric methods based on facial landmarks are lightweight alternatives. However, their performance limits and generalization capabilities remain underexplored in modern benchmarks. In this study, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the landmark-based gaze estimation. We introduce a standardized pipeline to extract and normalize landmarks from three large-scale datasets (Gaze360, ETH-XGaze, and GazeGene) and train lightweight regression models, specifically extreme gradient boosted trees and two neural architectures: a holistic multilayer perceptron (MLP) and a Siamese MLP designed to capture binocular geometry. We find that landmark-based models exhibit lower performance in the within-domain evaluation, likely due to noise introduced into the datasets by the landmark detector. Never-theless, in the cross-domain evaluation, the proposed MLP architectures show generalization capabilities comparable to those of the ResNet18 baselines. These findings suggest that sparse geometric features encode sufficient information for robust gaze estimation, paving the way for efficient, interpretable, and privacy-friendly edge applications. The source code and generated landmark-based datasets are available at: https://github.com/daniele-agostinelli/LandmarkGaze.git.

title: Analysis and Optimization of Spatially Correlated STAR-RIS Assisted Secure Massive MIMO Systems
authors: Dan Yang, Jun Wu, Chongyu Yu
abstract: The explosive growth of Internet of Things (IoT) networks necessitates innovative solutions for robust physical layer security (PLS) and ubiquitous connectivity. As a transformative technology, simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (STAR-RISs) effectively overcome the half-space coverage limitations of conventional surfaces. This paper investigates secure downlink transmission in STARRIS-assisted massive MIMO IoT systems employing the energysplitting protocol, specifically addressing practical challenges like spatially correlated fading and multi-antenna eavesdroppers. We establish a robust transmission framework where the base station designs data and artificial noise precoders using imperfect instantaneous channel state information (CSI), while STARRIS phase shifts and power allocation are optimized based on statistical CSI to minimize signaling overhead. We construct a linear minimum mean-square error (LMMSE) estimator for aggregated channels and derive tractable closed-form expressions for the ergodic secrecy rate. Furthermore, asymptotic analysis quantifies performance scaling laws as the antenna array dimensions increase. To tackle the secrecy maximization problem, we propose low-complexity iterative algorithms that jointly optimize STAR-RIS coefficients and power allocation. Numerical results corroborate our theoretical analysis, demonstrating that the proposed scheme yields substantial secrecy gains over passive RIS baselines, offering a sustainable security solution for future green IoT networks.

title: Multi-AUV Cooperative Path Planning Based on Plasticity Injection TD3 Framework for IoUT Data Collection
authors: Yuanbo Zhu, Guangjie Han, Chuan Lin, Fan Zhang, Yun Hou
abstract: Multi-agent reinforcement learning for multi autonomous underwater vehicle path planning faces significant technical challenges in data collection tasks within the Internet of Underwater Things. These challenges are particularly pronounced under dynamic underwater conditions, where the policy network tends to prematurely converge on suboptimal policies, thereby undermining its adaptability to environmental variations. As adaptability continues to decline during extended exploration, training stagnates, and learning efficiency decreases. To address these challenges, we propose an adaptive plasticity injection based twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient (PITD3) framework that integrates an adaptive trigger and a dual-mode plasticity injection method. The adaptive trigger continuously monitors fluctuations in reward and temporal difference loss, activating plasticity restoration when learning stagnates or exploration becomes inefficient. The dual-mode plasticity injection method reinitializes the actor head and critic heads through selective parameter injection, restoring learning capacity and preventing long-term performance degradation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed PITD3 framework out performs baseline frameworks, achieving substantial improvements in collision avoidance, data collection rate, and energy efficiency.

title: RDSAN-CDS: An RD Spectrum-Aware Network With Controllable Detection Sensitivity for HFSWR Target Detection
authors: Yuanzheng Ji, Aijun Liu, Shuai Shao, Zirui Guan, Changjun Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: High-Frequency Surface Wave Radar (HFSWR) faces severe challenges from complex interference in maritime monitoring, hindering weak target detection and false alarm control. To address the model mismatch inherent in traditional Constant False Alarm Rate (CFAR) detectors and the limited perception and lack of sensitivity adjustability in existing encoder-decoder deep neural network methods, this paper proposes an RD Spectrum-Aware Network with Controllable Detection Sensitivity (RDSAN-CDS). The network adopts an end-to-end encoder-decoder architecture, integrating Transformer modules at the deep feature extraction stage to capture the global long-range dependencies between targets and the environment within the Range- Doppler (RD) spectrum. Furthermore, a gating mechanism driven by a preset false alarm probability is designed as an auxiliary input channel. This mechanism dynamically adjusts multi-scale feature responses during decoding, endowing the network with flexible detection sensitivity control during inference. Experimental results on field-measured radar data demonstrate that RDSAN-CDS outperforms existing mainstream methods such as Residual Regression Network (RRN) and DeepLabv3 in complex clutter backgrounds, achieving an effective balance and precise control of the detection rate and false alarm rate under diverse mission requirements.

title: Robust Fine-Grained Oriented Ship Detection for Remote Sensing imagery via Controllable Generative Pretraining
authors: Da He, Haoran Liu, Zeyu Li, Yikai Zhao, Xikun Hu, Ping Zhong, Weijia Li, Qian Shi, Xiaoping Liu, Yanfei Zhong, Liangpei Zhang
abstract: Fine-grained ship recognition in remote sensing imagery is essential for maritime applications. However, its development is hindered by two challenges: (1) the limited granularity of existing ship detection datasets, and (2) the disturbance of complex maritime conditions as well as the arbitrary ship orientations and distributions. To address the first issue, we annotated a large-scale fine-grained ship instance detection dataset (LAFI), comprising 48,717 ship instances worldwide with 49 categories. To tackle the challenges of marine disturbance and diverse ship status, we proposed a controllable generative knowledge-driven ship detection framework (COSD). It employs a controllable generative model guided by ship-marine knowledge to generate millions of synthetic images that not only preserve ship structures but also cover diverse sea and weather conditions for robust pretraining. Furthermore, a heterogeneous feature alignment decoder is designed to align multi-modal metrics of orientation and distribution features in the latent space, allowing for accurate representation of diverse ship status. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets showed that our method respectively increased 0.093 and 0.129 mean average precision (mAP) over SOTA methods, particularly in scenarios involving small, densely packed and arbitrary oriented ships.

title: Accelerated Landweber regularization for vehicle velocity from high-rate GNSS positioning
authors: Kun Zhu, Yujia Zhang, Ao Shen, Yuan Yang
abstract: Difference method is generally used to calculate vehicle velocity from Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) position estimates. However, random errors in GNSS position measurements are significantly amplified by the differencing operation, with the amplification increasing as the sampling interval decreases. Therefore, velocity estimation from GNSS positioning is formulated as an ill-posed inverse problem. We propose an accelerated Landweber iterative regularization method to reconstruct high-rate vehicle velocity waveforms from real-time kinematic (RTK) positioning in urban canyons and open fields. The root mean square error (RMSE) of the reconstructed velocity is 0.367 m/s in urban canyons and 0.182 m/s in open fields, corresponding to error reductions of 87% and 57%, respectively. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method for high-rate GNSS-based vehicle velocity determination in challenging environments.

title: LassoNet-GNN: A Catalyst for Few-Shot Distorted SAR Image Classification
authors: Kezhen Dong, Zhongying Suo, Hao Chen, Junmin Liu, Fei Hao, Hongying Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Few-shot learning (FSL) has revolutionized SAR image classification by enabling robust recognition with limited high-quality labeled data. However, existing methods struggle with both identifying key features in noisy, high-variation SAR data and handling geometric distortions. To address these challenges, we propose a novel contrastive sparse few-shot distorted SAR image classification framework via LassoNet-GNN, which incorporates GNN-based FSL framework with an adopted episodic training protocol and a LassoNet-based sparsification technique into an end-to-end architecture for SAR image classification and interpretable feature selection with an optional plug-and-play contrastive learning (CL) regularization module that can be beneficial for improving robustness to geometric distortions on more complex data with richer intraclass variations in few-shot scenarios. Specifically, we first build upon a GNN-based FSL framework trained with an adopted episodic protocol that forms a learnable similarity graph over features extracted by a convolutional encoder, enabling effective message passing and strong base recognition performance under limited data. In addition, we introduce a LassoNet-type input-to-output skip-layer across the entire GNN-based network and apply a sparse penalty to its weights, which encourages the weights of unimportant nodes in the first hidden layer of the network to approach zero. Finally, an auxiliary plug-and-play contrastive regularization module is employed by utilizing geometric transformation augmentations to construct positive and negative sample pairs, which can be optionally activated on complex few-shot data when beneficial for robustness. Experimental results on common few-shot SAR image datasets verify the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed framework in classification accuracy and interpretability.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models as Substitutes for Human Affective Ratings in Naturalistic Paradigms
authors: Xuan Yang, Deepa Tilwani, Christian O'Reilly, Svetlana V. Shinkareva
abstract: Naturalistic paradigms provide ecologically valid insights into affective and cognitive processes but often require costly and time-consuming human annotations. Large language models (LLMs) provide a scalable tool for generating human-like affective ratings that could complement traditional behavioral approaches. In this study, we compared affective ratings of narrative segments obtained from young adults, five OpenAI's GPT models, Meta's Llama 3.1, and lexical-level norms from the SCOPE metabase. LLM-derived ratings of hedonic valence showed strong correlations with human ratings and outperformed lexical norms. When applied to fMRI data, LLM-derived ratings identified affective brain networks that substantially overlapped with those revealed by human ratings. These findings demonstrate that LLMs can approximate group-level affective ratings from young adults in naturalistic contexts and serve as a useful complement to traditional behavioral data collection, while underscoring the need for careful evaluation of their generalizability and potential biases.

title: ERMoV: Modeling Affective Mental States via Emotion Regulation for Dialogue Emotion Recognition
authors: Xiao Sun, Xuxiong Liu, Yueqi Jiang
abstract: To enable machines to engage in human-like emotional interactions, it is crucial to move beyond mere pattern recognition towards modeling the underlying cognitive processes. A key component of this is Affective Theory of Mind (ToM)—the ability to reason about one's own and others' emotional states. However, existing models for dialogue emotion recognition often simulate emotional dynamics (e.g., contagion, persistence) at a phenomenological level, without explicitly modeling the mental istic reasoning that drives these phenomena. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes ERMoV, a framework that operationalizes principles from cognitive science to build a computational model of Affective ToM. At its core, ERMoV introduces a graph based reasoning module, EmoGNN, which simulates the interplay between self-oriented emotion regulation (intra-personal mental modeling) and other-oriented emotional influence (inter-personal mental modeling). To ground this reasoning process in conversa tional context, ERMoV integrates discourse structure to capture relational cues and employs a multi-view fusion mechanism to synthesize diverse sources of evidence. Experiments on four public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our ToM-driven approach. ERMoV achieves competitive performance, obtaining an F1 score of 40.93% on EmoryNLP and a macro-F1 of 62.51% on DailyDialog, validating that modeling the underlying mentalistic dynamics improves recognition accuracy. Our work provides a new paradigm for building more interpretable and empathetic conversational AI.

title: High-Speed GaN Position Sensitive Detector for Visible-Blind Ultraviolet Detection
authors: Yifu Wang, Haifan You, Haiping Wang, Wenbin Yao, Yancheng Chen, Yongzhe Zhang, Jiandong Ye, Hai Lu, Dunjun Chen
abstract: In this work, a tetralateral GaN position sensitive detector (PSD) with a 4 mm × 4 mm effective area is demonstrated for visible-blind ultraviolet (UV) detection. By utilizing an epitaxially grown thin p-GaN layer as the lateral resistive channel, the PSD exhibits a low interelectrode resistance of 41 kΩ and a low terminal capacitance of 35 pF, enabling a fast response time of 0.271 μs. The device presents a peak external quantum efficiency over 67% under zero bias, a high UV-to-visible rejection ratio exceeding 3000, and a broad spectral response covering 200–360 nm range. With these characteristics, the device also demonstrates a low position nonlinearity of 2.3% and a high position resolution of 1.32 μm, enabling reliable real-time tracking of moving UV light sources. These results highlight the potential of GaN-based PSDs for high-speed visible-blind UV position detection.

title: An Adaptive Fusion Network for Breast Tumor Grading Based on Graph Structure Learning
authors: Lei Yang, Kang Li, Zhan Yu, Yaojun Jiang, Suya Han, Xin Zhao, Jianbo Gao, Huiqin Jiang, Ling Ma
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Breast cancer is a serious threat to women's life and health. Early grading of breast tumor malignancy is the key for improving patient survival rate. In clinical diagnosis, physicians often utilize multi-modal data information to accurately grade. As a new multi-modal data processing method, graph convolutional network is expected to improve the grading accuracy of breast tumors. To solve the problem that a single graph cannot effectively utilize multi-modal information, this paper proposes a multi-graph adaptive fusion network to perform accurate breast tumor grading by using multi-modal data. Firstly, a multi-modal information extraction network is designed to extract specific modal information and graph structure from multi-modal data, which is composed of graph structure learning layer and graph auto-encoder. Secondly, by calculating the attention scores of different modalities as weight coefficients, the modal aggregation features, and modal aggregation graphs are obtained by fusing in a weighted manner. Finally, a neighborhood adaptive aggregation module is designed to gather multi graphs and optimize the conventional graph convolution aggregation mode by calculating the neighborhood aggregation coefficient. Experimental results on both public and private breast tumor datasets show that the graph learning-based diagnosis model can effectively process multi-modal medical data and improve the accuracy of multi grading for breast tumors.

title: Global Fast Control for Finite-Time Consensus in Multi-Linear Motor Systems
authors: Guanyang Hu, Dezhi Xu, Tinglong Pan, Wentao Huang, Chengxi Zhang, Jianxing Liu
abstract: Position and speed inconsistencies of multi-linear motor systems (M-LMS) may lead to uneven load distribution, low collaborative precision, and even failure to achieve the preset motion planning. We propose an observer-based finite time consensus control protocol, aimed at realizing global fast consensus for M-LMS. Specifically, a finite-time disturbance observer (FTDO) is designed to estimate unknown disturbances in M-LMS, and incorporate it into the subsequent protocol design. Then, the protocol employs the global fast cooperative terminal sliding mode control (GFCTSMC) algorithm with the super twisting reaching law. Methods based on linear sliding surfaces can only guarantee the asymptotic convergence of the consensus error in M-LMS. In contrast, the proposed protocol improves the cooperative precision of M-LMS and ensures that the consensus error can converge within a finite time. Experimental results on consensus cooperative control of M-LMS demonstrate that, in comparison with the representative approaches, the consensus error index of this method is reduced by 95% and 64% on average, respectively.

title: Multi-Satellite Beam-Hopping Scheduling for Coexisting NGSO Satellite Networks Based on Electromagnetic Map-Driven Interference Avoidance
authors: Guowei Yang, Linling Kuang, Zhen Chen, Zhiyuan Lin, Chunxiao Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: To facilitate multi-constellation coexistence, we investigate a novel beam-hopping scheduling mechanism driven by electromagnetic maps to avoid inter-constellation interference. Most existing beam-hopping scheduling techniques focus exclusively on inter-beam and inter-satellite interference arising from satellites within the same constellation. However, in the context of coexisting mega-constellations comprising thousands of non-geostationary orbit (NGSO) satellites, the aggregate uplink interference generated by ground users from one constellation to satellites in another constellation becomes non-negligible, especially when user populations are substantial. To mitigate such inevitable inter-constellation interference, this paper fully exploits electromagnetic maps, which characterize the spatial distribution of interference intensity, to design a multi-satellite beam-hopping scheme, simultaneously achieving efficient resource management through dynamic beam allocation and effective interference avoidance through spatial isolation. Specifically, the proposed electromagnetic map-driven beam-hopping problem is decomposed into two solvable sub-problems, including user association and beam-hopping pattern design, followed by two effective algorithms to obtain the solutions respectively. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly outperforms existing benchmarks in both service efficiency and traffic satisfaction rate.

title: Camera-based Blood Pressure Monitoring Using Pulse Transit Time from Depolarized Skin Layers
authors: Yaling Lv, Yukai Huang, Huailei Lai, Jia Huang, Guowei Wang, Hongzhou Lu, Wenjin Wang
abstract: Camera-based blood pressure (BP) monitoring holds significant promise for non-invasive, continuous health assessment in clinical and daily-care settings. Pulse transit time (PTT), a key physiological indicator correlated with BP, has been widely explored for camera-BP estimation. We propose a new concept that measures the PTT based on the property of skin-tissue depolarization, termed skin-tissue depolarized PTT (SD-PTT). This method extracts PTT from camera-based photoplethysmography (PPG) signals at varying tissue depths based on a single skin site with a single wavelength. By employing time-division multiplexing to sequentially illuminate the skin with 0° and 90° polarized light, SD-PTT captures PPG signals from shallow tissues (via parallel polarization) and deeper tissues (via cross polarization), quantifying the phase delay between these signals, caused by different depolarization properties of skin layers as PTT for BP estimation. Laboratory experiments with cold and heat stimulation protocols (n = 20) reveal significant variations in SD-PTT that are consistent with the regulatory mechanisms of BP (P < 0.01). The SD-PTT is further validated in an intensive care unit scenario (n = 20) by modeling the relationship between SD-PTT and BP using a random forest regressor. Although the model performance is relatively limited under strict Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV), under a mixed modeling approach trained with 50% of personalized data, the method achieves remarkable SD-PTT-BP calibration performance, with mean absolute errors (MAE) of 4.85mmHg for systolic BP, 2.14mmHg for diastolic BP, and 2.85mmHg for mean BP, respectively. These results, achieved through 50% personalized calibration, meet the standards of the American Association for the Advancement of Medical Instrumentation (AAMI). The SD-PTT method offers a novel solution for camera-based monitoring, with strong potential for clinical applications.

title: BatteryGPT: A Generative Pre-Training Method for Battery State-of-Health and Remaining Useful Life Estimations
authors: Tianjing Wang, Ling Fu, Yuechuan Tao, Zhijun Zhang, Shuyin Lai, Zhao Yang Dong
abstract: Increased adoption of electric vehicles demands reliable battery health and lifespan predictions for safety and performance. However, current machine learning methods often struggle with limited labeled data and poor generalization across battery types and operating conditions, resulting in training inefficiencies and reduced prediction accuracy. To fill this gap, this study proposes a generative pre-training (GPT) method for battery degradation, named batteryGPT, to enhance the training efficiency and prediction accuracy of tasks for state of health (SOH) and remaining useful life (RUL) estimations. The proposed batteryGPT integrates attention-based multi-modal fusion, dynamic masking, and a Transformer with windowed multi-scale mechanisms during pre-training to capture robust temporal relationships across voltage, current, and temperature data. By leveraging these methods, batteryGPT can learn generalized features from heterogeneous data sources, improving its ability to adapt to diverse operational environments. Fine-tuning adapts the model to specific tasks through task-specific heads, ensuring accurate battery health and lifecycle predictions. Experimental results show that, when fine-tuned with only a few labeled cells, batteryGPT achieves SOH estimation errors below 0.005 and early-cycle RUL prediction errors of about 0.03 across multiple public datasets. These results are obtained under a few-sample fine-tuning setting, while the pre-training stage relies exclusively on unlabeled degradation data.

title:
Interfacial Charge Regulation in β-Ga
