title: Research on Lithium-Ion Battery Health Estimation Methods Based on SMA-GPR
authors: Bo Rao, Jie Tian, Jinqiao Du, Yizeng Wu, Jiuchun Jiang, Aina Tian
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This paper investigates the assessment of lithium-ion battery state of health (SOH), proposing a hybrid predictive model that integrates the Slime Mould Algorithm (SMA) with Gaussian Process Regression (GPR). Analysis of charge-discharge curves from the NASA battery dataset yielded seven health factors (HFs) exhibiting significant correlations, encompassing both timerelated and area-related characteristics. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed SMA-GPR model significantly outperforms conventional algorithms in predictive accuracy, exhibiting superior performance across evaluation metrics such as RMSE, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, and MAE. This model effectively captures the nonlinear variation trends in battery capacity, demonstrating robust generalisation capability and stability. It provides a reliable methodological foundation for lithium-ion battery lifespan prediction, holding significant application value for enhancing the performance of battery management systems.

title: Electricity-Carbon Joint Pricing Guided Coordinated Low-Carbon Operation Strategy for Coupled Vehicle-Road-Grid Systems
authors: Liang Feng, Xingyu Shen, Kai Liao, Zhengyou He
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In the coupled vehicle-road-grid paradigm, electric vehicles (EVs) offer low-carbon advantages; however, large-scale charging demand can reshape traffic flows, modifying gasoline vehicle (GV) route choices and affecting GV carbon emissions. To address this interaction, this paper proposes a coordinated low-carbon operation strategy guided by a joint electricity-carbon price. First, a road network equilibrium model is developed that simultaneously accounts for EV- and GV-related carbon emissions, while a distribution-network optimal power flow model is formulated to minimize generation cost under charging demand and operational constraints. Second, carbon emissions from GVs on the road network and EV charging on the distribution grid are quantified and traced to identify the spatiotemporal distribution of GV emissions and the equivalent generation-side carbon responsibility of EV charging loads. Finally, nodal carbon prices are derived from carbon-flow tracing results, and differentiated charging tariffs incorporating both electricity and carbon prices are designed for charging stations. The proposed electricity–carbon joint pricing guides EV charging decisions, enables coordinated low-carbon operation of the vehicle-road-grid system, reduces road travel cost and grid generation cost, and decreases the overall carbon emissions of both the distribution grid and the road network.

title: Design of a Fast Battery Balancer Based on Resonant Switching Capacitors
authors: Ming Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In contemporary society, electrical energy is increasingly being applied across all aspects of human production and daily life due to its economic, practical, clean, and easily controllable and convertible characteristics. It provides vital propulsive force for social development, technological advancement, and national economic growth. Owing to the low voltage and small capacity of individual lithium-ion cells, it is often necessary to connect multiple cells in series or parallel to meet load requirements. Due to variations in the production materials, manufacturing processes, and operating environments of individual series-connected cells within a battery pack, certain discrepancies exist between these cells. Such discrepancies typically manifest as inconsistencies in voltage levels between individual cells. The inconsistency among individual battery cells will gradually worsen with increasing charge-discharge cycles, resulting in varying usable capacities across cells within the battery pack. This leads to instances of overcharging or overdischarging. This paper addresses battery inconsistency issues by investigating a resonant switched-capacitor equalisation circuit. The analysis encompasses multiple factors including equaliser classification, concentrated energy transfer methods, and equaliser structure.

title: YOLO-IRPower: An Efficient Infrared-Based Power Equipment Detection Algorithm
authors: Qichen Fu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Infrared imaging is widely used for monitoring power equipment by capturing surface temperature distributions. However, challenges such as scale variation, low-resolution distant targets, complex thermal backgrounds, and weak features hinder accurate detection. To address these issues, this paper proposes YOLO-IRPower, an improved YOLOv12-based method for infrared power equipment detection. The model incorporates a Multi-Scale Thermal Feature Fusion (MSTFF) module to enhance multi-scale representation, an Infrared Scene-Guided Attention (ISGA) mechanism to focus on critical thermal regions, and a Remote Thermal Object Enhancement Unit (RTOEU) to improve feature representation of distant targets. Experimental results demonstrate that YOLO-IRPower achieves an mAP of 96.3% while maintaining real-time performance, outperforming YOLOv12 in accuracy and robustness under complex infrared conditions.

title: An Informer-Based Decision-Oriented Wind Power Forecasting Method with Two-Stage Training for Electricity Spot Markets
authors: Jiale Liu, Xinyi Lai, Qi Mo, Ming Cheng, Tao Wu, Siyu Lu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Wind power forecasting serves as a critical reference for wind power producers to participate in electricity spot markets, where their prediction accuracy directly impacts decision-making benefits. However, traditional forecasting models optimize for accuracy while neglecting the asymmetric cost impacts of overestimation and underestimation errors in electricity markets. Specifically, the penalty cost incurred by overestimating wind power is significantly higher than the cost associated with underestimation. To address this, an Informer-based decision-oriented wind power forecasting method with two-stage training for electricity spot markets is proposed. First, a piecewise loss function based on the deviation penalty mechanism of electricity markets is designed. Next, a forecasting model is constructed based on the Informer architecture and trained using a two-stage strategy: 1) the first stage conducts pre-training based on Mean Squared Error (MSE) to capture the long-term spatiotemporal features of wind power sequences using the Informer mechanism; 2) the second stage fine-tunes the Informer model using the proposed cost-oriented loss function, thereby embedding downstream decision-making risk preferences into the forecasting phase. Finally, a case study based on two years of data from a wind farm in southern China demonstrates that the proposed method reduces the Mean Cost Bias (MCB) index by 46.7%. This approach achieves an integrated optimization of forecasting and decision-making, offering a new paradigm for wind power forecasting in electricity market environments.

title: Research on Investment Optimization of Rural Distribution Network Under the Background of Electric Vehicles Going to the Countryside
authors: Yanbo Wang, Tizhe Deng, Yubo Fu, Yongcheng Guo, Zhengzhong Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In response to the national strategy of promoting new energy vehicles in rural areas, to resolve the contradiction between the popularization of electric vehicles and the insufficient carrying capacity of distribution networks in rural regions, and to promote the coordinated development of rural distribution networks and the electric vehicle industry, this paper conducts a study on the investment optimization of rural distribution networks against the background of the “Electric Vehicles Going to the Countryside” campaign. Firstly, a load forecasting model for rural distribution networks considering electric vehicle charging loads is established to quantify the impact of charging loads on the distribution network. Secondly, an investment optimization model for distribution networks is constructed with the objective of minimizing the total investment cost, while taking into account the constraints of power supply reliability and voltage quality. Finally, taking a rural area in Lingwu City, Ningxia as an example, combined with actual power grid data and electric vehicle promotion scenarios, the effectiveness and practicability of the proposed model are verified, and targeted investment suggestions are put forward.

title: StarPicker: A Technique for Selecting Dense Small Targets in AR-based Data Visualization Environments
authors: Huiyue Wu, Xinle Wang, Zhitong Ma, Boyu Gao, Huawei Tu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: In the era of Big Data, augmented reality (AR)-based 3D visualization technology is gradually becoming an essential tool of effective information dissemination. However, 3D data visualization inevitably faces a series of challenges, particularly when visualizing large datasets within limited physical spaces. This often results in high spatial density, characterized by smaller target objects and occlusion of data objects, which greatly complicate object selection and subsequent interaction. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multimodal progressive target selection technique—StarPicker, which integrates the SpotLight metaphor and Clock metaphor. By leveraging wrist rotation and gesture interactions, StarPicker facilitates a coarse-to-fine semantic disambiguation and precise selection of target objects. To validate the effectiveness of StarPicker, a comparative user study was conducted against the state-of-the-art techniques (GridWall and FlowerCone) under high-density (up to 360 objects) and small-target (1cm) conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that StarPicker significantly outperforms the baselines in terms of target selection accuracy and user satisfaction, while achieving comparable or better completion times, especially in denser scenes. This work offers a novel approach to target selection and interaction technology in the field of AR-based 3D Big Data visualization.

title: MMA++: Effective Multi-Modal Adaptation for Vision-Language Models
authors: Lingxiao Yang, Ru-Yuan Zhang, Yanchen Wang, Xiaohua Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Large scale pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown good generalization capabilities across diverse downstream tasks. However, adapting such large-scale models to few-shot generalization scenarios remains challenging due to the trade-off between preserving general knowledge and incorporating task-specific information. In this paper, we propose MMA++, an advanced and effective Multi-Modal Adapter framework for parameter-efficient VLM adaptation. Unlike prior works that independently inject adapters into each modality or uniformly across layers, MMA++ performs a dataset-level analysis to identify discriminative and generalizable features, and selectively applies adapters to the higher layers of both vision and text encoders. To bridge the modality gap, we further propose a shared feature projection space that enhances alignment between modalities. Beyond architecture design, we identify the fusion scale $\alpha$—which controls the strength of adapter integration—as a key factor in few-shot generalization. We empirically and theoretically demonstrate that $\alpha$ should not be static, but adapted based on training data size. To reduce the effort of tuning this value across different datasets, we propose the $\alpha$-consistency framework, consisting of: (1) a consistency training strategy under varying fusion scales; and (2) an $\alpha$-decoupling strategy that uses a larger fusion scale during training and a smaller one at inference to account for sample size mismatch. We evaluate MMA++ on a wide range of few-shot generalization tasks, including base-to-novel generalization, cross-dataset transfer, and domain generalization. Our method consistently achieves leading performance.

title: Robotic Steerable Sheath for Left Atrial Appendage Occlusion
authors: Nadia D’Alessandro, Selene Tognarelli, Izadyar Tamadon, Arianna Menciassi
abstract: Atrial fibrillation, the most common cardiac arrhythmia, increases stroke risk due to clot formation in the left atrial appendage (LAA), where over 90% of thrombi originate. Left atrial appendage occlusion (LAAO) reduces this risk by mechanically sealing the LAA through an occluder. A key challenge is achieving coaxial alignment of the delivery sheath with the LAA, which varies in shape and orientation among subjects. Fixed-curve sheaths lack adaptability, while steerable sheaths improve alignment but still expose operators to fluoroscopic radiation. This study introduces the first robotic steerable delivery sheath (SDS) for LAAO. The SDS incorporates a notched Nitinol tip with two bending segments, providing three steering degrees of freedom, rotation, and translation, all controlled remotely through motorized actuation. Bench-top experiments confirmed superior dexterity and reachable workspace compared to existing delivery sheaths. A phantom-based validation with 17 users demonstrated successful teleoperated coaxial alignment with the LAA. Quantitative evaluation showed high accuracy, with mean angular and positional errors of 5.52<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> and 1.99 mm for the central axis, and 5.00<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> and 2.33 mm for implant depth, respectively. These findings support the feasibility of robotic-assisted LAAO, with the potential to enhance procedural precision while reducing radiation exposure for operators.

title: Scalable and Robust Resource Provisioning via Adaptive Task Scheduling for Edge Devices
authors: Soonbeom Kwon, Yusu Noh, Youngwoo Jang, Illyoung Choi, Byungchul Tak, In-geol Chun, Young-Kyoon Suh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Edge devices, such as wearables, drones, and CCTV systems, are vital for real-time data collection in urban intelligence. However, their limited computational and storage capacities pose significant challenges. While offloading to public clouds offers scalability, it often incurs high latency and operational costs. Conversely, centralizing workloads on edge servers may result in the underutilization of high-performance edge devices. To address these limitations, we introduce ERPF, a Kubernetes-based Edge Resource Provisioning Framework that augments the capabilities of heterogeneous edge environments. ERPF orchestrates dynamic volume provisioning, GPU-aware resource allocation, execution context migration, and adaptive task distribution to improve system flexibility and efficiency. Building on this, we propose a novel adaptive task scheduling technique, termed eATS, composed of three key mechanisms: (i) Partition Smoothing Scheme for stable task granularity control, (ii) Resilient Edge Reintegration for failure detection and task reassignment, and (iii) Competitive Task Cloning for speculative execution with fastest-result commitment. The proposed eATS scheme reduces task execution time by up to 27.6%, lowers partition size variability by 8.7×, and improves scheduling robustness across heterogeneous edge devices over the baseline.

title: CAF-Net: A Cross-modal Adaptive Fusion Network for GNSS-R Soil Salinity Retrieval
authors: Dongmei Song, Xianjun Wang, Ronghan Xu, Erdenesukh Sumiya, Bin Wang, Song Dai, Mei Yong, Yuhai Bao, Lin Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Soil salinization is a major ecological barrier to sustainable agricultural development. Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R), as an emerging passive microwave remote sensing technology, has shown considerable potential for soil parameter monitoring due to its low cost, short revisit cycle, and strong penetrability. However, the significant heterogeneity between Delay–Doppler Maps (DDMs) and auxiliary features, together with the scarcity of in situ salinity observations, poses challenges for GNSS-R-based soil salinity retrieval. To address these issues, this study proposes a Cross-modal Adaptive Fusion Network (CAF-Net) that integrates GNSS-R DDM observations with multi-source auxiliary features through a cross-modal fusion framework to enhance feature representation and model generalization. In addition, a physical reference dataset is constructed by combining SMAP soil moisture products with dielectric models to alleviate the lack of ground salinity observations. Experiments conducted in the Yellow River Delta using Tianmu-1 GNSS-R data demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a correlation coefficient (R) of 0.9546 and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.5925 g/kg, outperforming several mainstream benchmark models. Moreover, the retrieved soil salinity shows good agreement with independent datasets in both spatial distribution and statistical characteristics. These results indicate that integrating GNSS-R observations with deep learning provides a promising approach for large-scale soil salinity monitoring.

title: Modeling and Analysis of Operating Limits of the Differential Boost Inverter
authors: Diana Lopez-Caiza, Marcelo A. Perez, Felipe Ruiz, Samir Kouro, Frede Blaabjerg
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The Differential Boost Inverter (DBI) is a single-stage step-up topology composed of two bidirectional boost converters whose outputs are connected in differential mode to generate an ac output voltage. The DBI poses significant modeling challenges, since all variables operate with both dc and ac components, requiring a highly complex dynamical model to accurately capture its behavior. Also, the operating range of this converter is restricted both in the modulation indexes, which is limited in the interval between 0 and 1, and in the capacitor voltage which must always be above the input voltage. In addition, a large harmonic content in the converter variables when working at certain operating points can increase the Total Harmonic Distortion (THD), degrading the performance. This work presents a model that considers both the dc and fundamental ac components of the converter variables. Based on this model, the allowable operating ranges for the modulation indexes and capacitor voltages are defined. Furthermore, the model is extended to determine the harmonic content of the capacitor voltage enabling the identification of the operating ranges in which a low THD is achieved. The proposed analysis introduces a flexible dc component in the modulation, without assuming a fixed dc bias, which broadens the valid operating region and enhances the potential for THD minimization. Finally, a DBI prototype has been built to validate the model and analysis, with experimental results confirming the mathematical relationships at representative operating points.

title: An Improved Sparse-Aware Efficient Bayesian Convolutional Neural Networks via Optimized Weight Sampling and Pruning
authors: Gang Wu, Chengfang Ling, Ruotong Niu, Caidie Zhu, Shengyi Ji, Chubo Liu
abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) achieve strong performance in many tasks, but their reliability often declines under uncertainty or limited training data. Bayesian Convolutional Neural Networks (BCNNs) improve robustness by modeling weights probabilistically, yet repeated sampling during inference causes substantial computational overhead. To address this issue, we propose IS-BCNN, which delays sampling and directly convolves the mean and variance of weight distributions, reducing the number of convolutions from s to two while preserving the Gaussian form of the output. We further introduce piecewise pruning, weight reordering, and index mapping to skip insignificant weights and improve sparsity. Experiments show that IS-BCNN reduces training time by up to 8.2%, limits accuracy loss to within 0.5%, and improves accuracy by 3%-6%.

title: Hadamard-Transform-Based CAP Modulation
authors: Grzegorz Stepniak, Juliusz Bojarczuk
abstract: The conventional carrierless amplitude-phase (CAP) is a 2-dimensional modulation format with orthogonality stemming from the Hilbert transform and the Nyquist filter prototype. In this letter, we propose an alternative construction based on the Hadamard transform. We demonstrate that this construction enables orthogonal transmission using Gaussian prototype filters, eliminating the need for the Nyquist pulse. CAP with Gaussian prototype has 1.5-2 dB lower PAPR, 30 % wider horizontal eye opening than the conventional format, and requires a filter of practically only 2.5 symbols. In a free-space transmission experiment employing a phosphorous white light-emitting diode (LED), up to 4 dB advantage over the conventional CAP and a total data rate increase from 1.2 to 2 Gbit/s are reported.

title: Wideband HF Ionospheric Channel Evolution Over Distances: Measurement and Statistical Modeling
authors: Rong Niu, Yang Zhang, Zhiyuan Zhang, Lihua Pang, Jun Liu, Jiandong Li
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter investigates the evolution of wideband high frequency (HF) ionospheric channels through a measurement campaign conducted across three distinct links (1651 km, 2123 km, and 3035 km). Using a universal software radio peripheral (USRP) architecture, we quantify channel behaviors on both large and small scales with respect to distance. While the large scale analysis reveals a distance dependence significantly weaker than the theoretical increment of free space path loss (FSPL), the small scale investigation scrutinizes the Rician <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$K$</tex-math></inline-formula> factor, root mean square (RMS) delay spread, RMS Doppler spread, and the number of clusters to capture the structural transformation of the propagation environment. Furthermore, statistics within clusters are examined to model microscopic scattering details, and the correlation matrix among parameters is derived, providing indispensable inputs for extending the Quasi Deterministic Radio Channel Generator (QuaDRiGa) to include the wideband HF ionospheric scenario.

title: An Untrimmed Cryogenic Voltage Reference Based on Saturation-to-Linear Transition Achieving 60.3 ppm/K TC and 0.56% Inaccuracy
authors: Sungbin Park, Youngwoo Ji, Jae-Yoon Sim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: This brief presents a cryogenic voltage reference (VR) employing a saturation-to-linear transition scheme and a 1/μCox-based proportional-to-absolute-temperature (PTAT) generation. Unlike conventional diode-connected architectures that restrict the core transistor to a single operating region, the proposed design independently controls the drain-to-source voltage (VDS) via feedback, enabling temperature-dependent region transition near 100 K. The saturation-mode square law suppresses strong device nonlinearity below ~100 K, while linear-region operation above ~100 K provides inherent linearity. Fabricated in 180-nm CMOS, 15 untrimmed chips achieve mean temperature coefficients (TCs) of 60.3 ppm/K in the wide-range mode (5.2–300 K) and 105.8 ppm/K in the cryogenic-range mode (5.2–100 K), with σ/μ of 0.56% and 0.52%, respectively, configured by an on-chip (n, R) selection. At 5.2 K, the VR achieves 0.073%/V line regulation, a power-supply rejection ratio (PSRR) of −56.3 dB at 100 Hz, and integrated noise of 23.6 μVrms (1–10 Hz), while consuming 15.2 μW in an active area of 0.063 mm².

title: Single Hole Spin Qubit Optimization in SOI Quantum Dots Via $\bm {k}\cdot \bm {p}$ Simulations and Perturbative Analysis
authors: Lorenzo Raschi, Antonio Gnudi
abstract: Hole spin qubits in silicon nanostructures offer fast, all-electrical control through electric-dipole spin resonance (EDSR), yet their performance strongly depends on device geometry. In this work, optimization criteria of Rabi frequency of single-hole spin qubits in silicon-on-insulator (SOI) quantum dots are identified by combining electrostatic and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm {k}\cdot \bm {p}$</tex-math></inline-formula> simulations with a perturbative model of the Rabi frequency linear in the magnetic field magnitude. Starting from a reference device deeply studied in the literature, the dominant terms governing the Rabi frequency are recalled remarking the key role of the component of the RF electric field parallel to the long axis of the nanowire cross-section. Guided by such insights, two alternative devices designed to enhance this field component are proposed, leveraging the gate geometries and the crystal orientation. In the strain-free case, a more than fourfold improvement in the peak Rabi frequency over the reference device is achieved, partially offset by a shorter dephasing time, yet still providing an improvement of more than 10% in the quality factor <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Q\_{2}^*$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The analyses are then repeated with uniform in-plane biaxial strain. Again, higher Rabi frequencies are generally obtained, accompanied by shorter dephasing times, resulting in improvements in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Q\_{2}^*$</tex-math></inline-formula> that can approach one order of magnitude, although strongly dependent on the strain value.

title: Frames of Participation: Design as Enquiry in Exploring Technological Futures of Emergency Care
authors: Aloha Ambe, Isaac Salisbury, Tobias Grundgeiger, Daniel Bodnar, Sean Rothwell, Nathan Brown, Ben Matthews
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: Flexible Electrically Small Yagi-Uda Antenna with Enhanced Bandwidth
authors: Qingli Lin, Ming-Chun Tang, Mei Li, Richard W. Ziolkowski
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a flexible electrically small (ES) Yagi-Uda antenna featuring a substantially enhanced bandwidth. A printed slow-wave transmission line (SW-TL)-based curved periodic grid is developed. It enables a low-frequency resonance in spite of the structure's electrically small size. The antenna adopts a classic Yagi-Uda configuration, comprising a driven element and a reflector both implemented as curved SW-TLs, plus a director. The mutual couplings between these elements facilitate the generation of three overlapping resonant modes, whose combination yields an exceptionally wide operational bandwidth. The optimized prototype was fabricated and tested. Its electrical size was ka = 0.94 < 1. In good agreement with simulations, the measured results confirm an impedance bandwidth of 36.9% (|S11| < -10 dB) that encompasses a unidirectional bandwidth (FTBR > 10 dB) of 29.4%. Furthermore, it is demonstrated that this ES Yagi-Uda antenna is highly flexible, maintaining very stable performance characteristics under various bending conditions.

title: Multi-Tier UAV Edge Computing Towards Long-Term Energy Stability for Low Altitude Networks
authors: Yufei Ye, Shijian Gao, Xinhu Zheng, Liuqing Yang
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: The agile mobility of Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) makes them ideal for low-altitude edge computing. This paper proposes a novel multi-tier UAV edge computing system where lightweight Low-Tier UAVs (L-UAVs) function as edge servers for vehicle users, supported by a powerful High-Tier UAV (H-UAV) acting as a backup server. The objective is to minimize task execution delays while ensuring the long-term energy stability of the L-UAVs, despite unknown future system states. To this end, the problem is decoupled using Lyapunov optimization, which adaptively balances the priorities of task delays and L-UAV energy cost based on their real-time energy states. An efficient vehicle to L-UAV matching scheme is designed, and the joint optimization problem for task assignment, computing resource allocation, and trajectory control of L-UAVs and H-UAV is then solved via a Block Coordinate Descent (BCD) algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate a reduction in L-UAV transmission energy of over 26% and superior L-UAV energy stability compared to existing benchmarks.

title: Cascade Skip-Connection BiLSTM Autoencoder for CPR Artifact Removal Prior to AED Shock Advisory
authors: Jaechan Lim, David Hicks, Matt Valentine, Ki H. Chon
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Background: Current automated external defibrillators require pauses in cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) for reliable ECG rhythm analysis, reducing chest compression fraction and delaying defibrillation. Methods: We propose a skip-connection BiLSTM autoencoder (SBAE) that removes CPR artifacts directly from one-dimensional ECG signals without auxiliary reference signals or time-frequency transformation. A cascade architecture employs a balanced model for initial denoising followed by biased models whose reconstruction is optimized toward one rhythm class. A conservative routing strategy labels cases where the two stages disagree as indeterminate. Performance was evaluated through 10-fold cross-validation with artifact-level data separation using an FDA-approved shock advisory algorithm. Results: The cascade SBAE achieved 97.51 $\pm$ 1.91% sensitivity for ventricular fibrillation, 94.58 $\pm$ 3.39% for ventricular tachycardia, 99.30 $\pm$ 0.28% specificity for normal sinus rhythm, 98.02 $\pm$ 3.02% for asystole, and 97.21 $\pm$ 1.05% for other non-shockable rhythms, exceeding American Heart Association thresholds for all categories. The indeterminate rate was 3.63 $\pm$ 1.10%. Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a compact, reference-free solution for shock advisory decision support in automated external defibrillators. Deployment on embedded AED hardware would require additional model optimization and platform-specific engineering.

title: TS-WAEM: A Two-Stage Waveform-Aware Adaptive Ensemble Method for Magnetic Core Loss Prediction
authors: Shuochen Nie, Xiangqun Li, Junhong Yue, Liye Jia
abstract: Abstract— Magnetic core loss critically affects the performance of power electronic systems. Traditional empirical core-loss models often break down under complex operating conditions, whereas existing machine-learning and deep-learning approaches are typically sensitive to variations in excitation waveforms and often require a large amount of training data to achieve reliable performance. Therefore, this paper proposes a two-stage waveform-aware adaptive ensemble method (TS-WAEM) for magnetic core loss prediction. The framework adaptively constructs optimal ensemble regression models for different waveforms, addressing the strong model dependence on specific combinations and improving prediction accuracy across all scenarios. In the Waveform Recognition and Task Assignment Stage, the model identifies the excitation waveform of each input sample and assigns it to the corresponding subtask given the known material type. In the Adaptive Ensemble Core-Loss Prediction Stage, a candidate pool of base regressors is constructed for each subtask, and an adaptive screening scheme that integrates a multivariate scoring mechanism and a type-aware penalty strategy is employed to determine the optimal base regressor combination. Finally, a stacking ensemble strategy feeds the outputs of selected base regressors into a meta-learner for secondary learning, further enhancing prediction accuracy. Experimental results show that the proposed model achieves an R2 exceeding 0.99 with an average relative error below 0.2%, demonstrating high prediction accuracy and strong adaptability. This provides intelligent decision support for the maintenance and replacement of magnetic core components.

title: DRL-based Trajectory Planning for UAV-Assisted Communication Systems
authors: Shiguo Wang, Binying Zhou, Xiukai Ruan, Qingyong Deng
abstract: Owing to the inherent superiority in high mobility and flexibility, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs)-enabled communication has emerged as a novel paradigm to enhance signal strength and coverage by serving as moving aerial relays. However, the existing schemes usually focus on one-way communication manner and are inflexible for changing environments and user number. To address these issues, we present a two-way UAV-enabled communication system when the practical factors, such as energy-constraint, limited resource and the potential interference source and obstacles, are considered jointly, and a joint optimization problem of trajectory and time allocation factor is formulated to maximize system throughput. To solve the formulated problem efficiently, the time-allocation factor is first derived in closed form, thereby reducing the original joint optimization to trajectory design. Then, a scalable deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based framework with a fixed-dimensional state representation and a two-stage reward mechanism is developed, and the soft actor–critic (SAC) algorithm is adopted to optimize the UAV trajectory. Simulation results with varying settings demonstrate that our proposed scheme can improve system throughput significantly. It is also validated that the proposed scheme has better performance in convergence speed and robustness compared to the benchmark schemes.

title: Data-Driven Machine Learning Analysis of Pulse Compression in Praseodymium-Doped Tapered Photonic Crystal Fiber
authors: V Madhumitha, Faouzi Bahloul, R. Vasantha Jayakantha Raja
abstract: We present a realistic numerical model based on data-driven machine-learning (ML) techniques for generating high-energy ultrashort pulses at 4.5 μm. To achieve this objective, we propose a nonlinear amplifying loop mirror (NALM) configuration employing a self-similarly designed Praseodymium doped As<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> (Pr<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3+</sup>: As<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>) tapered photonic crystal fiber (TPCF) compressor, which enables simultaneous pulse amplification and compression. Owing to the increasing complexity of Pr<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3+</sup>: As<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> TPCF design, arising from the large parameter space associated with both fiber geometry and pulse characteristics, conventional numerical optimization becomes computationally expensive. Therefore, ML techniques are employed to optimize the TPCF structure and the input pulse parameters. Using the optimized design, we demonstrate that a 5 ps pulse at 4.5 μm can be compressed to 282.6 fs after propagation through 1.65 m of fiber, corresponding to a maximum compression factor of 17.68, with a pedestal energy of 2.217%.

title: Shoveling-Induced Slip Prediction for Distributed Electric Wheel Loaders: A Feature-Enhance CNN-BiLSTM Approach
authors: Li Haomin, Yao Zongwei, Dong Wenwen, Li Xuefei, Bi Qiushi
abstract: Due to superior power density, inherent overload protection, and manufacturing cost constraints, current electric wheel loaders widely retain hydraulic systems, which constrains their inherent fast-response advantages, rendering existing slip prediction models inadequate for the transient characteristics of distributed electric drive systems. This study proposes a dual-level solution encompassing mechatronic system redesign and intelligent prediction methodologies, targeting small-to-medium loaders in emission-sensitive applications. First, a novel fully electric distributed architecture is proposed, comprising three walking motors and three electric cylinders, which completely eliminates hydraulic systems and achieves drive mechanism decoupling. Then, a CNN-BiLSTM hybrid model is constructed to enable real-time classification of driving anomalies. The model extracts deep spatial correlation features from multisource signals via 2-D convolutions, while bidirectional LSTM networks capture full-time domain state evolution patterns during shoveling operations. Experimental validation through about 2000 shoveling trials demonstrates significant improvements: F1-score reaches 90.44% with 14.2% recall enhancement, achieving peak prediction accuracy of 98.10%.

title: The Impact of Different Ionospheric Mapping Functions on Modeling Global Ionospheric Vertical Total Electron Content
authors: Jun Chen, Jiahuan Hu, Pan Li, Xiaodong Ren
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: A key step in ionosphere modeling is to convert slant total electron content (STEC) to vertical total electron content (VTEC) using an appropriate ionospheric mapping function (MF). This makes the accuracy of the applied MF a major factor affecting the performance of the built ionospheric model. Thus, this study evaluates ionospheric models constructed with three commonly used MFs, i.e., single-layer mapping function (SLM MF), modified single-layer mapping function (MSLM MF), and Klobuchar MF, from different aspects. Results show that the performance of the ionospheric model estimated with MSLM MF is the best, followed by SLM MF and Klobuchar MF. Compared with global ionosphere maps provided by the international Global Navigation Satellite System (IGSG), the MSLM MF-based ionospheric model achieves the best accuracy and consistency. During high solar activity, the SLM MF-based ionospheric model shows the largest discrepancies, which is up to about 40 TEC unit (TECU), while those of the MSLM MF-based model reduce to 10–15 TECU. Under low solar activity, the differences decrease, with the MSLM MF-based model maintaining the smallest bias and root mean square (RMS), which are -1.43 TECU and 2.48 TECU, respectively. The largest errors appear in equatorial regions (±15° latitude), where strong ionospheric gradients exist. Over oceanic regions, the MSLM MF-based ionospheric model still performs best, keeping bias smaller than 5 TECU and RMS smaller than 6 TECU, while the errors of the SLM MF-based ionospheric model can reach 15 TECU. Self-consistency assessments further confirm the stability of the MSLM MF-based ionospheric model, with bias and RMS below 1.0 TECU and 2.0 TECU, significantly outperforming those of the SLM MF-based and Klobuchar MF-based ionospheric models. Furthermore, the single-frequency precise point positioning tests also indicate that MSLM MF-based ionospheric corrections yield the best positioning accuracy, with horizontal and vertical errors typically ranging from 0.2–0.7 m and 0.4–1.0 m, respectively. Overall, the MSLM MF demonstrates the most reliable and consistent performance in ionospheric modeling under varied solar conditions, across diverse regions, and according to multiple metrics.

title: Hyperbolic Geometry-Guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptive Semantic Segmentation in Remote Sensing
authors: Jiaxin Yan, Wenzhe Liu, Hongfu Li, Jing Zhang, Qianqi Lu, Yanming Guo, Yuxiang Xie
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Unsupervised domain adaptive semantic segmentation (UDA-SS) for remote sensing imagery remains challenging due to the substantial distribution shifts across regions and sensors. Existing teacher–student methods mainly rely on pixel-level consistency and high-confidence pseudo-label fitting in Euclidean space. However, these strategies provide only local supervision and fail to preserve the global geometric structure of semantic classes, leading to cluster drift, weak class discrimination, and error accumulation in challenging regions such as object boundaries and fine-grained structures. To address these issues, we propose HyperDASeg, a hyperbolic geometry-guided framework for UDA-SS in remote sensing. Specifically, HyperDASeg adopts a two-stage adaptation paradigm on the Poincaré ball, transforming hyperbolic geometry from an auxiliary embedding choice into an explicit adaptation prior that jointly governs representation shaping and self-training. In the first stage, hyperbolic prototype distillation (HPD) leverages a vision foundation model as the teacher and performs prototype-based structured distillation in hyperbolic space, encouraging compact intra-class clustering and clear inter-class separation. In the second stage, we introduce a hyperbolic radius uncertainty-weighted (HRU) self-training strategy, which uses the hyperbolic radius to estimate pixel uncertainty and progressively emphasizes hard target regions that are often overlooked by conventional high-confidence-first learning. Extensive experiments conducted on the ISPRS and LoveDA datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. On the ISPRS dataset, HyperDASeg outperforms previous state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods by an average of 3.53% in terms of mean IoU (mIoU) across four adaptation tasks; on the LoveDA dataset, it outperforms previous SOTA methods by 1.32% in the mIoU value.

title: FT-ESO Based Fixed-Time Secondary Control for DC Microgrids with Unknown External Disturbances
authors: Ke-Xin Ma, Yu Zhang, Xiao-Kang Liu, Feng Xiao, Yan-Wu Wang
abstract: Reliable stability and fast response of DC microgrids are gaining wide attention, especially for those subject to disturbances imposed by renewable resources and uncertain loads. In this paper, a novel fixed-time extended state observer (FT-ESO) based secondary controller is constructed for DC microgrid with unknown external disturbances, enabling both voltage regulation and current sharing within a fixed settling time. In the control scheme, a novel compensation term together with an adaptive mechanism is designed to mitigate both disturbances and residual observation errors from FT-ESO, ensuring practical fixed-time convergence of the voltage and current tracking errors. This integration further improves the performance of convergence time and anti-disturbance. The proposed FT-ESO based controller is evaluated through MATLAB/Simulink simulations and experimental tests under several scenarios. Comparison studies further demonstrate the fast convergence rate and strong disturbance rejection capability of the proposed controller.

title: Real-Time 2-D-to-1-D Decomposition of Roesser Models via Sylvester Equation Transformation With Interval-Constrained Balanced Model Reduction
authors: Muhammad Imran, Hira Ambreen, Gaojuan Fan, Chongsheng Zhang
abstract: The complexity of 2-D systems makes them challenging to analyze and manage. Basic algebraic operations are more intricate in 2-D frameworks than in their 1-D counterparts. Standard 2-D systems can typically only be decomposed into 1-D subsystems if they are represented in a separable denominator form. This research introduces a novel transformation that facilitates the decomposition of a 2-D standard system into a diagonalized 2-D representation; this further enables its partitioning into two 1-D subsystems, bypassing the constraints of minimal rank decomposition criteria. The proposed framework (PF) retains the geometrical symmetry of the resultant 1-D submodels while maintaining balance across the submodels, making them suitable for model reduction in large-scale systems. Furthermore, a novel framework for model reduction is developed to address limitations in existing limited-interval (LI) methods, such as instability within the reduced system. The numerical findings indicate that the proposed transformations adequately diagonalize the standard 2-D system and ensure a stable reduced 2-D model.

title: Electrical Degradation and Trapped Charge Evolution in SiC MOSFETs Under Power-Cycling Stress
authors: Shijie Pan, Shanhai Gu, Shiwei Feng, Boyang Zhang, Xuan Li, Xiaozhuang Lu, Haibing Li, Maran Wu, Yamin Zhang
abstract: The reliability of SiC MOSFETs under repetitive power cycling is a critical concern for power converter design. This work investigates the degradation behavior in SiC MOSFETs under power-cycling stress, and the trap analysis is carried out based on transient current method. Five samples are stressed under forward conduction with load current from 52.5 to 73.2 A. An inverse relationship is observed between the applied current and the number of cycles, determined by a 5% increase in the on-state voltage under hot conditions. Post-stress electrical characterizations reveal the positive threshold voltage shift and increased drain leakage current, with the degradation being more pronounced with an increasing number of cycles. Based on the actual transient current variations of samples, the trapping behaviors governed by exponential decay are extracted using Bayesian deconvolution. Three trap levels are identified with increased amplitudes under larger cycle count, and the activation energies are almost unchanged after power-cycling by the temperature-dependent measurements. It can be attributed to the increase in traps near the SiC/SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> interface and within the gate oxide especially under higher cycle count. By directly linking macroscopic degradation to the evolution of microscopic traps, it provides profound insights for the reliability assessment of SiC MOSFETs in power electronic systems.

title: A Resilient Edge-Cloud Orchestration Framework for Sustaining Tactical Intelligence Continuity in LLM-Enabled V2X Systems
authors: Qingqing Liu, Peilong Zhang, Lingbing Meng, Huihui Han
abstract: Integrating Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) into Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) systems represents a fundamental prerequisite for achieving cognitive-level autonomous driving. However, the substantial computational requirements of MLLMs present a significant challenge to the inherent volatility of edge-cloud communication channels, often leading to severe reasoning interruptions during network jitters or stochastic link transitions.To bridge this chasm, we introduce the Resilient Edge-Cloud (REC) framework, a mission-critical orchestration system leveraging multimodal sensor streams (Camera and LiDAR) specifically engineered to perpetuate tactical intelligence continuity. The REC framework operates via three symbiotic components: (1) a RepDistiller (Module A) that executes cross-scale tactical semantic alignment via Knowledge Distillation (KD) to compress the cloud model’s reasoning heuristics into the edge surrogate; (2) a Robust Collaborative Fusion layer (Module B) that provides link-aware feature synchronization by internalizing transmission latency into Delay-aware Positional Encodings (DPE); and (3) an RL-CBF Orchestrator (Module C) that enforces task-offloading within a mathematically rigorous cognitive safety envelope by integrating Reinforcement Learning with Control Barrier Functions. Rigorous evaluations across LLM-CARLA and V2X-Sim 2.0 benchmarks demonstrate that REC achieves a superior trade-off between reasoning accuracy and inference latency. Specifically, REC maintains 86.5% intelligence continuity and achieves a 2.7x speedup relative to standard cloud-centric offloading by eliminating network-induced queuing delays during communication collapse,functioning as a robust contingency mechanism in scenarios where conventional static paradigms exhibit significant performance degradation.The official implementation of the REC framework is available at: https://github.com/peilongzhang88/REC-Framework.

title: Optimal Synthesis of Reconfigurable Sparse Arrays with Dynamic Range Ratio Constraints
authors: Xuhui Fan, Wen Fan, Tao Wang, H. C. So, Yang Jing
abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) are employed in a variety of applications, leading to a significant demand for advanced antenna systems that can perform multiple distinct tasks from a single, compact hardware platform. In response to this demand, we propose a unified synthesis framework for reconfigurable sparse arrays based on a min-max beampattern matching metric. This framework integrates null/notch constraints, common excitation magnitudes, and explicit dynamic range ratio (DRR) bounds. Our approach distinguishes itself from existing research by addressing the impact of constrained DRR on excitation magnitudes, which is crucial for mitigating mutual coupling and simplifying feed networks. To solve the resultant nonconvex and nonlinear optimization problem, we introduce a Boolean selection vector, transforming the antenna position optimization problem into an antenna selection optimization problem. We then employ alternating minimization to decouple the antenna selection vector from the excitation vector. For each subproblem related to the weight vector and the antenna selection vector, we utilize successive convex approximation to derive a convex surrogate function for the nonconvex components of the subproblems. To ensure the feasibility of the transformed problem, we further introduce slack variables. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the developed approach facilitates precise control of multiple patterns with hardware-efficient sparse configurations for dynamic electromagnetic operations.

title: CIG-MAE: Cross-Modal Information-Guided Masked Autoencoder for Self-Supervised WiFi Sensing
authors: Gang Liu, Yanling Hao, Yixuan Zou
abstract: Human Action Recognition using WiFi Channel State Information (CSI) has emerged as an attractive alternative to vision-based methods due to its ubiquity, device-agnostic nature, and inherent privacy-preserving capabilities. However, the high cost of manual annotation and the limited scale of publicly available CSI datasets restrict the performance of supervised approaches. Self-supervised learning (SSL) offers a promising avenue, but existing contrastive paradigms rely on data augmentations that conflict with the physical semantics of radio signals and require large-batch training, making them poorly suited for CSI. To overcome these challenges, we introduce CIG-MAE—a Cross-Modal Information-Guided Masked Autoencoder—that reconstructs both the amplitude and phase of CSI using a symmetric dual-stream architecture with a high masking ratio. Specifically, we propose an Adaptive Information-Guided Masking strategy that dynamically allocates attention to time–frequency regions with high information density to improve learning efficiency, and we incorporate a Barlow Twins regularizer to align cross-modal representations without negative samples. Experiments on three public datasets show that CIG-MAE consistently outperforms SOTA SSL methods and even surpasses a fully supervised baseline, demonstrating superior data efficiency, robustness, and representation generalization.

title: High Sample Utilization Reinforcement Learning Algorithm Based on MMD Distance Symmetric Causal Inference
authors: Jiazheng Sun, Weiguang Li
abstract: The training of reinforcement learning algorithms has been suffering from the problem of lack of judgment on causality, thus neglecting the hidden variables and model identification between the interaction between the agent and the environment. In this article, from the perspective of causality inference, we innovatively adopt a symmetric bidirectional generative network to train and model causality based on samples collected by the agent, through which the model can identify the hidden variables in the environment. Based on the hidden variables of the model, the algorithm proposed in this paper can generate counterfactual samples based on real samples, thus improving the sample utilization rate of reinforcement learning. To further improve the computational efficiency of the algorithm, this paper proposes the method of replacing the discriminant network from the maximum mean distance and constructs a symmetric two-way causality inference reinforcement learning algorithm based on the maximum mean distance. In the comparison experiments with related algorithms, the algorithm reflects higher training efficiency without adding additional instability.

title: Research on Identification Method of Critical Nodes for Power Grid Support Capability
authors: Weigang Jin, Fengjiao Li, He Zou, Lingxue Lin
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the large-scale integration of renewable energy sources and the increasing penetration of high-voltage direct current (HVDC) tratimission, power systems face escalating demands for enhanced support capability. Identifying critical nodes that influence power grid support capability is crucial for optimally configuring energy equipment to strengthen system stability. Therefore, this paper proposes an evaluation index system for indentifying critical support capability nodes, encompassing three primary indicators - power grid structure, voltage support, and frequency support, and five secondary indicators, including node electrical coupling degree and node equivalent inertia. To address the differences in indicator evaluation results, a hybrid evaluation model is established, integrating the entropy weight method and Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to an Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) as individual evaluations techniques, with the fuzzy Borda method as the combined evaluation approach for identifying critical support capability nodes. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through case studies on a modified IEEE 39bus system, demonstrating its practical applicability and robustness.

authors: Danielle Wilde, Mary Karyda

authors: Marcus Foth, Leo Rezayan, Kiara Fourie

title: DWDMixer: Detail-Aware Wavelet Decomposition and Mixing for Time Series Forecasting in IoT Systems
authors: Pengcheng Zhang, Wei Huang, Jie Wang, Ran Peng, Qiang Zhai, Xiaocao Ouyang
abstract: Accurate time series forecasting is essential for proactive management and resource allocation in intelligent IoT systems, where data are often affected by sensor noise, multi-scale periodicity, and cross-frequency interactions. Although decomposition-based frameworks are widely adopted, most existing approaches rely on time-domain modeling with limited nonlinear capacity or treat frequency decomposition as a generic preprocessing step. Such designs fail to align model inductive bias with the intrinsic multi-scale structure of seasonal dynamics, leading to incomplete seasonal representations and degraded forecasting performance. To address this limitation, we propose Detail-aware Wavelet Decomposition and Mixing (DWDMixer), a seasonal-centric forecasting architecture built on multi-scale representation learning and seasonal-trend decomposition, where trend components serve as complementary signals. To enable fine-grained seasonal modeling, we propose a Detail-aware Wavelet Transform Hybrid (DWTH) block that performs hierarchical wavelet decomposition guided by detail-sensitive representations. DWTH captures cross-scale and cross-frequency interactions while jointly modeling temporal and spectral information, yielding expressive multi-resolution representations. Extensive experiments on 10 benchmark datasets demonstrate that DWDMixer significantly outperforms state-of-the-art models, achieving error reductions of up to 9% in MSE for long-term forecasting and 22% in MAE for short-term forecasting. Our code is available at https://github.com/zpc2002zpc/DWDMixer.

title: Graph Continual Learning Based Lithium-Ion Battery State of Health Prediction With Longer-Range Patterns Discovering
authors: Kai Zhong, Yinglai Deng, Darong Huang, Jiaqiang Tian, Xinghua Liu, Peng Wang
abstract: Accurate state-of-health (SOH) prediction of lithium-ion batteries is critical for ensuring operational safety, reliability, and extending service lifetime in energy storage systems. Existing methods for predicting the SOH of lithiumion batteries have predominantly focused on per-cycle estimation, often overlooking two critical longer-range patterns: latent temporal dependencies across cycles within single battery and continual sharing knowledge across battery packs, both of which are critical for capturing degradation patterns. To address this, we propose a graph continual learning framework with longer-range patterns discovering. First, to explicitly characterize cross-cycle temporal dependencies, a temporal graph representation is formulated through an enhanced multi-order dynamic time warping, wherein directed edges deterministically encode inter-cycle relational patterns. Then, an edge-oriented network is designed to depict temporal representations and aggregation along directed edges, further augmented with a global cell state to guide the capture of persistent memory across batteries. After that, a replay continual learning strategy is adopted to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, combined with lightweight graph distillation to significantly reduce memory overhead while ensuring practical deployment. Finally, the proposed method achieves an average root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.693% in a real-world lithium-ion battery dataset, yielding a 23.09% average improvement over baseline methods, even with only 1.25% of original data volume, thereby demonstrating its effectiveness and strong generalization capability.

title: Context-Aware Itinerary Planning in Smart Tourism: IoT Enabled Design and Tourist Profiling
authors: Claudio Marche, Vlad Popescu, Luigi Atzori, Michele Nitti
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is increasingly supporting the tourism sector by enabling adaptive services that enhance the visitor experience. Among emerging applications, itinerary planning has gained significant attention, leveraging IoT data to deliver personalized and adaptive routes. However, current systems show key limitations. Personalization often depends on static forms rather than adaptive learning; validation is usually restricted to simulations, and the few real implementations mostly rely on mobile apps, tools that tourists are reluctant to download and often abandon after limited use. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes an itinerary planning system that integrates reinforcement learning for tourist profiling with a genetic algorithm for multi-objective optimization, implemented in a real-world scenario through a cloud infrastructure and delivered via an interactive totem that serves as the access point for tourists. Results confirm both the efficiency and scalability of the approach, showing that the system can be seamlessly extended to diverse urban contexts.

title: Learning Approach to Optimal Grasping Planning Using Vision-Based Tactile Perception
authors: Yingbai Hu, Ganyu Wu, Jianghua Duan, Yipai Du, Hu Cao, Fuzhou Niu, Yongquan Chen, Yueyue Liu, Alois Knoll
abstract: Wearable devices with tactile sensors can assist paralyzed or amputee patients by providing real-time feedback for precise control, leading to improved mobility, grasping functions, and rehabilitation outcomes. This paper introduces an innovative vision-based tactile sensor designed for effective grasping planning using gripper, combining a compact, modular architecture with comprehensive contact measurement capabilities. Determining optimal grasping points and forces is crucial for ensuring safe and stable object grasping. To address this, we present a deep learning framework for grasping force optimization, designed to ensure form-closure. Our approach employs a deep neural network to efficiently solve the optimization problem by finding optimal solutions within constraint regions, enabling reliable and secure grasp configurations. The effectiveness of our method is demonstrated through experiments with grippers equipped with vision-based tactile sensors, where results show that our approach successfully plans and executes stable grasps from various initial conditions, achieving form-closure with enhanced precision. Compared to traditional solvers, our deep learning-based method achieves nearly 3× faster inference speed in grasping force optimization, while maintaining comparable accuracy.

title: Symbolic-in-the-Loop: Leveraging Knowledge Graph Engineering to Ensure High-Assurance Generative Artificial Intelligence Outcomes
authors: Venkatesan Nadimuthu, Archy Das, Vatsalya Soni, Amit P. Sheth
abstract: Large language models excel at complex reasoning and natural language processing. However, probabilistic next-token prediction makes them inherently vulnerable to factual hallucinations. This unpredictability prevents their unmonitored use in highly regulated, mission-critical enterprise environments. To support high-assurance autonomous execution, we propose shifting from context engineering to knowledge graph (KG) engineering. We introduce the symbolic-in-the-loop (SITL) verification framework, a novel neurosymbolic architecture. Traditional retrieval augmented generation uses KGs for pregeneration context. Instead, SITL repositions the KG at the end of the pipeline as a formal verification layer. This postgeneration symbolic check reliably grounds probabilistic neural output into a deterministic ontology. The architecture seamlessly accommodates dynamic KG updates. This ensures validation against the most current symbolic state of an enterprise. We evaluate SITL across applications ranging from personal automation to multiagent orchestration. Experiments show this terminal KG approach successfully eliminates semantic drift. Ultimately, SITL ensures provably accurate outputs, replacing traditional human-in-the-loop models with scalable automation.

title: Special Issue on Quantum Networks: Part II
authors: Qun Li, Marcello Caleffi, Yu Wang
abstract: This second special issue on quantum networking brings together four contributions that collectively capture the state of the art, spanning theory, system design, and experimental implementation.

title: Frequency-Oriented HLS Accelerator Exploration via Congestion-Aware Mapping on Multi-Die FPGA
authors: Teng Wang, Yingxue Gao, Haoran Xue, Qianyu Cheng, Xianglan Chen, Lei Gong, Changlong Li, Chao Wang, Xuehai Zhou, Huaping Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Researchers use high-level synthesis (HLS) to develop large-scale accelerators, leveraging extensive resources to handle intricate computational tasks. Recent Multi-Die FPGAs feature several super logic regions (SLR) within one device to meet the high resource demands of advanced computation. However, implementing a large-scale accelerator on multi-die FPGAs presents three obstacles. The first is the considerable delay penalty caused by the interconnectivity of accelerator modules across multi-die boundaries. The second is the congestion caused by modules’ excessive use of local resources. The third is that the increasing number of accelerator modules expands the design space, making it difficult to find the optimal design point. All of these pose limitations on the maximum achievable frequency for developing large-scale accelerator designs on multi-die FPGAs. Therefore, this paper proposes a frequency-oriented design exploration with the congestion-aware module mapping algorithm, FrqBooster. It combines modules’ location and resource consumption to provide coarse-grained floorplanning optimization. Firstly, we analyze the connections between accelerator modules and build optimization objectives. Secondly, we analyze the resource cost of each module in accelerator design and propose a two-stage resource-balancing strategy, combined with resource consumption, to alleviate local congestion. Finally, we design a congestion-aware algorithm to complete the overall exploration for achieving higher potential performance. In the experiments, results show that FrqBooster achieves maximum frequency improvements of 73.15% and 81.22% on the U250 and U280 platforms, respectively, compared to the default implementation in Vivado. Compared to related works on accelerator DSE scenarios, FrqBooster achieved a maximum improvement of 25.84×, resulting in better overall performance.

title: Editorial
authors: N/A
abstract: Dear Readers, Welcome to the June issue of the IEEE Instrumentation & Measurement Magazine. In our regular issues, we take the opportunity to celebrate the achievements and ongoing initiatives within our community. We hear updates from the Society via Shervin Shirmohammadi and explore the highly anticipated New Products with Robert Goldberg. We also have a special Spotlight on I&Mer column where Max Cortner highlights Dr. Yunjie Yang from the University of Edinburgh, the recipient of the 2024 IEEE J. Barry Oakes Award. In our Collaboration and Engagement column, Ruth Dyer profiles the career and leadership of our Rising Star, Judy Amanor-Boadu.

title: Robust Domain Modeling of Electric Vehicles and its Optimal Operation Strategy for Energy-Frequency Regulation
authors: Bingyu Zhang, Qian Jiang, Wei Dai
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: As a typical flexible and adjustable resource, electric vehicles (EVs) can provide frequency regulation auxiliary services for the power grid after being aggregated by electric vehicle aggregators (EVAs). However, multiple uncertainties such as EV connection time, disconnection time, and energy demand, make it difficult for EVAs to fully utilize to the flexibility of large-scale EV clusters. This paper constructs a robust domain model for aggregated EV cluster power under multiple uncertainties, and proposes a profit-oriented two-stage scheduling strategy for EVAs participating in frequency regulation services. First, the complex mapping relationship between multiple uncertainties and the energy boundaries of adjustable power is quantified, based on which a robust domain model of aggregated power of EV clusters is established. Then, considering the cooperative and complementary capabilities of EV frequency regulation capabilities under multi-time scales, a day-ahead and intra-day two-stage optimal scheduling model aiming at maximizing the operational benefits of EVAs is constructed. The day-ahead stage solves the global optimal scheduling plan, and the intra-day stage adopts a rolling optimization method to locally optimize and adjust the scheduling plan. Simulation results show that the proposed scheduling strategy can accurately characterize the adjustable power capability of EV clusters under multiple uncertainties and improve the economic benefits of EVAs and EVs participating in frequency regulation auxiliary services.

title: CEEMDAN and Wavelet Thresholding for HVDC Power Quality Denoising
authors: Dezhi Zheng, Jiawen Ren, Chun Hu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Accurate perception of high-voltage direct current transmission systems is critical for grid security and stability. In such systems, strong corona-induced impulsive noise often obscures transient disturbance features, while conventional denoising methods lead to waveform distortion and inaccurate modal separation. To address this issue, a hybrid method combining adaptive modal decomposition and improved wavelet thresholding is proposed. A geometric impulsiveness index is introduced to distinguish noise-dominated and signal-dominated components, followed by nonlinear filtering of noisy modes. Results show that the method suppresses oscillations and amplitude distortion while preserving transient features such as commutation notches. Under a 10 dB noise condition, the signal-to-noise ratio is improved by 17.69 dB on average, demonstrating enhanced robustness and reconstruction accuracy in complex electromagnetic environments.

title: Neuromuscular Control of CT and LCA Muscles in Human Vocal Pitch and Register Regulation: Insights from sEMG Analysis_supp1-3695957.pdf
authors: Liang Wu
abstract: Objective: Precise regulation of vocal pitch and register relies on the activity of intrinsic laryngeal muscles. This study aims to elucidate the neuromuscular mechanisms of the cricothyroid (CT) and lateral cricoarytenoid (LCA) muscles during pitch modulation and register transitions, and to further investigate their associations with voice quality. Methods: Sixteen healthy participants were recruited to produce five sustained vowels while performing pitch glides spanning the Chest and Falsetto registers. Surface electromyography (sEMG) signals of the CT and LCA muscles were measured using a validated approach, along with synchronized speech signals. Subsequently, sEMG features were extracted and analyzed for correlations with pitch and voice quality metrics. Results: During pitch regulation, the CT and LCA muscles exhibited directionally symmetric activation patterns (“rise-fall-rise” during pitch ascent and “fall rise-fall” during pitch descent) regardless of vowel type. Their control over pitch was stable in the Chest/Falsetto register but less stable in the Transition region. Additionally, muscle activation improved voice quality in the Chest register, yet had constrained effects in the Transition region and Falsetto register. Conclusion: The CT and LCA muscles show activation patterns of different intensities, which contribute to precise pitch control and register transitions. The core function of these two muscles lies in pitch regulation, while voice quality modulation is not solely determined by them. Significance: This study provides non-invasive empirical evidence revealing muscular control strategies in human phonation, thereby advancing the understanding of the physiological mechanisms underlying voice production and offering implications for vocal training and clinical assessment.

title: Communication-Efficient Federated Learning via Fractional-Order Gradient Descent With Adaptive Momentum Under Non-IID Data
authors: Mahmudul Hasan, Yong Xiang, Md Palash Uddin, Jine Tang, John Yearwood, Longxiang Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without centralized data sharing, yet its practical deployment is often hindered by slow convergence and excessive communication overhead, particularly under non-Independent and non-Identically Distributed (non-IID) data distributions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes Federated Fractional Order Gradient Descent with Adaptive Momentum (FedFOGDAM), a novel client-side optimization framework that integrates fractional-order gradients with adaptive momentum and bias correction to accelerate convergence and reduce the number of communication rounds (CRs) required for model convergence. By leveraging the memory effect of fractional-order calculus, FedFOGDAM stabilizes local updates and mitigates client drift, while adaptive momentum enhances update consistency across heterogeneous clients. We provide a rigorous convergence analysis under non-convex objectives, demonstrating improved convergence behavior compared to conventional integer-order optimizers. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets under both IID and non-IID settings show that FedFOGDAM consistently achieves communication-efficient convergence and competitive accuracy.

title: Post-Silicon Deception: Evasive Hardware Trojan Through Adversarial Power Trace
authors: Behnam Omidi, Ihsen Alouani, Khaled N. Khasawneh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: The globalization of the Integrated Circuit (IC) supply chain, driven by time-to-market and cost considerations, has made ICs vulnerable to hardware Trojans (HTs). Against this threat, a promising approach is to use Machine Learning (ML)-based side-channel analysis, which has the advantage of being a non-intrusive method, along with efficiently detecting HTs under golden chip-free settings. In this paper, we question the trustworthiness of ML-based HT detection via side-channel analysis. We introduce an HT obfuscation (HTO) approach to allow HTs to bypass this detection method. Rather than theoretically misleading the model by simulated adversarial traces, a key aspect of our approach is the design and implementation of adversarial noise as part of the circuitry, alongside the HT. We detail HTO methodologies for ASICs and FPGAs, and evaluate our approach using the TrustHub benchmark. Interestingly, we found that HTO can be implemented with one single transistor for ASIC designs to generate adversarial power traces that can fool the defense with 100% efficiency. We also efficiently implemented our approach on a Spartan 6 Xilinx FPGA using 2 different variants: (i) DSP slices-based, and (ii) ring-oscillator-based design. Additionally, we assess the efficiency of countermeasures like spectral domain analysis, and we show that an adaptive attacker can still design evasive HTOs by constraining the design with a spectral noise budget. In addition, while adversarial training (AT) offers higher protection against evasive HTs, AT models suffer a considerable utility loss, potentially rendering them unsuitable for such a security application. We believe that this research represents a significant step in understanding and exploiting ML vulnerabilities in a hardware security context, and we make all resources and designs openly available online: https://github.com/beomidi/HW-Trojan-UAP-Generation

title: A Multi-Phase Multi-Path Hybrid SC Buck Converter with Enhanced DCR-Loss Reduction and Alleviated I CF Gathering for 9-48 V-Input PoL Conversions
authors: Huihua Li, Qiaobo Ma, Jiahao Shi, Sung-Wan Hong, Yang Jiang, Rui P. Martins, Pui-In Mak
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: This article presents a multi-phase multi-path hybrid switched-capacitor (SC) buck converter for 9-48 V-input point-of-load applications. The proposed topology uses interleaved SC cells operating at a higher frequency than the high-voltage stage, resulting in phase-balanced capacitor charge/discharge, thereby mitigating the effects of SC current gathering. In addition, the multi-phase operation decouples the high-voltage-stress power cells from high-current-conduction paths, reducing the inductor current under high-step-down conversions. Fabricated in a 180-nm SOI process with 5.7mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, the proposed converter achieves a measured peak efficiency of 88.3% and a current density of 176 A/cm³ over an input range of 9-48 V and an output of 0.8-1.2 V, using only 11.35 mm³ in passive volume.

title: A Novel n⁺/i-well Dot Ge₁₋ₓSnₓ-on-Si Single-Photon Avalanche Photodiode for High-Fill-Factor Room-Temperature Quantum Applications
authors: Harshvardhan Kumar, Advaita Sinha, P. Susthitha Menon, Jyoti Tater
abstract: We propose a novel design of Ge1-xSnx-on-Si single-photon avalanche photodiodes (SPADs) that aim to enhance the fill factor (FF) and minimize noise at room temperature. The device consists of a n+/i-well dot structure designed to eliminate the need for guard rings and multi-dot or array configurations typically used to enhance the active area. This study considers three distinct concentrations of Sn (4%, 6% and 8%) in the GeSn active layer and investigates their effect on performance metrics. The impact of the threading dislocation density in defective GeSn on the dark count rate (DCR) is also examined. The results show that a high Sn concentration has a notable impact on dark current. However, the DCR, single photon detection efficiency (SPDE), and noise-equivalent power (NEP) exhibit less sensitivity to variations in Sn concentration. All devices exhibit exceptionally low dark currents (<75 pA), a high GeSn absorption coefficient, and a high triggering probability (>95%), yielding a significant SPDE (>82.2%). Furthermore, the minimal DCR value (< 0.0095 Mcps) combined with a high SPDE results in a low noise equivalent power (< 0.02 fWHz-0.5) and high detectivity at λ = 1.55 μm and VEX = 5 V. The GeSn-on-Si SPADs here address the challenges faced by current Ge-on-Si, SiGe, InGaAs/AlGaAsSb, and InGaAs/InP SPADs regarding low operating temperatures, showcasing their promise for quantum photonics and communication applications at room temperature (T = 300 K).
