title: A Data-Driven Decision Architecture for Sustainable University Operations: Linking Regression-Based PCE Analytics to SDG-Oriented Improvement Regimes
authors: Mazin Alahmadi
abstract: Organizations increasingly face pressure to improve operational efficiency while advancing sustainability objectives. However, sustainability initiatives and digital transformation programs are often implemented without analytically grounded decision mechanisms capable of distinguishing between incremental improvement opportunities and the need for structural or technology-driven transformation. This study proposes a data-driven decision architecture that converts operational process analytics into sustainability-oriented governance guidance for higher education institutions. Using Lean value stream mapping across 61 end-to-end administrative processes in a large public university, Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE) was operationalized as the proportion of value-adding time within total process lead time. Structural waste drivers including waiting time, procedural complexity, and workflow fragmentation were modeled using multiple linear regression to estimate their marginal effects on PCE. After screening extreme delay observations, the final regression model estimated on 57 processes explains 36.23% of PCE variability (R2 = 0.3623, F = 10.04, it < 0.01), which is acceptable in complex service-process systems characterized by substantial unobservable and confounding operational factors. Each additional waiting day reduces efficiency by approximately 0.94%, while each additional workflow handoff reduces efficiency by 1.82%, whereas procedural step count is not statistically significant once structural waste is controlled. The regression intercept suggests an empirical operational efficiency boundary of approximately 70% under conditions of minimal structural waste, providing a comparative benchmark that distinguishes processes suitable for incremental Lean optimization from those requiring structural redesign or digital transformation. Predicted efficiency gains are translated into rule-weighted SDG proxy impacts related to SDGs 4, 9, 12, and 16. These SDG impacts are interpreted as operational sustainability contribution proxies rather than direct measures of SDG achievement. The proposed framework demonstrates how process analytics can support data-driven sustainability governance through linkage of operational efficiency improvements with sustainability-aligned institutional outcomes in service-intensive organizations.

title: Joint Communication and Computation Scheduling for MEC-enabled AIGC Services: A Game-Theoretic Stochastic Learning Approach
authors: Huaizhe Liu, Xinyi Zhuang, Jiaqi Wu, Yuan Luo, Bin Cao, Lin Gao
abstract: Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) powered by Generative Diffusion Models (GDMs) has emerged as a transformative paradigm for automated content creation. To satisfy the stringent latency requirements of AIGC services in many edge intelligence scenarios (e.g., smart cities), Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) provides critical computational support by deploying GDMs at edge servers (ES) close to end users. This paper investigates an MEC-enabled AIGC network comprising multiple ES, wireless access points (APs), and mobile users (UEs) with heterogeneous latency and accuracy demands. We formulate a Joint Communication Association and Computation Offloading (JCACO) game, where each UE strategically selects its serving AP, ES, and inference steps to minimize the overall service completion time while meeting accuracy constraints. The problem is challenging due to the network dynamics and the incomplete information. We prove that the JCACO game is a potential game under both complete and stochastic information settings, ensuring the existence of Nash Equilibrium (NE) in both cases. To derive the NE efficiently, we develop a distributed Multi-Agent Stochastic Learning (MASL) algorithm that provably converges to the NE with strict performance guarantees. Unlike conventional best-response schemes, MASL requires neither the knowledge of other players’ strategies nor global network information, making it fully distributed and adaptive to dynamic environments. We further provide a strict theoretical convergence analysis for MASL by using Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs). Simulation results demonstrate that MASL significantly reduces service completion time compared with benchmark methods while satisfying accuracy constraints, confirming its effectiveness and practicality for real-world MEC-enabled AIGC networks.

title: MARLTC: A Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning-based Interference-Aware Transmission Control for LoRaWAN IoT Devices
authors: Juan Aznar-Poveda, Laura Acosta-Garcia, Fabian Margreiter, Marlon Etheredge, Abolfazl Younesi, Stefan Pedratscher, Joan Garcia-Haro, Thomas Fahringer, Antonio-Javier Garcia-Sanchez
abstract: LoRaWAN has become a foundational technology in the Internet of Things (IoT) landscape due to its long-range communication and energy efficiency. However, its default Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) mechanism struggles to adapt to dynamic environments and dense network deployments, where co-channel interference and the coupling between transmission parameters limit its ability to ensure reliable and energy-efficient communication. To address these challenges, this paper proposes MARLTC, an ADR mechanism based on Multi-Agent Rein-forcement Learning (MARL) that jointly optimizes spreading factor and transmission power using realistic observable metrics, including recent transmission history, observed signal-to-noise ratios, and distribution of spreading factors in the network. The transmission configuration problem is modeled as a cooperative Markov Game and solved using the Centralized Training and Decentralized Execution (CTDE) paradigm, where pre-trained policies are deployed at end devices to infer suitable transmission parameters with reduced convergence time. The results using a realistic LoRaWAN simulator show that MARLTC achieves up to 67.9% faster convergence, 62.1% higher energy efficiency, and a 5.0% better packet delivery ratio compared to state-of-the-art approaches, highlighting its scalability and responsiveness in dense deployments. The practical feasibility of MARLTC is further validated using physical LoRaWAN hardware, proving that the resulting policies meet the memory and timing constraints of resource-constrained IoT devices.

title: Lightweight Mutual Authentication and Session Key Agreement for UAV Roaming in Multi-Fog Tactical Networks
authors: Jongkwan Lee, Minwoo Lee
abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) increasingly rely on fog-assisted tactical networks and must roam across multiple fog nodes (FNs) under disrupted, intermittent, and limited (DIL) connectivity. Roaming authentication in this setting faces stringent latency constraints, partial trust in FNs, and global state inconsistencies (e.g., stale or revoked credentials). This paper proposes a lightweight mutual authentication and session key agreement protocol that decouples a fast off-chain three-message handshake from permissioned-blockchain state anchoring, enabling consistent revocation and key-version enforcement without a centralized authority. Using only hash-based primitives (HMAC/HKDF), the protocol incurs low overhead (138 bytes per session) and is analyzed in a game-based security model. We explicitly define a scope-aware boundary: confidentiality is not claimed under exposure of an active FN masking key together with access to on-chain masked records; under compromise, the goal is to enforce revocation/version consistency and bound the exposure window via rotation. A trace-driven tail-sensitivity evaluation under Normal and DIL conditions shows stable roaming responsiveness, supporting practical deployment in tactical UAV networks.

title: Totoro$^+$: An Adaptive and Scalable Edge Federated Learning System
authors: Cheng-Wei Ching, Xin Chen, Taehwan Kim, Jian-Jhih Kuo, Dilma Da Silva, Liting Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) is an emerging distributed machine learning (ML) technique that enables in-situ model training and inference on decentralized edge devices. We propose Totoro<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^+$</tex-math></inline-formula>, a novel scalable FL system that enables massive FL applications to run simultaneously on edge networks. The key insight is to explore a distributed hash table (DHT)-based peer-to-peer (P2P) model to re-architect the centralized FL system design into a fully decentralized one. In contrast to previous studies where many FL applications shared one centralized parameter server, Totoro<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^+$</tex-math></inline-formula> assigns a dedicated parameter server to each application. Any edge node can act as any application's coordinator, aggregator, client selector, worker (participant device), or any combination of the above, thereby radically improving scalability and adaptivity. Totoro<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^+$</tex-math></inline-formula> introduces three innovations to realize its design: a locality-aware P2P multi-ring structure, a publish/subscribe-based forest abstraction, and a game-theoretic path planning model with a guarantee of an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon$</tex-math></inline-formula>-approximate Nash equilibrium. Real-world experiments on 500 Amazon EC2 servers show that Totoro<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^+$</tex-math></inline-formula> scales gracefully with the number of FL applications and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$N$</tex-math></inline-formula> edge nodes speeds up the total training time by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.2\times -14.0\times$</tex-math></inline-formula>, achieves <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(\log N)$</tex-math></inline-formula> hops for model dissemination and gradient aggregation with millions of nodes, and efficiently adapts to the practical edge networks and churns.

title: AlN and LGS Surface Acoustic Wave Resonators for Passive Wireless High-Temperature Sensing
authors: Yang Li, Zhen Li, Xiangshun Geng, Zijia Su, Guofang Yu, Jie Cui, Wenpu Cui, Jun Fu, Tian-Ling Ren
abstract: Surface acoustic wave (SAW) resonators demonstrate significant potential in passive wireless high-temperature sensing applications. However, their performance and reliability under extreme temperatures are fundamentally constrained by the thermal stability of the piezoelectric substrate. This paper presents the design, characterization, and sensing device of single-port SAW resonators based on aluminum nitride (AlN) and langasite (LGS) substrates. High-temperature experiments were conducted in air atmosphere from 25°C to 750°C using a muffle furnace, enabling real-time monitoring of the resonant frequency evolution. The AlN-based resonator, benefiting from a high phase velocity, achieved a resonant frequency of 2.1 GHz and exhibited superior temperature sensitivity, making it advantageous for developing compact, highly sensitive wireless sensors. However, it showed irreversible sensing characteristic drift during thermal cycling, limiting its stable operating temperature to below 600°C. In contrast, the LGS-based resonator maintains exceptional stability and repeatability over multiple thermal cycles up to 750°C. Furthermore, we successfully demonstrate a passive wireless sensing system using the LGS resonator, which enables precise temperature detection by monitoring shifts in the resonant frequency.

title: Dual-Beam Continuous-Scanning SSPP Leaky-Wave Antenna with Synergistic Phase-Reversal Modulation and Broadside Radiation Stabilization
authors: Jiong Zhao, Shuang Ma, Zhandong Li, Bo Wang, Xiaoyu Lan
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: A dual-beam continuous-scanning spoof surface plasmon polariton (SSPP) leaky-wave antenna (LWA) with synergistic phase-reversal modulation and broadside radiation stabilization is proposed and experimentally validated. The antenna is built on a composite SSPP guiding unit composed of T-shaped resonator elements and semi-elliptical patches, which provides SSPP-type slow-wave guidance and supports spatial-harmonic radiation. Periodic phase reversal is introduced to excite the target −1st spatial harmonic and alleviate the open-stopband (OSB) effect caused by Bragg-reflection accumulation near broadside. Different from an isolated radiating patch, the semi-elliptical patch is incorporated as part of the composite SSPP unit and participates in the modal and dispersion behavior. Owing to its relatively weaker field confinement, it further facilitates residual broadside energy leakage and stabilizes the radiation response. As a result, broadside-stable dual-beam continuous scanning is achieved without an obvious gain dip around broadside. The fabricated prototype scans continuously from −90° to 48° over 5.61–7.84 GHz, with a scanning rate of 4.16°/% and an average gain of 10.6 dBi. The measured results agree well with simulations.

title: TVRN: Invertible Neural Networks for Compression-Aware Temporal Video Rescaling
authors: Xinmin Feng, Li Li, Dong Liu, Feng Wu
abstract: To fit diverse display and bandwidth constraints, high-frame-rate videos are temporally downscaled to low-frame-rate (LFR) and later upscaled, requiring joint optimization for effective frame-rate rescaling. However, existing methods typically link the two operations via training objectives, without fully exploiting their reciprocal nature, which may cause high-frequency information loss. Moreover, they overlook the impact of lossy codecs on LFR videos, limiting real-world applicability. In this work, we propose an end-to-end framework for compression-aware frame-rate rescaling, named TVRN. To regularize high-frequency information lost during frame-rate downscaling, TVRN adopts an invertible architecture that combines a Multi-Input Multi-Output Temporal Wavelet Transform with a high-frequency reconstruction module. To enable end-to-end training through non-differentiable lossy codecs, we design a surrogate network that approximates their gradients. Finally, to improve robustness under various compression levels, we extend TVRN to an asymmetric architecture by incorporating compression-aware features learned via a learning-to-rank strategy. Extensive experiments show that TVRN outperforms existing methods in reconstruction quality under industrial video compression settings. Source code will be made publicly available.

title: Analysis of Electrical Characteristics of Ice-Shedding Jumping in Transmission Lines
authors: Jiayang Li, Minghao Wen, Qing Wan, Jieyu Chen, Yu Chen, Luchuan Fu, Shiming Zhang, Ke Han
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Under extreme weather conditions such as ice storms and freezing rain, power transmission lines are prone to ice accumulation. When ice detaches from transmission lines due to natural conditions like temperature changes, it causes vertical oscillations in the conductors known as ice-shedding jumps, posing a threat to the safe operation of the power grid. To analyze the electrical characteristics of transmission lines during ice-shedding jumps, this paper proposes a method combining finite element modeling with electrical parameter modeling for simulation. The finite element model provides the actual motion trajectory of ice shedding jumps, while the electrical parameter model employs a time-varying line model to simulate changes in electrical parameters caused by ice shedding jumps by altering line electrical parameters. Simulation analysis indicates that ice-shedding jumps on single-span transmission lines primarily affect the phase of the voltage difference between the two ends of the line. Furthermore, the electrical characteristics significantly diminish as the transmission line length increases. Considering the measurement accuracy of instrument transformers, it is impossible to extract the electrical characteristics of ice-shedding jumps on lines longer than 10 km.

title: Analysis and Handling of an Inter-Turn Short Circuit Fault in a Converter Transformer Winding
authors: Jin Wang, Sen Li, Xin Wang, Pengxiang Zhou, Sheng Feng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This paper elaborates on the analysis and handling process of a case of inter-turn short circuit fault in a converter transformer winding. Through comprehensive analysis of fault recordings, dissolved gas analysis (DGA), and diagnostic test data, the grid-side winding was identified with inter-turn short-circuit characteristics. Factory disassembly inspection revealed insulation wear and short-circuit traces in the grid-side winding, confirming the root cause as localized defects in turn insulation that ultimately failed under long-term vibration-induced wear, triggering the inter-turn short circuit. Based on this case study, improvement suggestions are proposed regarding converter transformer design/manufacturing, quality supervision, and operational maintenance testing, providing valuable references for ensuring the safe operation of converter transformers.

title: Dynamic Fault Probability Modeling and Risk Assessment of Source Network Equipment Under Extreme Cold Wave
authors: Yuhang Lin, Qing Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: As the key to ensure the safe operation of highproportion new energy power system, equipment fault modeling and risk assessment under extreme weather have important engineering value. However. Under the background of frequent cold waves, the traditional static model is difficult to describe the time series evolution law of faults. Its application is limited by the problems of icing growth, insufficient research on multimeteorological coupling failure mechanism and lack of Joule thermal ice melting effect of lines. Therefore, this paper introduces the time-varying state transition characteristics and multiphysical coupling failure mechanism, and proposes a dynamic modeling method for the failure probability of source network equipment under extreme cold wave. The dynamic fault models of wind power, photovoltaic and transmission lines are established, the failure process of multi-type equipment is described in detail, and the operation risk assessment process of cold wave full cycle system is constructed. The simulation results show that the proposed method accurately captures the dynamic coupling characteristics of meteorological elements and equipment faults, and effectively depicts the temporal evolution law of fault probability. By verifying the necessity of considering Joule heating ice melting, it provides a more realistic dynamic situation for evaluating line icing. This method refines the dynamic modeling system and provides a reliable basis for the risk early warning of power system in extreme cold wave weather.

title: Designing Community Trust through Collective Intergenerational Memory Sharing
authors: Sarah Nguyễn, Celestine Le, Rachel E Moran-Prestridge, Jason Young
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: Research on the Energy-Saving Effect of Active Grille Shutters
authors: Jingyuan Li, Hanzhengnan Yu, Ligang Lei, Xiaopan An
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This study systematically evaluates the energysaving performance of Active Grille Shutter (AGS) under lowtemperature conditions, addressing key gaps in existing research. By conducting controlled experiments on conventional internal combustion engine vehicles, we quantify AGS benefits through cabin warm-up rate analysis, speed-interval fuel consumption testing, and cycle-based evaluations. Empirical data confirm an average fuel reduction of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 \% - 7 \%}$</tex> under CLTC-P cycles, validated via dual-state test design with relative deviation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<4 \%$</tex>. A nationally weighted functional usage coefficient <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$K=0.3$</tex> is proposed, reflecting realistic operational frequencies. These results support refined evaluation frameworks for AGS integration into dual-credit policies, advancing lightweight vehicle energy efficiency standards.

title: A Joint Trading and Settlement Framework for Wind-Solar-Thermal-Storage Resources in the Unified National Electricity Market
authors: Fen Lin, Gengbo Tu, Xuejing Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To support the development of the unified national electricity market and improve renewable energy integration under high wind and photovoltaic (PV) penetration, this paper proposes a joint trading and settlement framework for wind-solar-thermal-storage resources. First, bidding strategies for different market participants in the energy market and the frequency regulation ancillary service market are designed, and a coordinated clearing model integrating security-constrained unit commitment (SCUC) and security-constrained economic dispatch (SCED) is established to optimize energy and ancillary service allocation in a unified manner. Second, a TIME-LLM-based day-ahead forecasting method for wind and PV output is introduced to provide renewable generation information for market bidding and clearing. Third, a corresponding settlement framework and a contribution-based internal revenue allocation rule for the wind-solar-thermal-storage coalition are developed to coordinate external market settlement and internal benefit sharing. Case studies are carried out on a modified IEEE 39-bus system using measured wind and PV data from Northwest China. The results show that the proposed framework can improve the coordinated operation of wind, solar, thermal, and storage resources, enhance renewable energy accommodation, and provide a practical reference for market-oriented renewable energy integration in the unified market environment.

title: A Hybrid QPSO-LSTM-CEEMDAN-Attention Model for Wind Power Forecasting
authors: Daixi Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In response to the issue of wind power generation being easily affected by random factors such as weather conditions, leading to insufficient prediction accuracy, this paper proposes a composite model for wind power prediction based on QPSO-LSTM-CEEMDAN-Attention. First, employ Complete Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition with Adaptive Noise (CEEMDAN) on wind power time series data, and then utilize the Attention mechanism to optimize the LSTM algorithm to accurately capture the key information affecting model predictions and enhance the weights of key features. Next, use the Quantum Particle Swarm Optimization (QPSO) algorithm to globally optimize the hyperparameters of the LSTM, and then build the LSTM model based on the obtained optimal hyperparameters to complete the prediction. Finally, the predicted results are fused to obtain the final prediction result, constructing the QPSO-LSTM-CEEMDAN-Attention wind power prediction model. Using the historical monitoring data of actual wind farms as the dataset, train and test the model. Experimental results show that the composite model effectively integrates the advantages of different algorithms. Compared with the LSTM model and the LSTM-Attention model, this model reduces RMSE to 8.5042 and MAE to 5.3372, significantly lowering prediction errors. The R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> value increases to 0.97953, indicating a substantial improvement in goodness of fit. It demonstrates superior accuracy and stability in wind power forecasting, providing a new methodological reference for precise wind power prediction.

title: Deformable 3D Point Cloud Perturbations using Cage-based Deformation for Semantic Consistency
authors: Kyo Seok Lee, Hak Gu Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Deep neural networks for 3D point cloud analysis are widely used in applications such as autonomous driving and robotics, yet they remain highly vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Existing methods typically minimize point-wise distances to preserve geometry, which constrains perturbations and leads to a trade-off between imperceptibility and attack strength. To address this limitation, we propose a cage-based adversarial deformation framework that generates semantically consistent perturbations aligned with natural intra-class variations. Our method refines a source cage, predicts adversarial cage displacements by fusing source–target features, and computes smooth point-wise offsets using solid-angle– and distance-aware weights. This enables globally coherent deformations that appear natural to humans while effectively misleading classifiers. Extensive experiments on ModelNet40, ShapeNet-Part, and ScanObjectNN show that our approach achieves consistently high attack success rates while simultaneously improving point uniformity and reducing local geometric distortions. Furthermore, the perturbations remain effective against various defense methods.

title: A 23-nW, −62-dBm Sensitivity, 920-MHz Temperature-Robust Wake-Up Receiver With Automatic Offset Calibration Technique
authors: Yao-Wei Huang, Bo-Cheng Juan, Yu-Te Liao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a highly integrated, temperature-resilient, and event-driven wake-up receiver (WuRX) that achieves a sensitivity of −62 dBm in the 920-MHz ISM band. Implemented in a 180-nm CMOS process, the proposed WuRX consumes only 23 nW from a 0.5-V supply at room temperature. To minimize the power consumption, a passive envelope detector (ED) employing a pseudo-differential architecture is adopted. An automatic offset calibration (AOC) mechanism is incorporated to compensate for DC offset variations arising from device mismatch and external interference. In addition, the WuRX integrates a temperature-independent voltage reference and a relaxation oscillator to detect the wake-up event over a wide temperature range (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$- 20~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$90~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C), with only a 3-dB degradation in sensitivity.

title: Orchestrating Generative AI Paradigms with Human-In-The-Loop for 3D Generation_supp1-3695993.pdf
authors: Emanuele Balloni

title: Dynamic Hypergraph-Guided Mamba for TLS Point Cloud Foliage Separation
authors: Weipeng Jing, Wenjun Zhang, Xuancheng Zhang, Donglin Di, Ajmal Mian, Muhammad Ibrahim, Chao Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Effective foliage-wood separation plays a crucial role in forestry applications such as Leaf Area Index (LAI) estimation and Quantitative Structure Models (QSM). Point clouds provide valuable support for this task. However, large-scale scenes, uneven density, and occlusions hinder the use of general 3D vision methods. Existing Transformer-based methods typically partition point clouds into local patches, but the high computational complexity restricts the feasible patch size, often fragmenting tree structures and causing semantic information loss. Moreover, the geometric similarity of fine-scale foliage, coupled with limited context in small patches, makes feature discrimination more difficult. These two issues severely limit the performance of existing methods on foliage segmentation tasks. To address these challenges, we propose the Dynamic Hypergraph-guided Mamba (DHMamba) model with two key innovations. First, we leverage a lightweight Mamba-based architecture whose linear complexity enables processing of larger patches, thereby expanding the receptive field and reducing erroneous segmentation of branches and leaves. Second, we introduce a dynamic hypergraph-based serialization strategy to capture higher-order topological dependencies within local regions, enhancing the model's ability to extract discriminative features. Moreover, by introducing prior global density and designing two geometric feature descriptors, planarity and linearity, our framework further enhances the multi-scale discrimination of subtle differences in canopy. Extensive experiments on individual-tree and plot-scale datasets demonstrate that DHMamba substantially advances segmentation accuracy and robustness, highlighting its strong potential for practical large-scale forest point-cloud analysis and sustainable forest-resource management. Our code will be made available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ZhangIceNight/foliage_segmentation</uri>.

title: Reconfigurable Mode-Division-Multiplexing Coherent Receiver for 2-D Spatial Modes Based on A Photodetector Array
authors: Zile Jiang, Xinzhou Su, Huibin Zhou, Abdulrahman Alhaddad, Muralekrishnan Ramakrishnan, Yuxiang Duan, Toshimasa Umezawa, Yuki Yoshida, Kouichi Akahane, Alan E. Willner
abstract: Mode-division multiplexing (MDM) may enhance the total capacity of free-space optical (FSO) communication links by transmitting independent data channels on orthogonal spatial modes. A photodetector (PD) array can be a promising MDM receiver architecture due to its compactness and reconfigurability. Previous PD array-based MDM receivers have primarily focused on demultiplexing one-dimensional (1-D) modal sets. In contrast, employing a two-dimensional (2-D) modal set—i.e., modes characterized by two spatial indices—may enable a larger number of orthogonal modes, thereby supporting more parallel data channels and higher link capacity. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate a reconfigurable PD array-based MDM FSO receiver capable of supporting 2-D spatial mode sets. The multiplexed data channels are demultiplexed and recovered using multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) digital signal processing (DSP). We demonstrate MDM links employing different types and numbers of 2-D spatial modes, including (i) a 60-Gbit/s link multiplexing six 2-D Laguerre–Gaussian (LG) modes detected by ten PD elements, and (ii) a 40-Gbit/s link multiplexing four 2-D Hermite–Gaussian (HG) modes detected by seven PD elements. In both cases, all channels achieve bit-error rates (BERs) below the forward error correction (FEC) threshold of 3.8e-3. We further demonstrate the capability of the PD array-based receiver to mitigate atmospheric turbulence effects in a 40-Gbit/s link multiplexing four 1-D orbital-angular-momentum (OAM) modes. With turbulence-compensating MIMO DSP, the error vector magnitudes (EVMs) of all four channels are reduced to below 35%, whereas they exceed 50% without DSP-based compensation.

title:
Ultrafast and Sensitive NiO/Ga
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Lateral Superjunction Photodetectors for Solar-Blind Ultraviolet Detection
authors: Xiaoxi Li, Yang Zhou, Zhifan Wu, Yu Wang, Shuqi Huang, Cizhe Fang, Xiangyu Zeng, Haoji Qian, Chengji Jin, Jiajia Chen, Xiang Zheng, Yan Liu, Yue Hao, Genquan Han
abstract: Ultrahigh responsivity has been reported in Ga2O3 solar-blind photodetectors, but their response speed still remains a significant challenge for practical applications. Here, lateral NiO/Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> superjunction p-n photodetectors are demonstrated, simultaneously achieving high responsivity and fast response. The devices exhibit a record photo to dark current ratio of 8.50 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">8</sup>, a high responsivity of 3.92 A/W, an excellent detectivity of 1.02 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">15</sup> Jones at 254 nm, and fast rise/decay times of 493/477 μs. The excellent performance is attributed to the periodically arranged NiO/Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> heterointerfaces, which introduce multiple localized built-in electric fields that promote efficient carrier separation, suppress recombination, and shorten the effective carrier transit distance. These results demonstrate the great potential of Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> superjunctions for solar-blind photodetection.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Robust Model-Free Predictive Current Control of PMSM Using Improved SMO and Adaptive Gain Identification
authors: Xiaozhuo Xu, Liangjie Wang, Zhonghua Wu, Zan Zhang
abstract: Deadbeat Predictive Current Control (DPCC) attains enhanced robustness via model-free predictive schemes with an ultra-local model. However, the bandwidth limitations of the observer and the controller gain's dependence on inductance parameters impair control performance. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Novel Model-Free Deadbeat Predictive Current Control (NMFPCC) strategy for PMSM. The primary contributions are twofold. First, a novel Sliding Mode Observer (SMO) is designed to replace the traditional Extended State Observer (ESO). By employing a variable exponential reaching law, the proposed SMO avoid the linear bandwidth constraints of ESOs, enabling fast disturbance estimation while effectively mitigating high-frequency chattering. Second, to resolve the gain mismatch issue, a novel gain identification algorithm is developed. This algorithm estimates the reciprocal of the inductance in real-time and adaptively updates the controller gain, thereby decoupling the control system from parameter mismatches. Experimental results validate that the proposed NMFPCC strategy solely relies on input-output data and achieves superior robustness and dynamic response compared to existing methods, maintaining high control accuracy even under significant parameter variations.

title: End-to-End Explainable Sequential Recommendation Based on Multi-Strategy Training and Adaptive Loss Balancing
authors: Yong Zhu, Ying-an Liu
abstract: Sequential recommendation has achieved significant progress in accuracy metrics, but practical deployment still faces two key challenges: existing models struggle to provide evidence-based recommendation explanations, and jointly training explanation generation with ranking tasks leads to gradient interference between the two objectives, often resulting in a trade-off where improving explanation quality degrades ranking performance and vice versa. This paper proposes T5ESR, a framework that deeply couples the SASRec sequence encoder with the T5 generative model to build an end-to-end explainable sequential recommendation system.We systematically compare four training strategies—Stop-Grad (gradient truncation), Two-Stage (staged training), fixed-weight Joint, and GradNorm-based dynamic weighting—and explore inference-time constraints including length normalization, coverage penalty, and entity alignment to improve explanation readability. Experiments on the MIND dataset demonstrate that T5ESR maintains ranking performance (Hit@10 reaches 23.06±0.09) while improving explanation quality (BLEU-4 increases from 57.14 to 59.35). Ablation studies reveal that the debiased loss contributes the primary ranking improvement (+15.7%), while explainable multi-task training introduces explanation capability without harming ranking performance. We acknowledge that under weak supervision settings, BLEU-based evaluation serves as a proxy measure and strict faithfulness alignment with ranking logic remains an open challenge requiring human evaluation or causal validation.

title: Enhancing Cross-User Generalization in Human Activity Recognition with Adaptive Noise Scheduling and Linear Attention
authors: Monu Nagar, Himani Sikarwar, Debasis Das
abstract: The rapid proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices in recent years has significantly advanced Human Activity Recognition (HAR) tasks. These devices are widely utilized in various HAR applications, such as fitness tracking, transportation, and smart home automation. However, learning meaningful patterns from IoT sensor data remains a complex challenge. The distribution of sensor data for an individual changes over time due to aging, changes in health conditions, or varying environments, even when the activities remain consistent. Current state-of-the-art HAR techniques face two main challenges: a) they often struggle to learn a generalized model across users due to the Non-Independent and Identically Distributed (Non-IID) variability of sensor data, and b) the high computational costs associated with these techniques impede their deployment on resource-constrained devices. To overcome these challenges, we introduce MLA-HAR, a Multi-Head Linear Attention-based HAR model. MLA-HAR uses a Gaussian noise injection mechanism with cosine annealing scheduling to enhance the model’s generalization, and a linear attention mechanism to improve computational efficiency. The model utilizes a novel learn-and-share design to capture local and global patterns from sensor data. We train and test the proposed model on four notable HAR datasets: PAMAP2, OPPORTUNITY, REALDISP, and MHEALTH. MLA-HAR outperforms existing leading methods across diverse HAR scenarios, attaining F1<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">w</sub> scores of 98.7%, 85.9%, 96.5%, and 97.6%, respectively. This superior performance is further validated through an indepth analysis of the results, which highlights the model’s effectiveness in HAR applications. The implementation code is available at: https://github.com/autovisionproject/MLA-HAR.

title: Fully Distance-Guided Refinement Graph Convolutional Networks for Skeleton-Based Human Action Recognition
authors: Chongyang Ding, Kai Liu, Shenglong Li
abstract: Graph convolutional networks (GCNs) have obtained impressive performance on skeleton-based human action recognition tasks in recent years. In GCNs, how to capture discriminative spatial temporal dependency features with graph modeling rules is the key for action recognition. However, previous GCNs usually relying on the heuristic spatial partition and unconstrained dynamic graph learning strategies may result in limited receptive field and misalignment with prior graph modeling rules. To solve the above issues, we propose a fully distance-guided refinement graph convolutional networks (FDR-GCN) to extract discriminative dependency features for increasing the performance and robustness of graph convolutions. In spatial graph modeling process, we construct a fully distance-guided graph containing all adjacent joint connections under different distance ranges to expand the spatial dependencies in one graph convolution operation. Refined graph convolution is then proposed to learn the distance-guided graph edge importance and keep it normalized simultaneously. In addition, we extend the bottleneck structure from temporal modeling to a unified spatial temporal graph modeling block, reducing the computational complexity with ratio up to 2.23× versus baseline method. Extensive experiments on three datasets, NTU-RGBD, NTU-RGBD 120 and NW-UCLA show that our proposed method surpasses previous state-of-the-art methods.

title: Alternating Subspace Method for Sparse Recovery of Signals_supp1-3695936.pdf
authors: Tiejun Li
abstract: Numerous renowned algorithms for tackling the compressed sensing problem employ an alternating strategy, which typically involves data matching in one module and denoising in another. We present a novel approach, the Alternating Subspace Method (ASM), which integrates the principles of the greedy methods (e.g., the orthogonal matching pursuit type methods) and the splitting methods (e.g., the approximate message passing type methods). Crucially, ASM enhances the splitting method by achieving fidelity in a subspace-restricted fashion. We reveal that such a restriction strategy guarantees global convergence via proximal residual control and establish its local geometric convergence on the LASSO problem. Numerical experiments on the LASSO, channel estimation, and dynamic compressed sensing problems demonstrate its high convergence rate and its capacity to incorporate different prior distributions. Overall, the proposed method is promising in terms of efficiency, accuracy, and flexibility, and has the potential to be competitive in different sparse recovery applications.

title: Dielectric Breakdown Behavior in Vapor Phase Dielectric Fluids: Understanding Voltage Isolation in Direct Immersion Power Electronics
authors: Mohammad Kokash, Alan Mantooth, Jie Li, David Huitink
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Two-phase direct immersion cooling offers significant thermal advantages for high-voltage power electronics, yet vapor formation fundamentally degrades dielectric performance. This work presents the first systematic comparative analysis of vapor-phase breakdown voltage (BDV) for four commercial dielectric fluids: SF10, HFE-347E, Novec 7200, and Novec 7500, using a custom vapor chamber enabling repeatable measurements across saturated liquid, vapor-liquid interface, and pure vapor conditions at 1.0mm electrode gaps. Vapor-phase operation consistently degraded dielectric strength by 35% to 47% across all fluids, with Novec 7500 demonstrating superior absolute performance (23.0 kV liquid, 14.8 kV vapor) and SF10 showing the most consistent cross-phase behavior. These findings establish that electrical insulation clearances in two-phase systems must be designed to vaporphase specifications.

title: Imagination, Reflexivity and Agency in Institutional Change and Public Policy
authors: Lucy Kimbell, Marzia Mortati
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
