title: Multi-scale Guided Cross-stage Refinement SAM2 for Seamless Steel Pipe Internal Surface Defect Detection
authors: Kunye Shen, Xiaofei Zhou, Zhi Liu
abstract: In recent years, vision foundation models (e.g., SAM2) have achieved remarkable success across various downstream tasks and have been increasingly adopted in Internet of Things (IoT)-enabled visual perception systems. However, the decoders of existing vision foundation models fail to effectively handle multi-scale contextual cues, leading to unsatisfactory performance in detecting defects of varying sizes in IoT-based inspection scenarios. Besides, the insufficient preservation of spatial information degrades detection accuracy, especially when handling small defect regions, which is critical for reliable perception in IoT devices. To address these issues, we propose a Multi-scale Guided Cross-stage Refinement SAM2 (MGCR-SAM2) for seamless steel pipe (SSP) internal surface defect detection. Specifically, a Multi-scale Information Compensation (MIC) module is designed for multi-scale feature aggregation in the decoder, which integrates contextual information from different receptive fields to enhance the representation of defects with diverse scales. Furthermore, a cross-stage refinement strategy is adopted to progressively enhance detection precision. In the first stage, we construct an encoder–decoder architecture based on SAM2 and the proposed MIC module to adequately capture multi-scale features from SSP images for coarse localization. In the second stage, a Spatial Information Compensation (SIC) module and CNN-based refinement network are employed to enhance local details and refine coarse predictions. Extensive experiments on SSP2000 and SD-Saliency-900 datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our MGCR-SAM2. Code is available at https://github.com/Kunye-Shen/MGCR-SAM2.

title: Deep Rhetoric: A Qarīna-Aware Multi-Task Learning Framework for Hierarchical Analysis of Qur’anic Metaphor
authors: Reema G. Al-Anazi, Wadee A. Nashir, Badriyya B. Al-Onazi, Anoud A. Alhamad, Hassan Alshamrani, Wala F. Bin Subait
abstract: This paper presents a specialized multi-task learning (MTL) framework for the computational analysis of Qur’anic metaphor (isti‘āra), bridging classical Arabic rhetoric (balāgha) with deep neural modeling. Unlike conventional approaches that treat metaphor as a binary detection problem, we operationalize isti‘āra as a hierarchy of three interdependent tasks: Metaphor Type, Semantic Origin, and Functional Context. Central to this framework is a novel Qarīna-Aware Interaction Layer, which employs span-based cross-attention with residual connections to model contextual indicators (qarīna) through architectural bias rather than explicit annotation. To validate this approach, we introduce a rigorously annotated dataset of 2,649 Qur’anic verses. Extensive experiments across five encoders—evaluated under a 100-run protocol (10-fold cross-validation×10 seeds)—reveal a consistent pattern of condition-dependent gains. Rather than acting as a universal booster, the proposed layer selectively enhances structurally under-specified representations. For general-purpose models (e.g., ARBERT and MARBERTv2), it helps recover missing dependencies, yielding gains of up to +1.15% in Metaphor Type and +0.76% in Functional Context, with clear impact on implicit metaphor detection. In contrast, limited or negative effects are observed in saturated or misaligned regimes, including specialized heritage models and multilingual representations lacking strong Arabic priors. These findings demonstrate that integrating such inductive biases into under-specified neural architectures can bridge the gap between linguistic representation and structured rhetorical analysis, positioning computational models as principled, context-aware tools for Arabic rhetorical study.

title: GNSS Positioning Aided with Pedestrian Dead Reckoning (PDR) in Urban Areas
authors: Huan Luo, Duojie Weng, Ahmed Mansour, Xiaolong Mi, Yang Yang, Wu Chen
abstract: Urban environments, characterized by dense high-rise buildings and narrow streets, present substantial challenges to GNSS positioning due to signal blockages and multipath effects. The conventional fault detection and exclusion (FDE) methods struggle in these settings because the majority of measurements contain multipath or non-line-of-sight (NLOS) errors. To address these challenges, a novel pedestrian dead reckoning (PDR)-aided FDE framework is proposed to enhance GNSS positioning accuracy in urban canyons. In this framework, the constraints are firstly derived from PDR and the receiver clock error, and these constraints are then used to enhance GNSS positioning through clustering. Both static and dynamic tests were carried out to evaluate the performance of the proposed system. Results show that the proposed approach achieves accuracies of 1.7m–11.5m, compared to 4.2m–57.4m for chip outputs, while the conventional FDE method is impractical in deep urban canyons since its availability extremely decreased to 3.2%. Kinematic tests in Hong Kong reveal a 52.4% enhancement in root mean square (RMS) accuracy (12.9m vs. 27.6m for chip outputs) with 100% availability. This work provides a computationally efficient, hardware-independent solution for reliable urban positioning on consumer devices.

title: Power Quality Disturbance Recognition Based on Lightweight Deep Neural Network Deployed in Edge Computing System
authors: Juncai Song, Qianqian Jiang, Zhongyue He, Zijie Wen, Zhi Peng, Zhiyong Hu, Xiaoxian Wang, Siliang Lu
abstract: This work proposes an efficient method for accurately classifying complex power quality disturbance (PQD). First, a 2D image encoding method termed multiscale scattering-fused symmetrized dot pattern (MSF-SDP) is introduced. After 1D PQD signal is transformed into SDP images, its multiscale scattering coefficient plots are extracted through the wavelet scattering transform. Then max pixel point fusion (MPPF) strategy is introduced to fuse above multiscale plots to enable deep complementarity and enhanced feature representation across multiscale information. Second, a novel self-knowledge distillation for MobileNetV4 (SKD-MV4) classifier is proposed. The proposed classification model is developed without reliance on a pre-trained large-scale teacher model. High classification performance is maintained, while the number of model parameters is effectively reduced, thereby preserving a lightweight model structure. Third, a physical experimental platform is established, achieving a classification accuracy of 99.52% with an inference time of 4.18ms. Finally, the proposed method is deployed on the NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX, RK3576, and Raspberry Pi 5 edge computing platforms, further confirming its superiority and practical applicability.

title: IDME: Interpretable Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction via Meta-Path Attribute Encoding
authors: Lishan Huang, Fei Yang, Huiyi Wang, Shaobo Luo, Zhenwan Peng, Yunfei He, Yiwen Zhang, Yun Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction aims to identify positive or negative interactions between drugs and is critical for drug development, clinical decision-making, and patient safety. DDIs are commonly modeled as graphs, where drugs are nodes and interactions are edges. Although heterogeneous graph neural networks (GNNs) can integrate diverse entities and relationships, existing methods usually treat all meta-paths equally and overlook meta-path attributes, such as edge type and path length. This limits their ability to capture distinct relational contexts and weakens the interpretability of DDI predictions. To address this issue, we propose IDME, an interpretable DDI prediction framework based on meta-path attribute encoding. IDME learns conversion factors for different edge types and attenuation factors for meta-path lengths, and uses them to modulate the message propagation process of GNNs. The framework contains three modules: meta-path attribute encoding, meta-path attribute aggregation, and DDI prediction. The encoding module assigns attribute-aware factors to meta-paths, the aggregation module integrates semantic information from diverse meta-paths to generate refined drug embeddings, and the prediction module produces interpretable DDI predictions. Experiments on three datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of IDME. Moreover, the learned conversion and attenuation factors reveal the importance of specific edge types and the influence of path length, providing useful insights into the underlying mechanisms of DDIs.

title: Decentralized Intelligence for Energy Efficient 6G TN–NTN: A Cooperative Multi-Agent DRL Framework for Active RIS-Aided UAV-NOMA Communications
authors: Monzur Morshed, Mostafa Zaman Chowdhury, Yeong Min Jang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Convergence between terrestrial networks (TN) and non-terrestrial networks (NTN) is one of the fundamental pillars of sixth generation (6G) connectivity. However, such joint integration gives rise to significant challenges in resource allocation, especially for the interference and blockage-afflicted cell-edge users. In this work, we investigate the deployment of active reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) mounted on unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) for dynamically controlling the propagation environment. In contrast to classical centralized optimization, we model a joint optimization problem, including UAV trajectory, active RIS beamforming, amplification, phase shifts, and non-orthogonal multiple access power allocation, as a decentralized one. We propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework based on the algorithm multi-agent proximal policy optimization with a paradigm called centralized training and decentralized execution. The Base Station, UAV, and RIS play the role of independent agents. They learn cooperative policies in order to maximize a multi-objective reward function, which combines network sum-rate, energy efficiency, and user fairness. Through extensive simulations, we demonstrate that our proposed MARL approach effectively learns complex cooperative strategies. It achieves better performance measures than a deterministic policy gradient baseline and also demonstrates better scalability than a centralized single-agent baseline. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed MARL framework achieves a high spectral efficiency of 19.31 bps/Hz and a peak energy efficiency of 1.61 Mbits/Joule. This reflects an energy efficiency improvement of over 40% and a spectral efficiency improvement of 7.2% compared to the centralized SARL baseline. Furthermore, the decentralized architecture reduces the real-time parallel inference latency to approximately 2.85 ms, effectively decreasing the operational computational delay by 57%. Rigorous ablation studies explain the benefits of optimizing each system component, while performance analysis under varying interference conditions provides important insights for robust 6G TN-NTN deployments.

title: Federated LoRA Fine-Tuning of LLMs with Only Transmitting Matrix A or B
authors: Yiming Chen, Ruanjun Li, Jiawei Shao, Lifeng Sun, Chi Zhang, Jun Zhang, Xuelong Li
abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have been largely driven by vast amounts of training data. As public data are exhausted, fine-tuning LLMs with private data has become increasingly important. However, due to privacy concerns associated with sensitive data, traditional centralized training approaches are not feasible. Federated learning (FL) offers a promising solution, as it enables collaborative model training across multiple mobile devices without data exchange. Nevertheless, it is challenging to fine-tune LLMs with FL on resource-constrained and heterogeneous clients. To reduce the cost, we employ Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for efficient fine-tuning. Existing federated LoRA fine-tuning methods suffer from inaccurate aggregation, and the resulting noise may lead to performance loss that may be more severe for LLMs. In this paper, we propose a novel method, FedOTAB, to mitigate this issue. FedOTAB first correctly aggregates all local LoRA modules and then fixes the matrix <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {A}$</tex-math></inline-formula> or <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {B}$</tex-math></inline-formula> to optimize the corresponding best approximate matrix <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {B}^*$</tex-math></inline-formula> or <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {A}^*$</tex-math></inline-formula> for each local LoRA module by minimizing the induced noise. In addition, we design a mechanism to freeze the matrices <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {A}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {B}$</tex-math></inline-formula> alternately to stabilize optimization. By transmitting only the updated part, that is, half of the LoRA module, FedOTAB enjoys favorable communication efficiency. By explicitly splitting LoRA into local and global parts, FedOTAB effectively integrates both local and global knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedOTAB excels in both homogeneous and heterogeneous rank scenarios, consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods with remarkably low communication and computational overhead. We hope that this work provides valuable insights for federated LoRA fine-tuning, offering a practical and scalable solution to fine-tuning large models in mobile environments.

title: Robust Gaussian Process-Based Sliding Mode Predictive Control for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles: A Short-Horizon Approach
authors: Run-Zhi Wang, Li-Ying Hao
abstract: Considering that the computational burden of model predictive control (MPC) mainly arises from the prediction horizon, this letter proposes a short-horizon sliding mode predictive control (SMPC) method for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs). A key innovation lies in the incorporation of a saturation-based sliding mode formulation into both the cost function and the constraints of the optimization problem, which ensures the convergence of sliding mode variables even under a short prediction horizon. To enhance robustness against model uncertainties and external disturbances, a prediction model based on Gaussian process regression (GPR) is incorporated into the SMPC framework, thereby reducing the dependence on highly accurate prior model knowledge through online learning of lumped uncertainty. Furthermore, a tailored terminal weight design is developed to support theoretical stability analysis. Rigorous proof is provided to establish the feasibility and global stability of the closed-loop system. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed controller achieves trajectory tracking with reduced computational complexity.

title: Multi-Modal Fusion Bearing Fault Diagnosis Based on CNN-GRU and Multi-Head Self-Attention Mechanism
authors: Shuaishuai Guo, Yang Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Failures of rolling bearings frequently lead to unexpected shutdowns and safety risks in rotating machinery, highlighting the need for reliable diagnosis. However, a single vibration or current signal cannot fully characterize fault features and is susceptible to noise interference. To overcome these limitations, we develop a multi-modal diagnostic model integrating CNN, GRU, and multi-head self-attention. The vibration signal is first transformed into a Gramian Angular Summation Field (GASF) image, while the original one-dimensional vibration and current sequences are preserved. Subsequently, a CNN processes the GASF image for spatial features, whereas two independent GRUs handle the temporal dynamics of the two one-dimensional signals. The features from the three branches are stacked and fed into a multi-head self-attention layer for dynamic fusion, ultimately achieving fault classification. Evaluated on the Paderborn bearing dataset, our method achieves 97.85 % accuracy and an F1-score of 0.978, substantially outperforming both single-modal baselines and conventional concatenation-based fusion.

title: Combined Multi-Parameter Diagnosis for Transformer DC Bias Induced by Metro Stray Current
authors: Shi Jiang, Bing Yu, Jiajun Lin, Shiyou Yang, Junxuan Hu, Lingzhi Li
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Addressing the issue of transformer DC bias induced by metro stray current and the limited generalization capability of conventional diagnostic approaches, this paper introduces a multi-parameter integrated diagnostic methodology that amalgamates neutral point current, vibration, and voiceprint characteristics. The equivalent DC is identified as the pivotal metric, and a feature screening framework is constructed, which incorporates classified Min-Max normalization and coefficient of variation weighting. Furthermore, a nonlinear exponential mapping model is established to quantify the comprehensive impact of bias. Experimental findings substantiate the efficacy and robust generalization of the proposed methodology.

title: Preventive Control of Voltage Instability in High-Renewable Sending-End Systems Under UHVDC Blocking
authors: Lanxin Zhang, Anjia Mao, Wenying Liu, Yanzhang Ou
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In typical regions of China characterized by high concentrations of wind and photovoltaic resources, the continuous increase in the proportion of installed renewable capacity has intensified the coupling constraints between renewable power output and ultra-high-voltage direct current (UHVDC) transmission capability. When a bipolar blocking fault occurs in the DC transmission system, the sending-end AC system is prone to significant transient overvoltage. Based on actual power grid operational data and simulation results, this paper investigates the evolution mechanism of local transient voltage instability in a renewable energy collection area of the sending-end grid under UHVDC bipolar blocking conditions, where renewable units fail to ride through high-voltage disturbances and subsequently trip in a cascading manner. To address this issue, this paper proposes a hierarchical transient overvoltage preventive control strategy across the rectifier, cluster, and collector stations, based on the coordinated operation of synchronous condensers and static var compensators (SVCs). The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through simulation studies. Simulation results indicate that the proposed control strategy can strictly limit the collectorstation bus voltages to within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 3 0}$</tex> p.u., effectively preventing voltage instability triggered by renewable units failing highvoltage ride-through (HVRT). The research findings provide theoretical support and technical guidance for mechanism analysis and engineering applications related to transient overvoltage stability in high-renewable-penetration DC sending-end systems.

title: Dynamic Power Supply Capability Evaluation Method of Micro-Grid Based on Risk Perception
authors: Yubing Zhang, Di Bai
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Aiming at large evaluation deviation, insufficient consideration of source-load nonlinear correlation, and lack of risk quantification in traditional static evaluation methods for microgrid (MG) power supply capacity under high renewable energy penetration, this paper proposes a dynamic MG power supply capacity evaluation method based on risk perception. Firstly, a trinity dynamic evaluation model integrating probability distribution fitting, nonlinear correlation characterization and risk quantitative measurement is constructed. Weibull distribution, Beta distribution and normal distribution are adopted to describe the stochastic characteristics of wind power, photovoltaic (PV) output and load demand respectively, and ternary D-Vine Copula function is introduced to depict the nonlinear dependence among wind speed, illumination intensity and load. On this basis, risk reserve power and risk shortage power indicators are innovatively proposed to quantify the impact of source-load uncertainty, and Monte Carlo simulation is used to calculate MG external power supply capacity under different confidence levels. Case simulation shows that the proposed ternary D-Vine Copula modeling increases the fitting goodness <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> from 0.9218 to 0.9865, the model corrects the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9 \%-10 \%$</tex> evaluation deviation of the traditional static summation method, and realizes quantitative mapping between power supply capacity and confidence level with result fluctuation no more than 3% under varying confidence levels. The proposed model has excellent evaluation accuracy and uncertainty adaptability, which can provide theoretical and technical support for operation scheduling and fault recovery of active distribution network (ADN) with high renewable energy penetration.

title: Source-Storage Dynamic Collaborative Optimization Method for Comprehensive Voltage Regulation in Low-Voltage Distribution Networks
authors: Runjia Li, Jian Le, Zhihang Zhu, Hao Geng, Jing Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the voltage violation issues in distribution networks integrated with distributed generation (DG), this paper proposes a source-storage dynamic collaborative optimization method for comprehensive voltage regulation in low-voltage distribution networks. By employing flexible configuration and optimized control of distributed energy storage systems (ESS), the proposed method effectively mitigates the impact of DG intermittency and uncertainty on grid voltage. First, based on a data-driven approach, the voltage-power sensitivity is accurately estimated using a Bayesian Regularization Neural Network (BR-NN) utilizing measured node voltage and injected power data. Second, a multi-objective weighted optimization model for low-voltage distribution network voltage regulation is constructed, which comprehensively considers objectives including minimizing voltage deviation, reducing network loss, and enhancing static voltage stability. Finally, by solving the model using the Sparrow Search Algorithm (SSA), the collaborative control of energy storage devices and reactive power compensation equipment is realized, ensuring the dynamic optimization of the distribution network. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed source-storage dynamic collaborative method, based on sensitivity analysis and SSA, can effectively resolve voltage violation issues in low-voltage distribution networks, thereby enhancing voltage quality, operational reliability, and economic efficiency.

title: Feedforward Phase-Angle Compensation for Transient Stability Enhancement of VSG
authors: Yaoqi Bai, Junru Chen, Yongping Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In response to the susceptibility of virtual synchronous generators to transient loss of synchronization under sudden grid phase-angle changes and low-voltage conditions, a feedforward phase-angle compensation strategy is proposed. This strategy establishes a feedforward path by integrating the active power deviation, thereby addressing the transient instability issue caused by phase-angle jumps and voltage sags. Analysis based on the equal-area criterion (EAC) demonstrates that this method significantly reduces the acceleration area and enhances the transient stability margin. Furthermore, small-signal eigenvalue analysis verifies that the strategy introduces positive electrical damping, thereby accelerating power-angle convergence without compromising inherent stability.

title: Cryptanalysis of the DIZY Stream Cipher with Provable Security
authors: Zhengting Li, Lin Ding, Xinhai Wang, Zheng Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: With the increasing deployment of resource-constrained devices in daily life, ultra-lightweight ciphers become a necessity to tackle the security and privacy concerns in resource-constrained devices. In 2023, Gül and Kara studied the question of how to design a secure ultra-lightweight stream cipher with a small internal state, and introduced a new small-state stream cipher called DIZY. The cipher utilizes Truncated Pseudorandom Permutations (TPP) and has a provable security in the indistinguishability model. It consists of two versions, called DIZY-128 with a 128-bit key and DIZY-80 with an 80-bit key, respectively. In this paper, effective key recovery attacks on DIZY-80 and DIZY-128 are proposed. Both attacks leverage the weakness of DIZY that the attacker can easily reach a weak state in the middle of the initialization using chosen IVs. Based on constructing Hellman tables, the key recovery attacks on DIZY-80 and DIZY-128 are further improved. The cryptanalytic results show that DIZY-80/DIZY-128 can only provide a 65/86-bit security level against the key recovery attack, while it is claimed to provide an 80/112-bit security level by the designers. Finally, an improved variant of DIZY, called DIZYa, is proposed. The analysis on DIZYa shows that the improved variant can provide better security resistance against all known attacks including our attacks on DIZY, while maintaining the commendable characteristics of DIZY. This makes DIZYa a more suitable small-state stream cipher choice for resource-constrained devices like RFID tags.

title: Threshold Issuance Selective Disclosure Credentials With Equivalence Class Signature_supp1-3696205.pdf
authors: Debiao He
abstract: Anonymous credentials offer privacy-preserving authentication and authorization by making assertions about identity in the digital realm. To overcome the reliance on a single trusted issuer, decentralized variants have emerged. A classic approach is to split the responsibility of issuing credentials among multiple issuers in a threshold manner (e.g., t-out-of-n). Unfortunately, among existing threshold protocols, non-interactive practical constructions can only guarantee security in honest majority settings, while interactive constructions face two primary efficiency bottlenecks: either they require an excessive number of interaction rounds, or they fail to support constant-size credential showings for selective disclosure. In this work, we address these challenges by presenting a threshold issuance anonymous credential (TIAC) protocol, built upon the recent advanced signatures, i.e., Equivalence Class Signatures (EQS). Our proposed solution involves a three-round protocol that realizes a standard threshold issuing functionality, providing composable security against a malicious adversary corrupting the majority of issuers. We thereafter introduce a provably secure construction of the TIAC protocol with constant-size showings by combining the proposed threshold issuing protocol and set commitments. We rigorously prove our protocol in the universal composability (UC) framework. The practicality of our protocol is demonstrated through benchmark comparisons with the state-of-the-art EQS-based solution (ASIACRYPT ’24). The benchmarking results show that, with 64 participating issuers, our improvements go up to 6.72× for the threshold issuance phase when observed over WAN.

title: Hyperledger Fabric and Ethereum for Emergency Response Vehicular Networks under DDOS Attacks: A Performance Analysis
authors: Aditya Pathak, Alark Kansara, Irfan Al-Anbagi, M. Shamim Hossain
abstract: In emergency response vehicular networks (ERVNs), where each second might dictate a life-or-death outcome, the performance of underlying communication systems is critical. Blockchain technology is a promising backbone for authenticating emergency response vehicles (ERVs) at smart traffic signal intersections. However, the decentralized nature of blockchain networks makes them vulnerable to distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks. A DDoS attack can compromise safetycritical functions by overwhelming the network with fake authentication requests. Therefore, this article presents a comparative performance analysis of two well-known blockchain technologies, namely Ethereum (permissionless) and Hyperledger Fabric (HLF, permissioned), under DDoS-based network congestion. We clarify that this article does not propose any DDoS detection or mitigation mechanisms; rather, we evaluate the ability of the abovedescribed blockchains to maintain service for legitimate transactions under DDoS attacks. We design three DDoS attack scenarios that simulate realistic threats to ERVNs and benchmark throughput and latency using Hyperledger Caliper. Our results show that HLF, because of its architecture, achieves up to 2.7 times higher throughput and lower latency compared to Ethereum. This finding provides an important insight: A permissioned blockchain offers better performance under DDoS attacks, making it a more reliable communication system for safety-critical transportation systems.

title: Remote State Estimation over Unreliable Channels with Unreliable Feedback: Strategies and Limits
authors: Touraj Soleymani, Mohamad Assaad, John S. Baras
abstract: In this article, we establish a comprehensive theoretical framework for remote estimation in a networked system composed of a source that is observed by a sensor, a remote monitor that needs to estimate the state of the source in real time, and a communication channel that connects the source to the monitor. The source is a partially observable dynamical process, and the communication channel is a packet-erasure channel with feedback. We consider a novel communication model that captures implicit information. Our main objective is to identify the optimal strategies and the fundamental performance limits of the underlying system in the sense of a causal tradeoff between the packet rate and the mean square error when both forward and backward channels are unreliable. We characterise an optimal coding policy profile consisting of a scheduling policy for an encoder and an estimation policy for a decoder, collocated with the source and the monitor, respectively. We derive the recursive equations that must be solved online by the encoder and the decoder. In addition, we prove that the value function, originally defined over an expanding information set, admits a lower-dimensional representation depending only on two variables. We discuss the structural properties of the optimal policies, and analyse the computational complexity of an algorithm proposed for their computation. We then examine a range of special cases derived from our main theoretical results. We complement the theoretical results with a numerical analysis, and compare the performance of different remote estimation tasks in various operating regimes.

title: Decentralized and Lightweight Server-Aided Revocable Attribute-Based Encryption for Mobile Cloud Computing
authors: Arthur Sandor Voundi Koe, Jianghong Wei, Xiaofeng Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Mobile cloud computing (MCC) optimizes storage and computation on resource-limited mobile devices by leveraging heterogeneous networks and robust cloud servers. However, outsourcing local data to cloud servers, which may be malicious, also raises concerns about data privacy. As a prominent solution for securing outsourced data, server-aided revocable attribute-based encryption (SR-ABE) enforces fine-grained access control over encrypted data while supporting lightweight user revocation. Yet, available SR-ABE schemes struggle in practice due to the following reasons. First, they rely on a fully trusted key generation center to manage attributes and issue long-term transformation and decryption keys. Second, they require setting up a fixed number of data users, and overlook attribute revocation. Third, they incorporate time-based revocation mechanisms that assume synchrony in the underlying network, which does not always hold in practice. This paper presents a secure and practical SR-ABE scheme for MCC that supports the decentralized generation of long-term user keys while integrating state-triggered user and attribute revocation mechanisms. The proposed SR-ABE scheme accommodates unlimited data users, minimizes the sizes of key and ciphertext updates via lazy revocation, and ensures verifiable outsourced decryption. We formally prove that the proposed SR-ABE scheme is adaptively secure, and conduct extensive experiments demonstrating its practicality.

title: Long-Term Trading Risk Management for Intra-Regional Market Operators with Participant Risk Preferences
authors: Zeyang Long, Fangmei Bie, Shu Liu, Qinghu Tang, Yi Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Energy Markets, Policy and Regulation
abstract: Under China's national unified electricity market with a two-level operation mechanism, the intra-regional market operator (IRMO) simultaneously participates in the inter regional market and operates the intra-regional market. In fulfilling this dual role, the IRMO must mitigate regional power balance risks via inter-regional forward trading while respecting intra-regional market participants' economic incentives and heterogeneous risk preferences to ensure efficient and executable market outcomes. Existing works inadequately characterize the financial risks in long-term power markets, particularly those associated with renewable energy curtailment and load-shedding costs. They also neglect participants' risk preferences and bud get balance constraints, which may lead to irrational trading decisions and the IRMO's actual inability to enforce contracts. To this end, a risk management inter-regional long-term trading model for IRMO is proposed that incorporates participant risk preferences within a Stackelberg game. A generalized additive model-based scenario generation framework is proposed to characterize the long-term uncertainties for market participants. The model also contains the budget balance constraint to trade off the imbalance funds against the social welfare. Case studies show that incorporating system risk improves social welfare by 3.23%, while considering participant risk preferences further enhances it by 0.2%-0.3%.

title: RVDeformer: Sparse Point Cloud-Guided Right Ventricle 3D Reconstruction in Echocardiograms
authors: Zhaohui Wang, Jun Shi, Minfan Zhao, Ziqi Zhu, Yida Li, Hong An
abstract: 3D reconstruction of the Right Ventricle (RV) from echocardiograms is crucial for accurate clinical evaluation of cardiac function. However, existing methods are hindered by the complex RV anatomy and the incomplete spatial information inherent in 2D multi-view echocardiograms. Therefore, we propose RVDeformer, a sparse point cloud-guided framework for RV 3D reconstruction. RVDeformer reformulates the reconstruction task as a mesh deformation problem, learning to deform a predefined template mesh to match the target structure under the guidance of the sparse anatomical point cloud. Specifically, this framework employs the end-to-end neural network RVDeformNet to extract the features of the point cloud and template mesh for predicting the displacement of each mesh vertex. We design a point cloud-mesh fusion module that can effectively align and fuse features from the two modalities to enhance the representation ability of the model. We conduct extensive validation on a clinical dataset of 1,278 cases and demonstrate that RVDeformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, achieving a Chamfer Distance (CD) of 2.24±0.55 mm, an F1-score of 0.74±0.10 at the 3 mm threshold, and a Volumetric Similarity (VS) of 91.53±2.28%, with significant potential for clinical applications. The code is available at https://github.com/onezh95/RVDeformer.

title: Efficient Microservice Autoscaling through $\mu$Opt
authors: Emilio Incerto, Roberto Pizziol, Mirco Tribastone
abstract: Microservices have become the architecture of choice for cloud applications requiring high performance and scalability. Autoscaling, which dynamically adjusts resource allocation based on workload fluctuations, is key to optimizing performance and controlling costs. This paper presents <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula>Opt, a computationally efficient, model-based autoscaler specifically designed for microservices. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula>Opt leverages a nonlinear optimization problem tied to a fluid approximation of a layered queuing network (LQN) model to determine optimal configurations that maximize key performance metrics—such as throughput, CPU usage, and response time—while minimizing operational costs. On a well-known benchmark application, our numerical experiments show that <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula>Opt achieves fast solution times, enabling responsiveness to dynamic workloads. Compared to a state-of-the-art LQN-based autoscaler employing genetic algorithms, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula>Opt delivers improved application performance using fewer resources. To demonstrate the robustness and generalizability of our underlying model, we validate its prediction accuracy across ten randomly generated applications with diverse architectures, showing that its performance is a reliable foundation for autoscaling. Finally, it also outperforms Horizontal Pod Autoscaler, a production-ready solution for Kubernetes deployments in Google Cloud Platform, consistently reducing resource usage while more accurately tracking CPU utilization targets across both synthetic and real-world workloads.

title: Design of the next generation of global carbon monitoring experimental satellite (TanSat-2) for global stocktake
authors: Zengshan Yin, Guohua Liu, Longfei Tian, Lei Ding, Yunmeng Liu, Chao Lin, Fuqi Si, Liangyun Liu, Liangfu Chen, Dongxu Yan, Tianhai Cheng, Zheng Tan, Lin Chen, Meng Fan, Shuang Gao, Denghui Hu, Yiguo Pang, Huiyuan Wang, Ruochen Yang, Xinglei Cheng, Xiaosong Yao
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine
abstract: TanSat-2 is the successor to TanSat, China's first dedicated carbon monitoring satellite. Compared to TanSat, TanSat-2 features improved designs with enhanced spatial and temporal coverage, a wider range of measurements, and higher measuring accuracy. TanSat-2 achieves near-daily global coverage by using a medium/medium-low earth orbit (M/MLEO) and large swath design, allowing it to monitor most land areas daily. This M/MLEO elliptical orbit intersects with the Van Allen radiation belts, requiring special radiation analysis and protection designs. TanSat-2 also features higher retrieval accu racy and carbon-pollution collaborative monitoring to identify anthropogenic emissions accurately. TanSat-2 carries three key spectrometers: (1) the Ultra-wide-field Carbon Pollution collab orative monitoring Instrument (UCPI), which provides a 2900 km swath and retrieval accuracy for XCO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> (1 ppm), XCH<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub> (8 ppb), NO2 (1.3×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">15</sup> molec cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup>) and SIF (0.3 mW m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup> Sr<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup> nm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>); (2) the Hotspot Greenhouse gas Emission Tracker, with a 500-m nadir resolution, designed for tracking carbon emission hotspots; and (3) the Cloud Aerosol Polarization Imager (CAPI). Additionally, TanSat-2 is also equipped with the On board Intelligent Hotspot Extraction and Distribution Instrument (OHEDI), providing high-performance computing capabilities and Beidou short-message capabilities for near-real-time hotspot detection and distribution. The high-precision global observation from TanSat-2 is expected to provide essential data for global assimilation systems to assess global anthropogenic emissions for global carbon stock and other scientific purposes. The near-real time hotspot detection and distribution capability is also expected to be combined with other space-ground observation programs.

title: Rapid Hotspot Temperature Prediction of Magnetic Couplers in WPT Systems via a Simulation-Based Data-Driven Model
authors: Jun Ma, Shuai Dong, Bolong Wei, Jiafang He, Yousu Yao
abstract: The magnetic coupler is crucial for energy transfer in wireless power transfer (WPT) systems. With increasing demand for miniaturization and lightweight designs, higher power densities are required, which intensify thermal management challenges and raise the risk of thermal runaway. Therefore, accurately and rapidly predicting the hotspot temperature is essential for assessing cooling system performance and ensuring the safe operation of WPT systems. This paper proposes a method for predicting hotspot temperatures in magnetic couplers using a simulation-based data-driven model. By analyzing the nonlinear coupling effects of power losses, misalignment, and cooling conditions on the hotspot temperature, an electromagnetic–thermal coupled simulation model is developed to generate a dataset. A simulation-based data-driven model for temperature prediction is then created using support vector regression (SVR) optimized by a genetic algorithm. The proposed method enables the fast and accurate calculation of hotspot temperatures, demonstrating its broad applicability. An experimental prototype is constructed to validate the effectiveness of the method.

title: Research on New Power System Grid-Connected Energy Storage Control Strategy Based on Adaptive Adjustment
authors: Zhiyin Tian, Shuyao Chen, Jianmin Ye
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The development of new power systems has encountered challenges in energy transition due to insufficient grid support capacity from renewable energy sources. To address the significant reduction in transient stability margin and inadequate grid integration stability when grid-connected energy storage converters are integrated with renewable energy systems, this study proposes an adaptive impedance-based parameter collaborative control strategy. First, we analyze the topology of grid-connected energy storage systems combined with renewable energy sources and establish an equivalent model for energy storage converter integration. Based on virtual synchronous control principles, we propose coordinated control of rotational inertia J and damping coefficient D to enhance transient support capacity and oscillation suppression performance. This enables grid-connected energy storage systems to adaptively adjust support intensity according to angular velocity rate of change and frequency deviations, achieving substantial improvement in system transient stability. Finally, simulation models of renewable-energy-storage hybrid systems were developed in Matlab/Simulink to validate the proposed solution. Results demonstrate that the approach ensures system stability under grid connection conditions and disturbances, while the adaptive impedance-based parameter collaborative control method simultaneously improves grid stability during fluctuations and optimizes energy storage converter output power.

title: Dreaming with MAKOTO: Co-Designing an "Older Adult" Voice Assistant Intervention for Implicit Ageism
authors: Katie Seaborn, Mizuki Watanabe, Émilie Fabre, Paul Riesch, Somang Nam, Yuto Mandai, Miu Kojima
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: Knowledge Infrastructuring in the Large-Scale: Sparring Partners and Reflective Spaces in Participatory Design

authors: Van Nguyen, Xingliang Yuan, Tingmin Wu, Surya Nepal, Marthie Grobler, Carsten Rudolph
