title: Study on the Hydrogen Embrittlement Mechanism of Key Metal Materials in Hydrogen Transmission Pipelines
authors: Mingtao Yi, Wanli Ma, Guoxi Chen, Mengtian Li, Liya Zhu, Fengshuang Han
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Hydrogen energy serves as a clean and efficient zero-carbon energy source and is a key vehicle for replacing fossil fuels and achieving a low-carbon transition in the energy system. However, its large-scale application faces a “storage and transportation” bottleneck. The low density and flammability of hydrogen, combined with the major safety hazard of “hydrogen embrittlement”—hydrogen-induced degradation of mechanical properties and crack initiation in materials—pose challenges for high-pressure pipeline transportation. This work focused on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha-\text{Fe}$</tex>, a mainstream material for hydrogen pipelines. We employed first-principles calculations based on Density Functional Theory (DFT) to systematically investigate hydrogen adsorption-dissociation, penetration, and bulk diffusion behaviors on different crystal planes at the atomic scale. We quantitatively determined the energy barriers for each step and identified the rate-limiting steps. We also evaluated the hydrogen embrittlement sensitivity of different surface structures. Our study provides a theoretical basis for the targeted design of hydrogen-embrittlement-resistant surfaces and for ensuring the safe operation of high-pressure hydrogen pipelines. The results show that the surface termination and atomic configuration significantly influence the energy barriers for hydrogen dissociation and penetration. We conclude that tailoring the preferred crystal orientation at the pipeline's inner surface can effectively mitigate hydrogen embrittlement risks. This finding offers crucial guidance for developing advanced high-performance hydrogen transportation pipeline materials.

title: Proactive Resilience-Enhancement Strategy for Power Grids Under Icing Operation Scenarios Based on Tracking Pre-Dispatching Trajectory of Flexible Resources
authors: Kaiyuan Yu, Jiayi Ruan, Yan Liu, Dan Zhang, Mingyang Li
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Under extreme icing operation scenarios, the accompanying severe ice accretion leads to a surge in heating loads and an abrupt drop in renewable energy generation, causing the power grid to face critical “source-load mismatch” risks and severe power supply shortages. With the enhancement of the grid's multi-tier flexible resource regulation capabilities, it is feasible to leverage the inter-temporal energy-shifting ability of energy storage systems and mobilize demand-side resources to mitigate power deficits under extreme conditions. This paper first constructs a “source-grid-load” uncertainty set under icing operation scenarios. Taking the minimization of the expected comprehensive operating cost under the worst-case probability distribution as the global optimization objective, it proposes a dayahead and intra-day proactive resilience-enhancement dispatch strategy based on energy storage pre-dispatch and adaptive state-of-charge tracking, thereby achieving adaptive source-load regulation during the evolution of the icing scenarios. Finally, a case study based on a modified IEEE 39-bus system verifies that the proposed method can effectively reduce the load-shedding risk of the system.

title: Stability Analysis and Computational Modeling of New Energy Integration for Smart Grids
authors: Zhiyu Xu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the randomness and intermittency introduced by large-scale wind and photovoltaic integration in smart grids, this paper investigates a regional system with a renewable penetration level of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{4 5 \%}$</tex> and develops a quantitative stability assessment framework. The mechanisms by which renewable fluctuations affect frequency, voltage, and rotor-angle stability are first analyzed, revealing that volatile injections can enlarge the frequency deviation to 0.25 Hz and increase the voltage fluctuation amplitude by 1.2 times under stressed operating conditions. An integrated computational model is then established by jointly considering renewable uncertainty, coordinated energy-storage regulation, and flexible-load response. The proposed model unifies static, transient, and small-signal stability assessment while improving probabilistic power-flow calculation efficiency. Multi-scenario simulations show that the average error of stability-margin estimation is only 2.8 %, which is 51 % more accurate than a conventional deterministic benchmark. After coordinated control is enabled, the voltage-stability margin increases by 32 % and the transient stability window is extended by 180 ms. The framework can rapidly identify weak buses and risky time intervals, providing useful support for renewable accommodation, operational scheduling, and security defense in high-renewable smart grids.

title: Active Power Support and Coordination Control Strategy for Grid-Forming STATCOM Based on Model Predictive Control
authors: Ziheng Ma, Haoyu Li, Baoni Sun, Shuailin Lv
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of renewable energy, modern distribution networks exhibit pronounced “weak grid” characteristics, rendering them highly susceptible to transient voltage sags and active power imbalances. Traditional Static Synchronous Compensators (STATCOMs) are fundamentally restricted to purely reactive power compensation due to the lack of an active power buffer, while conventional proportionalintegral (PI) controllers exhibit inherent dynamic delays during grid faults. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an active-power-supported multi-mode coordination control strategy for a grid-forming Energy Storage STATCOM (ES-STATCOM). Integrating a high-power-density supercapacitor (SC) into the DC link breaks the physical limitations of traditional devices, enabling simultaneous, decoupled four-quadrant power regulation. At the system level, a robust multi-mode power allocation logic navigates the boundaries between the inverter's maximum continuous overcurrent limits and the SC's state-of-charge (SOC) safety constraints. This ensures maximum emergency reactive power injection during faults while safely facilitating active power collaborative smoothing normally. At the algorithmic level, a discrete-time finite-control-set model predictive control (FCSMPC) is implemented. By directly evaluating a cost function without a modulation stage, it effectively overcomes the dynamic delays of PI architectures. Extensive Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) real-time experiments validate that the proposed strategy achieves rapid, steady-state-error-free tracking of transient commands within a 10 ms window, maximizing transient support efficacy while rigorously guaranteeing equipment safety for power systems.

title: A Survey on Wearable Inertial Sensing for Indoor Positioning
authors: Yinqi Zhang, Xu Feng, Zhiyuan Luo, Khuong An Nguyen
abstract: Despite two decades of research, ubiquitous infrastructure-free indoor positioning remains a major challenge. In recent years, the emergence of wearable devices equipped with multiple sensors has presented an opportunity to address this problem. However, despite the popularity of wearable inertial positioning research, the field still lacks a comprehensive review that consolidates progress and helps guide further work. Thus, this paper presents the first systematic review of this field, analysing over 120 studies using novel taxonomies. We propose four key analytical perspectives: sensor types, body placement, positioning algorithms, and performance evaluation. We also propose a new taxonomy that categorises positioning algorithms into inertial measurement unit based methods, classical stochastic frameworks, multi-sensor fusion, and Machine Learning techniques. We provide detailed comparisons of the studies across body parts, signal types, IMU types, algorithms, path characteristics, testbed types, 2D/3D dimensions, and error metrics. Our findings reveal that multi-location IMU configurations significantly outperform single-location setups, with upper body and whole body placements achieving the best results. When evaluated using relative pose error to account for differences in trajectory length, ML based methods achieve the lowest per-unit-distance drift, suggesting that their apparent underperformance in some benchmarks is substantially influenced by the longer paths over which they are typically tested. While IMU only systems dominate due to their cost-effectiveness, ultra-wideband (UWB) fusion delivers superior stability and accuracy. Our findings indicate that strategic multi-sensor integration offers the best balance between performance and complexity for real-world deployment.

title: Blockchain-Based Secure and Efficient Federated Learning Scheme for IoMT Consumer Electronics Devices
authors: Yang Xue, Yunkai Zhai, Yan Qiao, Di Zhang, Ahmed Ghoneim
abstract: The Internet of Medical Things consumer electronics (IoMT-CE) devices integrated with deep learning (DL) model have achieved remarkable success in the medical field, which provides intelligent diagnostic assistance. However, as DL technologies evolve rapidly, a key challenge lies in how to continuously enhance the intelligent assisted diagnostic capabilities of IoMT-CE devices. Federated learning (FL), an effective distributed learning framework, holds great potential to address this issue. In this article, we propose a novel secure and efficient FL scheme for IoMT-CE devices based on consortium blockchain. In this scheme, all participating hospitals, the aggregation server (AS), and the noise parameter server (NPS) establish a consortium blockchain, where hash values and signature data are stored. Paillier homomorphic encryption (HE) and noise mechanisms are employed to protect patient privacy, while an elliptic curve cryptography-based proxy re-encryption (ECC-PRE) strategy is adopted to ensure model security between the AS and IoMT-CE devices. Furthermore, this scheme guarantees that model updates are applied correctly, even when local models cannot be uploaded to hospitals. Finally, the performance of our proposed scheme is verified. The results demonstrate that the encryption mechanisms have almost no impact on model accuracy, and the proposed scheme proves to be robust and fault-tolerant in cases of disconnection.

title: MFDR++: Robust and Efficient Multivariate Time Series Forecasting with Deep Reconstruction
authors: Zhenyang Zhang, Mingqiang Yuan, Xiong Ge, Donghui Wang, Ruoxuan Luan, Zhiqi Song, Wei Li
abstract: Recent advances in deep learning–based multivariate time series (MTS) forecasting indicate that increasing model complexity does not necessarily yield better accuracy, largely due to improper inductive biases in modeling intra- and interseries relations. To address these issues, this paper presents MFDR++, an enhanced forecasting framework that substantially extends the original MFDR by improving both reconstruction-aware intraseries modeling and statistically grounded interseries dependency learning. For intraseries modeling, MFDR++ employswavelet-based multiresolution analysis while explicitly mitigating boundary distortions introduced by wavelet padding. A learnable truncated Gaussian weighting function is incorporated into the reconstruction loss to emphasize the end of the input sequence, which is most critical for ensuring continuity between input and prediction horizons. For interseries modeling, we revisit Cucconi attention and reveal its inefficiency in high-dimensional latent spaces. To overcome this limitation, MFDR++ transforms temporal feature maps into the frequency domain and preserves dominant shared spectral components, upon which a novel Cucconi spectral attention mechanism is constructed to capture interseries relations more effectively and efficiently. Extensive experiments on eight benchmark datasets demonstrate that MFDR++ consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance and improves robustness under diverse forecasting settings.

title: Lightweight Multi-Channel Generative Adversarial Network for Synthesizing Brain Perfusion CT Series from Healthy Subjects: Toward Radiation Dose Reduction in Clinical Imaging
authors: Lixin Zhou, Kaibing Hang, Wei Zhao, Yunfeng Yang, Qianhui Shen, Yuan Wang, Jiecheng Gao, Shuping Li, Yuanyuan Yang
abstract: Computed tomography perfusion (CTP) is essential for ischemic stroke assessment but involves high radiation exposure. This study proposes a lightweight multi-channel generative adversarial network (LM-GAN) to synthesize missing series from sparse CTP series, enabling substantial dose reduction. Specifically, by successfully synthesizing the nine unacquired volumes required for a complete 19-time point CTP series from an initial acquisition of only 10 sparsely acquired volumes per examination, our approach yields an estimated dose-equivalent reduction of 47.3% relative to the standard full-phase acquisition protocol. LM-GAN integrates a lightweight U-Net generator incorporating depthwise separable convolutions, attention mechanisms, and LiteViT modules for efficient spatiotemporal feature extraction, paired with a multi-scale PatchGAN discriminator. The model is optimized using LSGAN adversarial, L1 content, and L1 temporal consistency losses to ensure image authenticity, structural accuracy, and temporal coherence. Evaluated on healthy subjects, LM-GAN outperformed existing methods in image quality metrics and demonstrated superior temporal modeling, particularly in late-phase imaging. Synthesized time-density curves showed high congruence with real data, and key perfusion parameters achieved excellent consistency. Ablation studies confirmed the contribution of each module and the model's computational efficiency. LM-GAN lays a promising foundation for clinical low-dose brain perfusion imaging, and its potential applicability to broader patient populations merits continued investigation.

title: Quantum-Classical Dual LSTM Optimization for Internet of Intelligent Vehicles
authors: Muhammad Mustafa Umar Gondel, Uman Khalid, Trung Q. Duong, Een-Kee Hong, Hyundong Shin
abstract: The growing complexity of urban transportation networks demands intelligent, data-driven systems capable of real-time perception, optimization, and prediction. Within the Internet of intelligent Vehicles (IoIV), optimal sensor placement is essential for enhancing distributed edge intelligence, as it significantly improves traffic observability, ensures accurate data collection, and enables dynamic decision-making. In this context, we propose a dual approach for hybrid quantum-classical (HQC) optimization—classical for quantum and quantum for classical. First, we formulate the optimal sensor placement problem within the quantum approximate optimization algorithm (QAOA) framework using a minimum vertex cover approach to address its NP-hard nature. Moreover, we enhance the QAOA by integrating long short-term memory (LSTM) networks to adaptively optimize its parameters, thereby achieving faster convergence and improved surveillance coverage in urban transport networks. Second, we employ quantum LSTM (QLSTM) networks to predict traffic flow from data collected by the optimally placed sensors. The QLSTM model reduces the number of learnable parameters while maintaining the expressive capability of classical LSTM architectures. This semantic information is then leveraged to manage traffic flow more effectively, predicting traffic patterns and supporting proactive decision-making. Experimental results demonstrate that the LSTM-guided QAOA outperforms conventional optimization methods such as stochastic gradient descent, achieving faster and more reliable convergence. Likewise, the QLSTM model attains superior predictive accuracy, as evidenced by significant improvements in the explained variance score and root mean squared error. Collectively, these advancements represent a substantial step forward in intelligent traffic management and highlight the practical potential of HQC machine intelligence in IoIV systems.

title: A Terrain-Interactive Autonomous Switching Control Strategy for the Land-Air Bimodal Robot
authors: Song Yang, Wenshuai Yu, Shoubin Chen, Fei Ma, Zhou Liu, Qingquan Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: In recent years, land-air bimodal robots, which leverage the extended endurance of wheeled robots and the maneuverability of quadrotor robots, have garnered significant attention. This paper addresses the challenges of motion control and autonomous mode-switching in land-air bimodal robots navigating sloping terrains with unknown slope angles and varying surface materials. Firstly, we provide a comprehensive analysis of the robot’s dynamics models in both modes, representing them as a uniform second-order nonlinear state-space formulation incorporating unknown uncertainties. This uniformly represented formulation is beneficial to controller design for two modes and facilitates the deployment of the bimodal controller in a low-cost microcontroller. Then, a terrain-aware controller is proposed for land mode without the accurate dynamics models, and it can overcome the influence of unknown uncertainties brought by unstructured sloping terrains. Subsequently, in order to improve the robot’s endurance, an autonomous mode-switching control strategy is explored for maximizing the use of wheeled land mode to cross sloping terrains. Finally, we conduct the first real-world experiment with a self-developed land-air robot in some sloping terrains with different slope angles and surface materials to validate the accessibility in three-dimensional space of the land-air bimodal robot with the proposed switching control strategy.

title: A Bitmap-Guided Collision Tree Anti-collision Protocol for Large-Scale RFID Tags Identification
authors: Hongquan Zhou, Xiaolin Jia, Zhong Du, Hong Yang, Qi Jiang
abstract: During the collision resolution phase, many deterministic RFID anti-collision protocols require tags to repeatedly transmit identical ID segments across multiple query cycles. This redundant transmission leads to high communication overhead, increasing identification latency and energy consumption, thereby constraining system performance and scalability. This paper proposes the Bitmap-Guided Collision Tree (BGCT) protocol to address this limitation. BGCT reduces tag transmissions through a progressive information acquisition mechanism, and enables multi-way collision resolution by integrating vector-based grouping with a bitmap. Theoretical analysis and simulation results demonstrate BGCT’s performance advantages. Specifically, for identifying 10000 tags(ID=96 bits), BGCT reduces the total identification time by 27.8% compared to the best competitor in this scenario, EMDT. For 256-bit IDs, assuming uniform or pseudo-random ID distributions, the progressive mechanism mitigates the dependency of communication cost on ID length, maintaining a low expected overhead in contrast to the linear growth observed in other protocols. Furthermore, under non-uniform ID distributions, BGCT demonstrates a collision resolution efficiency 152.9% higher than that of the competing protocols.

title: Near-Data Compaction for LSM Tree on Rack-scale Disaggregated Storage
authors: Sungho Moon, Daegyu Han, Hera Koo, Sangeun Chae, Duck-Ho Bae, Euiseong Seo, Beomseok Nam
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: In LSM trees, background compaction tasks contend with foreground queries for CPU cycles, cache space, and also SAN bandwidth, if deployed in a disaggregated storage architecture. This study proposes Near-Data Compaction (NDC), which executes compaction on the storage node to utilize its underutilized computing resources. However, enabling NDC introduces several challenges. First, it has to support concurrent file access from both compute and storage nodes. In addition, it has to decide which compaction tasks to be executed on the storage node since the computing resources of a storage node are not unlimited. This study presents TetherDB, an LSM tree for disaggregated storage architecture that addresses these challenges through lightweight dual-node coordination and selective NDC admission policies. Our evaluation demonstrates that TetherDB improves throughput by up to 2.1× compared to RocksDB in write-heavy workloads.

title: A Self-Supervised Deep Stochastic Configuration Network with Contrastive Learning and Probabilistic Explanation for Complex Industrial Fault Detection
authors: Lin Hou, Yanfeng Geng, Ping Wang, Xiaogang Deng
abstract: Deep stochastic configuration network (DSCN) has demonstrated great potential in supervised machine learning tasks (e.g. data regression and pattern classification) due to its advantages of efficient model training and adaptive structure determination. However, its application in industrial fault detection scenarios remains unexplored, where only one class of normal training data is available. To handle this issue, this paper presents a self-supervised DSCN Model, called Contrast Learning-assisted One-class DSCN (CL-ODSCN), for fault detection in complex industrial systems. It consists of four contributions. (1) A one-class DSCN fault detection framework is built based on the self-learning philosophy, where feature information enhancement module is designed between two consecutive feature extraction layers to capture rich feature information for effectively monitoring the anomalous behaviors. (2) For effective fault detection, a modified objective function using contrast learning is constructed for strengthening the model discriminative capability for abnormal samples. (3) To implement automatic node configuration, a supervisory mechanism for model training is presented. (4) A probabilistic fault explanation strategy is developed by combining the Shapley value method and the Bayesian inference theory to locate fault-related input variables. Two industrial cases, including a benchmark chemical system and a cementing pump unit, are used to validate the proposed model. The results demonstrate the CL-ODSCN model achieves better fault detection performance than the compared models.

title: High-Velocity Target Detection and Estimation via Block-Sparse Recovery
authors: Yongkang Ding, Xiao-Ping Zhang, Wen-Jie Ma, Yuhan Dong, Zhenyu Liu
abstract: In this paper, we develop theories and an algorithm for the detection and estimation of high-velocity targets using pulse-Doppler radars. First, we prove that the high velocities of targets lead to range and velocity migrations proportional to the velocities, thus leading to block-sparse structure of the range-velocity spectrum. Then, based on this theory, we formulate the detection and estimation task as a block-sparse recovery problem and propose an algorithm named pulse-Doppler total variation regularized sparse Bayesian learning (PD-TVSBL) to solve this problem, thus recovering the range-velocity spectrum of the targets. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves a gain of 11 dB in terms of signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for accurate detection compared to the conventional iterative soft thresholding (IST) method. Moreover, the performance of the proposed method approaches that of a genie-aided method, whose performance is the theoretical optimum of block-sparse modeling, in the medium to high SNR regime. This paper provides a solid solution for accurate detection and estimation of high-velocity targets for pulse-Doppler radars.

title: Tuning Glass-Transition Dynamics and Ion Transport by Fluorination in DBU-Based Protic Ionic Liquids
authors: Li Niu, Runhong Wei, Jessica Mangialetto, Yuhang Zhang, Jinhai You, Michael Wübbenhorst
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Protic ionic liquids (PILs) offer a suitable model system for examining how structural dynamics and ionic transport are interrelated in glass-forming materials. Here, the glass-transition behavior and structural relaxation of two DBU-based PILs, [DBU][TFO] and [DBU][MS], are studied by combining differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) with broadband dielectric spectroscopy (BDS) over broad temperature and frequency ranges. The substitution of the methanesulfonic acid derived MS-anion by the fluorinated TFO- anion results in a clear downshift of the glass-transition temperature (Tg). Like in previous work, Tg’s were obtained consistently by DSC, the activation-energy fine-structure analysis and the temperature dependence of the structural relaxation time τ∝. The dc-conductivity σdc follows VFT behaviour and nearly collapses to a single curve when plotted on a reduced temperature scale, T/Tg. This indicates that ionic transport is primarily controlled by glass-transition dynamics, which is in line with a nearly Debye-Stokes-Einstein (DSE) behavior considering σdc and τ∝. Molecular dynamics simulations for [DBU][TFO] further support this interpretation by showing normal diffusive motion with a temperature dependence that qualifies the temperature dependence of the ionic diffusion coefficient as the dominant contribution to σdc(T). These results provide a conclusive picture for understanding DBU-based PILs and serve as a reliable basis for extending our analysis to more complex ionic liquid systems.

title: CVGD: Cross-View Guided Disentangler for Multi-View Radar Semantic Segmentation
authors: Yaoyu He, Yuan Feng, Tao Shan, Nan Wang
abstract: Neural networks continue to face challenges in effectively extracting information from the high-dimensional and sparse range-azimuth-Doppler (RAD) tensor. While multi-view architectures using three-axis projections of the RAD tensor improve efficiency and maintain accuracy, existing designs suffer from inefficient inter-view information interaction. To address this issue, this letter proposes a new multi-view information fusion viewpoint: instead of directly blending feature maps, the orthogonal relationships among the RAD coordinate axes are leveraged to guide feature decomposition across views, thereby disentangling the mixed semantics introduced during view compression. Based on this idea, a new module, termed Cross-View Guided Disentangler (CVGD), is introduced to enable inter-view information interaction in multi-view architectures. Extensive experiments on the public CARRADA dataset demonstrate that networks incorporating the proposed module achieve performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods, while utilizing 50% fewer parameters and attaining a 6-fold increase in inference speed. Additionally, promising results are also observed on RADIal, achieving near-SOTA performance with higher efficiency.

title: SSTA-X: GPU-Accelerated First-Order Block-Based Statistical Static Timing Analysis
authors: Chih-Chun Chang, Yi-Hua Chung, Wan Luan Lee, Boyang Zhang, Tsung-Wei Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Statistical static timing analysis (SSTA) is a widely used approach to evaluate circuit timing by modeling gate and path delays as random variables affected by process variations. While Monte Carlo-based SSTA methods offer high accuracy, their reliance on massive sampling leads to very long runtime. To alleviate this runtime overhead, first-order block-based SSTA (FB-SSTA) has been proposed to approximate timing distributions through linear propagation of statistical moments. However, existing FB-SSTA solutions are largely limited to CPU-based parallelism, and their scalability can no longer quickly respond to the ever-growing size of SSTA problems. To overcome this limitation, we propose SSTA-X, a GPU-accelerated FB-SSTA algorithm that significantly enhances the runtime performance by leveraging the massive parallelism of modern GPU. SSTA-X introduces GPU-efficient data structures and kernel algorithms to accelerate key yet time-consuming operations in statistical delay, slew, and moment propagation. Experimental results show that SSTA-X achieves 3.10×–12.47× speedup over a CPU-based baseline on a large-scale design with millions of gates.

title: Enhancing sea surface current retrieval from Geostationary Ocean Color Imager Observations: Multispectral Optimization and Validation with HF Radar
authors: Zifeng Hu, Xiaodong Chen, Junfang Lin, Katsuto Uehara, Linke Yuan, Jian Zhou
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Understanding surface current variability is critical for marine environmental monitoring and oceanographic applications. This study assesses the performance of the Geostationary Ocean Color Imager (GOCI) in retrieving sea surface currents using the Window Deformation Iterative Multigrid (WIDIM) method. We evaluated seven GOCI Level-2 optical products, including total suspended material (TSM), chlorophyll-a concentration (CHL), diffuse attenuation coefficient at 490 nm (Kd490), and normalized water-leaving radiance (nLw) at multiple wavelengths, over a 14-day period in the Korea/Tsushima Strait. The GOCI-derived surface currents are validated against concurrent high-frequency (HF) radar observations. Among all products, nLw(490) and nLw(555) consistently show superior performance, accurately capturing both spatial structures and temporal variability of surface currents, with higher correlations (>0.6), lower RMSEs (0.6) than other products. Our findings highlight the capability of GOCI, particularly in the blue green spectrum, for mapping sea surface currents with high spatiotemporal resolution, offering valuable input for regional ocean circulation studies.

title: Hierarchical Aggregation of Wind Farm Frequency Support Capability Based on Hybrid Control Strategy
authors: Songyan Zhang, Di Liu, Chao Lu
abstract: To address the challenge of scalable coordination among massive wind turbines (WTs) managed by different wind farms (WFs) in primary frequency control (PFC), this paper proposes a hierarchical framework for the aggregation and utilization of WF frequency support capability (FSC) to achieve a prescribed frequency performance indicator (PFPI). First, a hybrid control strategy is developed as the control foundation, whose parameters can be reverse-engineered from the PFPI. It enables secondary frequency drop (SFD) mitigation and smooth transition between rotor kinetic energy (RKE)–based support and persistent reserve release. Second, WT-FSC is defined as a WT's maximum duty factor of the system-level PFC loop within the hybrid control strategy. WT-FSC assessment is formulated as an optimization problem, which considers wake effect, rotor dynamics, WT safety constraints and time-varying system frequency response (SFR) model. Third, to support system-level frequency support service dispatch, WT-level FSC and control cost function are obtained and then aggregated into those of WF-level. Thus, the system operator only needs to make decisions on the duty factors of each WF to optimally utilize all the WT resources. Case studies demonstrate that our framework shows satisfactory assessment accuracy and utilization efficiency of WF-FSC and can enhance system frequency dynamics.

title: IEEE Power & Energy Society Information
authors: N/A

title: 3D Single-Tooth Reconstruction via Curriculum Learning and Topology-aware Generation
authors: Fucheng Niu, Jiaqi Chen, Mengqi Liang, Jingfei Cheng, Zhengqi Qin, Hui Liu, Lin Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Automated single-tooth reconstruction is a pivotal task in modern digital dentistry, yet existing point cloud completion methods often lack the specialized anatomical priors and spatial constraints necessary for clinical integration. In this paper, we propose Tooth-STR, a hierarchical generative framework designed to synthesize anatomically high-fidelity and collision-free tooth models from partial intraoral scans. Our Tooth-STR consists of a Topology-Aware Coarse Generation (TACG) module that utilizes differential edge convolutions to reason about the missing dental manifold based on the topological trends of the surrounding dentition, and a Curriculum Anatomical Constraint (C<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) strategy that implements a phased, differentiable boundary penalty to balance unconstrained geometric exploration with strict spatial regularization. Extensive experiments on the constructed Tooth-STR dataset demonstrate that our method significantly improves reconstruction performance across various tooth categories.
